attributes.title: Paishacha Rajasika — The Isolated Indulger Named after the Pishāchas, beings of the shadow: appetite unchecked by awareness The Paishacha-rajasic individual experiences life through the lens of appetite and compulsion. Their inner world is dominated by hunger—not just for food, but for sensory experience in all its forms. They are gluttonous, with a ravenous quality to their desires. They may live in luxury yet feel perpetually unsatisfied. They love company but often keep it in secret, and may struggle with secretive indulgences. They may show a lack of engagement in mental or intellectual activities and a tendency toward disregard for cleanliness and social norms. They may prefer isolation and struggle with self-regulation—the boundary between enough and too much is perpetually blurred. In lived experience, the Paishacha type is the person caught in cycles of bingeing and withdrawal—whether with food, substances, relationships, or experiences. Their suffering is the suffering of a void that cannot be filled from the outside. The path toward balance involves learning to turn awareness inward—to recognize that the hunger is not for more stimulation but for a quality of presence that can only be found through stillness and self-knowledge.
attributes.aspect: Paishacha Rajasika — The Isolated Indulger Named after the Pishāchas, beings of the shadow: appetite unchecked by awareness The Paishacha-rajasic individual experiences life through the lens of appetite and compulsion. Their inner world is dominated by hunger—not just for food, but for sensory experience in all its forms. They are gluttonous, with a ravenous quality to their desires. They may live in luxury yet feel perpetually unsatisfied. They love company but often keep it in secret, and may struggle with secretive indulgences. They may show a lack of engagement in mental or intellectual activities and a tendency toward disregard for cleanliness and social norms. They may prefer isolation and struggle with self-regulation—the boundary between enough and too much is perpetually blurred. In lived experience, the Paishacha type is the person caught in cycles of bingeing and withdrawal—whether with food, substances, relationships, or experiences. Their suffering is the suffering of a void that cannot be filled from the outside. attributes.title: Sarpa Rajasika — The Cunning Survivor Named after the Sarpa (serpent): watchful, coiled, and unpredictable The Sarpa-rajasic individual experiences life as a landscape of threat and opportunity. Their inner world is one of watchful tension—quick-witted but prone to anxiety and apprehension. They are usually indolent, yet become heroic in anger; timid in daily life but capable of sudden, intense aggression when they feel cornered. They are expert at counselling others but often use indirect means, trickery, or deceit to achieve their own aims. They have strong habits and are given to the pleasures of the senses, yet there is always a cautious, calculating quality to their indulgence. Their actions are often unpredictable, blending fear with cunning in a way that keeps others off balance. In lived experience, the Sarpa type is the person who is difficult to read—charming and helpful one moment, sharp and vengeful the next. Their suffering is the suffering of chronic vigilance: because they see the world as threatening, they can never fully relax, and their relationships are often undermined by the very suspicion and manipulation that they believe protects them. The path toward balance involves cultivating trust—learning, slowly, that not every situation demands a defensive posture, and that vulnerability is not the same as weakness.

attributes.title: Preta Rajasika — The Hungry Ghost Named after the Pretas, spirits of insatiable craving: desire without fulfilment The Preta-rajasic individual experiences life through a lens of chronic dissatisfaction and craving. Their inner world is marked by greed, irritability, and a pervasive sense of dejection. They love food and are deeply attached to material comforts, yet they are disinclined to work—the gap between desire and effort creates a perpetual frustration. They have no power of discrimination and are extremely jealous. Their conduct, character, and pastimes are such as to involve others in suffering. They may harbour personal vendettas or spread hatred, driven by a sense of having been denied what they deserve. There is a fundamentally acquisitive quality to their engagement with the world, yet no acquisition brings lasting satisfaction. In lived experience, the Preta type is the chronically dissatisfied person—the one who complains that others have what they deserve, who hoards yet feels impoverished, who desires yet resents the effort required to obtain. Their suffering is the suffering of the hungry ghost: surrounded by abundance yet unable to receive nourishment. The path toward balance involves the practice of gratitude and generosity—the deliberate cultivation of satisfaction with what one has, and the discovery that giving is the most effective cure for the feeling of lack.

attributes.title: Shakuni Rajasika — The Restless Seeker Named after the Shakuni (bird): always in flight, never settled The Shakuni-rajasic individual experiences life as a constant search for the next stimulation. Their inner world is one of fickleness and restless movement—they are always seeking food, recreation, and new experiences, but their attention never rests on anything for long. They are passionate but intolerant, incapable of sustained remunerative work, suspicious of others, and prone to strong attachments that form quickly and dissolve just as fast. They have strong sexual desires and a tendency toward excessive indulgence, yet their relationship with pleasure is characterized by variability—they are easily distracted by the next possibility before the current one has been fully experienced. They are unacquisitive in the long term because they lack the sustained attention needed for accumulation. In lived experience, the Shakuni type is the person who flits from interest to interest, relationship to relationship, plan to plan—leaving a trail of unfinished projects and unfulfilled promises. Their suffering is the suffering of scattering: because attention is always divided, no experience is ever fully received, and the deep satisfaction that comes from sustained engagement remains elusive. The path toward balance involves the practice of concentration—learning to rest attention on one thing, one person, one purpose long enough for depth to emerge.

additional_info.heading: Chapter 5: The Three Tamasic Archetypes
additional_info.text: The three tamasic personality types represent consciousness at its most veiled and constricted. Tamas is the quality of inertia, darkness, and concealment—the force that resists change, obscures understanding, and collapses awareness into the narrowest possible field. The tamasic types are named after beings at the threshold of sentience: animal, fish, and plant. This naming is not meant to be demeaning—it is a phenomenological description of what it feels like to experience life when the quality of awareness is at its most limited. It is also, crucially, a description of a state that can be transformed: the Vedic tradition holds that consciousness can evolve from tamas through rajas to sattva, and that no state is permanent.

attributes.title: Pashu (Pashava) Tamasa — The Creature of Habit Named after Pashu (animal): instinctive existence, unreflective and passive The Pashu-tamasic individual experiences life at the level of basic instinct and routine. Their inner world is one of dullness, absence of sustained reflection, and passivity. They are mentally deficient in terms of intellectual engagement—not necessarily incapable, but deeply resistant to the effort that thinking requires. They are absent-minded, slow-moving, timid, foolish, and lazy. They are dirty in dress and ignorant in their dealings. They are given to eating, drinking, and sensuality—not with the passionate intensity of the rajasic types, but with the mechanical quality of an animal satisfying a drive. They are sleepy and prone to rejecting everything—new ideas, new people, new experiences. Their default response to the world is withdrawal and resistance. In lived experience, the Pashu type is the person who seems to exist rather than live—going through motions without engagement, responding to the world with the minimum possible effort. Their suffering is the suffering of unconsciousness: they are unable to articulate what is wrong because self-awareness itself is the faculty that tamas most effectively suppresses. The path toward balance begins with simple physical activity—movement, routine, the creation of basic structure—which introduces just enough rajas to begin loosening the grip of inertia.

attributes.title: Matsya Tamasa — The Driven Drifter Named after Matsya (fish): restless motion without direction or understanding The Matsya-tamasic individual experiences life as a kind of frightened restlessness—they are driven by instinctual urges but lack the cognitive resources to make sense of or direct their movement. They are mentally deficient, cowardly, prone to stupidity, gluttonous, fickle, and susceptible to anger and sensuality in rapid alternation. They love to travel and are drawn to water—a restless quality that is often mistaken for rajasic activity but is actually a tamasic flailing without purpose. Their inner world is marked by apprehension and a reactive quality: they respond to stimuli not with understanding but with reflex. Their engagement with the world is primarily driven by the most basic needs—hunger, fear, and the pursuit of immediate sensory comfort. In lived experience, the Matsya type is the person who is always moving but never arriving—changing jobs, changing cities, changing partners, but always carrying the same confusion and restlessness with them. Their suffering is the suffering of disorientation: they lack the inner compass that would allow them to distinguish meaningful action from mere agitation. The path toward balance involves grounding—establishing stability in one place, one relationship, one practice—long enough for the turbid waters of the mind to begin to settle.

attributes.title: Vanaspati Tamasa — The Still Sleeper Named after Vanaspati (plant): existence without volition or awareness The Vanaspati-tamasic individual represents the deepest level of tamasic experience. Their inner world is one of near-total absence of mental activity—extreme laziness, complete indifference to surroundings, and minimal engagement with the world. They spend most of their time eating and sleeping. They are completely devoid of all mental faculties as traditionally defined—lacking initiative, curiosity, ambition, and even the restless agitation that still animates the Pashu and Matsya types. They show the lowest level of mental activity and significant indifference to everything around them. Psychologically, they are characterized by extreme passivity, a complete lack of initiative, and a mode of existence that resembles a static form of life. In lived experience, the Vanaspati type is the person who has essentially withdrawn from participation in life—not through the active choice of a contemplative, but through the weight of inertia that has compressed awareness to its minimal expression. Their suffering is invisible even to themselves because the capacity for self-reflection that would allow them to recognize suffering has been almost entirely suppressed. The path toward balance is the longest and most difficult of all the types, requiring external intervention: compassionate others who introduce stimulation, structure, and care—the first rays of light entering a room that has been dark for a very long time.

additional_info.heading: Chapter 6: The Developmental Arc — From Inertia to Illumination
additional_info.text:
Perhaps the most powerful aspect of the guna framework is its insistence that personality is not destiny. The Bhagavad-Gītā teaches that the improvement of an individual from bondage and suffering comes through thorough knowledge of the three gunas and their functioning. Once a person becomes emancipated from all three gunas, they enjoy ultimate happiness. This is the developmental arc that Mathew (2001) formalized as the Poorna Chakra model: a framework for the evolution and involution of consciousness.

The movement is clear in principle, though demanding in practice. A person dominated by Tamas must first introduce Rajas—activity, discipline, structure, effort—to break the grip of inertia. A person dominated by Rajas must cultivate Sattva—stillness, reflection, ethical awareness, meditation—to transform agitation into clarity. And a person who has established themselves in Sattva must eventually transcend even that luminous quality, releasing the last subtle attachment to being a “good” or “pure” person, and resting in the witness-consciousness (Purusha) that is beyond all gunas.

Critically, the movement need not be sequential. As Mathew observes, one can move from Inertia directly to Stability, or from Activation directly to Stability. The end of personal growth, or the evolution of mind, is the attainment of super-stability: absolute sensitivity, integration, stress tolerance, invulnerability, and transcendence. This is not a fixed destination but a living state—an awareness so broad and integrated that it can meet any situation with appropriate response, free from the distortions of fear, craving, or dullness.

The practical implications are profound. When the gunas are out of balance, we experience the oscillation between the obstructing forces of inertia and negativity and the mobile forces of distraction and turbulence. Understanding the play of the gunas empowers us to make informed decisions about our practices and lifestyle choices in order to enhance their balance. Diet, sleep, relationships, work, creative expression, spiritual practice, time in nature, exposure to beauty, service to others—all of these shift the guna-balance in predictable ways. According to Ayurveda and Yoga, when we live in harmony with our own nature, we are naturally healthy and at ease.

additional_info.heading: Chapter 7: Integration — The Gunas, the Doshas, and the Koshas
additional_info.text:
The guna framework does not operate in isolation. It is embedded in a larger Vedic map of the human being that includes the five sheaths of existence (Pancha Koshas), the three doshas, the five elements, and the chakra system. Understanding how these frameworks interrelate reveals the full sophistication of the Vedic model of personality.

The Pancha Koshas describe five layers of human existence, from the grossest to the most subtle: Annamaya Kosha (the physical body, nourished by food), Pranamaya Kosha (the vital body, sustained by breath and water), Manomaya Kosha (the mental body, sustained by the quality of one’s experiences), Vijnanamaya Kosha (the intellectual body, sustained by discriminative understanding), and Anandamaya Kosha (the bliss body, where perfect balance, harmony, and health reside). The gunas pervade all five sheaths: a tamasic imbalance in the Annamaya Kosha manifests as physical heaviness and lethargy; in the Manomaya Kosha, it manifests as mental dullness and depression; in the Vijnanamaya Kosha, as poor discrimination and confusion.

The Tridoshas (Vata, Pitta, Kapha) operate primarily at the physical and vital levels. Vata, composed of Space and Air, governs movement and communication. Pitta, composed of Fire, governs transformation and digestion. Kapha, composed of Water and Earth, governs structure and stability. When the doshas become disturbed, they create specific patterns of mental illness that the classical texts describe with remarkable precision: Vatonmad (Vata-disturbance, resembling what modern psychiatry calls schizophrenia), Pittonmad (Pitta-disturbance, resembling mania), and Kaphonmad (Kapha-disturbance, resembling depression). The gunas and doshas interact: a person with a Pitta-dominant body and a Rakshasa-rajasic mind will experience a particular combination of physical inflammation and psychological aggression; a person with a Kapha-dominant body and a Pashu-tamasic mind will experience a particular combination of physical heaviness and psychological withdrawal.

The Chakra system provides yet another layer of mapping. The five elements are distributed across the chakras—Earth at the Muladhara (stability and support), Water at the Swadhishthana (joy and well-being), Fire at the Manipura (wisdom and power), Air at the Anahata (compassion), and Space at the Vishuddha, Ajna, and Sahasrara (trust, creativity, intuition, knowledge, and oneness). Each guna-type will tend to have particular chakra centres that are more active or blocked, creating signature patterns of physical, emotional, and spiritual experience.

additional_info.heading: Epilogue: A Map for the Journey
additional_info.text:
The sixteen personality types of the Manasika Prakriti are not diagnoses. They are mirrors—carefully crafted by millennia of contemplative observation—in which a human being can recognize the patterns of their own inner life and begin to understand why they suffer in the particular ways that they do. The Brahma-sattvic individual suffers differently from the Rakshasa-rajasic individual, who suffers differently from the Vanaspati-tamasic individual. But all suffer, and all suffer because the gunas are out of balance.

The promise of this framework is that balance is always possible. The gunas are not fixed traits but dynamic qualities that respond to conscious intervention. Through diet, practice, relationship, service, study, and meditation—through the whole range of tools that the Vedic tradition has refined over thousands of years—any individual can begin to shift their inner composition toward greater clarity, greater compassion, and greater freedom.

This is what the guna framework offers that modern psychology largely does not: not merely a description of how people differ, but a developmental map of how consciousness itself can evolve—from the heaviness of Tamas, through the fire of Rajas, to the luminous stillness of Sattva, and ultimately beyond all three into the freedom that the tradition calls Moksha. It is a map not for categorizing human beings but for liberating them.

Based on the Charaka Samhitā, Sushruta Samhitā, Bhagavad-Gītā, Sāmkhya Kārikā,

and the research of Mathew, Das, Wolf, Marutham, and others in the Indian psychological tradition.

additional_info.heading: Additional Concepts from the Wider Scriptural Corpus
additional_info.text: The following concepts, drawn from the Upaniṣads and Vedāntic sources, deepen the treatment of the mind — the four-fold inner instrument (antaḥkaraṇa), the faculty of will (saṅkalpa), the chariot allegory of the Kaṭha Upaniṣad, the sixteen kalās of the person, and the ego-faculty (ahaṁkāra).

attributes.title: Ahamkara (Ego/I-am-ness)
attributes.aspect: अहंकार   ·   Chapter 16 The nature of this aspect. Ahamkara is the ego-sense, the feeling of 'I' and 'mine.' It is the false identification with the body-mind and individual personality. Ahamkara creates separation and attachment.

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When Ahamkara is healthy, a person has appropriate self-respect, sense of identity, and healthy self-esteem. The ego functions to protect and organize experience without being destructive.
facets.somatic: Confident and upright posture, healthy body image, self-care and respect for body, good physical boundaries, strong physical presence.
facets.emotional: Healthy self-esteem and self-respect, emotional confidence, ability to receive and give love appropriately, healthy emotional boundaries, emotional stability and security in identity.
facets.cognitive: Clear sense of self and identity, appropriate self-confidence, ability to stand by convictions, mental organization and clarity, healthy differentiation from others.
facets.psychic: The soul holds the ego as a useful instrument — the 'I' that acts and relates — without mistaking it for its true self.

balanced.text:
Overview: When Ahamkara is healthy, a person has appropriate self-respect, sense of identity, and healthy self-esteem. The ego functions to protect and organize experience without being destructive.

Somatic: Confident and upright posture, healthy body image, self-care and respect for body, good physical boundaries, strong physical presence.

Emotional: Healthy self-esteem and self-respect, emotional confidence, ability to receive and give love appropriately, healthy emotional boundaries, emotional stability and security in identity.

Cognitive: Clear sense of self and identity, appropriate self-confidence, ability to stand by convictions, mental organization and clarity, healthy differentiation from others.

Psychic: The soul holds the ego as a useful instrument — the 'I' that acts and relates — without mistaking it for its true self.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Excessive Ahamkara creates arrogance, superiority complex, defensiveness, inability to accept criticism, and lack of empathy. It blocks spiritual growth.
facets.somatic: Rigid and defensive posture, physical aggression or domineering presence, poor treatment of body as extension of ego, inability to relax.
facets.emotional: Emotional defensiveness and rigidity, arrogance and superiority, inability to receive feedback or criticism, lack of empathy and compassion, emotional reactions to perceived slights, constant need for validation.
facets.cognitive: Mental arrogance and pride, inability to admit mistakes, defensive thinking patterns, constant judging of others, spiritual arrogance, inability to learn from others.
facets.psychic: The soul is wholly identified with the ego, every experience referred back to 'me and mine.'

Psychic: The soul is wholly identified with the ego, every experience referred back to 'me and mine.'

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Deficient Ahamkara leads to poor self-esteem, identity confusion, inability to establish healthy boundaries, self-harm, susceptibility to manipulation.
facets.somatic: Poor posture and weak physical presence, self-neglect and poor self-care, poor physical boundaries, vulnerability to physical harm, physical hesitancy and lack of confidence.
facets.emotional: Poor self-esteem and self-worth, emotional insecurity and anxiety, difficulty asserting needs, susceptibility to emotional manipulation, emotional dependence on others' approval.
facets.cognitive: Identity confusion, lack of sense of self, inability to make independent decisions, susceptibility to suggestions and manipulation, poor self-trust, mental fragility.
facets.psychic: The 'I'-sense is so weakened the soul cannot function as an effective agent in the world.

attributes.title: Sankalpa-Vikalpa (Mental Oscillation)
attributes.aspect: संकल्प-विकल्प   ·   Chapter 2, Chapter 6 The nature of this aspect. Sankalpa-Vikalpa describes the chaotic, dualistic nature of the lower mind (Manas) as it constantly vacillates between acceptance (attraction/Sankalpa) and rejection (repulsion/Vikalpa). This oscillation is the fundamental mechanism of mental suffering.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental (the deliberating / oscillating mind) · Ontic mode: Process (the mind’s to-and-fro of ‘yes / no’) — a function of manas · Valence: Neutral faculty (deliberation is useful; endless churn is the fault) · Guṇa: Rajas-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Mechanism (manas weighing alternatives) · Key relation: Function of manas; steadied by Sankalpa (directive will).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Process (the mind’s to-and-fro of ‘yes / no’) — a function of manas
coordinate.valence: Neutral faculty (deliberation is useful; endless churn is the fault)
coordinate.key_relation: Function of manas; steadied by Sankalpa (directive will)

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When mental oscillation is transcended, there is steadiness and equanimity. The mind maintains equilibrium without being tossed between acceptance and rejection.
facets.somatic: Steady and calm physical presence, relaxed and balanced body, peaceful breathing, good nervous system regulation, physical ease despite circumstances.
facets.emotional: Emotional stability and equanimity, acceptance of circumstances without emotional turmoil, emotional peace despite contrasting experiences, emotional resilience.
facets.cognitive: Steady and focused mind, freedom from mental oscillation, mental clarity and peace, ability to observe thoughts without being swept away, mental stillness.
facets.psychic: The soul watches the mind's oscillation between yes and no without being dragged by every swing.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: When oscillation is excessive and chaotic, the mind is tormented by constant back-and-forth movement between desires and aversions. There is no peace or stability.
facets.somatic: Physical agitation and restlessness, tremors and nervous movements, poor sleep and constant movement, tension throughout the body, nervous system dysregulation.
facets.emotional: Emotional turbulence and constant mood swings, emotional suffering from constant back-and-forth, inability to feel stable, emotional reactivity, emotional exhaustion.
facets.cognitive: Racing and scattered thoughts, constant mental conflict, inability to settle on anything, obsessive mental oscillation, mental torment and suffering, inability to concentrate.
facets.psychic: The soul is caught in ceaseless oscillation, doubt and counter-doubt churning without rest.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Deficiency manifests as mental dullness where the mind cannot even properly oscillate. There is stagnation, lack of discernment about options, and inability to decide.
facets.somatic: Sluggish and heavy body, poor physical responsiveness, lack of physical motivation or engagement, stagnant physical presence.
facets.emotional: Emotional dullness and apathy, inability to feel engaged, emotional heaviness, lack of emotional response or motivation, emotional flatness.
facets.cognitive: Mental lethargy and dullness, inability to think clearly about choices, confusion, inability to discriminate between options, mental heaviness.
facets.psychic: The soul's mind is so dulled it cannot even weigh alternatives, decision collapsing into apathy.

attributes.title: Sankalpa (Will as Higher Than Mind)
attributes.aspect: Chapter 7, Section 4 The nature of this aspect. Sankalpa is the directive power of consciousness—the will that organizes mind, determines action. It is higher than mere mind because mind follows what sankalpa wills. The ascending teaching: meditate on Will as Brahman, recognizing that what you will is what you become. Insight. Sankalpa (Will) is higher than Manas (Mind). Mind merely receives; Will commands. The will-power directed at right ends transforms life. Misdirected will creates samsara; well-directed will creates liberation.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental (directive will / resolve) · Ontic mode: Faculty (the organizing will — higher than the deliberating mind) · Valence: Faculty to be cultivated (steady will organizes the mind) · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant (clarified will), rajasic when willful · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Mechanism (organizes and directs the lower mind) · Key relation: Antagonist-complement to the oscillating mind — the resolve that ends deliberation.

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: Conscious cultivation of right Sankalpa. Will directed toward liberation, not bondage. Powerful intentional living.
facets.somatic: Body follows directed will, no scattered action.
facets.emotional: Emotional life organized by clear intent.
facets.cognitive: Mind serves wisely-directed will.
facets.psychic: The soul exercises directive will, the higher faculty that organizes the mind and steadies intention toward a goal.

facets.emotional: Emotional rigidity from over-willed life.
facets.cognitive: Mind in constant control-mode.
facets.psychic: The soul's will hardens into willfulness, forcing outcomes and overriding wisdom.

facets.emotional: Emotional life as reactive drift.
facets.cognitive: Mind without organizing will, scattered.
facets.psychic: The soul's will is feeble, intentions dissolving before they can organize anything.

attributes.title: Chariot Allegory (Rathopama)
attributes.aspect: रथोपमा   ·   1.3.3-9 The nature of this aspect. Know the Self as the rider; body as the chariot; intellect as charioteer; mind as reins; senses as horses; sense-objects as paths. He whose mind is uncontrolled never reaches the destination.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic (a map of the inner apparatus and the Self) · Ontic mode: Framework (an allegorical model of the inner instrument) · Valence: Neutral — an orienting model · Guṇa: Sattva (a clarifying map) · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Framework (orders the relation of Self, intellect, mind, and senses) · Key relation: Whole — integrates buddhi (charioteer), manas (reins), senses (horses), and the Self (rider).

facets.emotional: Senses (horses) controlled by reins (mind), emotions disciplined.

facets.psychic: The soul keeps the chariot in order — intellect as charioteer, mind as reins, senses as horses — and rides toward the Self.

attributes.title: Antahkarana (Four-Fold Inner Instrument)
attributes.aspect: Throughout commentary The nature of this aspect. Sankara's psychological model: antahkarana (inner instrument) operates through four functions. Manas = emotional appraisal (modern: affect). Buddhi = executive cognition (modern: discrimination). Ahamkara = self-schema ('I-am-doer'). Chitta = unconscious memory/tendencies. All four are one organ in different modes. Personality is their interaction. Insight. The inner instrument (antahkarana) has four functions: Manas (desire/aversion, doubt), Buddhi (discrimination, decision), Ahamkara ('I am the doer'), Chitta (memory, latent tendencies). These are one organ appearing as four functions, not independent faculties.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic (the whole inner instrument) · Ontic mode: Faculty — the composite inner organ (manas, buddhi, ahaṁkāra, citta) · Valence: Neutral faculty (the instrument of all inner processing) · Guṇa: Spans all three (each faculty colored differently) · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Instrument (the apparatus between awareness and world) · Key relation: Whole — the aggregate of which Manas, Buddhi, Ahaṁkāra, and Citta are the parts.

facets.somatic: Body's responses coordinated through harmonized antahkarana.
facets.emotional: Manas regulated; emotional appraisals proportionate.
facets.cognitive: Buddhi clear, dominant; ahamkara functional not inflated; chitta gradually purified.
facets.psychic: The soul works its inner instrument well — mind, intellect, ego, memory each in its place — a coordinated whole.

attributes.title: Phase 3: Manas Re-orientation (Affective Integration)
attributes.aspect: Sankara's third phase The nature of this aspect. Third phase: emotional re-orientation through cultivated dispassion. Pleasures and pains are witnessed without being driven by them. This dissolves affect-driven compulsion. The emotional life is not eliminated but recalibrated—feelings still arise but don't dictate. Insight. Third phase: dispassion (vairagya), emotional de-conditioning, witnessing pleasure and pain. The manas (emotional center) is re-oriented from compulsive seeking to balanced witnessing.

attributes.title: The Waking State (Jāgrat): The World of Sensory Perception
attributes.aspect: The waking state of consciousness is the consciousness of the five senses. It is the plane of perception: we see, we hear, we touch, we taste, we smell. It is here that the vast majority of human beings spend the vast majority of their lives. The teachings describe it as the plane of “my body, my house, my wealth, my work.” It is the domain of identification with the physical and the material, the plane where the self is experienced as coterminous with the body and its possessions. When a person’s consciousness is predominantly anchored in the waking plane, their lived experience is characterised by several distinctive qualities. First, reality is defined entirely by sensory input. What can be seen, measured, and touched is real; what cannot is dismissed or ignored. Second, the sense of self is bound to external identity markers—name, profession, social role, physical appearance, material assets. Third, emotional life is largely reactive: happiness arises when sensory conditions are favorable, suffering when they are not. There is very little inner space between stimulus and response. The elemental disturbances described in the Ayurvedic and Yogic traditions are experienced most acutely at this level. When fire is in excess, the person at the waking plane is entirely consumed by irritability, inflammation, and competitive rage—there is no vantage point from which to witness these states. When air is disturbed, anxiety scatters the mind and the person feels helpless before it. When earth predominates, heaviness and inertia feel like the whole of reality. The person at this plane has no tools for disidentification. They are what they feel, fully and without remainder. The waking consciousness is also where the five constituent elements of the human frame—body, mind, intellect, ego, and soul—are experienced primarily through the first and most gross element alone: the body. The being, the soul, the fifth element, is present but buried beneath the four coverings of body, mind, intellect, and ego. At this plane, a person is alive but not yet truly awake to what lies within.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Mental · Ontic mode: Plane — a material plane of consciousness (waking) · Valence: Neutral (a plane of experience) · Guṇa: Rajas–Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Ground (the sensory world) · Key relation: First of the three material planes; corresponds to Vaiśvānara (the four states).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Plane — a material plane of consciousness (waking)
coordinate.key_relation: First of the three material planes; corresponds to Vaiśvānara (the four states)

attributes.title: The Dreaming State (Svapna): The World of Samskāras
attributes.aspect: The second layer of material consciousness is the dreaming plane. The teachings describe it as the consciousness of samskāras—that which is stored up from previous lives and from the accumulated impressions of this one. While the waking plane corresponds to the gross body and its sensory apparatus, the dreaming plane corresponds to the subtle body and its storehouse of latent impressions. In the ordinary dreaming state, the consciousness replays and recombines the material of waking experience. When a person fights with their neighbour in a dream, runs out of fear, or eats when hungry, this is dreaming of the material plane—the second layer processing the first. But there is a critical distinction the teachings make: sometimes, even in the dream state, content arises that has nothing to do with the earth plane or earth consciousness. Visions from different planes may appear. When they do, they are not called dreams but revelations. This indicates that even within the ordinary structure of sleep, there are moments when consciousness touches something beyond the material. For a person whose consciousness is habitually situated in the dreaming layer (in a functional, not literal sense), lived experience is colored heavily by the past. Such a person is less reactive to immediate sensory events than the person anchored in waking consciousness, but they are deeply conditioned by accumulated patterns—habitual emotional responses, relational dynamics that repeat across different relationships, inherited fears and desires that feel inexplicable in terms of present-life circumstances. The weight of samskaric material shapes perception, creating a kind of filter through which all present experience is interpreted. This person may appear thoughtful or introspective but is often caught in loops of memory, fantasy, and unconscious repetition. The ancient analysis of matter recognized that in subtle matter, the forms consciousness assumes are freer and more rapid, but also more volatile, elastic, and swiftly mutable. This is precisely the quality of dream-life and of the inner experience of a person dominated by their samskaric conditioning: everything moves quickly, shifts shape, and lacks the stability of waking perception, yet also lacks the limitation of being tied to immediate physical facts.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Vital · Ontic mode: Plane — a material plane (dream) · Valence: Neutral · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Ground (the world of saṁskāras) · Key relation: Second material plane; corresponds to Taijasa; the subtle-impression world.
coordinate.key_relation: Second material plane; corresponds to Taijasa; the subtle-impression world

attributes.title: The Sleeping State (Suṣupti): The World of the Psychic
attributes.aspect: The third layer of material consciousness is the sleeping plane, and here the teachings reveal something unexpected. Sound sleep, dreamless sleep, is not unconsciousness. It is what the tradition calls the consciousness of Samādhi. The consciousness is present during sleep, but it is so subtle that the mind cannot catch it. It is subtler than mind. Sound sleep and samādhi have very little difference. Sound sleep is described as a small death; death is nothing but a long sound sleep. In this state, the consciousness touches what the teachings call the psychic plane—the domain of the soul element within the human frame. When dreamless sleep is truly deep and undisturbed, what is experienced is not the world of perception (waking) or the world of samskāras (dreaming), but a kind of pure blissful existence. The ancient seers called this the Sleep State and found that in causal matter, consciousness took the shape merely of the pure sense of blissful existence; they could discover no other distinguishing sensation. Very few people have awareness in the sleeping state of consciousness. This means that the deepest layer of the material plane—the one closest to the spiritual domain—is for most human beings entirely unconscious. Yet it shapes lived experience profoundly. The quality of a person’s sleep, the degree to which they emerge from sleep feeling renewed or depleted, the rare and luminous quality of certain nights where one wakes feeling touched by something beyond the ordinary—all of this relates to the sleeping plane. Those yogis who have developed awareness even in deep sleep report a qualitative transformation: they need far less sleep, yet feel vastly more fulfilled. Half an hour of sleep gives more vitality than seven or eight hours of ordinary sleep. This is the first indicator that consciousness is beginning to transcend the material planes.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Physical · Ontic mode: Plane — a material plane (deep sleep) · Valence: Neutral · Guṇa: Tamas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Ground (the causal seed-state) · Key relation: Third material plane; corresponds to Prājña; the causal ground.
coordinate.key_relation: Third material plane; corresponds to Prājña; the causal ground

additional_info.heading: Part III: The Transcendental Plane — The Pivot of Transformation

attributes.title: The Transcendental State (Turīya): The Bridge Between Matter and Spirit
attributes.aspect: If there is a single teaching that emerges from all the source texts with overwhelming emphasis, it is the centrality of the transcendental plane. The fourth layer of consciousness is the link between the consciousness in the material plane and the consciousness in the spirit plane. It is the transcendental that makes matter meaningful and spirit so important. Without the transcendental, matter would be dead, inconscient. Without the transcendental, spirit would be abstract and meaningless. The teachings state with extraordinary directness: the key to rapid progress lies in the expansion of the transcendental. Not in the waking plane, not in the dreaming plane, not in the sleeping plane, and—remarkably—not directly in the God, Unity, or Cosmic planes either, because those higher planes can play their role only through the transcendental. Everything pivots here. How the transcendental is reached. Remaining in the waking plane, when a person aspires to ascend, the force of that aspiration pushes consciousness toward the transcendental plane. But the transcendental cannot be reached without passing through the dreaming and sleeping layers. This is why the practices of yoga, meditation, and sādhanā inevitably surface dream content, disturb sleep patterns, and bring hidden material to the surface. The aspirant must pass through the accumulated impressions of the dreaming plane and the deep psychic territory of the sleeping plane before breaking through to the transcendental. The experience of falling back. In the early stages, the seeker cannot remain in the transcendental. Upon entering it, they fall back to the waking plane, but they bring with them a little of the transcendental consciousness. This happens repeatedly. Each ascent deposits a residue of the higher consciousness in the lower planes. Gradually, waking life begins to be colored by transcendental quality: the person experiences transcendental waking (aware but not so identified with awareness), transcendental dreaming (visions rather than mere replays of sensory life, movement with angels and gods rather than fights with neighbors), and transcendental sleeping (sleep that is much reduced but deeply fulfilling). These three experiences—transcendental waking, transcendental dreaming, and transcendental sleeping—are described as direct proof that a person is making progress on the path. And conversely, if these experiences do not arrive within a reasonable period of sincere practice, the tradition holds that the time for the spiritual path may not yet have come, and the person’s development should focus on expansion within the material realm. The transformation of lived experience. For the person whose consciousness begins to stabilize in the transcendental, the quality of daily life undergoes a profound shift. They are still in the world—going to market, arguing in court, eating dinner—but their consciousness is no longer fully captured by these activities. There is an inner witness that persists. The teachings use the metaphor of a skilled driver navigating crowded streets: he is not looking at anyone specifically, but he sees everyone. He is careful, alert, yet not consumed by any single stimulus. The practical consequences are remarkable. As long as one is able to remain in the fourth plane of consciousness, many diseases will not affect the person. Ninety percent of sickness comes from germs, bacteria, and viruses that operate at the earth-plane wavelength. When consciousness rises above this wavelength, the body becomes less susceptible to their influence. This is offered not as magical thinking but as an explanation for the well-documented phenomenon of saints and seers remaining healthy in environments that devastate others—in epidemic zones, in extreme cold with bare bodies, in conditions that would ordinarily destroy the physical frame. The consciousness, when established beyond body and mind, simply does not register the physical plane’s disturbances with the same force. The transcendental is also described as the psychic realm—the plane of the soul. When the being within, long buried under the coverings of body, mind, intellect, and ego, begins to reassert itself, it first recaptures the mental plane, then the vital, then the physical. This reassertion of the being across all planes of the human frame is what constitutes genuine spiritual awakening, and it proceeds through and from the transcendental.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: Plane — the transcendental pivot (Turīya) · Valence: Neutral / the goal · Guṇa: Sattva, beyond the guṇas · Scale: Individual, transcendent · Causal status: Bridge / substratum · Key relation: The pivot between matter and spirit; the fourth; the witness of the three states.
coordinate.key_relation: The pivot between matter and spirit; the fourth; the witness of the three states

additional_info.heading: Part IV: The Three Spiritual Planes — God, Unity, and Cosmic Consciousness
additional_info.text: When the seeker has crossed more than fifty percent of the transcendental sphere, they no longer fall back to the gross world so frequently. A pull begins to be felt from the other side of consciousness—the consciousness of the spirit. From the transcendental, one sometimes enters the God plane, sometimes the Unity plane, sometimes the Cosmic plane. But these entries come initially as flashes, not as stable states. The teachings use the image of lightning illuminating a dark landscape: each flash reveals a little more of the terrain, and the traveler proceeds in the intervals of darkness on the strength of what was briefly seen.

attributes.title: God Consciousness: The Experience of Supreme Love
attributes.aspect: God consciousness is defined in these teachings not as an intellectual concept but as a concept of direct experience. It is described as the simultaneous experience of sat (being) and chit (consciousness) in their totality. While the transcendental plane allows one to witness, and the state of the Ātman (the Being) allows one to rest in pure awareness, God consciousness adds a dimension that neither of these states possesses: love. In the state of the Being there is no love—it is simply witnessing. Love comes beyond Being, and love comes from God. For the person who begins to receive flashes of God consciousness, the lived experience is characterized by a flow of devotion that is qualitatively unlike any ordinary emotion. It does not depend on mutual exchange. The teachings describe this with remarkable precision: you do not share food, shelter, emotions, or whims with the object of this devotion, and yet the love flows. It is unilateral, uncaused, and overwhelming. This is because through the experience of God consciousness, one is getting the rare opportunity of experiencing chit and sat at the same time—a unitary experience that ordinary relationships, which are always bilateral and conditional, can never provide. The person in this state may fluctuate wildly between exalted experiences and ordinary mundane concerns. One moment, the awareness arises: I am Brahman, I am Divine, I am He. The next moment, anxiety about tomorrow’s meal returns. The teachings insist that both experiences are genuine. The exalted state is not hypocrisy or fantasy; it is a real flash of a higher plane. But the person cannot hold it, because to hold it, the vital must be transparent, the mind must be still, and the physical must be strong. When any or all of these conditions are lacking, the experience fades. Those who have not undergone this process may judge the person as unstable or self-deluding. But the tradition recognizes this oscillation as a natural and necessary part of the journey. The process proceeds through repeated flashes in which the finite within is expanding as the infinite. Each flash, though temporary, leaves behind an awareness that gradually becomes the background of all experience.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Vital · Ontic mode: Plane — a spiritual plane (supreme love) · Valence: Neutral (a plane of realization) · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Cosmic (Ādhidaivika), lived individually · Causal status: Ground (the plane of divine love) · Key relation: First spiritual plane; the experience of supreme love.
coordinate.key_relation: First spiritual plane; the experience of supreme love

attributes.title: Unity Consciousness: The Transcendence of Prakriti
attributes.aspect: Unity consciousness is described as the consciousness of the Guru—the rarest and most difficult attainment. It comes only when one has transcended prakriti, the entire domain of natural forces. The process of attaining it proceeds through flashes of experiential awareness: what you are experiencing, you cannot really explain. Even if you try, you will be able to explain only a drop of it. But because of this experience, it will remain in your awareness, and you will never be prepared to lose it. The lived experience at this level is characterized by a paradox. On one hand, there is a total absence of attraction for anything in the world of chit (consciousness as normally experienced). Only a survival compulsion keeps the person functional. This is the stage when the paramount importance is for survival—somehow the yogi must maintain the body. On the other hand, there is an attraction to something so immeasurably higher that the impulse to withdraw from the world is not repulsive (as it might be for someone merely disgusted with life) but magnetic—the pull of a force that the person cannot fully comprehend. The critical distinction between God-realized souls and those in Unity consciousness is their capacity for action in the world. Those who have attained merger in the God plane cannot willfully descend back to the waking, dreaming, or sleeping planes. They depend on Divine grace and counsel others to pray. But those who are in the Unity plane can directly descend to the waking plane and intervene. This is why the masters who operate from Unity consciousness are able to function in the material world with full effectiveness while simultaneously abiding in the highest spiritual reality. The Gita’s description of the brahmī sthiti—the Supreme Divine State—refers to this level. When a person enters Unity and does not fall back, when they remain there and descend only out of their own sweet will, this is called Brahma nirvāṇa if attained after leaving the body, and jīvanmukta (liberated while living) if attained while still embodied.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: Plane — a spiritual plane (transcendence of prakṛti) · Valence: Neutral / the goal · Guṇa: Beyond the guṇas · Scale: Cosmic / transcendent · Causal status: Ground · Key relation: Second spiritual plane; the transcendence of prakṛti.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Plane — a spiritual plane (transcendence of prakṛti)
coordinate.key_relation: Second spiritual plane; the transcendence of prakṛti

attributes.title: Cosmic Consciousness: The Totality of Sat and Chit
attributes.aspect: Cosmic consciousness, described as the Purushottama consciousness or the Krishna consciousness, represents the totality of chit and sat—the Supreme Being fully manifest. The person established in cosmic consciousness possesses an extraordinary quality: they feel hunger like a hungry person, they experience the material world fully, yet their consciousness is never diluted by any condition. Food or no food, comfort or discomfort, honor or dishonor—the consciousness remains whole, undimmed, and fully operative on every plane simultaneously. Sri Aurobindo’s evolutionary framework illuminates this state from a different angle. He envisions it not merely as an individual attainment but as the next stage of evolution for consciousness itself. Just as life developed out of matter, and mind out of life, a still higher form of consciousness—what he calls the supramental Truth-Consciousness—is destined to develop out of the mind. This highest consciousness knows the universe from inside, as the Divine knows it. If there is any limitation of knowledge or power at this level, it is a willed and conscious diminution for the sake of the harmony and development of the whole. The lived experience of cosmic consciousness, as described across these traditions, is one in which every apparent contradiction is resolved—not intellectually but existentially. The person is simultaneously fully in the world and fully beyond it, fully individual and fully universal, fully active and fully still. This is not a paradox to be understood but a reality to be inhabited.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: Plane — a spiritual plane (Sat-Chit totality) · Valence: Neutral / the goal · Guṇa: Beyond the guṇas · Scale: Cosmic / transcendent · Causal status: Ground · Key relation: Third spiritual plane; the totality of Sat and Chit.
coordinate.key_relation: Third spiritual plane; the totality of Sat and Chit

additional_info.heading: Part V: The Dynamics of Transformation — How Consciousness Shifts Between Layers
The Expansion of the Transcendental
additional_info.text:
The teachings are extraordinarily specific about how transformation actually occurs. The process is not a simple linear ascent. Rather, the transcendental plane is expanded through the constant bombardment of cosmic energy directed at the lower planes. This expanded transcendental creates a severe churning on the layers of consciousness below. It puts pressure on the waking life, brings hidden material from the dreaming life to the surface, and disturbs the sleeping life. This is why sincere spiritual practice often makes life harder before it makes it easier—the churning is not a sign of failure but of active transformation.

If the seeker’s system is able to handle this churning—processing whatever arises, whether exalted or terrible, through aspiration and one-pointed devotion—then the system becomes transparent. A transparent system is one in which the coverings of body, mind, intellect, and ego no longer obstruct the light of the being within. And a transparent system is what allows the push to the higher planes: transcendental God, transcendental Unity, transcendental Cosmic.

If the system is not transparent, the push never comes. One cannot consciously experience the Divine; it is absolutely a grace that enables this experience. The seeker can consciously work on the transcendental, can consciously cultivate devotion and purity, but the actual flash of higher consciousness arrives unbidden, as grace.

additional_info.heading: The Role of the Three Energies
additional_info.text:
The entire creation, according to the Gita and the commentarial tradition, is a manifestation of three basic energies: sattva (psychic energy, purity, light), rajas (creative energy, activity, passion), and tamas (destructive energy, inertia, darkness). The whole universe, the solar system, all beings float in this ocean of energy waves. Which energy dominates a person’s system at any given time profoundly shapes their experience of the seven planes.

A person established in sattva experiences the waking plane with clarity and equanimity. The same person, when rajas dominates, experiences the same external circumstances with restlessness and craving. When tamas dominates, the same life feels heavy, meaningless, and dark. The advanced practitioner can discern not only which energy is dominant but which plane of their being—physical, vital, mental, or psychic—is affected by which energy. This discriminative awareness is itself a sign of consciousness operating beyond the ordinary waking level.

The teachings give a specific warning for those who have attained stability of the Being but are seeking merger with the Divine: if the system is even unconsciously exposed to rajasic and tamasic energy, the consciousness itself will not be affected—but the recurring flashes of transcendental God, transcendental Unity, and transcendental Cosmic will become less frequent. The person will not lose what they have attained, but their forward progress will stall. This is why the tradition emphasizes regular meditation, group practice, and the conscious avoidance of non-spiritual vibrations—not as moralistic injunctions but as practical necessities for maintaining the conditions in which transformation can continue.

additional_info.heading: The Physical Cost of Spiritual Evolution
additional_info.text:
One of the most striking and honest revelations in these teachings concerns the physical cost of higher consciousness. When the vital system, the physical system, is damaged because no one was present to care for the yogi during states of deep samādhi—when they may have remained hungry for days, sitting in scorching sun or freezing cold or driving rain, because their consciousness was entirely inner—the body suffers. This is why many of the greatest spiritual masters, by the time they come to teach, already carry physical damage to their vital systems. The body must obey natural law even while consciousness transcends it.

Even for highly evolved yogis who receive the best food and highest honor, robust health is rare. When the system is exposed to tamasic or rajasic vibrations, the consciousness remains unaffected, but the effect is released through the body in the form of physical suffering. Everything is neutralized through the body. This teaches us something crucial about the relationship between consciousness and form: the body is not merely the servant of consciousness but its sacrificial instrument. The very transparency that allows the highest light to pass through also makes the physical frame vulnerable to every disturbance it can no longer deflect through unconsciousness.

additional_info.heading: Part VI: Practical Implications for Lived Experience
Where Is Your Consciousness Most of the Time?
additional_info.text:
The fundamental question these teachings pose to every human being is deceptively simple: In which plane of consciousness are you remaining? This is not a question about what you are doing but about where you are doing it from. The same action—cooking a meal, conducting a negotiation, caring for a child—is qualitatively different depending on whether it is performed from the waking plane, the transcendental plane, or beyond.

The teachings offer a remarkable self-assessment criterion: out of twenty-four hours of the day, how many hours or how many minutes are you really in God consciousness? Even when chanting or listening to sacred music, is the thought linked with the Divine, or is the mouth uttering words while the thought is elsewhere? On that depends what percentage of transformation has come to the system. This is an honest, unflinching metric that cuts through all pretension and self-deception.

For the practitioner, the instruction is clear: whatever you are doing, let the consciousness remain in the transcendental state. You may be going to market, arguing in court, eating a sumptuous dinner—but let not the consciousness descend to the physical plane. This requires, the teachings acknowledge, “little practice” for those who are already meditating regularly. It is presented not as an impossibly elevated ideal but as a practical skill, analogous to the alertness of a good driver in a crowded street.

additional_info.heading: The Continuity of Practice and Its Consequences
additional_info.text:
The teachings use a vivid analogy for the importance of regular practice: a person who eats every day at a fixed time enjoys good health, while a person who gorges on all their nutrition in one day and then fasts for three days will survive but will not thrive. Similarly, daily meditation charges the psychic energy and accumulates force. Those who meditate regularly see visions and experience higher states; those who practice irregularly move one step forward and two steps backward. The continuous forward march requires continuity.

When practice is sustained, the being that was pushed into the innermost corner of the human frame—the psychic, the soul—begins to recapture the other parts of the human frame. First the mental plane, then the vital, then the physical. This progressive reassertion is not dramatic or instantaneous. It is gradual, often imperceptible, marked by subtle shifts: dreams becoming more luminous, sleep becoming more restful, waking life feeling less burdensome, relationships carrying less friction, the body feeling more alive and less like a cage.

additional_info.heading: Signs of Genuine Transformation
additional_info.text:
The tradition offers specific indicators that transformation is occurring. In the dreaming state, dreams become more and more transcendental and less and less material. If this is happening, one can be sure the transformation process is active. In the sleeping state, sleep becomes reduced but deeply fulfilling. In the waking state, there is awareness without excessive identification—one is present but not consumed.

More subtle signs include the capacity to hear inner sounds—the sound of a conch, a flute, or an undefined tone in the right ear after meditation—which is described as the nād brahma, the cosmic sound wave. The ability to perceive different energies—sattva, rajas, tamas—moving through the system is another indicator. And for those still further advanced, the ability to know not only which energy is dominant but which plane of the being is affected: this is an awareness that operates from beyond the waking plane, using the subtle discriminative faculty that belongs to the transcendental and above.

additional_info.heading: Part VII: The Evolutionary Horizon — Sri Aurobindo’s Complementary Vision
additional_info.text:
Sri Aurobindo’s contribution to this understanding is to place the seven planes of consciousness within an evolutionary framework that extends both backward into cosmological origins and forward into possibilities yet unrealized. His central thesis is that consciousness is the fundamental reality, and what we call matter is consciousness in its most self-forgetful state—“exclusive concentration” so complete that it has forgotten its own nature.

The hierarchy of planes he describes—from the Overmind through Intuition, Illumined Mind, Higher Mind, down through ordinary mental consciousness, vital life, and physical matter—corresponds to the Vedantic scheme while adding specificity about the gradations of mental and supramental consciousness. Our ordinary human mentality is considered to be only the most primitive form of mental consciousness, most ego-bound, most dependent on the physical senses. Above it stands the unitary Higher Mind of self-revealed wisdom, the Illumined Mind where truths are seen rather than thought, the plane of the Intuitive Mind where truth is inevitable and perfect, and finally the supramental Truth-Consciousness which is the origin and foundation of all.

What makes Aurobindo’s vision distinctive is his insistence that the higher planes of consciousness are not merely to be visited in meditation or experienced as flashes. They are to be embodied. Just as there was a time when life had not yet appeared in matter, and a later time when mind had not yet appeared in life, there will come a time when the supramental consciousness becomes an organic, incarnate aspect of physical life. The evolution of consciousness is not finished with the appearance of the human mind. It is ongoing, and those who consciously participate in it—through yoga, through inner aspiration, through the expansion of the transcendental—are participating in the next stage of evolution itself.

This vision reframes the entire discussion of the seven layers. The movement from waking to cosmic consciousness is not merely a personal spiritual journey. It is the universe becoming conscious of itself through the individual, the involutionary arc completing its return. Every human being who moves from the surface layers of sensory perception toward the depths of transcendental and spiritual consciousness is enacting, in miniature and in real time, the vast evolutionary movement that is the meaning and purpose of existence.

additional_info.heading: Conclusion: Consciousness as the Ground of All Experience
additional_info.text:
The seven layers of consciousness—waking, dreaming, sleeping, transcendental, God, Unity, and Cosmic—are not abstract theological categories. They are the living architecture of human experience. Where a person’s consciousness habitually rests determines everything: how they perceive the world, how they relate to others, how they experience their own body and mind, what they suffer, what they enjoy, and what they are capable of becoming.

A person confined to the waking plane lives in a world defined entirely by sensory input and external circumstance. Their elemental imbalances are overwhelming, their emotional life is reactive, and their sense of meaning is precarious, dependent on conditions they cannot control. A person whose consciousness has expanded into the transcendental lives in the same world but from a fundamentally different vantage point. The elements still move, the gunas still operate, the body still demands its due—but there is a witness, a space, a quality of awareness that transforms the relationship to all of it.

For those rare individuals whose consciousness touches the God plane, the dimension of love enters as a transformative force unlike anything the material planes can produce. For those who reach Unity, the capacity to act freely in any plane while remaining rooted in the Absolute becomes available. And for those who attain Cosmic consciousness, the distinction between individual and universal dissolves, and the full purpose of the evolutionary journey stands revealed.

The practical teaching that runs through all these texts is both humbling and empowering: the work of transformation happens primarily in the transcendental plane. It happens through regular practice, through the cultivation of sattva, through the aspiration of the individual being, and through grace. The seeker’s task is to expand the transcendental, to make the system transparent, and to remain alert. Everything else—the flashes of higher consciousness, the progressive liberation from suffering, the gradual divinization of the human frame—follows from this.

As the teachings remind us: we should be always conscious of the plane of consciousness in which plane we are remaining. This single instruction, faithfully followed, is the beginning and the essence of the entire path.

additional_info.heading: Sources
additional_info.text:
1. Swami Sri Atmananda, “Planes of Consciousness” (Lecture L01, July 14, 2002) — foundational discourse on the sevenfold division of consciousness into matter and spirit, the role of the transcendental, and the mechanics of ascent and descent.

2. Swami Sri Atmananda, “Concept of Supreme God and Unity Consciousness” (Lecture L43, October 11, 2002) — detailed treatment of the journey from the Being (Atman) to Brahman through God consciousness, the process of merger, and the experiential dynamics of flashes, churning, and transformation.

3. Swami Sri Atmananda, “Layers of Consciousness” (August 11, 1995) — early discourse on the five elements of the human frame, the seven planes of consciousness, visions, revelations, and practical indicators of spiritual progress.

4. Swami Sri Atmananda, “Layers of Consciousness” (text transcript) — extended transcript covering the transcendental state, its health implications, the cosmic sound (nād brahma), and the relationship between regular sādhanā and spiritual development.

5. Matthijs Cornelissen, “Sri Aurobindo’s Evolutionary Ontology of Consciousness” — scholarly analysis of Aurobindo’s synthesis of Vedantic consciousness with evolutionary theory, covering involution, evolution, planes of consciousness, and the prospect of supramental transformation.

additional_info.heading: Additional Concepts from the Wider Scriptural Corpus
additional_info.text: The following concepts, drawn chiefly from the Māṇḍūkya and other Upaniṣads, deepen the treatment of consciousness — the waking, dream, and deep-sleep states (Vaiśvānara, Taijasa, Prājña) and Turīya, the fourth, examined as the substratum of the other three.

attributes.title: Three States of Consciousness (Jagrat-Svapna-Sushupti)
attributes.aspect: जाग्रत्-स्वप्न-सुषुप्ति   ·   Chapter 27, 28 The nature of this aspect. Indian psychology classifies consciousness into three states, each with a corresponding body. The three are levels of identification. Liberation is becoming aware in each state—knowing oneself as the witness beyond all three. Insight. In waking, Self is deluded by Maya and identifies with body. In dream, it experiences pleasure/pain in self-created field. In deep sleep, overpowered by tamas, it experiences happiness as merger. The three correspond to gross body (sthula), subtle body (sukshma), and causal body (karana). Beyond all three is Turiya, the fourth—the witness.

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When one becomes aware in all three states—knowing that 'body walks in me, mind dreams in me, sleep happens in me'—the witness is established. Turiya is realized.
facets.somatic: Healthy in waking, recuperative in sleep, body functioning naturally.
facets.emotional: Emotionally balanced in waking, learning from dreams, peace in deep sleep.
facets.cognitive: Witness-awareness maintained across states, recognition of one's true nature beyond states.
facets.psychic: The soul observes itself passing through waking, dream, and deep sleep, sensing the awareness continuous through all three.

balanced.text:
Overview: When one becomes aware in all three states—knowing that 'body walks in me, mind dreams in me, sleep happens in me'—the witness is established. Turiya is realized.

Somatic: Healthy in waking, recuperative in sleep, body functioning naturally.

Emotional: Emotionally balanced in waking, learning from dreams, peace in deep sleep.

Cognitive: Witness-awareness maintained across states, recognition of one's true nature beyond states.

Psychic: The soul observes itself passing through waking, dream, and deep sleep, sensing the awareness continuous through all three.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Pathological dominance of one state—obsessive waking activity, dream-addiction, oversleeping. Or premature claims of Turiya without genuine awareness in three states.
facets.somatic: Imbalanced sleep, exhaustion, or oversleeping.
facets.emotional: Stuck in waking concerns, dream-disturbance, depressive sleep.
facets.cognitive: Mental imbalance from poor state regulation.
facets.psychic: The soul fixates on analyzing the states, mapping rather than recognizing the witness behind them.

excess.text:

Somatic: Imbalanced sleep, exhaustion, or oversleeping.

Emotional: Stuck in waking concerns, dream-disturbance, depressive sleep.

Cognitive: Mental imbalance from poor state regulation.

Psychic: The soul fixates on analyzing the states, mapping rather than recognizing the witness behind them.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Unconscious movement through three states—no awareness of dreams, no recovery from sleep, no witness in waking. Identification with all three.
facets.somatic: Mechanical functioning, no awareness of body across states.
facets.emotional: Caught in each state's content without awareness.
facets.cognitive: No witness consciousness, complete identification with state-contents.
facets.psychic: The soul takes each state as a separate, disconnected reality, never sensing the continuity beneath.

attributes.title: Turiya as the Substratum (Krishnananda's Treatment)
attributes.aspect: Mandukya verse 7 The nature of this aspect. Krishnananda emphasizes Turiya is not a fourth state in series but the substratum. The three states (waking, dream, deep sleep) appear and disappear in Turiya; Turiya itself never changes. It is the screen, they are the movie. This radically reorients spiritual practice. Insight. Turiya is not a fourth state alongside the other three but the SUBSTRATUM in which the three appear and disappear. It is not 'fourth' in sequence but the screen on which waking, dream, deep sleep play. This understanding shifts everything.

facets.emotional: Emotional states recognized as movements in unchanging awareness.
facets.cognitive: Mind's three states all seen against unchanging Turiya-background.
facets.psychic: The soul recognizes turīya not as a fourth state in series but as the ever-present ground in which the other three appear.

attributes.title: Four States: Vaishvanara (Waking)
attributes.aspect: वैश्वानर   ·   Verse 3 The nature of this aspect. Vaishvanara—the waking state. Consciousness turned outward through seven limbs, nineteen mouths. Experiences gross objects. The first state of consciousness with corresponding body.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical (outward-turned, the gross sensory world) · Ontic mode: State (the first of the four states) · Valence: Neutral — a natural state to inhabit fully · Guṇa: Rajas-dominant · Scale: Individual, with cosmic correlate (Virāṭ) · Causal status: Manifestation (the gross state) · Key relation: Part — first of the four states; corresponds to ‘A’ of AUM.
coordinate.ontic_mode: State (the first of the four states)
coordinate.valence: Neutral — a natural state to inhabit fully
coordinate.key_relation: Part — first of the four states; corresponds to ‘A’ of AUM

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: Healthy waking state—senses functioning, world engaged with awareness.
facets.somatic: Body active and healthy in waking.
facets.emotional: Emotional engagement with the world.
facets.cognitive: Mind alert in waking state, conscious participation.
facets.psychic: The soul inhabits the waking state fully and consciously, present to the outer world without being lost in it.

balanced.text:
Overview: Healthy waking state—senses functioning, world engaged with awareness.

Somatic: Body active and healthy in waking.

Emotional: Emotional engagement with the world.

Cognitive: Mind alert in waking state, conscious participation.

Psychic: The soul inhabits the waking state fully and consciously, present to the outer world without being lost in it.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Workaholic excess of waking-state activity, never resting.
facets.somatic: Body exhausted from over-activity.
facets.emotional: Emotional overload from constant waking-engagement.
facets.cognitive: Mental fatigue from excess waking activity.
facets.psychic: The soul is wholly captured by waking experience, the outer world its entire reality.

excess.text:
Overview: Workaholic excess of waking-state activity, never resting.

Somatic: Body exhausted from over-activity.

Emotional: Emotional overload from constant waking-engagement.

Cognitive: Mental fatigue from excess waking activity.

Psychic: The soul is wholly captured by waking experience, the outer world its entire reality.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Half-asleep in waking—drifting, no conscious engagement, life as somnambulism.
facets.somatic: Body mechanical, no presence.
facets.emotional: Emotional flatness in waking life.
facets.cognitive: Mind absent during waking, unconscious living.
facets.psychic: The soul moves through waking life half-present, never fully arriving in the moment it is in.

deficient.text:
Overview: Half-asleep in waking—drifting, no conscious engagement, life as somnambulism.

Somatic: Body mechanical, no presence.

Emotional: Emotional flatness in waking life.

Cognitive: Mind absent during waking, unconscious living.

Psychic: The soul moves through waking life half-present, never fully arriving in the moment it is in.

attributes.title: Four States: Taijasa (Dreaming)
attributes.aspect: तैजस   ·   Verse 4 The nature of this aspect. Taijasa—the dreaming state. Consciousness turned inward, experiencing subtle objects created by mental impressions. Second state with corresponding subtle body.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental (inward-turned, the subtle dream-world of impressions) · Ontic mode: State (the second of the four states) · Valence: Neutral — a natural state · Guṇa: Rajas–Sattva (subtle activity) · Scale: Individual, with cosmic correlate (Hiraṇyagarbha) · Causal status: Manifestation (the subtle state) · Key relation: Part — second of the four states; corresponds to ‘U’ of AUM.
coordinate.seat: Mental (inward-turned, the subtle dream-world of impressions)
coordinate.ontic_mode: State (the second of the four states)
coordinate.key_relation: Part — second of the four states; corresponds to ‘U’ of AUM

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: Healthy dream life—subconscious processing, integration of waking experiences, possible higher dimensions.
facets.somatic: Subtle body active healthily in dream.
facets.emotional: Emotional integration through dreams.
facets.cognitive: Mind processing creatively in dream state.
facets.psychic: The soul observes the dreaming state, recognizing the mind's power to create whole worlds from its own impressions.

balanced.text:
Overview: Healthy dream life—subconscious processing, integration of waking experiences, possible higher dimensions.

Somatic: Subtle body active healthily in dream.

Emotional: Emotional integration through dreams.

Cognitive: Mind processing creatively in dream state.

Psychic: The soul observes the dreaming state, recognizing the mind's power to create whole worlds from its own impressions.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Nightmare-dominated sleep, dream-obsession, escaping waking life into dreams.
facets.somatic: Restless dream-disturbed sleep.
facets.emotional: Emotional turbulence in dreams.
facets.cognitive: Mind escaping into dreams instead of waking engagement.
facets.psychic: The soul is consumed by its inner dream-world of fantasy and projection, even while awake.

excess.text:

Emotional: Emotional turbulence in dreams.

Cognitive: Mind escaping into dreams instead of waking engagement.

Psychic: The soul is consumed by its inner dream-world of fantasy and projection, even while awake.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Dreamless sleep without integration—missing the processing function of dreams.
facets.somatic: Sleep without REM cycles.
facets.emotional: Lack of emotional processing through dreams.
facets.cognitive: Mental rigidity from lack of dream-creativity.
facets.psychic: The soul dismisses the dreaming mind entirely, blind to the impressions that shape it from within.

deficient.text:
