attributes.title: Four States: Prajna (Deep Sleep)
attributes.aspect: प्राज्ञ   ·   Verses 5-6 The nature of this aspect. Prajna—deep dreamless sleep. Where one desires nothing, sees no dream, is unified. Consciousness becomes a mass of bliss. The lord of all, the knower of all, the inner controller, the source of all.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic (the causal, undifferentiated state) · Ontic mode: State (the third of the four states) · Valence: Neutral — the seed of undivided awareness · Guṇa: Tamas-dominant (undifferentiated) · Scale: Individual, with cosmic correlate (Īśvara) · Causal status: Causal state (the seed-condition of manifestation) · Key relation: Part — third of the four states; corresponds to ‘M’ of AUM.
coordinate.ontic_mode: State (the third of the four states)
coordinate.valence: Neutral — the seed of undivided awareness
coordinate.key_relation: Part — third of the four states; corresponds to ‘M’ of AUM

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: Deep restorative sleep, contact with the blissful causal level. Refreshment of the whole being.
facets.somatic: Deep restorative sleep, body fully recovered.
facets.emotional: Emotional restoration through deep sleep.
facets.cognitive: Mind refreshed through contact with bliss-state.
facets.psychic: The soul senses, in dreamless sleep, the seed of undivided awareness — the unity it touches each night unknowing.

balanced.text:

Emotional: Emotional restoration through deep sleep.

Cognitive: Mind refreshed through contact with bliss-state.

Psychic: The soul senses, in dreamless sleep, the seed of undivided awareness — the unity it touches each night unknowing.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Oversleeping, depressive sleep as escape, tamasic excess.
facets.somatic: Body weakened by oversleeping.
facets.emotional: Depression manifesting as excessive sleep.
facets.cognitive: Mind escaping into sleep, no engagement.
facets.psychic: The soul mistakes the blankness of deep sleep for the goal, courting oblivion over awakening.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Chronic insomnia, no deep sleep, exhaustion from never contacting the bliss-state.
facets.somatic: Body exhausted, no recovery.
facets.emotional: Emotional dysregulation from sleep deprivation.
facets.cognitive: Mental dysfunction from chronic sleep lack.
facets.psychic: The soul takes deep sleep as mere unconsciousness, missing the unity that abides within it.

attributes.title: Turiya (Fourth State - Pure Awareness)
attributes.aspect: तुरीय   ·   Verse 7 The nature of this aspect. Turiya—not inward-knowing, not outward-knowing, not both, not a mass of consciousness, not knowing, not not-knowing. Unseen, ungraspable, indefinable, unthinkable, indescribable. The essence of the Self. Peaceful, blissful, non-dual.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic (pure awareness) · Ontic mode: State-beyond-states (the ‘fourth,’ the ground of the other three) · Valence: Neutral — the awareness to be realized · Guṇa: Beyond the guṇas · Scale: Transcendent (Ādhidaivika), realized individually · Causal status: Ground (the substrate of waking, dream, and sleep) · Key relation: Ground — the fourth; the silence beyond A-U-M.
coordinate.ontic_mode: State-beyond-states (the ‘fourth,’ the ground of the other three)
coordinate.valence: Neutral — the awareness to be realized
coordinate.causal_status: Ground (the substrate of waking, dream, and sleep)
coordinate.key_relation: Ground — the fourth; the silence beyond A-U-M

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: Recognition of Turiya as the witnessing presence in all three states—the constant pure awareness underlying everything.
facets.somatic: Body recognized as appearance in Turiya.
facets.emotional: Emotions seen as movements in Turiya.
facets.cognitive: Mental contents witnessed by Turiya.
facets.psychic: The soul rests as turīya, the pure awareness neither inward nor outward, the ground of waking, dream, and sleep.

facets.somatic: Body neglected with Turiya-claim.
facets.emotional: Bypassing emotions with claim.
facets.cognitive: Concept of Turiya without realization.
facets.psychic: The soul grasps at turīya as a special attainment, the very seeking obscuring the ever-present.

facets.emotional: Emotions taken as self.
facets.cognitive: Mind taken as ultimate, no fourth dimension known.
facets.psychic: The soul knows only the three ordinary states, the fourth never even suspected.

attributes.title: Vaishvanara Vidya (Universal Self Meditation)
attributes.aspect: वैश्वानर विद्या   ·   5.11-18 The nature of this aspect. Six householders go to King Ashvapati to learn Vaishvanara Vidya. Each had a partial conception of Self (as heaven, sun, air, ether, water, earth). The king teaches: the whole cosmos is the Universal Self. The mouth is heaven, eyes are sun, breath is air, body is ether, bladder is water, feet are earth. To meditate on a part is to miss the whole.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic (meditation on the universal Self) · Ontic mode: Practice (an upāsana — meditation on the cosmic person) · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual, on a cosmic object (Ādhidaivika) · Causal status: Practice (a means to the recognition of the universal Self) · Key relation: Correspondence — takes the waking / cosmic person (Virāṭ) as the object of meditation.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Practice (an upāsana — meditation on the cosmic person)
coordinate.causal_status: Practice (a means to the recognition of the universal Self)
coordinate.key_relation: Correspondence — takes the waking / cosmic person (Virāṭ) as the object of meditation

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: Meditation on cosmic Self as one's own body—seeing the whole universe as one's expanded body. Universal Self realized.
facets.somatic: Body experienced as microcosm of the universe.
facets.emotional: Emotional connection with cosmos through bodily meditation.
facets.cognitive: Mind expands to cosmic dimensions through Vaishvanara meditation.
facets.psychic: The soul meditates on the universal Self present in all, recognizing the cosmic person as its own larger being.

attributes.title: Three States Teaching (Waking, Dream, Deep Sleep)
attributes.aspect: त्रि अवस्था   ·   4.3.9-22 (Yajnavalkya to Janaka) The nature of this aspect. Yajnavalkya teaches Janaka about the three states. In waking, the Self uses the body and external senses. In dream, the Self uses only the subtle world made of its own impressions—'creates by its own light.' In deep sleep, the Self enters into itself, beyond desire, beyond fear, beyond all activity. The same Self moves through all three states.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic (tracing the Self through the states) · Ontic mode: Framework (a discriminative teaching — tracing the Self through the three states) · Valence: Neutral — a discriminative teaching · Guṇa: Sattva (a clarifying inquiry) · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Mechanism (an analytic method reaching the witness beyond the states) · Key relation: Method — analyzes the three states to disclose their witnessing ground.
coordinate.seat: Mental–Psychic (tracing the Self through the states)
coordinate.ontic_mode: Framework (a discriminative teaching — tracing the Self through the three states)
coordinate.causal_status: Mechanism (an analytic method reaching the witness beyond the states)
coordinate.key_relation: Method — analyzes the three states to disclose their witnessing ground

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: Awareness of one Self moving through all three states—not different selves but one consciousness in different conditions.
facets.somatic: Body recognized in each of its state-functions.
facets.emotional: Emotional content of each state seen against the underlying Self.
facets.cognitive: Mind recognizes the same Self in waking, dream, deep sleep.
facets.psychic: The soul learns, like Janaka under Yājñavalkya, to trace the Self through all three states to the freedom beyond them.

facets.emotional: Emotional content of one state dominating.
facets.cognitive: Mental imbalance from inadequate state-cycling.
facets.psychic: The soul over-intellectualizes the teaching, analyzing the states rather than recognizing the Self.

attributes.title: Annamaya Kośa — The Sheath of Matter

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical · Ontic mode: Sheath — the food-sheath (annamaya kośa) · Valence: Neutral (the outermost layer) · Guṇa: Tamas-predominant (Earth) · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Constituent layer (the physical body) · Key relation: Outermost of the five sheaths; the gross body (sthūla śarīra); Earth-dominant (Dim 1).
coordinate.key_relation: Outermost of the five sheaths; the gross body (sthūla śarīra); Earth-dominant (Dim 1)

additional_info.heading: The Layer: Earth-Dominant, Tamas-Predominant
additional_info.text: Annamaya literally means “made of food.” This outermost sheath is the physical body—the structure of skin, bone, muscle, organ, and tissue that is born from matter, sustained by matter, and dissolves back into matter at death. It is composed of the five gross elements (pañca mahābhūtas) and linked to the root chakra and the earth element. Its dominant guṇa is tamas—inertia, density, and unconsciousness. Its characteristic shakti is āvaraṇa shakti, the power of concealment, which veils the subtler dimensions of being behind the dense screen of physicality.

additional_info.heading: The Lived Experience of Dwelling Here
additional_info.text:
A person who resides predominantly at the level of annamaya kośa believes that they are only the physical body. Their identity is fused with the material form. They are attached and consumed solely by the physical: the pleasures of the body, the comforts of matter, the satisfaction of sensory appetites. Such persons orient their lives around physical fitness, bodily appearance, food, dress, comfort, sports, and the tangible accumulation of material security. They are drawn to activities like bodybuilding, athletics, and aerobics—not as doorways to subtler awareness, but as ends in themselves.

The experiential quality of this dwelling is characterized by what the tradition calls inertia. There is a solidity and groundedness to this person’s existence, but also a heaviness, a density that resists the upward movement of consciousness. Their concerns are survival concerns: food, shelter, safety, physical pleasure, and physical pain. Their suffering is predominantly physical—illness, injury, deprivation, the anxiety of bodily vulnerability, and the terror of death, which for them is absolute annihilation since they have no experiential reference to anything beyond the body.

When annamaya kośa is out of balance—through poor diet, lack of movement, excess tamasic foods, or neglect of the body’s elemental needs—the consequences ripple outward into every other dimension. The physical body is the foundation. If the foundation is unstable, the entire edifice of subtle experience totters. This is why the tradition, even while pointing to the transcendence of the physical, never dismisses it. All modern medicine acts on this kośa, and the first practices prescribed by yoga—āsana, fasting (upavāsa), inner purification (tatvashudhi), and austerity (tapashcharyā)—are aimed precisely at refining this sheath.

“The personality or constitution of the individual—comprising physical, mental, social and emotional traits—all depend on the condition of annamaya kośa, the formation of which continues in each birth or life one has.”  — Koshas-Gunas

attributes.title: Prāṇamaya Kośa — The Sheath of Vital Energy

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Vital · Ontic mode: Sheath — the vital-sheath (prāṇamaya kośa) · Valence: Neutral · Guṇa: Rajas-predominant (Water / Fire) · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Constituent layer (the vital body) · Key relation: Second sheath; part of the subtle body; the prāṇas (Dim 6).
coordinate.key_relation: Second sheath; part of the subtle body; the prāṇas (Dim 6)

additional_info.heading: The Layer: Water/Fire-Linked, Rajas-Predominant
additional_info.text: Moving inward from the body, we encounter the prāṇamaya kośa—the sheath of vital energy, the dimension of life-force (prāṇa) that animates the otherwise inert physical form. This sheath contains the five prāṇas—prāṇa (inward movement), apāna (downward excretion), udāna (upward expression), samāna (digestive assimilation), and vyāna (circulatory distribution)—the vital winds that govern breathing, digestion, circulation, elimination, and expression. Its dominant guṇa is rajas—activity, movement, dynamism. Its characteristic shakti is kriyā shakti, the power of action.

additional_info.heading: The Lived Experience of Dwelling Here
additional_info.text:
A person who dwells predominantly at the level of prāṇamaya kośa experiences themselves as a field of energy rather than merely a lump of matter. They have, at least temporarily, intuited that they are the finer force animating the physical form, not the form itself. Such people are characteristically very active and energetic. They are drawn to practices that cultivate and direct vital energy—prāṇāyāma, vigorous exercise, dynamic meditation, outdoor activity—and they give importance to both physical movement and the subtle currents of vitality that underlie it.

The experiential quality of this dwelling is characterized by movement, dynamism, and a heightened sensitivity to energetic states. This person notices when their energy is high or depleted, when a room feels charged or stagnant, when certain foods enliven them and others drag them down. Their suffering arises not from physical pain alone but from energetic disturbance—fatigue, restlessness, the scattered quality of unregulated prāṇa, the feeling of being “wired but tired,” the anxiety that comes when the vital winds blow chaotically rather than rhythmically.

The prāṇamaya kośa serves as the critical bridge between body and mind. It interconnects the annamaya kośa with the manomaya, vijñānamaya, and ānandamaya sheaths. It transmits the intentions of the mental and emotional sheaths into physical action through alterations in the breath—which is why the breath changes when we are afraid, aroused, concentrated, or at peace. This is not metaphor; it is mechanism. The tradition understands that the rhythms of the breath are intimately tied to the earliest and most fundamental layers of mental life.

When prāṇamaya kośa is imbalanced, the person experiences a characteristic set of disturbances: irregular breathing, digestive irregularity, circulatory problems, chronic fatigue alternating with manic energy, and a pervasive sense that their “battery is low” or erratically charged. The polyvagal lens described by Stephen Porges maps onto this dimension with striking precision: the dorsal vagal immobilization response corresponds to tamasic prāṇic collapse, the sympathetic fight-flight response to rajasic prāṇic agitation, and the ventral vagal social engagement response to sattvic prāṇic equilibrium.

attributes.title: Manomaya Kośa — The Sheath of Mind

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Vital · Ontic mode: Sheath — the mind-sheath (manomaya kośa) · Valence: Neutral · Guṇa: Sattva–Tamas mixed (Fire / Air) · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Constituent layer (the sensory-emotional mind) · Key relation: Third sheath; the emotional-sensory mind; part of the subtle body.
coordinate.key_relation: Third sheath; the emotional-sensory mind; part of the subtle body

additional_info.heading: The Layer: Fire/Air-Linked, Sattva-Tamas Mixed
additional_info.text: The manomaya kośa is the mental sheath—the dimension of thoughts, emotions, desires, memories, and imagination. It contains both the jñānendriyas (organs of perception—hearing, touch, sight, taste, smell) and the karmendriyas (organs of action—speech, hands, legs, reproduction, excretion). It is nourished by knowledge and education. Its guṇa composition is a mixture of sattva and tamas—it possesses both the capacity for knowing and the tendency toward veiling. Its characteristic shakti is icchā shakti, the power of will and desire.

additional_info.heading: The Lived Experience of Dwelling Here
additional_info.text:
The person who resides predominantly at the level of manomaya kośa lives in a world of feelings, desires, impressions, and emotional reactions. Their identity is woven from thoughts and desires that identify with form, name, position, and qualities. They are emotional by nature. This person may possess keen aesthetic sensitivity—a fine appreciation for music, dance, drama, visual art, and the emotional textures of human experience—but they lack the cognitive discriminative capacity of the next sheath. They feel intensely but cannot always discern why they feel what they feel, or whether what they feel is leading them toward truth or illusion.

The lived quality of this dwelling is characterized by fluctuation. The mind, in the Vedantic view, is inherently vikari—changeful, restless, oscillating between saṅkalpas (constructive intentions) and vikalpas (aversive rejections). The person at manomaya experiences life as an emotional rollercoaster: joy alternating with sorrow, attraction with repulsion, confidence with self-doubt, love with fear. Their vrittis (mental modifications)—lust, anger, greed, attachment, pride, jealousy—are in continual fluctuation. This is the sheath where most of humanity dwells for most of their lives, caught in the weather systems of the mind.

The suffering that arises from dwelling at this layer is psychological suffering in its most recognizable forms: anxiety, depression, obsession, emotional reactivity, the anguish of unfulfilled desires, the bitterness of perceived injustice, the loneliness of feeling misunderstood. Because manomaya kośa processes experience through the lens of preference and aversion, everything that happens is immediately sorted into “for me” or “against me,” “pleasant” or “unpleasant,” “wanted” or “unwanted.” This binary sorting mechanism is extraordinarily useful for navigating the sensory world, but it is also the engine of suffering, because it can never rest. There is always something to want and something to fear.

The tradition prescribes specific practices for refining this sheath: meditation, alternate nostril breathing (nāḍī shodhana), the yamas and niyamas (ethical observances), and selfless service (karmayoga). These practices do not suppress the mind but gradually teach it to observe itself, to notice the arising of a thought or emotion without being swept away by it, to create a sliver of space between stimulus and response in which choice becomes possible.

attributes.title: Vijñānamaya Kośa — The Sheath of Discernment

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Sheath — the wisdom-sheath (vijñānamaya kośa) · Valence: Neutral · Guṇa: Sattva–Rajas mixed (Air) · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Constituent layer (the discerning intellect) · Key relation: Fourth sheath; the intellect / buddhi; part of the subtle body; kin to Vijñāna (Dim 28).
coordinate.key_relation: Fourth sheath; the intellect / buddhi; part of the subtle body; kin to Vijñāna (Dim 28)

additional_info.heading: The Layer: Air-Dominant, Sattva-Rajas Mixed
additional_info.text:
Vijñāna literally means intellect or discerning wisdom. This fourth sheath is the dimension of buddhi—the discriminative faculty that lies beneath the processing, reactive mind. It is composed of the intellect combined with the five sensory organs, but its function is qualitatively different from the mind’s function. Where manas (mind) receives, reacts, and processes, buddhi evaluates, discriminates, and decides. Where manas says “I want,” buddhi asks “Is this truly good for me?” Where manas is caught in the immediacy of experience, buddhi can step back and see the larger pattern, the longer arc, the deeper truth.

Its dominant guṇa composition is sattva mixed with rajas—clarity energized by purposeful activity. Its characteristic shakti is jñāna shakti, the power of wisdom. It is related to the throat chakra and the air element, and it is precisely this sheath that distinguishes human beings from animals. Animals possess annamaya, prāṇamaya, and manomaya—bodies, energy, emotions, and basic mental processing—but they lack buddhi, the capacity for self-reflective discrimination between what is good and bad, right and wrong, eternal and illusory.

additional_info.heading: The Lived Experience of Dwelling Here
additional_info.text:
The person who resides predominantly at the level of vijñānamaya kośa is knowledgeable and wise. They love literature, creative expression, and intellectual inquiry. They are good orators, clear thinkers, and natural leaders. This fourth sheath is the wisdom that lies beneath the processing, thinking aspect of the mind. This person knows, decides, judges, and discriminates between the information they process. They are innovative. Discovery, research, and management are the domains where these people naturally gravitate.

The experiential quality of this dwelling is characterized by clarity, purposefulness, and the capacity for what we might call moral and intellectual vision. The person at vijñānamaya does not merely react to life; they understand it. They can see through the surface turbulence of emotional experience to the structural patterns beneath. They make decisions not from impulse or emotional pressure but from a considered assessment of what is true, what is valuable, and what will genuinely serve the larger good. Their inner life is characterized not by the rollercoaster of manomaya but by a steadier flame—still subject to flickering, but fundamentally oriented toward light.

The suffering that arises at this layer is subtler but no less real: the suffering of excessive analysis, of intellectual pride, of ego-identification with one’s intelligence and judgment. Because vijñānamaya is nourished by ego (ahaṃkāra) as well as by wisdom, there is a danger of confusing one’s capacity for discernment with omniscience—of mistaking the map for the territory, the model for reality. The person who dwells exclusively at this level may become overly cerebral, disconnected from the emotional and physical dimensions of life, trapped in a crystalline palace of ideas that is brilliant but cold.

The tradition addresses this by prescribing the study of scriptures (svādhyāya), right inquiry (“Who am I?”), and deep meditation—practices that turn the discriminating intellect upon itself, revealing that even the most refined thought is still a modification of consciousness, still a sheath, still a covering over something more essential. Vijñānamaya kośa is the doorway to transcendence, but it is not itself the destination.

attributes.title: Ānandamaya Kośa — The Sheath of Bliss

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: Sheath — the bliss-sheath (ānandamaya kośa) · Valence: Neutral (the innermost, subtlest veil) · Guṇa: Malina-sattva (near-pure Sattva) · Scale: Individual, toward the transcendent · Causal status: Constituent layer (the causal body) · Key relation: Innermost of the five sheaths; the causal body (kāraṇa śarīra); nearest the Self; kin to Ānanda (Dim 27).
coordinate.key_relation: Innermost of the five sheaths; the causal body (kāraṇa śarīra); nearest the Self; kin to Ānanda (Dim 27)

additional_info.heading: The Layer: Space-Aligned, Malina-Sattva (Pure Sattva Mixed with Traces of Rajas and Tamas)
additional_info.text:
Ānanda means bliss—not pleasure, not happiness in the ordinary sense, but the unconditioned, objectless joy that is the fundamental stuff of existence itself. The ānandamaya kośa is the innermost sheath, the most subtle veil covering the Ātman (the Self). It resides in the causal body (kāraṇa sharīra) and contains the vāsanās—the latent impressions and seed tendencies that are the causative blueprint for all the outer sheaths. It is the storehouse of all our impressions and latent energies; when these hidden impressions express themselves as feelings and thoughts, they take the form of the subtle body, and the same material works out as perceptions and actions in the gross body.

Its characteristic shakti is bhoga shakti—the power of pure experience, joy, and pleasure in the deepest sense. We glimpse this kośa every night in dreamless sleep, when the agitation of the mind and the striving of the intellect are temporarily suspended and consciousness rests in itself. Upon waking, we say “I slept well”—that sense of refreshment, that residual feeling of having been immersed in something restful and whole, is the afterglow of contact with the ānandamaya kośa.

additional_info.heading: The Lived Experience of Dwelling Here
additional_info.text:
Persons experiencing ānandamaya kośa are stable in behavior and firm in decision-making. They are happy in every state of life. They appreciate a higher order of things and thinking—nature, prayer, meditation, connection with the divine—not as occasional refuges from the stress of life but as the very ground of their being. They are self-realized persons. Their equanimity is not the forced calm of someone suppressing emotion but the natural ease of someone who has found, through direct experience, that their essential nature is already whole, already at peace, already sufficient.

The experiential quality of this dwelling is characterized by what the tradition calls intuition and idea-generation—not the discursive reasoning of buddhi, but a direct, unmediated knowing that arises from the depths of being. This person does not figure things out; things reveal themselves. Their creativity is not effortful but effortless, arising from a place of fullness rather than lack. Their relationships are characterized by unconditional warmth—not because they have worked hard to be loving, but because love is what naturally flows when the barriers of ego, fear, and self-protection are thinned to near-transparency.

Even at this refined level, the tradition is careful to note that the ānandamaya kośa is still a sheath—still a covering, however gossamer, over the Ātman. The bliss experienced here, while incomparably more refined than any sensory or emotional pleasure, is still conditioned, still a modification of consciousness, still material in the Vedantic sense. The ultimate aim of the pañca kośa viveka—the discriminative analysis of the five sheaths—is to recognize that even this bliss is not the final reality, that the Self is beyond all five sheaths, beyond all three bodies, beyond all states of consciousness.

“A person remains always happy and has a cheerful personality if he attains the Ānandamaya Kośa, a state of eternal peace, love and harmony. This transformation makes us videha—free from the body, detached from the body.”  — Pancha-Kosha Theory of Personality

additional_info.heading: IV. The Interplay: Kośas, Guṇas, and the Nervous System
additional_info.text:
One of the most important insights from the tradition is that the five kośas are not truly separate; they merge into one another like the colors of a rainbow. Each sheath influences and is influenced by every other. The physical state is an embodiment of a mental state; the mental state is shaped by the flow of prāṇa; the flow of prāṇa is guided by the discriminations of buddhi; and buddhi itself is colored by the vāsanās stored in the causal body.

Furthermore, all five kośas are equally material in the Vedantic sense—that is, they are all subject to the three guṇas (sattva, rajas, and tamas), the fundamental operating principles of all manifested nature. Even our feelings and thoughts are material entities governed by the same rules as our muscles and organs. This means that the guṇas are fundamentally unstable—they are always flowing into one another. Whatever seems sattvic today may have exactly the opposite impact tomorrow. By understanding this, we save ourselves the vast frustration of seeking a permanent solution that we will never find, and learn instead to attend to the living, shifting reality of each moment.

The correspondence between the kośas and the autonomic nervous system, as observed through the lens of Stephen Porges’ polyvagal theory, offers a compelling bridge between ancient and modern understanding. The dorsal vagal immobilization response—collapse, shutdown, dissociation—corresponds to tamasic dominance, the state in which consciousness retreats into the densest sheaths. The sympathetic fight-flight response—agitation, anxiety, reactivity—corresponds to rajasic dominance, the turbulent middle ground. The ventral vagal social engagement response—connection, presence, regulated calm—corresponds to sattvic equilibrium, the state in which the subtler sheaths become accessible and the whole system functions in integrated harmony.

additional_info.heading: V. The Path of Evolution: From Outer to Inner
additional_info.text:
The pañca kośa model is not static. It describes a trajectory of evolution—a progressive inward movement from identification with the outermost, densest sheath toward realization of the innermost, most subtle reality. This evolution is not automatic; it requires what the tradition calls tapas—internal research, sustained practice, conscious effort. Through the associated practices at each level—āsana and diet for annamaya, prāṇāyāma for prāṇamaya, yamas and niyamas for manomaya, self-inquiry and scripture study for vijñānamaya, and samādhi for ānandamaya—the practitioner gradually becomes freed from the bondages and constrictions of each successive sheath.

This does not mean abandoning or destroying the outer sheaths. The person who has awakened to ānandamaya kośa still has a body, still breathes, still thinks and feels and discriminates. But they are no longer imprisoned by any of these functions. They use the body without being the body. They ride the breath without being blown about by it. They observe the mind without being enslaved by its endless commentary. They exercise discrimination without mistaking their judgments for absolute truth. And they rest in bliss without clinging to it, knowing that even this is a sheath, and what they truly are lies beyond.

The causal body—the storehouse of vāsanās—is the key to understanding why we find ourselves dwelling at the particular kośa we do. Our latent impressions from this life and previous lives determine the default layer at which our consciousness operates. When the causal body is instilled with the suggestion of health, the subtle body entertains thoughts of health, and the gross body is bound to be healthy. The same principle applies in reverse: unresolved impressions of fear, craving, or ignorance in the causal body perpetuate patterns of suffering through the subtle and gross bodies, life after life.

This is precisely why the tradition describes transcendence of the kośas as the goal of human life. Not transcendence as escape, but transcendence as the full realization of what we already are—the Ātman, the Self that is distinct from all five sheaths, the consciousness that was never born and never dies, that is not diminished by the body’s decay or enhanced by the mind’s brilliance, that shines equally in the saint and the sinner, the genius and the fool, the newborn and the dying.

Kośā taih āvritah svātmā, vismṛtiyā saṃsṛtiṃ vajet  —  Enveloped by these sheaths, the Self, through forgetfulness, wanders in the cycle of becoming

The invitation of the kośa model is not to condemn any layer of our being but to know each one fully, to refine each one through appropriate practice, and to discover, through direct experience, that we are not any of them—that we are the light by which all sheaths are seen, the awareness in which all experience arises and dissolves, the silent witness that remains when every covering has been recognized for what it is: not a prison, but a sheath; not a limitation, but a lens through which the infinite chooses, for a time, to know itself in finite form.

Page 1

additional_info.heading: Additional Concepts from the Wider Scriptural Corpus
additional_info.text: The following concepts, drawn from the Taittirīya and other Upaniṣads, extend the treatment of the five sheaths — including the inquiry into the levels of bliss (ānanda-mīmāṁsā) and the mapping of the four states of consciousness onto the sheaths.

attributes.title: Four States and Five Sheaths (Indirect Reference)
attributes.aspect: चतुर्-अवस्था   ·   Question 4 The nature of this aspect. In waking, senses and mind are active. In dream, only mind active without senses. In deep sleep, prana alone remains. The fourth state (Turiya implied) is pure awareness witnessing all.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic (mapping the sheaths across the states of consciousness) · Ontic mode: Framework (a correspondence between the sheaths and the states) · Valence: Neutral — an orienting map · Guṇa: Sattva (a clarifying schema) · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Framework (relates the five sheaths to waking, dream, sleep, and Turīya) · Key relation: Cross-dimensional correspondence — the sheaths (Dim 5) mapped onto the four states of consciousness (Dim 4).
coordinate.seat: Mental–Psychic (mapping the sheaths across the states of consciousness)
coordinate.ontic_mode: Framework (a correspondence between the sheaths and the states)
coordinate.causal_status: Framework (relates the five sheaths to waking, dream, sleep, and Turīya)
coordinate.key_relation: Cross-dimensional correspondence — the sheaths (Dim 5) mapped onto the four states of consciousness (Dim 4)

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: Awareness through all states—waking activity, dream awareness, deep sleep recognition. The witness present in all.
facets.somatic: Body's three states clearly recognized.
facets.emotional: Emotional clarity about state-shifts.
facets.cognitive: Mind aware of its own modulations through states.
facets.psychic: The soul maps how the sheaths operate across waking, dream, and sleep, locating itself beyond all of them.

Psychic: The soul maps how the sheaths operate across waking, dream, and sleep, locating itself beyond all of them.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Pathological imbalance between states—insomnia, dream-disturbance, oversleeping.
facets.somatic: Sleep dysfunction.
facets.emotional: Emotional dysregulation from state imbalance.
facets.cognitive: Mental dysfunction from inadequate state-cycling.
facets.psychic: The soul over-systematizes the correspondences, lost in the scheme rather than the Self it reveals.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Unconscious cycling through states—no awareness of transitions, no recognition of the witness present in each.
facets.somatic: Mechanical sleep-wake cycles.
facets.emotional: Emotional contents of each state taken as ultimate.
facets.cognitive: Mind without awareness of state-transitions.
facets.psychic: The soul identifies wholly with the active sheath of the moment, never sensing what lies beneath.

attributes.title: Five Koshas (Sheaths of Self)
attributes.aspect: पञ्च कोश   ·   Brahmananda Valli The nature of this aspect. Five sheaths cover the Self: Annamaya (food/body), Pranamaya (vital energy), Manomaya (mind), Vijnanamaya (intellect), Anandamaya (bliss). Each subtler than the last; the Self lies beyond all five.

coordinate.raw: Seat: All layers (the sheaths are the layered coverings of the Self) · Ontic mode: Structure — the five-fold covering (a layered anatomy of the person) · Valence: Neutral — coverings to be recognized and peeled inward · Guṇa: Spans all three (from tamasic Annamaya to sattvic Ānandamaya) · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Structure (the graded coverings over the Ātman) · Key relation: Whole — the aggregate of which the five individual sheaths are the parts.

coordinate.causal_status: Structure (the graded coverings over the Ātman)
coordinate.key_relation: Whole — the aggregate of which the five individual sheaths are the parts

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: Recognition of all five sheaths and the Self beyond them. Progressive penetration through each level.
facets.somatic: Annamaya-kosha healthy.
facets.emotional: Pranamaya, manomaya healthy and integrated.
facets.cognitive: Vijnanamaya developed, anandamaya glimpsed, Self recognized as beyond all.
facets.psychic: The soul recognizes the five sheaths as coverings it wears, peeling inward toward the Self they conceal.

balanced.text:

Emotional: Pranamaya, manomaya healthy and integrated.

Cognitive: Vijnanamaya developed, anandamaya glimpsed, Self recognized as beyond all.

Psychic: The soul recognizes the five sheaths as coverings it wears, peeling inward toward the Self they conceal.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Identification with one kosha (commonly body or mind), or claiming to be 'beyond koshas' without realization.
facets.somatic: Identifying solely with annamaya.
facets.emotional: Identifying with pranamaya-emotions only.
facets.cognitive: Identifying with manomaya or vijnanamaya as ultimate.
facets.psychic: The soul mistakes a subtler sheath — even the blissful one — for the Self, stopping short of the center.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: No awareness of the koshas—life lived entirely at one level without recognition of others.
facets.somatic: Body taken as final self, no recognition of subtler levels.
facets.emotional: Emotional life without recognition of subtler dimensions.
facets.cognitive: Mind without map of inner reality.
facets.psychic: The soul is fused to the outermost sheath, the body, with no sense of the depths within.

attributes.title: Annamaya Kosha (Food Sheath)
attributes.aspect: अन्नमय कोश   ·   Brahmananda Valli 2.1 The nature of this aspect. Annamaya—the food sheath, the physical body. From food beings are born, by food they live, into food they return. The first sheath, the gross body.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical (the gross, food-built body) · Ontic mode: Constituent — the outermost sheath · Valence: Neutral faculty (the vessel — to be tended, not identified with) · Guṇa: Tamas-predominant (earth-dominant) · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Gross substrate (the densest covering) · Key relation: Part — outermost of the five sheaths; corresponds to Earth and the waking state.
coordinate.valence: Neutral faculty (the vessel — to be tended, not identified with)
coordinate.key_relation: Part — outermost of the five sheaths; corresponds to Earth and the waking state

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: Body well-nourished, healthy, recognized as a sheath of Self (not Self itself). Proper relationship with food.
facets.somatic: Healthy body, balanced nutrition.
facets.emotional: Emotional appreciation of body without identification.
facets.cognitive: Mental recognition of body as sheath, not Self.
facets.psychic: The soul tends the food-body well as the outermost vessel, neither despising nor over-identifying with it.

facets.emotional: Emotional life tied to body-image.
facets.cognitive: Mind dominated by body-concerns.
facets.psychic: The soul takes the physical body as its whole self, all identity invested in form and sensation.

facets.emotional: Emotional rejection of embodiment.
facets.cognitive: Mind dismissive of body's needs.
facets.psychic: The soul neglects the body, treating the food-sheath with contempt as if it did not matter.

attributes.title: Anandamaya (Bliss Sheath/Brahmananda)
attributes.aspect: आनन्दमय कोश   ·   Brahmananda Valli 2.5 The nature of this aspect. Anandamaya—the bliss sheath. The most subtle covering of Self. Brahmananda (bliss of Brahman) is described as infinitely greater than all human happiness combined—calculated through ascending blisses.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic (the subtlest covering, a reflection of the Self’s joy) · Ontic mode: Constituent — the innermost sheath · Valence: Neutral — the last covering, easily mistaken for the Self it veils · Guṇa: Sattva-predominant (space-aligned) · Scale: Individual, opening to the transcendent · Causal status: Causal (the causal-body sheath) · Key relation: Part — innermost of the five sheaths; corresponds to the causal body and deep sleep.

facets.emotional: Emotional bliss as recognition of deeper nature.
facets.cognitive: Mind dwelling in or moving toward anandamaya.
facets.psychic: The soul recognizes the bliss-sheath as the subtlest covering, a reflection of the Self's joy, and presses on toward the source.

attributes.title: Ananda Mimamsa (Inquiry into Bliss Levels)
attributes.aspect: आनन्द मीमांसा   ·   Implicit in 8.7-8.15 The nature of this aspect. Indra and Virochana go to Prajapati to learn the Self. Prajapati gives progressive teachings, but Virochana stops at 'body is Self' and goes happy. Indra realizes the inadequacy of each teaching and returns four times. Body-Self → Dream-Self → Deep-Sleep-Self → finally True Self (pure consciousness). This represents the levels of bliss-realization Indians later mapped onto the five koshas.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic (a graded inquiry into the levels of joy) · Ontic mode: Framework (an analytic ascent through the gradations of bliss) · Valence: Neutral — a discriminative inquiry · Guṇa: Sattva (a clarifying ascent) · Scale: Individual, ascending to the transcendent (Ādhidaivika) · Causal status: Mechanism (a method ascending from sensory pleasure to the bliss of Brahman) · Key relation: Ladder — the graded scale of joy culminating in Brahmānanda.

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: Indra-like persistence—not settling at any partial realization but inquiring through all levels until the true Self is found.
facets.somatic: Body engaged in continued spiritual labor across long time.
facets.emotional: Emotional readiness to be unsettled, willing to admit each realization is incomplete.
facets.cognitive: Mind continuously checking realization against the deepest truth.
facets.psychic: The soul inquires into the gradations of bliss, ascending from sensory pleasure toward the unconditioned joy of Brahman.

attributes.title: Anandamaya Vidya (Bliss Sheath Meditation)
attributes.aspect: आनन्दमय विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. Meditation on the Bliss-sheath as the most subtle covering of Self. Recognition that all human happinesses are tiny fractions of Brahmananda. The ascending calculation reveals the magnitude of true bliss.

attributes.title: Mitahara (Moderation in Diet)
attributes.aspect: GS 5.16-32 The nature of this aspect. Before pranayama, dietary preparation: 'Half the stomach with food, one quarter with water, one quarter empty for air.' Avoid bitter/sour/salty/pungent extremes, fermented food, eating late. Sweet, oily, cooling, easily digestible food preferred. Insight. Eat until ¾ full, not to fullness. Leave one-quarter of the stomach empty so the body can actually digest. This single discipline transforms energy and clarity.

attributes.title: Place & Time of Practice
attributes.aspect: GS 5.3-15 The nature of this aspect. Practice not in distant country, forest, capital, or crowd. Choose a good country, hut, well, raised platform. Avoid extreme seasons (winter cold, summer heat, monsoon, autumn). Spring and autumn ideal for beginning practice. Insight. Where and when you practice matters enormously. Create a dedicated space at home. Choose a specific time. Same place, same time = the nervous system shifts faster.

attributes.title: Nadi Shuddhi
attributes.aspect: GS 5.33-45 The nature of this aspect. Before regulated pranayama, channels must be cleansed. Samanu (mental, with bija mantras and visualization of elements) or Nirmanu (physical via shatkarmas). The Vayu cannot enter impure nadis. Insight. Don't try advanced breathing techniques on a polluted system. Clean the basics first: sleep, food, hydration, gentle alternate-nostril breathing. The advanced builds on the basic.

attributes.title: Sahita Kumbhaka
attributes.aspect: GS 5.46-57 The nature of this aspect. Pranayama with retention; can be Sagarbha (with bija mantra repetition and visualization) or Nirgarbha (without). Standard ratio 1:4:2 (inhale:retain:exhale). Three levels: lowest 12 matras, middling 16, best 20. Insight. Even a 4:16:8 ratio breath, done for 5 minutes, transforms the nervous system. Add a mantra silently during retention for added concentration benefit.

attributes.title: Surya Bheda Kumbhaka
attributes.aspect: GS 5.58-68 The nature of this aspect. Right-nostril (solar) inhalation, retention with Jalandhara, left-nostril exhalation. Until perspiration breaks at nail-tips and hair-roots. Destroys decay, awakens Kundalini, increases bodily fire. Insight. When sluggish or cold or in low energy, a few rounds of right-nostril breathing brings warmth and alertness within minutes — a natural alternative to caffeine.

attributes.title: Ujjayi Kumbhaka
attributes.aspect: GS 5.69-72 The nature of this aspect. Draw breath through both nostrils with throat constriction; retain with Jalandhara; exhale through left nostril. Cures phlegm disorders, nerve diseases, dysentery, consumption, cough, fever. Insight. Soft Ujjayi breathing while walking, working, or in stressful meetings is invisible but transformative. The throat sound steadies mind and breath simultaneously.

attributes.title: Bhramari Kumbhaka
attributes.aspect: GS 5.78-82 The nature of this aspect. Practice at past midnight in silence, ears closed. Inner sounds emerge progressively: crickets, lute, thunder, drum, beetle, bells, gongs, trumpets. The last is Anahata nada — the unstruck sound at the heart. Leads to Samadhi. Insight. Hum on the exhale for 5-10 minutes before bed. The vibration activates the vagus nerve, settles the nervous system, and often opens awareness of inner sound — the doorway to deep meditation.

attributes.title: Pancha Vayu
attributes.aspect: GS 5.60-65 The nature of this aspect. Five primary pranas: Prana (heart, intake), Apana (anus, elimination), Samana (navel, digestion), Udana (throat, expression), Vyana (whole body, circulation). Plus five subsidiary vayus controlling burp, blink, sneeze, yawn, and post-death sound. Insight. Notice where breath enters: chest (prana), belly (samana), throat (udana). Different breathing patterns activate different vayus — and different psychological states.

attributes.title: Ajapa Gayatri (So'ham)
attributes.aspect: GS 5.84-91 The nature of this aspect. Every breath naturally says 'so' (inhale) and 'ham' (exhale) — totaling 21,600 repetitions daily. Every living being unconsciously chants this; the yogi makes it conscious. 'I am That.' Insight. Throughout the day, listen to your breath as 'so-hum.' Each breath is a silent mantra; you've been chanting it your whole life. Just become aware of it.

attributes.title: Kevala Kumbhaka
attributes.aspect: GS 5.84-96 The nature of this aspect. Spontaneous, effortless breath suspension — neither inhale nor exhale needed. The breath naturally pauses on its own. Considered the highest pranayama; what one cannot accomplish through this is nothing on earth. Insight. The pauses between breaths get longer as practice matures. Don't force them — notice them. The pause is where mind quiets naturally.

attributes.title: Pranayama
attributes.aspect: HYP 2.1-3 The nature of this aspect. Conscious regulation of breath. Svatmarama's central insight (2.2): 'When the breath moves, the mind moves; when breath is still, the mind is still.' Insight. When agitated, don't try to calm the mind directly—slow and lengthen the breath. The mind follows automatically.

attributes.title: Nadi Shodhana
attributes.aspect: HYP 2.7-10 The nature of this aspect. Alternate nostril breathing—inhale through one nostril, exhale through the other, alternating; purifies the 72,000 nadis (energy channels) within three months of practice. Insight. 10 rounds of slow alternate-nostril breathing morning and evening rebalances the nervous system more reliably than any pill.

Victorious breath—slight throat constriction creating an ocean-like sound; can be done sitting or walking; clears phlegm, increases digestive fire. Insight. When stressed in traffic, before a difficult conversation, or while walking, slip into Ujjayi—a few breaths reset the autonomic state.

attributes.title: Bhastrika
attributes.aspect: HYP 2.59-67 The nature of this aspect. Bellows breath—rapid, forceful inhalation and exhalation like a blacksmith's bellows; awakens Kundalini, pierces the three knots, increases digestive fire. Insight. When sluggish, lethargic, or stuck—do 20-30 rapid bellows breaths. Wakes up the body-mind faster than caffeine.

When hot, irritated, or burned out, 10 rounds of Shitali tangibly cools the body and the emotional temperature.

attributes.title: Kundalini Shakti
attributes.aspect: HYP 3.1-5, 3.104-130 The nature of this aspect. Coiled serpent power at the base of the spine; when awakened pierces all chakras and grants liberation. Insight. The point isn't dramatic awakening — it's noticing the difference between your default depleted energy and what's possible when you live aligned.

attributes.title: Three Granthis
attributes.aspect: HYP 2.67, 4.70-76 The nature of this aspect. Three psychic knots: Brahma granthi (chest—matter/body attachment), Vishnu granthi (throat—emotion/heart attachment), Rudra granthi (brow—knowledge/identity attachment). Insight. Notice which knot binds you most: bodily craving (Brahma), emotional attachment (Vishnu), or identity/intellect (Rudra). Work consciously with that knot.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Psychic (the three psychic knots) · Ontic mode: Structural obstruction (the knots along the central channel) · Valence: Affliction-as-obstacle (to be loosened) · Guṇa: Tamas (binding, obstructing) · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Condition (the obstructions that must yield for energy to rise) · Key relation: Correspondence — the gates on the Suṣumnā that the rising Kuṇḍalinī must pass.

attributes.title: Ida, Pingala, Sushumna
attributes.aspect: HYP 3.4, 2.46 The nature of this aspect. Three primary nadis: Ida (left, lunar, cooling, receptive), Pingala (right, solar, heating, active), Sushumna (central, the spiritual highway). Insight. Check which nostril dominates throughout the day—that's a real-time biofeedback on which mode you're in. Active work? Pingala. Rest, creativity? Ida.

attributes.title: Eulogy of Chant on Breath (Prana Stotra)
attributes.aspect: Chapter 1, Third Brahmana The nature of this aspect. The gods discovered that demons (negative forces) could pierce all sense-functions with evil except Prana. Hence chanting performed in alignment with breath (prana-stotra) carries inherent cosmic protection. This is the basis of all breath-chant practices—rhythmic chanting that follows the breath's natural cycle. Insight. Prana is the only sense-function that demons could not pierce with evil. Therefore, the chant performed on Prana (prana-stotra) carries supreme cosmic protection. Aligning one's chanting with the breath links it to the indestructible cosmic life-principle.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Psychic (reverence for the breath) · Ontic mode: Framework / teaching (a hymn extolling prāṇa) · Valence: Neutral — a devotional recognition · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual, with cosmic dimension · Causal status: Framework (establishes the breath as chief among the powers) · Key relation: Correspondence — exalts prāṇa as the incorruptible power on which the senses depend.

attributes.title: Rayi and Prana (Matter and Life Force)

attributes.title: Sixteen Kalas (Parts of the Person)
attributes.aspect: षोडश कला   ·   Question 6 The nature of this aspect. The Purusha within has sixteen kalas (parts): prana, faith, space, air, fire, water, earth, senses, mind, food, virility, austerity, mantras, karma, worlds, name. When realized, all sixteen merge into the Self.

attributes.title: Pancha-Agni Vidya (Five Fires Doctrine)
attributes.aspect: पञ्चाग्नि विद्या   ·   5.4-10 The nature of this aspect. Reality is portrayed as a continuous sacrificial fire-cycle through five fires: the heavenly world, the rain cloud, the earth, man, and woman. Through each fire, the previous offering becomes the next form. Soul transmigrates through these five fires. Understanding this cycle reveals the continuity of life beyond apparent death.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Mental (contemplation of the cosmic fire-cycle) · Ontic mode: Doctrine / practice (a contemplative vidyā) · Valence: Neutral — a contemplative teaching · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Cosmic (Ādhidaivika), contemplated individually · Causal status: Framework (maps the sacrificial round of birth and rebirth) · Key relation: Correspondence — weaves the individual into the cosmic cycle of the five fires.

facets.cognitive: Mind grasps the systematic nature of life-death-rebirth.
facets.psychic: The soul contemplates the cosmic fire-cycle, seeing its life woven into the great sacrificial round of existence.

attributes.title: Pancha Prana (Five Vital Forces)
attributes.aspect: पञ्च प्राण   ·   1.5.21-23, 3.9.26 The nature of this aspect. Five pranas (vital forces) animate the body: Prana (in-breath, head), Apana (down-breath, elimination), Vyana (circulating breath, throughout body), Udana (up-breath, throat/speech), Samana (equalizing breath, digestion). All five are forms of cosmic Prana. When prana is gathered to the heart at death, the body falls.

attributes.title: Samvarga Vidya (All-Absorbent Meditation)
attributes.aspect: संवर्ग विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. All-absorbent meditation. Process: harmonising Prana, concentration on unity of psychic beings of individual and universe. Atonement of Prana and Vayu enables reaching Maha-Prana (Hiranyagarbha). Denying food to a guest = denying food to Cosmic Life-Principle. Knower becomes cosmic consumer of food (Hiranyagarbha).

facets.overview: Overview: No recognition that Prana is the cosmic absorber—life lived as fragmented sense-engagements with no unifying principle.
facets.somatic: Body's pranas scattered, no integration.
facets.emotional: Emotional fragmentation across senses.
facets.cognitive: Mind doesn't recognize Prana as the unifying principle.
facets.psychic: The soul never gathers its powers, its energies scattered with nothing to absorb them.

attributes.title: Prana Vidya (Vital Life Principle)
attributes.aspect: प्राण विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. Meditation on Prana as Brahman itself. The Mukhya-Prana (chief prana) is the starting point of superphysical life. Recognition that all sense-functions exist only because of Prana; without it, no eye sees, no ear hears.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Psychic (meditation on prāṇa as Brahman) · Ontic mode: Practice (an upāsana on the life-force) · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual, opening to the cosmic · Causal status: Practice (a means to realize prāṇa as the doorway to the absolute) · Key relation: Correspondence — reveres prāṇa as Brahman; the meditative counterpart to ‘Prāṇa as Ruler of the Body.’
coordinate.causal_status: Practice (a means to realize prāṇa as the doorway to the absolute)
coordinate.key_relation: Correspondence — reveres prāṇa as Brahman; the meditative counterpart to ‘Prāṇa as Ruler of the Body.’

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: Prana recognized as the supreme principle in body; all functions seen as Prana's manifestations. Direct contact with the Life-Principle.
facets.somatic: Body's prana cultivated and recognized as foundational, healthy vital function.
facets.emotional: Emotional vitality from Prana-recognition.
facets.cognitive: Mind acknowledges Prana as the source of all its functions.
facets.psychic: The soul meditates on prāṇa as Brahman itself, the chief life-force revered as the doorway to the absolute.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Forced pranayama techniques without preparation, pranic disorders.
facets.somatic: Pranic imbalances from forced practice.
facets.emotional: Emotional volatility from improper Prana work.
facets.cognitive: Mental disturbance from forced prana techniques.
facets.psychic: The soul fixates on prāṇa-phenomena, chasing energetic experience over realization.

facets.cognitive: Mind without recognition of its prana-dependence.

attributes.title: Valence (Sukha–Duḥkha Tone of Experience)
attributes.aspect: -  Phenomenological Dimension: Valence (Sukha–Duḥkha Tone of Experience) Valence is the first vibration of experience—the immediate yes or no that arises before thought, before story, before morality. In Vedic terms, it is the raw movement of rāga (pull) and dveṣa (push), the subtle evaluator that precedes cognition and quietly governs karma. Below, valence is explored across three states of being, followed by two contrasting vignettes situated in a single modern scenario.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital (the pleasure–pain tone of experience) · Ontic mode: Experiential axis (a dimension along which experience varies) · Valence: Neutral faculty (balance is equanimity, Samatā) · Guṇa: Variable (rajasic in Rāga / Dveṣa dominance) · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Manifestation (the felt tone; governed by attraction and aversion) · Key relation: Bipolar axis (Sukha–Duḥkha) — driven by Rāga and Dveṣa.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Experiential axis (a dimension along which experience varies)
coordinate.causal_status: Manifestation (the felt tone; governed by attraction and aversion)
coordinate.key_relation: Bipolar axis (Sukha–Duḥkha) — driven by Rāga and Dveṣa

balanced.label: 1. Equilibrium — Balanced Valence (Samatā)

facets.overview: Pleasant and unpleasant are both allowed to appear without commandeering the self. Internal Landscape External Conduct Decisions are made without urgency. Work is engaged sincerely but without emotional overinvestment. Relationships have space—affection without clinging, disagreement without aversion. Habits feel clean because they are not compensatory. Day-in-the-Life Vignette (Equilibrium) He receives a message: a project proposal has been rejected. A brief contraction in the chest, a dull unpleasantness. He notices it while standing at the sink. He exhales, lets the shoulders drop. The unpleasant tone does not become a story about failure. Ten minutes later, he drafts a revised plan. In the evening, he enjoys dinner without replaying the email. The day remains whole.
facets.somatic: The body feels responsive rather than reactive. Pleasant sensations are enjoyed without tightening; unpleasant sensations are registered without collapse. There is a gentle elasticity in the gut and chest—no bracing, no chasing.
facets.emotional: Pleasure does not demand repetition. Discomfort does not demand escape. The emotional field is fluid, with no dominant hunger. There is an underlying tone of trust—that experience can be met as it comes.
facets.cognitive: Thought does not immediately label sensations as “good for me” or “bad for me.” The mind stays descriptive rather than evaluative. Meaning is allowed to emerge rather than being imposed.
facets.psychic: At the soul level, there is samatva—equanimity. Sukha and duḥkha are known as movements within prakṛti, not verdicts on the self. Valence is seen, not obeyed.

balanced.text:

Work is engaged sincerely but without emotional overinvestment. Relationships have space—affection without clinging, disagreement without aversion. Habits feel clean because they are not compensatory. Day-in-the-Life Vignette (Equilibrium) He receives a message: a project proposal has been rejected. A brief contraction in the chest, a dull unpleasantness. He notices it while standing at the sink. He exhales, lets the shoulders drop. The unpleasant tone does not become a story about failure. Ten minutes later, he drafts a revised plan. In the evening, he enjoys dinner without replaying the email. The day remains whole.

Somatic: The body feels responsive rather than reactive. Pleasant sensations are enjoyed without tightening; unpleasant sensations are registered without collapse. There is a gentle elasticity in the gut and chest—no bracing, no chasing.

Emotional: Pleasure does not demand repetition. Discomfort does not demand escape. The emotional field is fluid, with no dominant hunger. There is an underlying tone of trust—that experience can be met as it comes.

Cognitive: Thought does not immediately label sensations as “good for me” or “bad for me.” The mind stays descriptive rather than evaluative. Meaning is allowed to emerge rather than being imposed.

Psychic: At the soul level, there is samatva—equanimity. Sukha and duḥkha are known as movements within prakṛti, not verdicts on the self. Valence is seen, not obeyed.

excess.label: 2. Excess — Hyper-Positive or Hyper-Negative Valence (Rāga / Dveṣa Dominance)

facets.overview: Experience is hijacked by attraction or aversion. Internal Landscape External Conduct Work becomes compulsive or avoidant. Relationships become transactional—people are valued for how they regulate one’s internal tone. Habits harden into addictions, distractions, or rigid control patterns. Day-in-the-Life Vignette (Excess) She wakes already scanning for something pleasant—her phone, a message, a hit of reassurance. When none arrives, irritation blooms. At work, mild feedback feels like an attack. She replays it all day. In the evening, she binge-watches, eats past fullness, scrolls long after exhaustion. Pleasure is pursued frantically, yet relief never arrives.
facets.somatic: The body is pulled forward or recoils. In craving, there is a leaning tension—jaw tight, breath shallow. In aversion, there is bracing—gut clenched, shoulders raised. Sensation feels urgent.
facets.emotional: Pleasure carries desperation; discomfort carries threat. There is a constant undertone of “this must continue” or “this must stop.” Emotional neutrality feels intolerable.
facets.cognitive: The mind rationalizes: “I need this to feel okay” or “I can’t handle this.” Experience is misinterpreted as a command. Valence masquerades as truth.
facets.psychic: The witnessing self collapses into the wave. The soul forgets its seat and identifies with sensation. Rāga or dveṣa becomes destiny.

Relationships become transactional—people are valued for how they regulate one’s internal tone. Habits harden into addictions, distractions, or rigid control patterns. Day-in-the-Life Vignette (Excess) She wakes already scanning for something pleasant—her phone, a message, a hit of reassurance. When none arrives, irritation blooms. At work, mild feedback feels like an attack. She replays it all day. In the evening, she binge-watches, eats past fullness, scrolls long after exhaustion. Pleasure is pursued frantically, yet relief never arrives.

Somatic: The body is pulled forward or recoils. In craving, there is a leaning tension—jaw tight, breath shallow. In aversion, there is bracing—gut clenched, shoulders raised. Sensation feels urgent.

Emotional: Pleasure carries desperation; discomfort carries threat. There is a constant undertone of “this must continue” or “this must stop.” Emotional neutrality feels intolerable.

Cognitive: The mind rationalizes: “I need this to feel okay” or “I can’t handle this.” Experience is misinterpreted as a command. Valence masquerades as truth.

Psychic: The witnessing self collapses into the wave. The soul forgets its seat and identifies with sensation. Rāga or dveṣa becomes destiny.

deficient.label: 3. Deficiency — Flattened Valence (Anhedonia / Numbness)

facets.overview: Pleasant and unpleasant lose their signal value. Internal Landscape External Conduct Work is done mechanically. Relationships feel distant, not hostile. Habits drift toward inertia. Life continues, but without intimacy. Day-in-the-Life Vignette (Deficiency) He completes his tasks efficiently but without satisfaction. Compliments land flat. Criticism barely stings. In the evening, he sits quietly, unsure what he wants to do. Nothing feels wrong, but nothing feels alive.
facets.somatic: The body feels dull or distant. Sensations are muted, as if filtered through cotton. There is little warmth, little sharpness.
facets.emotional: Neither desire nor aversion moves strongly. Life feels gray—not painful enough to flee, not pleasant enough to approach. Motivation is minimal.
facets.cognitive: Thoughts circle around “What’s the point?” Meaning feels inaccessible. Even positive events fail to register as rewarding.
facets.psychic: The soul’s signal is faint. Not lost—but veiled. The lesson here is not restraint but re-contact.

additional_info.heading: I. The Three States of Valence
II. Two Contrasting Vignettes
additional_info.text:
One Scenario: Evening Fatigue After Work

Scenario A: The Binding Path (Low Epistemic Integrity)

Internal Landscape

A wave of unpleasant valence arises—tightness, heaviness. The mind mislabels it: “I’m stressed. I deserve relief.” The discomfort is assumed to be an enemy.

Conduct (Agāmi Karma)

He reaches for alcohol, junk food, endless scrolling. Action is driven by escape, not understanding.

Outcome (Saṃskāra Reinforced)

The nervous system learns: unpleasant = avoid immediately. The saṃskāra of rāga–dveṣa deepens. The threshold for discomfort lowers. Tomorrow, the same wave will feel stronger.

Scenario B: The Liberating Path (High Epistemic Integrity)

Internal Landscape

The same unpleasant wave arises. This time, it is named honestly: “There is heaviness. This is valence, not a command.” He stays with the sensation—no narration, no resistance.

Conduct (Path of Engagement)

He chooses Karma Yoga in a small form—takes a walk, prepares a simple meal attentively, breath steady. Action without self-soothing agenda.

Outcome (Saṃskāra Exhausted)

The wave completes itself. Nothing was suppressed or indulged. The old saṃskāra weakens. Valence is experienced as weather, not fate. A quiet dignity returns.

Closing Integration

Valence is not the problem. Misidentification with valence is.

When pleasantness and unpleasantness are seen clearly:

They guide without ruling

Inform without enslaving

Pass without leaving residue

In Vedic terms, liberation does not come from eliminating sukha or duḥkha, but from refusing to grant them epistemic authority.

To live with phenomenological integrity is to feel fully—

without being owned by what is felt.

attributes.title: Arousal (Intensity / Activation)
attributes.aspect: Phenomenological Dimension: Arousal (Intensity / Activation) Prāṇic amplitude of lived experience Arousal is the volume knob of consciousness. It determines how forcefully experience arrives, how urgently it demands response, and how compressed or dilated the moment feels. In Vedic terms, it is the movement of prāṇa under the influence of rajas (over-activation) and tamas (under-activation), with sattva as regulated flow. Arousal does not decide what is felt—that is valence. It decides how intensely life presses upon the system.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Vital (nervous activation / prāṇa flow) · Ontic mode: Experiential axis (a dimension of first-person experience) · Valence: Neutral faculty (balance is regulated arousal) · Guṇa: Spans the triad (sattvic balance, rajasic overdrive, tamasic collapse) · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Manifestation (the activation level of body and mind) · Key relation: Bipolar axis (hyperarousal–hypoarousal) — tracks the guṇas and prāṇa.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Experiential axis (a dimension of first-person experience)
coordinate.guna: Spans the triad (sattvic balance, rajasic overdrive, tamasic collapse)
coordinate.causal_status: Manifestation (the activation level of body and mind)
coordinate.key_relation: Bipolar axis (hyperarousal–hypoarousal) — tracks the guṇas and prāṇa

balanced.label: 1. Equilibrium — Regulated Arousal (Sattvic Prāṇa Flow)

facets.overview: Energy is available without agitation; rest is possible without collapse. Internal Landscape External Conduct Work unfolds in focused blocks. Breaks are taken before depletion. Relationships feel responsive rather than reactive. Habits support rhythm—sleep, food, movement align naturally. Day-in-the-Life Vignette (Equilibrium) She begins the morning with a clear sense of the day’s demands. During a challenging meeting, her heart rate rises slightly, sharpening attention. When it ends, the activation settles on its own. By evening, energy tapers gently. She sleeps without replaying the day. Nothing feels forced; nothing feels avoided.
facets.somatic: The body feels alert but not tight. Breath is full and rhythmic. Muscles are engaged only as needed. There is readiness without bracing.
facets.emotional: Emotion has momentum but not urgency. Interest arises without compulsion. Calm is not dullness; excitement is not frenzy. There is a steady inner hum.
facets.cognitive: Thoughts appear at an appropriate pace. The mind can hold complexity without racing or freezing. Attention moves voluntarily rather than being yanked.
facets.psychic: At the soul level, there is sthira–sukham—steadiness with ease. Prāṇa serves awareness rather than hijacking it. One feels capable.

balanced.text:
Energy is available without agitation; rest is possible without collapse. Internal Landscape External Conduct Work unfolds in focused blocks. Breaks are taken before depletion. Relationships feel responsive rather than reactive. Habits support rhythm—sleep, food, movement align naturally. Day-in-the-Life Vignette (Equilibrium) She begins the morning with a clear sense of the day’s demands. During a challenging meeting, her heart rate rises slightly, sharpening attention. When it ends, the activation settles on its own. By evening, energy tapers gently. She sleeps without replaying the day. Nothing feels forced; nothing feels avoided.

Somatic: The body feels alert but not tight. Breath is full and rhythmic. Muscles are engaged only as needed. There is readiness without bracing.

Emotional: Emotion has momentum but not urgency. Interest arises without compulsion. Calm is not dullness; excitement is not frenzy. There is a steady inner hum.

Cognitive: Thoughts appear at an appropriate pace. The mind can hold complexity without racing or freezing. Attention moves voluntarily rather than being yanked.

Psychic: At the soul level, there is sthira–sukham—steadiness with ease. Prāṇa serves awareness rather than hijacking it. One feels capable.

excess.label: 2. Excess — Hyperarousal (Rājasic Overdrive)

facets.overview: Energy exceeds the system’s capacity to metabolize it. Internal Landscape External Conduct Work becomes frantic or perfectionistic. Multitasking replaces presence. Relationships suffer from irritability or emotional flooding. Habits skew toward stimulants, overwork, or constant distraction. Day-in-the-Life Vignette (Excess) He wakes already late, heart pounding before his feet touch the floor. Emails pile up faster than he can answer them. A minor delay spikes irritation. Even in the evening, his body won’t downshift—scrolling, snacking, pacing. Exhausted but wired, he collapses into shallow sleep.
facets.somatic: The body is tight, keyed up. Breath is shallow or rapid. Jaw clenched, shoulders lifted. There is a sense of always being “on.”
facets.emotional: Urgency dominates. Even neutral situations feel charged. There is impatience with slowness, silence, or uncertainty. Rest feels unsafe.
facets.cognitive: Thoughts race, fragment, or catastrophize. The mind jumps ahead, scanning for threat or opportunity. Stillness is interpreted as danger or waste.
facets.psychic: The witnessing self is drowned out by momentum. Prāṇa runs the show. The soul experiences itself as chased by time.

excess.text:
Energy exceeds the system’s capacity to metabolize it. Internal Landscape External Conduct Work becomes frantic or perfectionistic. Multitasking replaces presence. Relationships suffer from irritability or emotional flooding. Habits skew toward stimulants, overwork, or constant distraction. Day-in-the-Life Vignette (Excess) He wakes already late, heart pounding before his feet touch the floor. Emails pile up faster than he can answer them. A minor delay spikes irritation. Even in the evening, his body won’t downshift—scrolling, snacking, pacing. Exhausted but wired, he collapses into shallow sleep.

Somatic: The body is tight, keyed up. Breath is shallow or rapid. Jaw clenched, shoulders lifted. There is a sense of always being “on.”

Emotional: Urgency dominates. Even neutral situations feel charged. There is impatience with slowness, silence, or uncertainty. Rest feels unsafe.

Cognitive: Thoughts race, fragment, or catastrophize. The mind jumps ahead, scanning for threat or opportunity. Stillness is interpreted as danger or waste.

Psychic: The witnessing self is drowned out by momentum. Prāṇa runs the show. The soul experiences itself as chased by time.

deficient.label: 3. Deficiency — Hypoarousal (Tāmasic Collapse)

facets.overview: Energy falls below what engagement requires. Internal Landscape External Conduct Work is postponed or done mechanically. Social contact feels draining. Habits drift toward passivity—sleeping too much, scrolling without engagement, avoiding challenge. Day-in-the-Life Vignette (Deficiency) She sits at her desk staring at the screen. The task isn’t hard, but starting feels impossible. Hours pass without momentum. Even pleasant invitations feel like burdens. At night, she goes to bed early—not from satisfaction, but from depletion.
facets.somatic: The body feels heavy, slow, or inert. Breath is shallow but sluggish. Limbs feel weighted. Initiation requires effort.
facets.emotional: Emotion is muted. There is little urgency—but also little interest. Desire feels distant; aversion feels blunted. The dominant tone is fatigue.
facets.cognitive: Thoughts move slowly or loop repetitively. Concentration is difficult. Tasks feel overwhelming not because they are intense, but because energy is insufficient.
facets.psychic: The soul feels veiled, not absent. Awareness remains, but prāṇa does not rise to meet life. The lesson here is re-ignition, not restraint.
