attributes.title: Rāja Yoga — The Path of the Integrator
attributes.aspect: "Elevate yourself through the power of your mind, and do not degrade yourself, for the mind can be the friend and also the enemy of the self." — Bhagavad Gītā 6.5 Rāja Yoga, the “royal path,” is the discipline of aligning body, breath, and mind through systematic practice. Codified by Patañjali in the Yoga Sūtras as the eightfold path (Aṣṭāṅga Yoga), it moves through eight limbs—from ethical conduct (Yama and Niyama) through physical posture (Āsana), breath regulation (Prāṇāyāma), sensory withdrawal (Pratyāhāra), concentration (Dhāraṇā), meditation (Dhyāna), and finally Samādhi, the transcendental state. Vivekananda regarded it as the surest and quickest method of attaining salvation, while acknowledging it requires immense faith in oneself as well as great physical and mental strength. The Integrator’s engagement with reality is fundamentally regulatory. It is the path of those who process experience through the management of inner states. The driving question is not “What must be done?” or “What is true?” or “What do I love?” but “How do I align?”

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Psychic (inner discipline and prāṇa) · Ontic mode: Practice — a mārga (mode of engagement through disciplined integration) · Valence: Cultivated path · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Means (a path to freedom through inner alignment) · Key relation: One of the four mārgas — the path of the Integrator; the eightfold Yoga (Dim 11) is its method.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Practice — a mārga (mode of engagement through disciplined integration)
coordinate.causal_status: Means (a path to freedom through inner alignment)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the four mārgas — the path of the Integrator; the eightfold Yoga (Dim 11) is its method

balanced.label: 4.1 Equilibrium: The Centered Self

facets.somatic: In equilibrium, the body of the Rāja Yogi is the most tangibly “known” of any path. There is an intimate relationship with the body’s architecture: the practitioner feels the quality of their breath, the alignment of their spine, the state of their digestion, not as medical data but as lived texture. The body feels “centered”—a word that is overused but here carries precise meaning: the felt sense of weight distributed evenly, of muscles neither gripping nor collapsing, of the nervous system in a state of alert relaxation. Energy is managed, not merely consumed; there is an understanding that sleep, food, and movement are not obligations but calibrations.
facets.emotional: The emotional tone is steadiness. Not the absence of emotion but a quality of resilience—the capacity to hold strong feelings without being capsized by them. In a chaotic environment, the balanced Integrator’s internal state remains steady, like a gyroscope that maintains its orientation regardless of how the ship pitches. There is a high degree of impulse control that does not feel like suppression but like sovereignty—the felt sense that one’s responses are chosen rather than compelled.
facets.cognitive: The thoughts of the balanced Integrator are characterized by a quality that Patañjali describes as ekāgratā—one-pointedness. The mind streams toward a single focus without distraction or interruption. This is not the furrowed concentration of effort but the easeful attention of a mind that has been trained. The inner monologue is relatively quiet; there is more awareness than commentary. When the mind does think, it does so clearly, without the overlapping chatter of competing narratives.
facets.psychic: At the soul level, balanced Rāja Yoga is the experience of the mind becoming transparent to something deeper. As Kṛṣṇa explains, when the mind is restrained and detached from material desires, it experiences inner strength and peace; one can unite the self with the Almighty and attain a bliss that is beyond the experience of mundane situations. The practitioner begins to sense the Turiya—the fourth state of consciousness beyond waking, dreaming, and dreamless sleep—not as a mystical abstraction but as a quality of clarity that permeates all three.

sections.vignette_a_tuesday_in_the_life_of_the_ce: He wakes at 5:30 and sits for twenty minutes of meditation before the house stirs. He does not experience fireworks—just the gradual quieting of the night’s residual mental chatter, like a snow globe settling. Breakfast is deliberate: he eats slowly, noticing when he is full. On the commute, someone cuts him off in traffic. He feels the surge of adrenaline, notes it (‘there it is’), and lets it pass through him rather than feeding it with narrative. At work, a crisis erupts mid-morning—a product launch has a critical bug. While colleagues spiral, he feels the room’s energy like weather. He takes one slow breath, then says: ‘What’s the single most important thing to fix in the next hour?’ The question cuts through the panic. In the evening, he does thirty minutes of yoga—not for fitness but as maintenance, the way a musician tunes an instrument. He sleeps deeply, his nervous system already having processed the day’s friction through the morning’s practice.

excess.label: 4.2 Excess: The Rigid Ascetic

facets.overview: When Rāja Yoga dominates, discipline hardens into rigidity. The tools of self-regulation—routine, restraint, practice—become ends in themselves rather than means to liberation. The Integrator tips into what might be called a “perfectionist’s burnout,” where the focus on purity and control extinguishes the very spontaneity and warmth that make life worth living.
facets.somatic: The body of the rigid ascetic is tightly controlled but curiously brittle. The posture is excellent but locked; there is a quality of holding that prevents the body from responding fluidly to the unexpected. The person may be extremely fit but lack the softness of real physical ease. Minor disruptions to routine—a late flight, a missed meal, an unavailable gym—produce disproportionate stress responses. The body has been disciplined but not befriended; it is a servant, not a partner.
facets.emotional: The emotional landscape is characterized by a cold, clinical detachment that the person may mistake for equanimity. There is a subtle judgment directed toward those who lack discipline—the colleague who eats fast food, the friend who sleeps in on weekends, the partner who cannot sit still for five minutes. Intimacy suffers because vulnerability feels like a loss of control. The emotional hunger is for perfection, which is, by definition, a hunger that can never be satisfied.
facets.cognitive: The inner monologue is a continuous audit of compliance: “Did I meditate long enough? Was my posture correct? Did I eat the right thing?” The mind, trained to monitor itself, becomes a merciless supervisor. There is a particular form of spiritual anxiety that emerges: the fear that one’s practice is “not enough,” that enlightenment is being thwarted by some subtle failure of discipline. The spontaneity that characterizes genuine spiritual opening is precisely what this rigidity prevents.
facets.psychic: At the soul level, excess Rāja Yoga is the paradox of using the cage of discipline to seek freedom. The ātman cannot be reached through control alone—it requires surrender, which is the domain of Bhakti. The spiritual lesson being offered is that the final limb, Samādhi, is not an achievement of willpower but a grace that descends when the practitioner finally relaxes their grip.

sections.vignette_a_tuesday_in_the_life_of_the_ri: She rises at 4:30 AM—exactly 4:30, never 4:31—and begins her practice. Forty minutes of meditation, twenty minutes of prāṇāyāma, forty-five minutes of āsana. She eats a precisely measured breakfast of soaked almonds, fresh fruit, and warm water with lemon. At work, a team lunch is organized at a local restaurant. She declines. The food is not “clean” enough. She eats her prepared meal alone at her desk, feeling a familiar mix of superiority and loneliness. When her partner suggests a spontaneous weekend trip, she hesitates—it would disrupt her Saturday practice schedule. They go, but she is tense the entire time, mentally calculating how to fit in her routine at the hotel. On Sunday night, she cannot sleep. Not because the trip was stressful, but because her body, trained to expect a precise schedule, does not know what to do with the deviation. She lies in the dark, rigid and alert, the mind doing its auditing work: what was missed, what must be compensated for, how to restore order.

She lies in the dark, rigid and alert, the mind doing its auditing work: what was missed, what must be compensated for, how to restore order.

deficient.label: 4.3 Deficiency: The Scattered Self

facets.overview: When Rāja Yoga is deficient, the individual lacks the internal architecture of self-regulation. Without discipline, the mind and body become mirrors of the environment—if the room is chaotic, the mind is chaotic; if the mood of the group shifts, the person’s state shifts with it. There is no gyroscope, no center.
facets.somatic: The body of the person with deficient Rāja Yoga is reactive and unpredictable. Energy levels spike and crash according to no discernible rhythm. Sleep is erratic—sometimes too much, sometimes too little, rarely restorative. The body feels like a vehicle with a faulty transmission: it accelerates when it should brake, stalls when it should move. There is a characteristic disconnection between what the body needs and what the person provides it; meals are skipped or binged, exercise is all-or-nothing, rest is replaced by collapse.
facets.emotional: The emotional landscape is turbulent. Without the regulatory framework of discipline, every stimulus produces a full-scale response. A critical email triggers a cascade of shame. A compliment produces a disproportionate high. The person cannot modulate their emotional volume; everything is either muted or overwhelming. Habits are impossible to maintain—not from lack of desire but from lack of the inner structure that converts desire into consistent action.
facets.cognitive: The thoughts are scattered, rapid, and unfinished. The inner monologue resembles a browser with forty-seven open tabs, each competing for attention. Plans are made and abandoned. Insights are glimpsed and lost. There is a particular frustration that accompanies this state: the person can see what they should be doing but cannot organize themselves to do it. The mind, without the training of dhāraṇā (concentration) and dhyāna (meditation), defaults to its unregulated state of compulsive fluctuation—what Patañjali calls citta-vṛtti, the turbulence of the mind-stuff.
facets.psychic: At the soul level, deficient Rāja Yoga is the condition of a lamp in a windstorm. The light of the ātman is present but cannot be perceived because the flame of attention is perpetually guttering. The spiritual lesson is the most basic one of the path: that the mind, left to its own devices, is the enemy of the self—but the same mind, trained and directed, becomes its greatest friend.

sections.vignette_a_tuesday_in_the_life_of_the_sc: He wakes at different times every day. Today it’s 8:20, twenty minutes after he should have left. He skips breakfast, grabs coffee, and drives in a state of low-grade panic that feels like his baseline. At his desk, he opens four applications simultaneously and accomplishes nothing substantial in any of them. He checks his phone sixty-three times by noon—not because anything important arrives but because the checking itself provides micro-hits of stimulation that fill the void left by sustained attention. He had planned to start a workout routine this week; the equipment is still in its box. By mid-afternoon, a colleague’s offhand frustration sends his mood plummeting. By evening, his partner’s enthusiasm for a cooking project lifts it. He is a weather vane, responding to every current. At midnight, unable to sleep, he scrolls through meditation apps—reading about discipline rather than practicing it—and falls asleep with the phone on his chest.

attributes.title: Bhakti Yoga — The Path of the Lover
attributes.aspect: "Of those who fix their minds upon the Almighty, constantly glorify Him and possess great faith—such a one is considered a Bhakta." — Bhagavad Gītā 12.2 Bhakti Yoga is the path of devotion and love—the most emotionally accessible of the four paths, emphasizing a deep, personal relationship with the divine through prayer, chanting, singing, and surrender. The Lover’s engagement with reality is fundamentally relational. It is the path of those who process experience through the heart, whose primary question is not “What must be done?” or “What is true?” or “How do I align?” but “What do I love?” The tradition describes a progression from Gauṇī Bhakti (devotion supported by external aids—rituals, images, mantras) to Parā Bhakti (supreme devotion, a spontaneous and unconditional love that transcends all forms). At its highest expression, Vivekananda taught, there is no difference between Parā Bhakti and the highest knowledge (Jñāna)—in the highest state of love, the lover loses himself in the Beloved and attains the state of Non-duality. Five emotional attitudes (Bhāvas) characterize the devotee’s relationship: peaceful reverence (Śānta), devoted service (Dāsya), sacred friendship (Sakhya), parental tenderness (Vātsalya), and the most intimate communion of lover and beloved (Mādhurya).

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Psychic (the heart and devotion) · Ontic mode: Practice — a mārga (mode of engagement through love and surrender) · Valence: Cultivated path · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant (warm) · Scale: Individual, in relation to the Divine · Causal status: Means (a path to freedom through devotion) · Key relation: One of the four mārgas — the path of the Lover; faculty of heart and emotion.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Practice — a mārga (mode of engagement through love and surrender)
coordinate.scale: Individual, in relation to the Divine
coordinate.causal_status: Means (a path to freedom through devotion)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the four mārgas — the path of the Lover; faculty of heart and emotion

balanced.label: 5.1 Equilibrium: The Open Heart

facets.somatic: In equilibrium, the body of the Bhakti Yogi carries a quality of openness, particularly in the chest and the face. The breath is naturally deep and full—not regulated by technique but released by the simple fact of being at ease. There is a warmth to the physical presence that others register instinctively: people want to sit near this person. The body responds to beauty with real physiological signals—the skin prickles at a piece of music, the eyes moisten at an act of kindness, the chest expands with a sunrise. These are not exaggerated reactions but the body’s natural resonance when the heart is undefended.
facets.emotional: The emotional tone is gratitude—not the performative gratitude of journals and affirmations but the organic experience of finding the world, on the whole, beautiful and worth engaging with. The balanced Bhakti practitioner sees relationships as deep and meaningful and finds significance in the mundane: a cup of tea, a conversation with a stranger, the way afternoon light falls through a window. The underlying drive is toward connection—not clinging but genuine intimacy, the willingness to be touched by life without needing to control it.
facets.cognitive: The thoughts of the balanced Lover are characterized by a quality of receptivity. The mind is not passive but hospitable—it welcomes experience rather than fortifying against it. There is a natural tendency to see the divine in the particular: this person, this moment, this difficulty. The cognitive framework is relational rather than analytical; understanding comes not through dissection but through empathy and imaginative participation in the experience of others.
facets.psychic: At the soul level, balanced Bhakti is the experience of being held—the sense that one is not alone in the universe, that there is a presence (however one names it) that receives one’s offerings of attention and returns something nourishing. The spiritual lesson is that surrender is not weakness but the highest form of strength: the ego’s dissolution into love is not a loss but the recovery of one’s true identity. As the tradition teaches, in the state of Parā Bhakti, love knows no bargaining, no fear, and no rival.

sections.vignette_a_tuesday_in_the_life_of_the_op: She wakes to the sound of birds and lies still for a moment, feeling something she can only describe as ‘full.’ Not full of plans or energy but of presence. On the commute, she catches the eye of an elderly man on the bus and smiles without thinking. He smiles back. The small exchange warms her for an hour. At work, a junior colleague makes an error that creates extra work. Instead of irritation, she feels a flash of recognition—she made the same mistake once. She sits with the colleague and walks through the correction with the patient attention of someone who genuinely cares about the person, not just the output. In the evening, she lights a candle—not as a ritual but as a gesture toward the day’s end that feels right, like a period at the end of a sentence. She reads a poem aloud to no one in particular and finds her eyes wet. Not from sadness. From the simple, unexplainable fact of being alive and aware of it.

excess.label: 5.2 Excess: The Overwhelmed Heart

facets.overview: When Bhakti dominates, the heart’s openness becomes a liability. The person becomes emotionally volatile, tossed about by feelings the way the Jñāna-deficient person is tossed by opinions. There is a loss of grounding in practical reality—bills go unpaid, health is neglected, responsibilities are deferred because the person is overly focused on emotional or spiritual “highs.” The Gītā’s teaching that the Bhakta must control their senses and remain mentally composed in all situations is precisely the corrective needed here—and precisely what the excess Bhakti practitioner has not yet integrated.
facets.somatic: The body of the overwhelmed heart is porous—it absorbs the emotional atmosphere of every room. The nervous system is hypersensitive: crowds are exhausting, conflict is physically painful, even positive emotional intensity can produce a crash. There may be psychosomatic symptoms that mirror emotional states—chest tightness during relational tension, stomach distress during emotional processing. The boundary between one’s own somatic experience and someone else’s becomes blurred.
facets.emotional: The emotional landscape is a roller coaster without brakes. Joy is ecstatic, sorrow is devastating, love is all-consuming, disappointment is catastrophic. There is a quality of emotional addiction—the person seeks intensity rather than depth, confusing the height of the wave with the profundity of the ocean. Relationships become codependent; the person’s sense of self depends on the emotional feedback of others. When that feedback withdraws, there is a kind of existential free-fall.
facets.cognitive: The inner monologue is dominated by relational processing: “Do they love me? Did I say the wrong thing? What does this feeling mean?” The mind, rather than serving clarity, is co-opted by the heart’s turbulence. Practical thinking—logistics, planning, analysis—feels dry and burdensome. The person may develop a quiet contempt for the “mundane” aspects of life, which they experience as obstacles to feeling rather than containers for it.
facets.psychic: At the soul level, excess Bhakti is the attachment to the experience of devotion rather than to the Beloved itself. It is Gauṇī Bhakti that has not yet matured into Parā Bhakti—devotion that still depends on external stimulation and emotional highs. The spiritual lesson is that true love, as Vivekananda taught, knows no bargaining; it persists regardless of what is returned. The overwhelmed heart must learn that devotion which requires constant emotional reward is not yet devotion—it is need.

sections.vignette_a_tuesday_in_the_life_of_the_ov: She wakes feeling fragile—not from any specific event but from a dream she can’t remember that left a residue of longing. She checks her messages immediately, hoping for something warm. A friend’s casual response to her heartfelt text feels like a small rejection. She cries in the shower, then laughs at herself for crying, then cries again. At work, she pours herself into a mentoring conversation with such emotional intensity that the mentee becomes slightly uncomfortable. She notices and feels a wave of shame. She has trouble focusing on her quarterly report because numbers feel ‘soulless.’ In the evening, she puts on devotional music and loses herself in it for an hour, tears streaming—then realizes she forgot to eat dinner and the electricity bill is overdue. She pays it hastily, resenting the intrusion of the practical into what felt like a sacred evening. She sleeps poorly, the emotional residue of the day still swirling.

deficient.label: 5.3 Deficiency: The Dry World

facets.overview: When Bhakti is suppressed, the individual lives in a world that is functional but drained of meaning. Life operates, but it does not sing. The tradition describes this as a spiritual aridity in which the heart’s natural capacity for connection, wonder, and devotion has been sealed off—sometimes by trauma, sometimes by the excessive dominance of one of the other paths (particularly an intellectualism that has trained the person to distrust feeling), and sometimes by the gradual erosion of neglect.
facets.somatic: The body of the person with deficient Bhakti often carries a quality of contraction, particularly in the chest and throat. Breathing is adequate but confined—the deep, expansive breaths of genuine emotional engagement are absent. There is a physical guardedness: the shoulders are slightly raised, the arms tend to fold across the chest, the facial muscles around the eyes are held tight. The body is functional—it works, it digests, it sleeps—but it does not resonate. Beauty passes through the visual field without producing a corresponding physical response.
facets.emotional: The emotional landscape is flat—not depressed in the clinical sense but arid. The person might describe themselves as “numb” or “apathetic,” though they are often highly functional. Everything is logical and operational, but nothing feels significant or beautiful. Relationships are maintained but not nourished; they are efficient rather than intimate. The underlying tone is a quiet cynicism: the sense that idealism is naivety, that hope is self-delusion, that the world is fundamentally mechanical rather than meaningful.
facets.cognitive: The thoughts are efficient but colorless. The inner monologue is dominated by logistics and analysis—the language of utility. “Is this useful?” replaces “Is this beautiful?” Aesthetic, spiritual, and emotional considerations are filed under “irrational” and dismissed. There is a particular resistance to vulnerability in thought—the mind avoids the open-ended, the uncertain, the mysterious, because these require a faculty (the heart) that is currently offline.
facets.psychic: At the soul level, deficient Bhakti is the experience of exile from one’s own depths. The ātman is not absent but inaccessible—sealed behind layers of protective rationality or practical busyness. The spiritual lesson is that the soul speaks through the heart, and when the heart is closed, the soul’s communications—the sudden catch of beauty, the inexplicable tenderness, the longing for something unnamed—are mistaken for weakness and suppressed. The tradition teaches that all people will experience emotions such as love, compassion, and devotion at points along the journey, regardless of which path predominates. Bhakti deficiency is the resistance to this universal truth.

sections.vignette_a_tuesday_in_the_life_of_the_dr: He is looking at a sunset. It is, objectively, spectacular—the sky is streaked with color, the light is doing extraordinary things to the ordinary buildings. He notices this the way he might notice a well-designed graph: with appreciation for the data but without being moved by it. His partner says, ‘Isn’t that incredible?’ He says, ‘Yeah, it’s nice.’ He means it. But the word ‘nice’ contains the entire poverty of his response—there is no resonance, no ‘Aha!’ His inner world feels like a desert. At dinner, his daughter tells him about a friendship drama at school. He listens, offers practical advice, and misses the fact that she didn’t want solutions—she wanted him to feel it with her. Later, alone, he picks up a novel his wife recommended. He reads ten pages, finds it ‘well-written but slow,’ and switches to a report for work. The truth he cannot yet see is that the novel is not slow—his heart is.

additional_info.heading: Chapter 6: The Integrated Self — When All Paths Converge
additional_info.text:
"All streams of Yoga lead to the same goal. Do it by work, worship, philosophy, or psychic control—by any one or two or all of these—and be free." — Swami Vivekananda

The most important insight from the Vedantic tradition is that these four paths are not competing options on a spiritual menu but complementary dimensions of a single, integrated human life. Vivekananda’s great contribution was his insistence on Jñāna-Karma-Bhakti Samuccaya—the synthesis of all paths—against the classical view that they were incompatible. He recognized that every individual identifies with mind, intellect, heart, and body, and hence must practice techniques from each path, even while choosing the one that best suits their temperament.

In lived experience, this integration is not a theoretical achievement but a felt quality of wholeness. It is the experience of being simultaneously effective and relaxed (Karma + Yoga), discerning and warm (Jñāna + Bhakti), disciplined and spontaneous (Yoga + Bhakti), active and contemplative (Karma + Jñāna). When all four paths are functioning in reasonable equilibrium, the person experiences what the Gītā describes as the state of the sthita-prajña—the person of steady wisdom—who is present, responsive, and unshaken by the fluctuations of fortune.

additional_info.heading: 6.1 The Phenomenology of Integration
additional_info.text:
Integration does not mean that all four faculties are equally strong at all times. It means that each faculty is available when needed and that no single faculty has colonized the others. The integrated person can shift fluidly between modes: acting decisively when the moment calls for it (Karma), stepping back to analyze when clarity is needed (Jñāna), centering themselves when the environment becomes chaotic (Yoga), and opening their heart when connection is what the situation requires (Bhakti).

The somatic signature of integration is a body that breathes fully and responds proportionately. The emotional signature is a capacity for the entire range of human feeling without being captured by any single emotion. The cognitive signature is a mind that thinks clearly when thinking is called for and rests when it is not. And the psychic signature is a growing sense of participation in something larger than one’s personal narrative—the quiet, persistent intuition that the ātman and Brahman are not two.

additional_info.heading: 6.2 The Diagnostic Value of the Framework
additional_info.text:
Perhaps the most practical application of this framework is as a diagnostic tool for self-understanding. When life feels “stuck,” the four-path model can help identify which dimension is out of balance. The person who is exhausted but cannot stop working has a Karma excess and a Bhakti deficiency—they have forgotten how to receive. The person who understands everything but changes nothing has a Jñāna excess and a Karma deficiency—knowledge has not yet become action. The person who is disciplined but lonely has a Yoga excess and a Bhakti deficiency—control has displaced connection. And the person who is emotionally rich but practically chaotic has a Bhakti excess and a Yoga deficiency—the heart has overwhelmed the container that should hold it.

The remedy, in every case, is not to suppress the dominant path but to activate the deficient one. The workaholic does not need less action but more devotion. The intellectual does not need less thinking but more embodied practice. The ascetic does not need less discipline but more love. And the emotionally overwhelmed person does not need less feeling but more structure. The paths correct each other when held in dynamic balance—and it is precisely this balance, this samuccaya, that constitutes the fullness of the human experience the tradition calls yoga—union.

additional_info.heading: Closing Vignette: A Tuesday in the Life of the Integrated Self
additional_info.text: She finishes a long day of work. She is tired but satisfied—not because she ‘won,’ but because she did the work well. She handled a difficult conversation with a colleague by first pausing to center herself (Yoga), then speaking clearly about what she observed (Jñāna), then acting on what was needed (Karma), and finally telling the colleague, genuinely, ‘I appreciate you hearing me out’ (Bhakti). In the evening, she sits for ten minutes of quiet breathing. She does not have a mystical experience. She simply notices the breath entering and leaving, the quiet hum of the house, the fact that she is here. A wave of something rises—gratitude, perhaps, or just the simple recognition that this moment is enough. She is not searching for something. She is in something. The four rivers of action, knowledge, discipline, and love have, for this quiet moment, converged into a single stream.

additional_info.heading: Sources & Acknowledgments
additional_info.text:
This narrative draws upon the Bhagavad Gītā (particularly Chapters 2–5, 6, and 12), Patañjali’s Yoga Sūtras, the interpretive work of Swami Vivekananda on the four paths of Yoga (Karma, Jñāna, Rāja, and Bhakti Yoga), the Hatha Yoga Pradīpikā, and contemporary scholarly analyses including Amit Kumar Batabyal’s work on Vivekananda’s philosophy of liberation and applied research on the four paths in education and clinical settings. Sanskrit transliterations follow standard IAST conventions throughout.

additional_info.heading: Additional Concepts from the Wider Scriptural Corpus
additional_info.text: The following concepts, drawn from the Bhagavad Gītā and the Upaniṣads, extend the treatment of the four paths — the foundational definitions of Karma, Jñāna, Yoga, and Bhakti, action without bondage, the two paths of Sāṁkhya and Yoga, and the subtle states of action and intent.

attributes.title: Karma (Action and Consequence)
attributes.aspect: कर्म   ·   Chapter 2, Chapter 3, Chapter 4 The nature of this aspect. Karma is the universal law of cause and effect. Every action creates consequences that shape future experiences and destiny. Karma is neutral—neither inherently good nor bad—but actions create corresponding results.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Vital (action and its consequences) · Ontic mode: Process (the law of action-and-consequence unfolding over time) · Valence: Neutral — a law of reality to understand and align with · Guṇa: Takes the coloring of the action · Scale: Individual, within a cosmic moral order · Causal status: Cause-and-effect law (sowing and reaping) · Key relation: The principle to which Karma Yoga (the path) is the response; kin to saṁskāra and prārabdha.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Process (the law of action-and-consequence unfolding over time)
coordinate.valence: Neutral — a law of reality to understand and align with
coordinate.guna: Takes the coloring of the action
coordinate.scale: Individual, within a cosmic moral order
coordinate.key_relation: The principle to which Karma Yoga (the path) is the response; kin to saṁskāra and prārabdha

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When Karma is understood properly, a person acts with awareness of consequences, takes responsibility for their actions, maintains ethical conduct, and accepts results with equanimity.
facets.somatic: Physical health benefits from ethical action, good energy and vitality, absence of stress-related illness, positive physical circumstances aligned with good karma, physical ability to manifest intended outcomes.
facets.emotional: Emotional peace from taking responsibility, absence of guilt and shame, emotional freedom from acceptance of consequences, emotional resilience and adaptability, inner calm about past and future.
facets.cognitive: Clear thinking about cause and effect, responsibility and accountability in thinking, wisdom in foreseeing consequences, ability to learn from experience, mental clarity about one's role in circumstances.
facets.psychic: The soul acts with awareness of consequence, sowing wisely and accepting the harvest of its deeds.

balanced.text:
Overview: When Karma is understood properly, a person acts with awareness of consequences, takes responsibility for their actions, maintains ethical conduct, and accepts results with equanimity.

Somatic: Physical health benefits from ethical action, good energy and vitality, absence of stress-related illness, positive physical circumstances aligned with good karma, physical ability to manifest intended outcomes.

Emotional: Emotional peace from taking responsibility, absence of guilt and shame, emotional freedom from acceptance of consequences, emotional resilience and adaptability, inner calm about past and future.

Cognitive: Clear thinking about cause and effect, responsibility and accountability in thinking, wisdom in foreseeing consequences, ability to learn from experience, mental clarity about one's role in circumstances.

Psychic: The soul acts with awareness of consequence, sowing wisely and accepting the harvest of its deeds.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Excessive focus on Karma can lead to paralysis, obsessive calculation of karmic debts, fatalism, and inability to forgive oneself or others.
facets.somatic: Physical paralysis and inability to move forward, stress-related illness from anxiety about karma, tightness and rigidity in body, exhaustion from excessive self-monitoring, health problems from inability to act.
facets.emotional: Anxiety about past actions and future consequences, obsessive guilt and self-blame, inability to forgive self or others, emotional paralysis and indecision, fatalism and hopelessness, spiritual anxiety and dread.
facets.cognitive: Obsessive thinking about karma and debts, analysis paralysis from fear of consequences, rumination about past actions, inability to make decisions due to karma anxiety, victim mentality despite understanding karma.
facets.psychic: The soul becomes anxiously fixated on consequences, paralyzed by calculation of every result.

excess.text:
Overview: Excessive focus on Karma can lead to paralysis, obsessive calculation of karmic debts, fatalism, and inability to forgive oneself or others.

Somatic: Physical paralysis and inability to move forward, stress-related illness from anxiety about karma, tightness and rigidity in body, exhaustion from excessive self-monitoring, health problems from inability to act.

Emotional: Anxiety about past actions and future consequences, obsessive guilt and self-blame, inability to forgive self or others, emotional paralysis and indecision, fatalism and hopelessness, spiritual anxiety and dread.

Cognitive: Obsessive thinking about karma and debts, analysis paralysis from fear of consequences, rumination about past actions, inability to make decisions due to karma anxiety, victim mentality despite understanding karma.

Psychic: The soul becomes anxiously fixated on consequences, paralyzed by calculation of every result.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Lack of understanding of Karma leads to reckless actions, irresponsibility, harm to others without consequences, lack of accountability.
facets.somatic: Physical health problems from reckless behavior, accidents and injuries from carelessness, poor health from harmful choices, physical chaos from disorganization, weak or disease-prone body.
facets.emotional: Emotional immaturity and lack of responsibility, emotional harm to others and to self, lack of remorse or accountability, emotional reactivity without understanding, relationship damage from irresponsibility.
facets.cognitive: Lack of understanding of cause and effect, poor decision-making from ignoring consequences, refusal to take responsibility, blaming others for circumstances, inability to learn from mistakes, scattered thinking.
facets.psychic: The soul acts heedless of consequence, surprised again and again by the harvest of its own sowing.

deficient.text:
Overview: Lack of understanding of Karma leads to reckless actions, irresponsibility, harm to others without consequences, lack of accountability.

Somatic: Physical health problems from reckless behavior, accidents and injuries from carelessness, poor health from harmful choices, physical chaos from disorganization, weak or disease-prone body.

Emotional: Emotional immaturity and lack of responsibility, emotional harm to others and to self, lack of remorse or accountability, emotional reactivity without understanding, relationship damage from irresponsibility.

Cognitive: Lack of understanding of cause and effect, poor decision-making from ignoring consequences, refusal to take responsibility, blaming others for circumstances, inability to learn from mistakes, scattered thinking.

Psychic: The soul acts heedless of consequence, surprised again and again by the harvest of its own sowing.

attributes.title: Bhakti (Devotion)
attributes.aspect: भक्ति   ·   Chapter 12 The nature of this aspect. Bhakti is devotion and love directed toward the divine. It is a path of surrender, emotional connection, and personal relationship with the sacred. Bhakti transforms the heart and connects the individual to transcendent reality.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital (the heart’s capacity for devotion) · Ontic mode: Faculty (the heart’s capacity for love and surrender) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual, toward the Divine · Causal status: Mechanism (the heart’s opening toward the Divine) · Key relation: The capacity underlying Bhakti Yoga (the path); ripens into self-offering.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Faculty (the heart’s capacity for love and surrender)
coordinate.causal_status: Mechanism (the heart’s opening toward the Divine)
coordinate.key_relation: The capacity underlying Bhakti Yoga (the path); ripens into self-offering

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When Bhakti is balanced, a person experiences love, surrender, inner peace, emotional healing, connection to the divine, and purpose. Spiritual practice becomes joyful rather than forced.
facets.somatic: Open chest and heart area, free and easy breathing, physical ease and relaxation, healthy circulation, radiant appearance, good health from love and peace, physical expression of affection and warmth.
facets.emotional: Deep love and compassion, inner peace and contentment, emotional openness and vulnerability, joy in spiritual practice, connection to others through love, emotional healing and wholeness, genuine care for others.
facets.cognitive: Mental peace and simplicity, clarity of purpose through devotion, connection between intellect and heart, trust and faith in divine, mental surrender to what is, wisdom through love.
facets.psychic: The soul opens its heart in devotion, love and surrender carrying it toward the Divine.

balanced.text:
Overview: When Bhakti is balanced, a person experiences love, surrender, inner peace, emotional healing, connection to the divine, and purpose. Spiritual practice becomes joyful rather than forced.

Somatic: Open chest and heart area, free and easy breathing, physical ease and relaxation, healthy circulation, radiant appearance, good health from love and peace, physical expression of affection and warmth.

Emotional: Deep love and compassion, inner peace and contentment, emotional openness and vulnerability, joy in spiritual practice, connection to others through love, emotional healing and wholeness, genuine care for others.

Cognitive: Mental peace and simplicity, clarity of purpose through devotion, connection between intellect and heart, trust and faith in divine, mental surrender to what is, wisdom through love.

Psychic: The soul opens its heart in devotion, love and surrender carrying it toward the Divine.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Excessive Bhakti can lead to emotional dependence, loss of personal agency, blind faith, and spiritual bypassing of practical responsibilities.
facets.somatic: Loss of physical self-care, weakness from neglecting body, obsessive rituals and practices, physical dependence on spiritual teacher, poor health from over-restriction, physical rigidity in devotional postures.
facets.emotional: Emotional dependence on divine figure or guru, loss of emotional autonomy and boundaries, emotional manipulation under guise of devotion, loss of critical emotional discernment, emotional fusion with another person or ideal.
facets.cognitive: Blind faith without discrimination, loss of critical thinking, mental dependence on authority figure, refusal to question teachings, mental slavery to orthodoxy, inability to trust own insight.
facets.psychic: Devotion inflates into emotional excess or possessive attachment, fervor mistaken for depth.

excess.text:
Overview: Excessive Bhakti can lead to emotional dependence, loss of personal agency, blind faith, and spiritual bypassing of practical responsibilities.

Somatic: Loss of physical self-care, weakness from neglecting body, obsessive rituals and practices, physical dependence on spiritual teacher, poor health from over-restriction, physical rigidity in devotional postures.

Emotional: Emotional dependence on divine figure or guru, loss of emotional autonomy and boundaries, emotional manipulation under guise of devotion, loss of critical emotional discernment, emotional fusion with another person or ideal.

Cognitive: Blind faith without discrimination, loss of critical thinking, mental dependence on authority figure, refusal to question teachings, mental slavery to orthodoxy, inability to trust own insight.

Psychic: Devotion inflates into emotional excess or possessive attachment, fervor mistaken for depth.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Lack of Bhakti results in a cold, intellectual-only spiritual path, disconnection from heart and emotion, difficulty with surrender, and emotional hardness.
facets.somatic: Closed and tight chest area, restricted breathing, physical tension and rigidity, poor circulation, lack of physical warmth and affection, cold demeanor, disconnection from body.
facets.emotional: Emotional coldness and detachment, difficulty with love and compassion, fear of emotional vulnerability, inability to surrender or let go, emotional loneliness despite relationships, emotional hardness and cynicism.
facets.cognitive: Purely intellectual spiritual approach, cold and analytical thinking, inability to access intuitive wisdom through heart, mental rigidity and skepticism, inability to experience transcendence through emotion.
facets.psychic: The soul's heart stays closed, with no thread of devotion to soften or lift it.

deficient.text:
Overview: Lack of Bhakti results in a cold, intellectual-only spiritual path, disconnection from heart and emotion, difficulty with surrender, and emotional hardness.

Somatic: Closed and tight chest area, restricted breathing, physical tension and rigidity, poor circulation, lack of physical warmth and affection, cold demeanor, disconnection from body.

Emotional: Emotional coldness and detachment, difficulty with love and compassion, fear of emotional vulnerability, inability to surrender or let go, emotional loneliness despite relationships, emotional hardness and cynicism.

Cognitive: Purely intellectual spiritual approach, cold and analytical thinking, inability to access intuitive wisdom through heart, mental rigidity and skepticism, inability to experience transcendence through emotion.

Psychic: The soul's heart stays closed, with no thread of devotion to soften or lift it.

attributes.title: Jnana (Knowledge/Wisdom)
attributes.aspect: ज्ञान   ·   Chapter 7, Chapter 10 The nature of this aspect. Jnana is transcendent knowledge and wisdom, particularly knowledge of the Self and ultimate reality. It goes beyond intellectual understanding to direct intuitive insight into the nature of existence.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental (discriminative knowledge) · Ontic mode: Faculty (the capacity for discriminative wisdom) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Mechanism (discerns the real from the unreal) · Key relation: The capacity underlying Jñāna Yoga (the path).
coordinate.causal_status: Mechanism (discerns the real from the unreal)
coordinate.key_relation: The capacity underlying Jñāna Yoga (the path)

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When Jnana is developed appropriately, a person experiences clarity, discernment, wisdom in decision-making, freedom from illusion, understanding of truth, and spiritual insight.
facets.somatic: Clear bright eyes and alert posture, healthy nervous system, good coordination between brain and body, physical vitality from clear understanding, balanced and efficient movement.
facets.emotional: Emotional clarity and freedom, peace from understanding truth, compassion from seeing interconnection, emotional freedom from illusion, emotional wisdom and maturity.
facets.cognitive: Crystal clear thinking, ability to discern truth from falsehood, intuitive insight and wisdom, understanding of root causes, freedom from confusion and delusion, direct perception of reality.
facets.psychic: The soul pursues the knowledge that liberates, discriminating the real from the unreal.

balanced.text:
Overview: When Jnana is developed appropriately, a person experiences clarity, discernment, wisdom in decision-making, freedom from illusion, understanding of truth, and spiritual insight.

Somatic: Clear bright eyes and alert posture, healthy nervous system, good coordination between brain and body, physical vitality from clear understanding, balanced and efficient movement.

Emotional: Emotional clarity and freedom, peace from understanding truth, compassion from seeing interconnection, emotional freedom from illusion, emotional wisdom and maturity.

Cognitive: Crystal clear thinking, ability to discern truth from falsehood, intuitive insight and wisdom, understanding of root causes, freedom from confusion and delusion, direct perception of reality.

Psychic: The soul pursues the knowledge that liberates, discriminating the real from the unreal.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Excessive Jnana can manifest as intellectual pride, spiritual arrogance, detachment from practical life, and inability to feel or connect emotionally.
facets.somatic: Disembodied quality, disconnection from physical sensations, poor physical health from neglecting body, tension in neck and head from over-thinking, physical rigidity.
facets.emotional: Spiritual arrogance and pride, emotional coldness masked as wisdom, inability to connect emotionally with others, dismissiveness of others' feelings, emotional superiority complex.
facets.cognitive: Analysis paralysis and overthinking, intellectual pride and arrogance, dismissal of other valid paths, inability to trust intuition or emotion, mental rigidity despite knowledge, disconnection from practical application.
facets.psychic: The soul over-intellectualizes, mistaking accumulated knowledge for realization.

excess.text:
Overview: Excessive Jnana can manifest as intellectual pride, spiritual arrogance, detachment from practical life, and inability to feel or connect emotionally.

Somatic: Disembodied quality, disconnection from physical sensations, poor physical health from neglecting body, tension in neck and head from over-thinking, physical rigidity.

Emotional: Spiritual arrogance and pride, emotional coldness masked as wisdom, inability to connect emotionally with others, dismissiveness of others' feelings, emotional superiority complex.

Cognitive: Analysis paralysis and overthinking, intellectual pride and arrogance, dismissal of other valid paths, inability to trust intuition or emotion, mental rigidity despite knowledge, disconnection from practical application.

Psychic: The soul over-intellectualizes, mistaking accumulated knowledge for realization.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Lack of Jnana results in ignorance, delusion, poor decision-making, inability to discriminate right from wrong, and spiritual blindness.
facets.somatic: Foggy and confused physical presence, poor coordination, health issues from poor understanding of body needs, sluggish and disorganized physical state.
facets.emotional: Emotional confusion and delusion, difficulty understanding own emotions, emotional gullibility, emotional instability from poor understanding, emotional blindness to others' states.
facets.cognitive: Mental fog and confusion, inability to discern truth from falsehood, living according to false beliefs, poor decision-making, inability to understand deeper meaning, vulnerability to deception.
facets.psychic: The soul never seeks the higher knowledge, content with information that leaves it unchanged.

deficient.text:
Overview: Lack of Jnana results in ignorance, delusion, poor decision-making, inability to discriminate right from wrong, and spiritual blindness.

Somatic: Foggy and confused physical presence, poor coordination, health issues from poor understanding of body needs, sluggish and disorganized physical state.

Emotional: Emotional confusion and delusion, difficulty understanding own emotions, emotional gullibility, emotional instability from poor understanding, emotional blindness to others' states.

Cognitive: Mental fog and confusion, inability to discern truth from falsehood, living according to false beliefs, poor decision-making, inability to understand deeper meaning, vulnerability to deception.

Psychic: The soul never seeks the higher knowledge, content with information that leaves it unchanged.

attributes.title: Yoga (Union/Practice)
attributes.aspect: योग   ·   Multiple chapters The nature of this aspect. Yoga refers to union with the divine and the practice or discipline to achieve this union. It encompasses various paths including Karma Yoga (action), Bhakti Yoga (devotion), Jnana Yoga (knowledge), and Raja Yoga (meditation).

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Psychic (disciplined practice toward union) · Ontic mode: Practice (the discipline that joins the self to its source) · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Means (the disciplined path to union) · Key relation: The discipline underlying Rāja Yoga (the path); genus of the many yogic methods.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Practice (the discipline that joins the self to its source)
coordinate.key_relation: The discipline underlying Rāja Yoga (the path); genus of the many yogic methods

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When Yoga is practiced with proper discipline and understanding, a person experiences spiritual progress, integration of body-mind-spirit, increasing peace, and movement toward enlightenment.
facets.somatic: Strong yet flexible body, good health and energy, integrated nervous system, balanced breath and circulation, physical strength serving spiritual purpose, sense of unity in physical being.
facets.emotional: Emotional stability and peace, increasing compassion and love, emotional resilience, emotional integration and wholeness, joyfulness in practice, emotional freedom and liberation.
facets.cognitive: Mental clarity and focus, increasing wisdom and insight, mental peace and stillness, integration of intellect with intuition, deep meditation and contemplative states, transformation of consciousness.
facets.psychic: The soul takes up disciplined practice toward union, the methods that join it to its source.

balanced.text:
Overview: When Yoga is practiced with proper discipline and understanding, a person experiences spiritual progress, integration of body-mind-spirit, increasing peace, and movement toward enlightenment.

Somatic: Strong yet flexible body, good health and energy, integrated nervous system, balanced breath and circulation, physical strength serving spiritual purpose, sense of unity in physical being.

Emotional: Emotional stability and peace, increasing compassion and love, emotional resilience, emotional integration and wholeness, joyfulness in practice, emotional freedom and liberation.

Cognitive: Mental clarity and focus, increasing wisdom and insight, mental peace and stillness, integration of intellect with intuition, deep meditation and contemplative states, transformation of consciousness.

Psychic: The soul takes up disciplined practice toward union, the methods that join it to its source.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Excessive Yoga practice can lead to obsession, escapism from responsibilities, physical injury, spiritual bypassing, and withdrawal from relationships.
facets.somatic: Physical injury from overtraining, muscular strain and tension, physical weakness from neglecting proper rest, poor nutrition from excessive restriction, physical rigidity from overdoing practices.
facets.emotional: Spiritual addiction and escape from life, emotional withdrawal from relationships, spiritual pride about practices, emotional imbalance from forced states, difficulty with normal human emotions.
facets.cognitive: Obsessive focus on spiritual experience, mental fixation on practices and states, escapism from practical concerns, inability to balance spiritual and worldly life, spiritual bypassing of psychological work.
facets.psychic: The soul over-invests in technique, the practice becoming an end rather than a means to union.

excess.text:
Overview: Excessive Yoga practice can lead to obsession, escapism from responsibilities, physical injury, spiritual bypassing, and withdrawal from relationships.

Somatic: Physical injury from overtraining, muscular strain and tension, physical weakness from neglecting proper rest, poor nutrition from excessive restriction, physical rigidity from overdoing practices.

Emotional: Spiritual addiction and escape from life, emotional withdrawal from relationships, spiritual pride about practices, emotional imbalance from forced states, difficulty with normal human emotions.

Cognitive: Obsessive focus on spiritual experience, mental fixation on practices and states, escapism from practical concerns, inability to balance spiritual and worldly life, spiritual bypassing of psychological work.

Psychic: The soul over-invests in technique, the practice becoming an end rather than a means to union.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Lack of Yoga practice results in spiritual stagnation, lack of discipline, undeveloped spiritual potential, and inability to transform consciousness.
facets.somatic: Poor physical health, weak and undisciplined body, poor posture and breathing, physical and mental heaviness, sedentary and inactive lifestyle.
facets.emotional: Emotional stagnation and lack of growth, emotional heaviness and depression, difficulty experiencing joy or peace, emotional reactivity without transformation, emotional disconnection from purpose.
facets.cognitive: Spiritual stagnation and lack of insight, mental scattered without practice, inability to concentrate, undeveloped consciousness, lack of wisdom and understanding, mental heaviness and dullness.
facets.psychic: The soul practices no discipline, its aspiration toward union left a mere wish.

deficient.text:
Overview: Lack of Yoga practice results in spiritual stagnation, lack of discipline, undeveloped spiritual potential, and inability to transform consciousness.

Somatic: Poor physical health, weak and undisciplined body, poor posture and breathing, physical and mental heaviness, sedentary and inactive lifestyle.

Emotional: Emotional stagnation and lack of growth, emotional heaviness and depression, difficulty experiencing joy or peace, emotional reactivity without transformation, emotional disconnection from purpose.

Cognitive: Spiritual stagnation and lack of insight, mental scattered without practice, inability to concentrate, undeveloped consciousness, lack of wisdom and understanding, mental heaviness and dullness.

Psychic: The soul practices no discipline, its aspiration toward union left a mere wish.

attributes.title: Aparigraha (Non-Possessiveness/Non-Grasping)
attributes.aspect: अपरिग्रह   ·   Chapter 4, Chapter 6, Chapter 18 The nature of this aspect. Aparigraha is the mental freedom from hoarding, accumulating, or clinging to material things. It releases the human experience from the anxiety of loss and the burden of over-consumption.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental (the loosening of grasping) · Ontic mode: Virtue — a restraint (one of the yamas) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual, with a material dimension · Causal status: Mechanism (frees from the grip of hoarding and clinging) · Key relation: A yama (also treated in Dim 11); antagonist to grasping.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Virtue — a restraint (one of the yamas)
coordinate.causal_status: Mechanism (frees from the grip of hoarding and clinging)
coordinate.key_relation: A yama (also treated in Dim 11); antagonist to grasping

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When Aparigraha is present, a person uses what they need without compulsive accumulation. There is generosity, simplicity, and freedom from material anxiety.
facets.somatic: Healthy minimalism in possessions, physical lightness and ease, freedom from physical burden of excess stuff, body unencumbered by material attachment.
facets.emotional: Emotional freedom from possessions, security that isn't dependent on accumulation, joy in simplicity, emotional generosity, freedom from loss anxiety.
facets.cognitive: Mental clarity unburdened by managing excess possessions, freedom from grasping thoughts, mental simplicity and focus, wisdom about true needs vs. wants.
facets.psychic: The soul holds its possessions lightly, free from the grip of hoarding and clinging.

balanced.text:
Overview: When Aparigraha is present, a person uses what they need without compulsive accumulation. There is generosity, simplicity, and freedom from material anxiety.

Somatic: Healthy minimalism in possessions, physical lightness and ease, freedom from physical burden of excess stuff, body unencumbered by material attachment.

Emotional: Emotional freedom from possessions, security that isn't dependent on accumulation, joy in simplicity, emotional generosity, freedom from loss anxiety.

Cognitive: Mental clarity unburdened by managing excess possessions, freedom from grasping thoughts, mental simplicity and focus, wisdom about true needs vs. wants.

Psychic: The soul holds its possessions lightly, free from the grip of hoarding and clinging.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Excessive Aparigraha can become extreme renunciation that rejects appropriate self-care, leading to deprivation, self-neglect, and impractical living.
facets.somatic: Physical deprivation and lack of basic necessities, health problems from extreme renunciation, neglect of body and shelter needs, austerity that harms.
facets.emotional: Emotional rejection of appropriate joy in legitimate possessions, harsh emotional austerity, denial of human needs, emotional pride in renunciation.
facets.cognitive: Rigid mental rejection of all material concerns, inability to function practically, mental escapism through renunciation, dogmatic anti-materialism.
facets.psychic: The soul makes non-possession a performance of austerity, clinging to its very renunciation.

excess.text:
Overview: Excessive Aparigraha can become extreme renunciation that rejects appropriate self-care, leading to deprivation, self-neglect, and impractical living.

Somatic: Physical deprivation and lack of basic necessities, health problems from extreme renunciation, neglect of body and shelter needs, austerity that harms.

Emotional: Emotional rejection of appropriate joy in legitimate possessions, harsh emotional austerity, denial of human needs, emotional pride in renunciation.

Cognitive: Rigid mental rejection of all material concerns, inability to function practically, mental escapism through renunciation, dogmatic anti-materialism.

Psychic: The soul makes non-possession a performance of austerity, clinging to its very renunciation.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Absence of Aparigraha manifests as compulsive hoarding, anxious accumulation, materialism, and chronic anxiety about possession and loss.
facets.somatic: Cluttered and burdensome physical environment, physical health affected by stress of accumulation, body weighed down by possessions, hoarding behaviors.
facets.emotional: Chronic anxiety about losing possessions, emotional attachment to things over people, emotional emptiness covered by accumulation, fear and grasping.
facets.cognitive: Mental preoccupation with accumulating and protecting possessions, obsessive thinking about wealth and things, mental burden of managing excess, anxiety-driven thinking about loss.
facets.psychic: The soul grasps and accumulates, weighed down by the possessions it mistakes for security.

deficient.text:
Overview: Absence of Aparigraha manifests as compulsive hoarding, anxious accumulation, materialism, and chronic anxiety about possession and loss.

Somatic: Cluttered and burdensome physical environment, physical health affected by stress of accumulation, body weighed down by possessions, hoarding behaviors.

Emotional: Chronic anxiety about losing possessions, emotional attachment to things over people, emotional emptiness covered by accumulation, fear and grasping.

Cognitive: Mental preoccupation with accumulating and protecting possessions, obsessive thinking about wealth and things, mental burden of managing excess, anxiety-driven thinking about loss.

Psychic: The soul grasps and accumulates, weighed down by the possessions it mistakes for security.

attributes.title: Udasinata (Objectivity/Neutrality)
attributes.aspect: औदासीन्य   ·   Chapter 6, Chapter 14 The nature of this aspect. Udasinata is the ability to stand apart from personal biases. It represents the mindset of an arbitrator or neutral observer who treats friends, enemies, and strangers with equal clarity and fairness.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental (dispassionate objectivity) · Ontic mode: Virtue — a disposition of impartial neutrality · Valence: Virtue to cultivate (indifference is its distortion) · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Condition (enables clear, unentangled perception) · Key relation: Allied to the witness-stance and to Vairāgya.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Virtue — a disposition of impartial neutrality
coordinate.valence: Virtue to cultivate (indifference is its distortion)
coordinate.key_relation: Allied to the witness-stance and to Vairāgya

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When Udasinata is present, a person can be present to all parties with fair perception. There is the wisdom of the neutral observer that allows for genuine understanding and just action.
facets.somatic: Calm and balanced physical presence with all people, neutral body language that doesn't favor one party, healthy nervous system in conflict situations, grounded physical poise.
facets.emotional: Emotional fairness toward friends and enemies alike, freedom from emotional partiality, emotional clarity in conflicts, ability to feel without being swept by allegiances.
facets.cognitive: Clear and objective perception of situations, ability to see all sides fairly, freedom from biased thinking, wise judgment unaffected by personal preference.
facets.psychic: The soul stands apart from its biases, able to see clearly because it is not personally entangled.

balanced.text:
Overview: When Udasinata is present, a person can be present to all parties with fair perception. There is the wisdom of the neutral observer that allows for genuine understanding and just action.

Somatic: Calm and balanced physical presence with all people, neutral body language that doesn't favor one party, healthy nervous system in conflict situations, grounded physical poise.

Emotional: Emotional fairness toward friends and enemies alike, freedom from emotional partiality, emotional clarity in conflicts, ability to feel without being swept by allegiances.

Cognitive: Clear and objective perception of situations, ability to see all sides fairly, freedom from biased thinking, wise judgment unaffected by personal preference.

Psychic: The soul stands apart from its biases, able to see clearly because it is not personally entangled.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Excessive Udasinata can become emotional coldness, disengagement from genuine relationships, and inability to take a stand when needed.
facets.somatic: Physical coldness and detachment, inability to engage warmly, mechanical physical presence in relationships.
facets.emotional: Emotional coldness and disengagement, inability to genuinely connect, false neutrality that masks emotional avoidance, lack of appropriate partiality with loved ones.
facets.cognitive: Inability to take wise positions when needed, false equivalence between right and wrong, paralysis through forced neutrality, dismissive intellectual distance.
facets.psychic: The soul's neutrality chills into indifference, a detachment that no longer cares.

excess.text:
Overview: Excessive Udasinata can become emotional coldness, disengagement from genuine relationships, and inability to take a stand when needed.

Somatic: Physical coldness and detachment, inability to engage warmly, mechanical physical presence in relationships.

Emotional: Emotional coldness and disengagement, inability to genuinely connect, false neutrality that masks emotional avoidance, lack of appropriate partiality with loved ones.

Cognitive: Inability to take wise positions when needed, false equivalence between right and wrong, paralysis through forced neutrality, dismissive intellectual distance.

Psychic: The soul's neutrality chills into indifference, a detachment that no longer cares.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Absence of Udasinata creates chronic bias, partiality based on personal feelings, inability to perceive fairly, and unjust treatment.
facets.somatic: Differential physical treatment of people based on bias, body language that reveals partiality, physical favoritism.
facets.emotional: Emotional partiality that distorts relationships, inability to be fair with friends or enemies, biased emotional response, allegiance overriding fairness.
facets.cognitive: Biased thinking and perception, inability to see fairly, distorted judgment based on preferences, partial reasoning, mental unfairness.
facets.psychic: The soul is wholly partisan, every perception bent by its preferences and aversions.

deficient.text:
Overview: Absence of Udasinata creates chronic bias, partiality based on personal feelings, inability to perceive fairly, and unjust treatment.

Somatic: Differential physical treatment of people based on bias, body language that reveals partiality, physical favoritism.

Emotional: Emotional partiality that distorts relationships, inability to be fair with friends or enemies, biased emotional response, allegiance overriding fairness.

Cognitive: Biased thinking and perception, inability to see fairly, distorted judgment based on preferences, partial reasoning, mental unfairness.

Psychic: The soul is wholly partisan, every perception bent by its preferences and aversions.

attributes.title: Kshama (Forbearance/Patient Endurance)
attributes.aspect: क्षमा   ·   Chapter 10, Chapter 16 The nature of this aspect. Kshama is not just passive forgiveness, but the psychological strength to remain unaffected by insults, injuries, or provocations without harboring a desire for vengeance. It is active, strong-hearted patience.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital (patient endurance) · Ontic mode: Virtue — a disposition of patient strength · Valence: Virtue to cultivate (doormat passivity is its distortion) · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Condition (bears provocation without reactivity) · Key relation: A divine quality (Dim 24); antagonist to reactive anger.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Virtue — a disposition of patient strength
coordinate.valence: Virtue to cultivate (doormat passivity is its distortion)
coordinate.key_relation: A divine quality (Dim 24); antagonist to reactive anger

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When Kshama is present, a person remains internally undisturbed by provocations and harms. There is strength of patience without weakness, and forgiveness without enabling.
facets.somatic: Physical calm and steadiness despite provocations, relaxed nervous system in challenging situations, healthy body unaffected by stress of others' actions, grounded physical strength.
facets.emotional: Emotional resilience and strength, freedom from need for vengeance, healing emotional capacity, peace in face of insult, emotional dignity preserved despite attacks.
facets.cognitive: Mental peace and clarity despite provocations, freedom from revenge fantasies, wisdom in responding to harm, mental strength to absorb and transform negativity.
facets.psychic: The soul bears provocation with patient strength, unshaken rather than merely passive.

balanced.text:
Overview: When Kshama is present, a person remains internally undisturbed by provocations and harms. There is strength of patience without weakness, and forgiveness without enabling.

Somatic: Physical calm and steadiness despite provocations, relaxed nervous system in challenging situations, healthy body unaffected by stress of others' actions, grounded physical strength.

Emotional: Emotional resilience and strength, freedom from need for vengeance, healing emotional capacity, peace in face of insult, emotional dignity preserved despite attacks.

Cognitive: Mental peace and clarity despite provocations, freedom from revenge fantasies, wisdom in responding to harm, mental strength to absorb and transform negativity.

Psychic: The soul bears provocation with patient strength, unshaken rather than merely passive.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Excessive Kshama can become passive doormat behavior, enabling continued harm, suppressing legitimate anger, and failing to protect oneself or others.
facets.somatic: Physical acceptance of harm without protection, inability to physically defend oneself or others, posture of victimhood, allowing physical mistreatment.
facets.emotional: Suppressed anger that doesn't process, enabling of toxic relationships, emotional masochism, inability to set healthy emotional boundaries.
facets.cognitive: Mental refusal to recognize legitimate grievances, denial of harm done, inability to think strategically about protecting self/others, false saintliness.
facets.psychic: The soul's forbearance becomes doormat passivity, enduring what should be addressed.

Cognitive: Mental refusal to recognize legitimate grievances, denial of harm done, inability to think strategically about protecting self/others, false saintliness.

Psychic: The soul's forbearance becomes doormat passivity, enduring what should be addressed.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Absence of Kshama creates reactive vengefulness, inability to endure any difficulty, chronic grudges, and cycles of retaliation.
facets.somatic: Physical aggression in response to provocations, stress-related illness from chronic anger, tension and reactivity in body, fight responses to minor stimuli.
facets.emotional: Chronic grudges and vengeful feelings, inability to let go of injuries, emotional storms when provoked, perpetual emotional reactivity, bitterness.
facets.cognitive: Obsessive thoughts of revenge, inability to mentally release injuries, plotting and rumination about wrongs, mental fixation on grievances.
facets.psychic: The soul has no patience, reacting to every provocation with immediate heat.

Emotional: Chronic grudges and vengeful feelings, inability to let go of injuries, emotional storms when provoked, perpetual emotional reactivity, bitterness.

Cognitive: Obsessive thoughts of revenge, inability to mentally release injuries, plotting and rumination about wrongs, mental fixation on grievances.

Psychic: The soul has no patience, reacting to every provocation with immediate heat.

attributes.title: Arjavam (Straightforwardness/Alignment)
attributes.aspect: आर्जव   ·   Chapter 16, Chapter 17 The nature of this aspect. Arjavam is absolute integrity and simplicity in human conduct. It is the absence of duplicity, where a person's thoughts, words, and actions are perfectly aligned. This creates psychological wholeness and trustworthiness.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Vital (integrity of word and deed) · Ontic mode: Virtue — a disposition of straightforwardness · Valence: Virtue to cultivate (tactless bluntness is its distortion) · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual, with interpersonal expression · Causal status: Condition (aligns conduct with inner truth) · Key relation: A divine quality (Dim 24); kin to Satya (truthfulness).
coordinate.valence: Virtue to cultivate (tactless bluntness is its distortion)
coordinate.key_relation: A divine quality (Dim 24); kin to Satya (truthfulness)

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When Arjavam is present, a person lives with complete integrity. Thoughts, speech, and action are aligned, creating trustworthiness, simplicity, and inner harmony.
facets.somatic: Natural and unforced physical presence, body language matches inner state, healthy nervous system from lack of internal conflict, authentic physical expression.
facets.emotional: Emotional honesty and authenticity, peace from lack of internal contradictions, genuine feelings expressed appropriately, emotional wholeness and integrity.
facets.cognitive: Mental clarity and simplicity, thoughts that align with values, freedom from mental duplicity, wisdom of authentic living, integrated thinking.
facets.psychic: The soul lives in straightforward integrity, its outer conduct aligned with its inner truth.

balanced.text:

Somatic: Natural and unforced physical presence, body language matches inner state, healthy nervous system from lack of internal conflict, authentic physical expression.

Emotional: Emotional honesty and authenticity, peace from lack of internal contradictions, genuine feelings expressed appropriately, emotional wholeness and integrity.

Cognitive: Mental clarity and simplicity, thoughts that align with values, freedom from mental duplicity, wisdom of authentic living, integrated thinking.

Psychic: The soul lives in straightforward integrity, its outer conduct aligned with its inner truth.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Excessive Arjavam can become brutal bluntness, inability to be diplomatic, harsh honesty that disregards others' feelings, and naive transparency in inappropriate situations.
facets.somatic: Harsh and blunt physical communication, lack of social grace in body language, physical insensitivity to context.
facets.emotional: Emotional bluntness that hurts others, inability to be appropriately tactful, lack of emotional intelligence in expression, harshness disguised as honesty.
facets.cognitive: Rigid thinking about expression, inability to recognize when discretion is needed, mental insistence on full disclosure regardless of impact, naive transparency.
facets.psychic: The soul's candor turns to blunt tactlessness, honesty wielded as a weapon.

excess.text:

Cognitive: Rigid thinking about expression, inability to recognize when discretion is needed, mental insistence on full disclosure regardless of impact, naive transparency.

Psychic: The soul's candor turns to blunt tactlessness, honesty wielded as a weapon.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Absence of Arjavam creates duplicity, hypocrisy, internal fragmentation, and a life where thoughts, words, and actions are misaligned. This creates psychological suffering.
facets.somatic: Physical signs of internal conflict (tension, illness), body language that contradicts spoken words, nervous system stress from constant pretense, exhaustion from inauthenticity.
facets.emotional: Emotional dishonesty and pretense, internal emotional conflict from fragmentation, emotional exhaustion from maintaining facades, lack of genuine emotional connections.
facets.cognitive: Mental fragmentation from duplicity, exhausting cognitive load of maintaining inconsistencies, internal contradictions, distorted thinking from constant pretense.
facets.psychic: The soul lives in duplicity, its words and deeds at odds with its inner state.

attributes.title: Dhrti (Fortitude/Unwavering Will)
attributes.aspect: धृति   ·   Chapter 18 The nature of this aspect. Dhrti is the psychological stamina and grit that enables a person to sustain effort through immense hardship, failure, and emotional fatigue without quitting. It is the will that holds firm against adversity.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental (steady resolve and grit) · Ontic mode: Virtue — the faculty of sustained resolve · Valence: Virtue to cultivate (stubbornness in excess, collapse in deficiency) · Guṇa: Sattva–Rajas · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Condition (sustains effort through difficulty and setback) · Key relation: A divine quality (Dim 24); the durable counterpart to the steadying wave Dhṛti (Dim 9).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Virtue — the faculty of sustained resolve
coordinate.valence: Virtue to cultivate (stubbornness in excess, collapse in deficiency)
coordinate.causal_status: Condition (sustains effort through difficulty and setback)
coordinate.key_relation: A divine quality (Dim 24); the durable counterpart to the steadying wave Dhṛti (Dim 9)

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When Dhrti is present, a person maintains steady effort and resolve through difficulties. There is endurance combined with wisdom about when to persist and when to adapt.
facets.somatic: Physical stamina and resilience, ability to sustain effort over long periods, healthy nervous system that recovers, strong and enduring body, physical perseverance.
facets.emotional: Emotional resilience through difficulties, ability to maintain commitment despite setbacks, emotional strength in face of failure, sustaining motivation, emotional grit.
facets.cognitive: Mental stamina for long-term goals, ability to maintain focus through obstacles, wisdom about persistence, clear vision sustained through difficulties, mental fortitude.
facets.psychic: The soul holds steady fortitude, the grit to sustain effort through difficulty and setback.

balanced.text:
