excess.label: The Excess: Spiritual Bypassing

facets.overview: Can there be an excess of samādhi? The tradition suggests there can, in a nuanced sense. The person who has tasted samādhi but has not fully integrated its implications may use the memory of the experience as an escape from the demands of embodied life. They may claim a realization they have not stabilized. They may dismiss the concerns of the body, of relationships, of ethical conduct as “illusory”—not from genuine realization but from a spiritual bypassing that uses the language of transcendence to avoid the work of living. Patañjali’s own structure guards against this: samādhi is the eighth limb, built upon the foundation of the other seven. A samādhi that is not grounded in ethical conduct, personal discipline, embodied awareness, and sensory mastery is not kaivalya; it is a peak experience that the ego has co-opted. The tradition addresses this directly: “Yet even these [dhāraṇā, dhyāna, and samādhi] are external limbs in relation to seedless samādhi” (III.8). Even the highest experiences, as long as they contain any seed of individuality, any residual sense of “I am experiencing this,” are preliminary. The final samādhi is nirbīja—seedless—and in that state, there is no one left to claim the experience, no one left to misuse it. The excess, then, pertains only to the preliminary stages: the region where genuine glimpses are available but full stabilization has not yet occurred.

excess.text: Can there be an excess of samādhi? The tradition suggests there can, in a nuanced sense. The person who has tasted samādhi but has not fully integrated its implications may use the memory of the experience as an escape from the demands of embodied life. They may claim a realization they have not stabilized. They may dismiss the concerns of the body, of relationships, of ethical conduct as “illusory”—not from genuine realization but from a spiritual bypassing that uses the language of transcendence to avoid the work of living. Patañjali’s own structure guards against this: samādhi is the eighth limb, built upon the foundation of the other seven. A samādhi that is not grounded in ethical conduct, personal discipline, embodied awareness, and sensory mastery is not kaivalya; it is a peak experience that the ego has co-opted. The tradition addresses this directly: “Yet even these [dhāraṇā, dhyāna, and samādhi] are external limbs in relation to seedless samādhi” (III.8). Even the highest experiences, as long as they contain any seed of individuality, any residual sense of “I am experiencing this,” are preliminary. The final samādhi is nirbīja—seedless—and in that state, there is no one left to claim the experience, no one left to misuse it. The excess, then, pertains only to the preliminary stages: the region where genuine glimpses are available but full stabilization has not yet occurred.

deficient.label: The Deficiency: The Unawakened Life

facets.overview: The deficiency of samādhi is, in Patañjali’s framework, the ordinary human condition. It is not a pathology; it is the starting point—the condition of a consciousness that has not yet recognized itself. The person in samādhi-deficiency identifies completely with the contents of the mind: with thoughts, emotions, memories, desires, and the body that houses them. They take the passing show for reality. They take the instrument of perception for the perceiver. And yet, even in this state, the puruṣa is present. Awareness has not departed; it has merely been occluded, the way the sun is occluded by clouds. The person goes about their day, works, loves, suffers, laughs, grieves, and through all of it, awareness is silently witnessing—always present, always available, waiting only for the moment when the mind, exhausted by its own restlessness, finally grows still enough to notice what was there all along. This is, perhaps, the most compassionate insight of the entire Yoga tradition: that the liberation being sought is not something to be achieved but something to be uncovered. The self is not becoming anything it is not already. It is simply remembering what it has always been.

deficient.text: The deficiency of samādhi is, in Patañjali’s framework, the ordinary human condition. It is not a pathology; it is the starting point—the condition of a consciousness that has not yet recognized itself. The person in samādhi-deficiency identifies completely with the contents of the mind: with thoughts, emotions, memories, desires, and the body that houses them. They take the passing show for reality. They take the instrument of perception for the perceiver. And yet, even in this state, the puruṣa is present. Awareness has not departed; it has merely been occluded, the way the sun is occluded by clouds. The person goes about their day, works, loves, suffers, laughs, grieves, and through all of it, awareness is silently witnessing—always present, always available, waiting only for the moment when the mind, exhausted by its own restlessness, finally grows still enough to notice what was there all along. This is, perhaps, the most compassionate insight of the entire Yoga tradition: that the liberation being sought is not something to be achieved but something to be uncovered. The self is not becoming anything it is not already. It is simply remembering what it has always been.

additional_info.heading: Epilogue: The Path Beneath the Path
additional_info.text:
The eight limbs of Patañjali’s yoga are not eight separate practices that happen to be bundled together in a text. They are eight dimensions of a single transformation—a transformation that begins at the outermost layer of human conduct and proceeds, with surgical precision, through the body, the breath, the senses, and the mind to the innermost core of consciousness itself. Each limb prepares the ground for the next, and each is impoverished without the others.

What the threefold analysis of equilibrium, excess, and deficiency reveals is that these are not static categories but dynamic states that fluctuate within a single person across hours, days, and years. The same individual may experience yama-equilibrium in one relationship and yama-deficiency in another. They may practice āsana in excess while neglecting pratyāhāra entirely. The value of the framework is not as a fixed diagnostic but as a mirror—a way of seeing, with precision and without judgment, where the system is flowing freely and where it is blocked.

Patañjali’s path is, at its heart, a path of progressive subtilization. Each limb addresses a finer layer of the human system, and the movement from gross to subtle is the movement from suffering to freedom. The yamas address the grossest dimension: behavior. The niyamas address the next layer: personal character. Āsana and prāṇāyāma address the physical and energetic bodies. Pratyāhāra addresses the sensory interface between inner and outer. Dhāraṇā and dhyāna address the mind itself. And samādhi addresses—or rather, dissolves—the last and subtlest knot: the identification of consciousness with the instrument of consciousness.

The lived experience of this path is not a linear ascent from darkness to light. It is more like the turning of a spiral: the same themes recur at deeper and deeper levels. The person who has established the yamas externally must eventually confront their internal analogues—the subtle violences of self-criticism, the small dishonesties of self-deception, the covert acquisitiveness of spiritual ambition. The person who has mastered āsana physically must eventually discover that the real posture is the posture of the mind: steady and comfortable, even in the midst of difficulty.

In every age, in every culture, human beings have intuited that there is a path beneath the path—a deeper order within the apparent chaos of inner life. Patañjali’s contribution is to have mapped that path with a precision that remains, after more than two millennia, both intellectually rigorous and experientially verifiable. The map does not replace the territory. But for those willing to walk the territory, the map is invaluable.

Page 1

additional_info.heading: Additional Concepts from the Wider Scriptural Corpus
additional_info.text: The following concepts, drawn from the Yoga Sūtras and their commentarial tradition (notably Bryant), substantially extend the treatment of the eightfold path — the individual yamas and niyamas, the eight limbs in detail, the samādhi states (samprajñāta, asamprajñāta, dharma-megha), and the core definitions and practices of Patañjali’s system.

attributes.title: Santosha (Contentment)
attributes.sanskrit: सन्तोष   ·   Implied in philosophy of equanimity
attributes.aspect: सन्तोष   ·   Implied in philosophy of equanimity The nature of this aspect. Santosha is contentment and acceptance of what is. It is not resignation or defeat, but rather satisfaction with present circumstances while still working toward improvement.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Virtue — a niyama (contentment) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Condition (frees from the fever of wanting) · Key relation: A niyama (part of the Niyama limb); antagonist to craving.
coordinate.causal_status: Condition (frees from the fever of wanting)
coordinate.key_relation: A niyama (part of the Niyama limb); antagonist to craving

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When Santosha is present, a person experiences peace despite circumstances, gratitude for what they have, freedom from constant wanting, and motivation to improve from wholeness.
facets.somatic: Physical ease and relaxation despite circumstances, good health from reduced stress, appearance of peace and satisfaction, natural rest and recovery, resilient immune system.
facets.emotional: Emotional peace and tranquility, gratitude and appreciation, freedom from chronic dissatisfaction, emotional resilience and equanimity, joy in simple things.
facets.cognitive: Mental peace and stillness, freedom from constant wanting and craving, clarity and wisdom, ability to enjoy present experience, contentment without complacency.
facets.psychic: The soul rests in contentment, satisfied with what comes and so freed from the fever of wanting.

balanced.text:
Overview: When Santosha is present, a person experiences peace despite circumstances, gratitude for what they have, freedom from constant wanting, and motivation to improve from wholeness.

Somatic: Physical ease and relaxation despite circumstances, good health from reduced stress, appearance of peace and satisfaction, natural rest and recovery, resilient immune system.

Emotional: Emotional peace and tranquility, gratitude and appreciation, freedom from chronic dissatisfaction, emotional resilience and equanimity, joy in simple things.

Cognitive: Mental peace and stillness, freedom from constant wanting and craving, clarity and wisdom, ability to enjoy present experience, contentment without complacency.

Psychic: The soul rests in contentment, satisfied with what comes and so freed from the fever of wanting.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Excessive contentment can lead to stagnation, lack of motivation, passive acceptance of injustice, inability to pursue legitimate goals, and complacency.
facets.somatic: Physical stagnation and lack of vitality, poor health from lack of motivation to improve, sluggish appearance, lack of physical drive, tendency toward lethargy.
facets.emotional: Emotional flatness and lack of passion, acceptance of unacceptable circumstances, inability to advocate for self or others, emotional passivity, lack of care about suffering.
facets.cognitive: Complacency and lack of motivation, lack of ambition, acceptance of injustice, inability to pursue worthy goals, mental stagnation and lack of growth.
facets.psychic: Contentment slides into complacency, the soul excusing stagnation as peace.

Cognitive: Complacency and lack of motivation, lack of ambition, acceptance of injustice, inability to pursue worthy goals, mental stagnation and lack of growth.

Psychic: Contentment slides into complacency, the soul excusing stagnation as peace.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Lack of Santosha results in constant dissatisfaction, chronic desire for more, inability to enjoy what one has, stress, and endless accumulation.
facets.somatic: Stress-related illness from chronic dissatisfaction, poor health from endless striving, nervous system dysregulation, exhaustion from constant pursuit, tension and restlessness.
facets.emotional: Chronic anxiety and dissatisfaction, inability to enjoy present moment, emotional exhaustion from endless wanting, emotional stress and reactivity, relationship strain from demanding.
facets.cognitive: Mental restlessness and dissatisfaction, constantly thinking about what's lacking, inability to be present, mental anxiety and worry, mental exhaustion from constant seeking.
facets.psychic: The soul is chronically discontent, its peace always one acquisition away.

Emotional: Chronic anxiety and dissatisfaction, inability to enjoy present moment, emotional exhaustion from endless wanting, emotional stress and reactivity, relationship strain from demanding.

Cognitive: Mental restlessness and dissatisfaction, constantly thinking about what's lacking, inability to be present, mental anxiety and worry, mental exhaustion from constant seeking.

attributes.title: Citta-Vritti-Nirodha (Cessation of Mental Fluctuations)
attributes.aspect: चित्तवृत्तिनिरोध   ·   Sutra 1.2 The nature of this aspect. The foundational definition of yoga—the stilling, cessation, or restraint of the fluctuations of the mind-stuff (citta). This single sutra summarizes the entire system: when mental waves cease, the seer abides in its own true nature (Sutra 1.3). Insight. Vyāsa: The mind has five states—wandering, dull, distracted, one-pointed, and stilled; yoga occurs only in the last two. Vijñānabhikṣu: Since vrittis are products of prakriti completely distinct from the soul (purusha), they must all be restrained for the Self to be realized as autonomous.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: State-and-aim (the stilling of the mind — yoga’s very definition) · Valence: The goal (to be realized) · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond the guṇas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Fruit / aim (the goal of the whole path) · Key relation: The aim to which all eight limbs are the means (Yoga Sūtra 1.2).
coordinate.ontic_mode: State-and-aim (the stilling of the mind — yoga’s very definition)
coordinate.causal_status: Fruit / aim (the goal of the whole path)
coordinate.key_relation: The aim to which all eight limbs are the means (Yoga Sūtra 1.2)

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When citta-vritti-nirodha is established, the mind becomes still and the true Self (Purusha) is realized as separate from the mind. This is the goal of yoga.
facets.somatic: Body becomes a calm vehicle, nervous system in deep rest, no physical agitation from mental waves, vitality from stilled energy patterns.
facets.emotional: Profound emotional stillness, freedom from emotional turbulence, peace independent of circumstances, witness consciousness of all feelings.
facets.cognitive: Complete cessation of mental modifications, witness consciousness established, direct knowing of Self, mind becomes transparent.
facets.psychic: The soul stills the fluctuations of mind, resting as the awareness that remains when the waves subside.

balanced.text:

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Forced or premature nirodha without proper foundation creates psychological imbalance, dissociation, or catatonic states mistaken for samadhi.
facets.somatic: Forced suppression of vital functions, body shut down rather than refined, ungrounded physical state.
facets.emotional: Emotional suppression masquerading as transcendence, dissociation from feelings, false detachment.
facets.cognitive: Forced thought suppression that creates rebound, dissociative states, false claims of samadhi, mental dullness mistaken for stillness.
facets.psychic: The soul wars against its thoughts directly, the forcing stirring more waves than it stills.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Without any movement toward nirodha, the mind is dominated by endless vrittis, creating suffering and bondage to samsara.
facets.somatic: Body driven by constant mental activity, stress-related illness, nervous system in perpetual agitation.
facets.emotional: Emotional reactivity dominates life, no peace possible, slavery to feelings as they arise.
facets.cognitive: Constant mental chatter, identification with thoughts, no witness consciousness, life lived in vrittis.
facets.psychic: The soul's mind churns ceaselessly, never approaching the stillness that is yoga's aim.

attributes.title: Pranava (Om - The Sacred Syllable)
attributes.aspect: प्रणव   ·   Sutra 1.27, 1.28 The nature of this aspect. Pranava (Om) is the sacred sound-symbol of Ishvara. Patanjali prescribes its repetition (japa) combined with contemplation of its meaning (artha-bhavana) as a primary practice. The sound holds the essence of all sacred sound and unites the practitioner with the Divine. Insight. Vyāsa: As one repeats Om and contemplates its meaning, the mind becomes one-pointed and the inner Self becomes manifest. Vijñānabhikṣu: Repetition without meditation on meaning is mere ritual; both together create the transformative practice.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Psychic · Ontic mode: Practice (repetition of the sacred syllable) · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual, toward the Divine · Causal status: Means (a support for concentration and surrender) · Key relation: Correspondence — the emblem of Īśvara; aid to Īśvara-praṇidhāna.
coordinate.causal_status: Means (a support for concentration and surrender)
coordinate.key_relation: Correspondence — the emblem of Īśvara; aid to Īśvara-praṇidhāna

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When Pranava is practiced with both sound and meaning, it becomes a powerful unifying practice that brings one-pointedness and Self-realization.
facets.somatic: Body resonates with sacred sound, healthy physiological effects of mantra, nervous system attuned, vibrations purifying.
facets.emotional: Emotional unification through sacred sound, devotional warmth from Om, emotional integration.
facets.cognitive: Mental one-pointedness from sound and meaning meditation, contact with deeper reality, mental purification.
facets.psychic: The soul repeats OM as the emblem of the Divine, the sacred sound gathering and lifting the mind.

balanced.text:

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Mechanical chanting without meaning becomes empty ritual; obsessive practice can create imbalances if not properly guided.
facets.somatic: Mechanical chanting without engagement, physical exhaustion from forced practice.
facets.emotional: Empty emotional response to mantra, ritualism without devotion.
facets.cognitive: Mechanical mind during chanting, no meaning contemplation, missing the transformative essence.
facets.psychic: The soul repeats the syllable mechanically, the sound emptied of its meaning.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Without any sacred sound practice, the mind lacks one of yoga's most powerful unifying tools. Practice is harder without mantra support.
facets.somatic: Body without mantra-vibration support, missing somatic benefits of sacred sound.
facets.emotional: Emotional life without devotional sound practice, missing this powerful tool.
facets.cognitive: Mind without japa-anchor, missing efficient concentration technique, harder to fix mind.
facets.psychic: The soul has no sacred sound to gather it, the mind left to its unbroken churn.

attributes.title: Asana (Posture/Steady Seat)
attributes.aspect: आसन   ·   Sutra 2.46, 2.47, 2.48 The nature of this aspect. Asana is the third limb—the steady, comfortable seat for meditation. In Patanjali, asana is not the physical postures of modern yoga but specifically the meditation seat. Mastery means: (1) steadiness, (2) ease, (3) effortlessness, and (4) absorption in the infinite, leading to freedom from dvandvas (pairs of opposites). Insight. Vyāsa: Lists various traditional asanas (padmasana, virasana, etc.) but emphasizes the criteria: sthira (steady) and sukha (comfortable). Vācaspati Miśra: True asana is when the body becomes effortless and the mind merges into the infinite. Hariharānanda: Asana mastery means the body no longer disturbs meditation.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical · Ontic mode: Practice (a steady, comfortable posture) · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva–Tamas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Condition (a steady seat for the mind to settle) · Key relation: The concept underlying Āsana (the 3rd limb).
coordinate.causal_status: Condition (a steady seat for the mind to settle)
coordinate.key_relation: The concept underlying Āsana (the 3rd limb)

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When asana is mastered, the body is no longer a distraction—it sits effortlessly steady and comfortable, allowing deep meditation. The yogi becomes immune to dualities like heat/cold, hunger/thirst.
facets.somatic: Effortless steady seat for long periods, body that doesn't fatigue, freedom from physical reactivity to extremes, transcendence of dvandvas.
facets.emotional: Emotional steadiness mirrored in physical steadiness, freedom from emotional reactivity to physical conditions.
facets.cognitive: Mind freed from body's complaints, available for deeper practice, transcendent of dualistic experience.
facets.psychic: The soul finds a steady, comfortable seat, the body quieted so the mind can settle.

balanced.text:

Cognitive: Mind freed from body's complaints, available for deeper practice, transcendent of dualistic experience.

Psychic: The soul finds a steady, comfortable seat, the body quieted so the mind can settle.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Excessive focus on physical posture without meditation becomes mere gymnastics or postural perfectionism; effort overrides ease.
facets.somatic: Forced posture causing injury, over-effort, rigid mastery without true ease, physical perfectionism.
facets.emotional: Pride in postural achievement, attachment to physical form, missing the meditative purpose.
facets.cognitive: Mind focused on body instead of meditation, attachment to specific postures, mistaking physical for spiritual.
facets.psychic: The soul over-emphasizes physical posture, mistaking athletic accomplishment for the inner steadiness it serves.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Inability to sit steadily and comfortably means the body constantly distracts from meditation; physical reactivity to dvandvas dominates.
facets.somatic: Body that cannot sit still, fidgeting, constant discomfort, physical reactivity to slight discomfort.
facets.emotional: Emotional reactivity to physical discomfort, complaints about conditions, suffering from dvandvas.
facets.cognitive: Mind constantly pulled by body's complaints, unable to focus, distracted by physical sensations.
facets.psychic: The soul has no steady seat, the restless body keeping the mind unsettled.

attributes.title: Pranayama (Breath Control/Life-Force Regulation)
attributes.aspect: प्राणायाम   ·   Sutra 2.49, 2.50, 2.51, 2.52, 2.53 The nature of this aspect. Pranayama is the fourth limb—regulation of the breath (prana = life-force, ayama = extension/control). It has three modes: exhalation (rechaka), inhalation (puraka), and retention (kumbhaka). Its purpose is to remove the veil over the inner light (sattva) and prepare the mind for concentration. Insight. Vyāsa: Pranayama dwindles the cover of impurity on the mind's sattva, revealing the inner light. Vijñānabhikṣu: Breath and mind are intimately connected—stilling breath stills mind. Hariharānanda: Pranayama prepares the mind for dharana by removing the predominant rajas and tamas in breath.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Vital · Ontic mode: Practice (breath-regulation) · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Mechanism (steadies the mind via the breath) · Key relation: The concept underlying Prāṇāyāma (the 4th limb); genus of the breath disciplines (Dim 6).
coordinate.causal_status: Mechanism (steadies the mind via the breath)
coordinate.key_relation: The concept underlying Prāṇāyāma (the 4th limb); genus of the breath disciplines (Dim 6)

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When pranayama is properly practiced, the breath becomes long, subtle, and refined. The veil over the inner light dissipates and the mind becomes fit for dharana.
facets.somatic: Slow deep breathing, healthy respiratory system, balanced autonomic nervous system, vital energy refined and abundant.
facets.emotional: Emotional calming from breath regulation, balanced nervous system supporting emotional balance, peace from breath.
facets.cognitive: Mind becoming fit for concentration, inner light revealed, mental clarity from sattva uncovered, prepared for dharana.
facets.psychic: The soul regulates the breath to steady the mind, the life-force gathered and refined.

Emotional: Emotional calming from breath regulation, balanced nervous system supporting emotional balance, peace from breath.

Cognitive: Mind becoming fit for concentration, inner light revealed, mental clarity from sattva uncovered, prepared for dharana.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Excessive forceful pranayama without preparation creates imbalances—prana disorders, anxiety, nervous system damage, mental disturbance.
facets.somatic: Forced breath causing physical harm, hyperventilation, prana disorders, nervous system imbalance, dizziness or fainting.
facets.emotional: Emotional disturbance from forced practice, anxiety from improper pranayama, fear states.
facets.cognitive: Mental imbalance from forced practice, headaches, mental disturbance, kundalini disorders if improperly done.
facets.psychic: The soul over-manipulates the breath, forcing techniques that agitate rather than calm.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.somatic: Shallow restricted breathing, poor oxygenation, depleted prana, weak vitality, poor health.
facets.emotional: Emotional reactivity from disregulated breath, anxiety, no calming through breath.
facets.cognitive: Mind covered by impurities, dullness, no clarity, unable to access deeper practice.
facets.psychic: The soul leaves the breath unregulated, the uneven breath keeping the mind stirred.

Psychic: The soul leaves the breath unregulated, the uneven breath keeping the mind stirred.

attributes.title: Pratyahara (Sense Withdrawal)
attributes.aspect: प्रत्याहार   ·   Sutra 2.54, 2.55 The nature of this aspect. Pratyahara is the fifth limb—the withdrawal of the senses from their objects. When the mind is withdrawn from sense objects, the senses naturally follow and disconnect from external stimuli. This is the bridge between the external limbs (yamas, niyamas, asana, pranayama) and the internal limbs (dharana, dhyana, samadhi). Insight. Vyāsa: Like bees following the queen bee, the senses follow the mind when the mind is withdrawn. Vijñānabhikṣu: This is the natural result of the previous limbs—senses become subjugated automatically. Hariharānanda: Pratyahara is the bridge between external and internal yoga.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Practice (sense-withdrawal) · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Condition (gathers the attention inward) · Key relation: The concept underlying Pratyāhāra (the 5th limb).
coordinate.key_relation: The concept underlying Pratyāhāra (the 5th limb)

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When pratyahara is established, the senses follow the mind inward. External stimuli no longer disturb. The yogi can be in the world but the mind is gathered within.
facets.somatic: Senses able to disengage from objects at will, body present but not driven by stimuli, healthy sense functioning when needed.
facets.emotional: Emotional freedom from sensory triggering, ability to choose engagement, peace from inner gathering.
facets.cognitive: Mind gathered within, senses subjugated, ready for inner practice, freedom from external pull.
facets.psychic: The soul withdraws the senses from their objects, gathering attention for the inner work.

balanced.text:
Overview: When pratyahara is established, the senses follow the mind inward. External stimuli no longer disturb. The yogi can be in the world but the mind is gathered within.

Somatic: Senses able to disengage from objects at will, body present but not driven by stimuli, healthy sense functioning when needed.

Emotional: Emotional freedom from sensory triggering, ability to choose engagement, peace from inner gathering.

Cognitive: Mind gathered within, senses subjugated, ready for inner practice, freedom from external pull.

Psychic: The soul withdraws the senses from their objects, gathering attention for the inner work.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Forced sensory withdrawal becomes harsh asceticism, denial of senses, harm to sense organs through forced suppression rather than natural withdrawal.
facets.somatic: Atrophied sensory function from forced denial, harm to sense organs, sensory deprivation.
facets.emotional: Emotional rejection of sensory life, harsh asceticism, denial of natural human experience.
facets.cognitive: Mental forcing of senses, suppression rather than transcendence, eventual breakdown of forced control.
facets.psychic: Withdrawal hardens into rigid sensory shutdown, a brittle isolation.

excess.text:
Overview: Forced sensory withdrawal becomes harsh asceticism, denial of senses, harm to sense organs through forced suppression rather than natural withdrawal.

Somatic: Atrophied sensory function from forced denial, harm to sense organs, sensory deprivation.

Emotional: Emotional rejection of sensory life, harsh asceticism, denial of natural human experience.

Cognitive: Mental forcing of senses, suppression rather than transcendence, eventual breakdown of forced control.

Psychic: Withdrawal hardens into rigid sensory shutdown, a brittle isolation.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Without any pratyahara, the senses dominate—mind constantly pulled by sensory input, no inner gathering possible, sensory addictions.
facets.somatic: Senses ruling the body, constant sensory stimulation needed, addictions, depleted from sensory pursuit.
facets.emotional: Emotional life dominated by sensory experience, no inner emotional life, sensory cravings.
facets.cognitive: Mind dragged by senses, inability to focus inward, no foundation for inner limbs of yoga.
facets.psychic: The soul cannot withdraw, dragged outward by every sense-object.

deficient.text:

Somatic: Senses ruling the body, constant sensory stimulation needed, addictions, depleted from sensory pursuit.

Emotional: Emotional life dominated by sensory experience, no inner emotional life, sensory cravings.

Cognitive: Mind dragged by senses, inability to focus inward, no foundation for inner limbs of yoga.

Psychic: The soul cannot withdraw, dragged outward by every sense-object.

attributes.title: Dharana (Concentration/Fixing the Mind)
attributes.aspect: धारणा   ·   Sutra 3.1 The nature of this aspect. Dharana is the sixth limb—concentration, the fixing of the mind on one place or object. It is the first of the three internal limbs. The mind is bound to one location (desha-bandha), though the attention may be intermittent. Compared to a series of distinct water droplets. Insight. Vyāsa: Lists possible objects—navel, heart, brain, tip of nose. Vācaspati Miśra: Recommends concentration on the personal form of God (saguna meditation). Hariharānanda: Like water dripping in distinct droplets—intermittent but on one object.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental · Ontic mode: Practice (fixing attention on one point) · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Mechanism (one-pointed fixing) · Key relation: The concept underlying Dhāraṇā (the 6th limb); first of the three inner limbs.
coordinate.key_relation: The concept underlying Dhāraṇā (the 6th limb); first of the three inner limbs

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When dharana is established, the mind can fix on one object for sustained periods, though attention may still flicker. This is the foundation for dhyana.
facets.somatic: Body steady to support concentration, healthy nervous system, focused physical engagement.
facets.emotional: Emotional focus and dedication to practice, ability to commit, focused devotion.
facets.cognitive: Mind capable of fixing on one object, sustained attention, beginning of one-pointedness, intermittent but returning focus.
facets.psychic: The soul fixes attention on a single point, the scattered mind gathered into steadiness.

balanced.text:
Overview: When dharana is established, the mind can fix on one object for sustained periods, though attention may still flicker. This is the foundation for dhyana.

Somatic: Body steady to support concentration, healthy nervous system, focused physical engagement.

Emotional: Emotional focus and dedication to practice, ability to commit, focused devotion.

Cognitive: Mind capable of fixing on one object, sustained attention, beginning of one-pointedness, intermittent but returning focus.

Psychic: The soul fixes attention on a single point, the scattered mind gathered into steadiness.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Forced concentration without proper foundation creates mental strain, frustration, headaches, and inability to relax.
facets.somatic: Tension and strain from forced concentration, headaches, eye strain, physical stress.
facets.emotional: Emotional frustration with concentration difficulties, anxiety about practice, self-criticism.
facets.cognitive: Mental strain, forced effort that prevents natural focus, frustration that makes concentration harder.
facets.psychic: The soul cramps around its focus, the forced concentration straining and snapping.

excess.text:

Somatic: Tension and strain from forced concentration, headaches, eye strain, physical stress.

Emotional: Emotional frustration with concentration difficulties, anxiety about practice, self-criticism.

Cognitive: Mental strain, forced effort that prevents natural focus, frustration that makes concentration harder.

Psychic: The soul cramps around its focus, the forced concentration straining and snapping.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Without dharana, the mind cannot stay on one object even briefly—scattered, distracted, unable to develop further yogic capacities.
facets.somatic: Restless body unable to support concentration, scattered physical energy.
facets.emotional: Emotional scatteredness, lack of focused engagement, surface-level emotional life.
facets.cognitive: Completely scattered mind, monkey-mind, inability to fix attention even briefly, foundation lacking for higher practices.
facets.psychic: The soul's attention will not hold, scattering the moment it is placed.

attributes.title: Dhyana (Meditation as 7th Limb)
attributes.aspect: ध्यान   ·   Sutra 3.2 The nature of this aspect. Dhyana is the seventh limb—meditation as the continuous uninterrupted flow of attention to one object. Unlike dharana (intermittent droplets), dhyana is a continuous stream. There is still subject-object distinction but absorption is complete. Insight. Vyāsa: Continuous flow of the same thought without distraction by any other. Hariharānanda: Like oil or honey poured in one thick uninterrupted stream, compared to dharana's water droplets. Vijñānabhikṣu: The arrow-maker engrossed in his arrow not noticing the king passing—total absorption.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Stage / practice (sustained meditative flow) · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Condition (unbroken attention) · Key relation: The 7th limb; between Dhāraṇā and Samādhi.
coordinate.key_relation: The 7th limb; between Dhāraṇā and Samādhi

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When dhyana is achieved, attention flows continuously and effortlessly on the object. The mind is fully absorbed without losing the object.
facets.somatic: Body completely settled, physical absorption in meditation, prolonged sitting effortless.
facets.emotional: Profound emotional immersion in object of meditation, devotional absorption, emotional peace.
facets.cognitive: Continuous unbroken flow of attention, deep absorption, mind completely engaged with object, oil-like flow.
facets.psychic: The soul sustains an unbroken flow of attention toward its object, concentration ripening into meditation.

balanced.text:
Overview: When dhyana is achieved, attention flows continuously and effortlessly on the object. The mind is fully absorbed without losing the object.

Somatic: Body completely settled, physical absorption in meditation, prolonged sitting effortless.

Emotional: Profound emotional immersion in object of meditation, devotional absorption, emotional peace.

Cognitive: Continuous unbroken flow of attention, deep absorption, mind completely engaged with object, oil-like flow.

Psychic: The soul sustains an unbroken flow of attention toward its object, concentration ripening into meditation.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Excessive attachment to meditation states becomes spiritual materialism, addiction to experiences, attachment to the meditator-meditation duality.
facets.somatic: Physical withdrawal for excessive meditation, neglect of life responsibilities, prolonged sitting damaging body.
facets.emotional: Spiritual materialism, attachment to peaceful states, escape from life into meditation.
facets.cognitive: Mental attachment to dhyana experiences, addiction to meditation states, ego subtly identifying with achievement.
facets.psychic: The soul strains for absorption, the grasping disturbing the very flow it seeks.

excess.text:
Overview: Excessive attachment to meditation states becomes spiritual materialism, addiction to experiences, attachment to the meditator-meditation duality.

Somatic: Physical withdrawal for excessive meditation, neglect of life responsibilities, prolonged sitting damaging body.

Emotional: Spiritual materialism, attachment to peaceful states, escape from life into meditation.

Cognitive: Mental attachment to dhyana experiences, addiction to meditation states, ego subtly identifying with achievement.

Psychic: The soul strains for absorption, the grasping disturbing the very flow it seeks.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Without dhyana, dharana cannot deepen into samadhi. Practice remains shallow, no deep absorption possible.
facets.somatic: Body never reaches deep meditative absorption, no settled state.
facets.emotional: Emotional life without deep meditative healing, surface practice only.
facets.cognitive: Mind never sustains uninterrupted attention, no flow state, practice plateaus.
facets.psychic: The soul's attention is never sustained, meditation collapsing back into distraction.

attributes.title: Samadhi (Meditative Absorption)
attributes.aspect: समाधि   ·   Sutra 3.3, 1.41-51 The nature of this aspect. Samadhi is the eighth limb—meditative absorption where the mind so identifies with the object that subject-object distinction collapses. Only the object shines forth in pure awareness. There are many types: samprajnata (with seed/cognition), asamprajnata (without seed), and the cloud of virtue (dharma-megha) being the highest. Insight. Vyāsa: In samadhi, only the object remains in awareness, the meditator dissolves. Vijñānabhikṣu: This is the culmination where subject-object distinction collapses. Hariharānanda: Samadhi is when meditation becomes meditation on its own essence.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: State (absorption) · Valence: Fruit / attained state · Guṇa: Sattva, beyond the guṇas · Scale: Individual, transcendent · Causal status: Fruit (the culmination) · Key relation: The 8th limb; the fruit of Dhyāna.
coordinate.key_relation: The 8th limb; the fruit of Dhyāna

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When samadhi is achieved, consciousness merges with the object of meditation. Subject-object duality dissolves; only essence remains. This is the goal of the eight-limbed path.
facets.somatic: Body in deep state of stillness, vital functions reduced to minimum, somatic peace beyond normal states.
facets.emotional: Beyond emotional states, profound bliss in some samadhis, peace beyond emotion.
facets.cognitive: Subject-object collapse, pure awareness of essence, beyond ordinary cognition, transcendent state.
facets.psychic: The soul absorbs so fully into its object that the sense of a separate observer dissolves.

balanced.text:
Overview: When samadhi is achieved, consciousness merges with the object of meditation. Subject-object duality dissolves; only essence remains. This is the goal of the eight-limbed path.

Somatic: Body in deep state of stillness, vital functions reduced to minimum, somatic peace beyond normal states.

Emotional: Beyond emotional states, profound bliss in some samadhis, peace beyond emotion.

Cognitive: Subject-object collapse, pure awareness of essence, beyond ordinary cognition, transcendent state.

Psychic: The soul absorbs so fully into its object that the sense of a separate observer dissolves.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Attachment to samadhi states becomes the most subtle bondage—seeking experiences, clinging to bliss, mistaking states for liberation.
facets.somatic: Physical withdrawal for samadhi-seeking, neglect of life, body damaged by extreme practices.
facets.emotional: Attachment to bliss states, spiritual addiction, samadhi-seeking as compensation.
facets.cognitive: Subtle ego claiming samadhi, attachment to experiences, mistaking states for final liberation.
facets.psychic: The soul chases absorption as a peak experience to collect, the seeking obstructing it.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Without ever reaching samadhi, the yogic path remains incomplete. The deepest insights and liberation depend on samadhi.
facets.somatic: Body never knows deep samadhi-state benefits.
facets.emotional: Emotional life never knows samadhi-peace.
facets.cognitive: Mind never knows transcendence, remains in subject-object duality.
facets.psychic: The soul never approaches absorption, the divide between knower and known never thinning.

attributes.title: Samyama (Combined Practice of the Three Inner Limbs)
attributes.aspect: संयम   ·   Sutra 3.4, 3.5, 3.6 The nature of this aspect. Samyama is the combined application of dharana, dhyana, and samadhi on a single object. It is the technical practice through which the yogi gains direct knowledge (prajna) of whatever object is the focus. Most of Book III's siddhis (powers) arise from samyama applied to specific objects. Insight. Vyāsa: Samyama is the technical practice that yields all the siddhis described in Book III. Vijñānabhikṣu: Through samyama on different objects, different powers and insights arise. Hariharānanda: Samyama is the unified application of the three inner limbs as one continuous practice.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Practice (the combined application of the three inner limbs) · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Mechanism (penetrates an object’s essence) · Key relation: Whole — Dhāraṇā, Dhyāna, and Samādhi applied together; yields the siddhis.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Practice (the combined application of the three inner limbs)
coordinate.key_relation: Whole — Dhāraṇā, Dhyāna, and Samādhi applied together; yields the siddhis

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When samyama is mastered, the yogi can apply it to various objects to gain insight, knowledge, and yogic powers. It is the master-key of advanced practice.
facets.somatic: Body able to sustain prolonged samyama, vital functions supporting deep practice.
facets.emotional: Emotional stability enabling sustained samyama, dedication to deep practice.
facets.cognitive: Ability to apply dharana-dhyana-samadhi as one continuous practice, gain direct knowledge of objects, develop prajna.
facets.psychic: The soul applies concentration, meditation, and absorption together on one object, penetrating to its essence.

balanced.text:

Somatic: Body able to sustain prolonged samyama, vital functions supporting deep practice.

Cognitive: Ability to apply dharana-dhyana-samadhi as one continuous practice, gain direct knowledge of objects, develop prajna.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Excessive samyama for siddhis becomes obstacle—Patanjali warns these powers can themselves bind the yogi.
facets.somatic: Body damaged by samyama-seeking for powers, neglect of basic needs.
facets.emotional: Power-seeking emotions, attachment to siddhis, ego inflation.
facets.cognitive: Pursuit of siddhis as goal, distraction from liberation, mental obsession with powers.
facets.psychic: The soul fixates on the powers samyama yields, chasing siddhis rather than insight.

excess.text:

Psychic: The soul fixates on the powers samyama yields, chasing siddhis rather than insight.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Without samyama, advanced yogic insights and powers don't develop. The yogic path remains incomplete in its potential.
facets.somatic: Body never develops yogic capacities through samyama.
facets.emotional: Emotional life lacks the transformations samyama brings.
facets.cognitive: Mind never gains direct prajna-knowledge of objects, no advanced insights.
facets.psychic: The soul never combines the inner limbs, its practice too scattered to penetrate anything.

attributes.title: Samprajnata Samadhi (Cognitive/Seeded Absorption)
attributes.aspect: सम्प्रज्ञात समाधि   ·   Sutra 1.17 The nature of this aspect. Samprajnata samadhi is samadhi 'with seed'—absorption that still includes some content (object, idea, bliss, or ego-sense). It has four progressive stages: savitarka (with reasoning), nirvitarka (without reasoning), savichara (with subtle insight), nirvichara (without subtle insight). Insight. Vyāsa: Has four stages—savitarka, nirvitarka, savichara, nirvichara—progressing from gross to subtle objects. Vijñānabhikṣu: Each stage corresponds to subtler aspects of prakriti. Hariharānanda: The first stage of true samadhi but still 'with seed'—latent samskaras remain.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: State (seeded absorption — with an object) · Valence: Attained state (a stage toward the seedless) · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual, transcendent · Causal status: Fruit (a graded absorption) · Key relation: Part — the seeded form of Samādhi; precedes Asamprajñāta.
coordinate.valence: Attained state (a stage toward the seedless)
coordinate.key_relation: Part — the seeded form of Samādhi; precedes Asamprajñāta

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When samprajnata is properly attained, the yogi has direct cognition of objects in their essence. Wisdom from samadhi shines forth.
facets.somatic: Body in deep absorption supporting samadhi, vital functions minimized.
facets.emotional: Profound bliss (ananda samadhi), emotional unity with object.
facets.cognitive: Direct cognition of object's essence, wisdom (prajna) arising, transformed knowing.
facets.psychic: The soul rests in seeded absorption, deeply concentrated while still supported by a subtle object.

balanced.text:
Overview: When samprajnata is properly attained, the yogi has direct cognition of objects in their essence. Wisdom from samadhi shines forth.

Somatic: Body in deep absorption supporting samadhi, vital functions minimized.

Emotional: Profound bliss (ananda samadhi), emotional unity with object.

Cognitive: Direct cognition of object's essence, wisdom (prajna) arising, transformed knowing.

Psychic: The soul rests in seeded absorption, deeply concentrated while still supported by a subtle object.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Attachment to samprajnata states with their bliss and insights becomes obstacle to higher asamprajnata samadhi.
facets.somatic: Attachment to physical bliss in samadhi, seeking experiences.
facets.emotional: Attachment to ananda samadhi bliss, spiritual hedonism.
facets.cognitive: Mental attachment to insights and powers gained, identification with samadhi experiences.
facets.psychic: The soul clings to the refined object of absorption, mistaking the seeded state for final freedom.

excess.text:

Somatic: Attachment to physical bliss in samadhi, seeking experiences.

Emotional: Attachment to ananda samadhi bliss, spiritual hedonism.

Cognitive: Mental attachment to insights and powers gained, identification with samadhi experiences.

Psychic: The soul clings to the refined object of absorption, mistaking the seeded state for final freedom.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Without reaching samprajnata, the deeper realizations from yogic practice don't arise. Subtle insights of prakriti remain hidden.
facets.somatic: Body never knows deep absorption.
facets.emotional: Never experiences ananda-samadhi bliss.
facets.cognitive: Mind never gains direct knowledge through samadhi.
facets.psychic: The soul never reaches even seeded absorption, its mind too restless to settle on anything.

Emotional: Never experiences ananda-samadhi bliss.

attributes.title: Asamprajnata Samadhi (Supraconscious Absorption)
attributes.aspect: असम्प्रज्ञात समाधि   ·   Sutra 1.18 The nature of this aspect. Asamprajnata samadhi is samadhi 'without seed' or 'beyond cognition'—the higher samadhi where all mental content has ceased and only latent impressions (samskaras) remain. There is no object, only pure absorption. Insight. Vyāsa: When all vrittis are restrained, only samskaras remain in the citta; this is beyond cognition. Vijñānabhikṣu: This is the seedless samadhi where even subtle objects dissolve. Hariharānanda: Higher than samprajnata, but kaivalya is yet higher.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: State (seedless absorption — objectless) · Valence: Fruit (the higher absorption) · Guṇa: Beyond the guṇas · Scale: Transcendent · Causal status: Fruit (objectless absorption) · Key relation: Part — the seedless form of Samādhi; follows Samprajñāta, opening toward Kaivalya.
coordinate.key_relation: Part — the seedless form of Samādhi; follows Samprajñāta, opening toward Kaivalya

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When asamprajnata samadhi is attained, all mental fluctuations are restrained. The yogi rests in pure consciousness beyond all cognition. Liberation is near.
facets.somatic: Body in deepest state of cessation, vital functions at minimum.
facets.emotional: Beyond emotional states entirely, pure stillness.
facets.cognitive: All vrittis stilled, no object of cognition, pure samadhi without seed.
facets.psychic: The soul rests in seedless absorption, all objects dissolved, awareness abiding in its own nature.

balanced.text:
Overview: When asamprajnata samadhi is attained, all mental fluctuations are restrained. The yogi rests in pure consciousness beyond all cognition. Liberation is near.

Somatic: Body in deepest state of cessation, vital functions at minimum.

Emotional: Beyond emotional states entirely, pure stillness.

Cognitive: All vrittis stilled, no object of cognition, pure samadhi without seed.

Psychic: The soul rests in seedless absorption, all objects dissolved, awareness abiding in its own nature.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Premature claims of asamprajnata samadhi or attachment to formless states create subtle bondage.
facets.somatic: Catatonia mistaken for asamprajnata, harmful states.
facets.emotional: Emotional escape disguised as samadhi.
facets.cognitive: False claims of seedless samadhi, dissociation mistaken for absorption.
facets.psychic: The soul grasps at the seedless state, the very reaching keeping a seed alive.

excess.text:

Somatic: Catatonia mistaken for asamprajnata, harmful states.

Emotional: Emotional escape disguised as samadhi.

Cognitive: False claims of seedless samadhi, dissociation mistaken for absorption.

Psychic: The soul grasps at the seedless state, the very reaching keeping a seed alive.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Without ever reaching asamprajnata, the deepest yogic state remains unrealized; samskaras continue to fructify.
facets.somatic: Body never knows this deepest state.
facets.emotional: Never reaches beyond emotional dimensions.
facets.cognitive: Mind never knows cessation, samskaras remain active.
facets.psychic: The soul knows only object-bound consciousness, the seedless ground never approached.

deficient.text:

Emotional: Never reaches beyond emotional dimensions.

Cognitive: Mind never knows cessation, samskaras remain active.

attributes.title: Dharma-Megha Samadhi (Cloud of Virtue Samadhi)
attributes.aspect: धर्ममेघ समाधि   ·   Sutra 4.29 The nature of this aspect. Dharma-megha samadhi is the 'cloud of virtue' samadhi—the supreme samadhi that comes when even the fruits of yogic accomplishment (including omniscience) hold no interest. It showers dharma (virtue, righteousness) on the yogi, destroying all kleshas and karma. It precedes kaivalya (final liberation). Insight. Vyāsa: This 'rain-cloud of virtue' showers dharma down, eliminating all kleshas and karma. Vijñānabhikṣu: This is the threshold of kaivalya, the final liberation. Hariharānanda: The yogi who has no more attachment even to omniscience attains this supreme samadhi.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: State (the supreme absorption — the cloud of virtue) · Valence: The pinnacle / fruit · Guṇa: Beyond the guṇas · Scale: Transcendent · Causal status: Fruit (the culminating absorption, raining down liberation) · Key relation: Sequence — the highest Samādhi; the threshold of Kaivalya.
coordinate.ontic_mode: State (the supreme absorption — the cloud of virtue)
coordinate.causal_status: Fruit (the culminating absorption, raining down liberation)
coordinate.key_relation: Sequence — the highest Samādhi; the threshold of Kaivalya

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When dharma-megha samadhi arises, all afflictions and karma are destroyed. The yogi stands at the threshold of kaivalya—final liberation.
facets.somatic: Body completely transformed by ultimate samadhi, fully purified instrument.
facets.emotional: Beyond all emotional bondage, transcendent peace, dharmic spontaneity.
facets.cognitive: All kleshas destroyed, dharma flowing spontaneously, mind at threshold of pure consciousness.
facets.psychic: The soul rests in the cloud-of-virtue absorption, where even the highest discernment is released and freedom rains down.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Cannot be 'excessive' as it is the supreme state—however, false claims of dharma-megha create dangerous spiritual delusion.
facets.somatic: False physical states claimed as dharma-megha.
facets.emotional: False emotional transcendence claims.
facets.cognitive: False claims of supreme realization, dangerous spiritual ego.
facets.psychic: The soul grasps at this supreme state as an attainment, the grasping a last subtle bondage.

Emotional: False emotional transcendence claims.

Cognitive: False claims of supreme realization, dangerous spiritual ego.

Psychic: The soul grasps at this supreme state as an attainment, the grasping a last subtle bondage.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Without dharma-megha, kaivalya is not yet attained. Kleshas may continue subtly.
facets.somatic: Body still subject to karmic fruition.
facets.emotional: Subtle emotional residue remaining.
facets.cognitive: Mind still has subtle attachment, even to spiritual attainments.
facets.psychic: The soul, far from this height, is still bound by its cravings and confusions.

attributes.title: Kriya-Yoga (Yoga of Action)
attributes.aspect: क्रिया योग   ·   Sutra 2.1, 2.2 The nature of this aspect. Kriya-yoga is the 'yoga of action' Patanjali presents in II.1—the integrated practice of tapas (austerity), svadhyaya (study), and Ishvara-pranidhana (devotion to the Lord). It is the action-oriented preparatory path that combines elements of karma, jnana, and bhakti yoga. Its purpose is to attenuate kleshas and bring samadhi. Insight. Vyāsa: For minds not yet capable of direct meditation, this active path is provided. Vijñānabhikṣu: Patanjali's kriya-yoga combines all three Gita paths—karma, jnana, bhakti. Hariharānanda: This is the preparatory practice for samadhi for those whose minds are still under rajas/tamas.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Practice (the preliminary yoga of action) · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Means (the practical engine of transformation) · Key relation: Whole — the triad of Tapas, Svādhyāya, and Īśvara-praṇidhāna (the last three niyamas).
coordinate.key_relation: Whole — the triad of Tapas, Svādhyāya, and Īśvara-praṇidhāna (the last three niyamas)

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When kriya-yoga is properly practiced, the three components synergize: discipline purifies, study illumines, and devotion surrenders. The kleshas weaken, samadhi approaches.
facets.somatic: Body disciplined through tapas, well-cared-for through svadhyaya wisdom, surrendered through devotion.
facets.emotional: Emotional refinement through tapas, devotional warmth through pranidhana, illumined through svadhyaya.
facets.cognitive: Mental purification through discipline, intellectual development through study, surrender through devotion—mind balanced.
facets.psychic: The soul takes up the yoga of action — discipline, self-study, surrender — as the practical engine of transformation.

balanced.text:
Overview: When kriya-yoga is properly practiced, the three components synergize: discipline purifies, study illumines, and devotion surrenders. The kleshas weaken, samadhi approaches.

Somatic: Body disciplined through tapas, well-cared-for through svadhyaya wisdom, surrendered through devotion.

Emotional: Emotional refinement through tapas, devotional warmth through pranidhana, illumined through svadhyaya.

Cognitive: Mental purification through discipline, intellectual development through study, surrender through devotion—mind balanced.

Psychic: The soul takes up the yoga of action — discipline, self-study, surrender — as the practical engine of transformation.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Imbalanced kriya-yoga (too much of one component) creates distortion—harsh asceticism, dry intellectualism, or blind devotionalism.
facets.somatic: Excessive tapas damaging body, ascetic injury, neglect of other limbs.
facets.emotional: Dry intellectualism without devotion, harsh self-discipline without warmth.
facets.cognitive: Imbalanced practice, missing the integration of three approaches.
facets.psychic: The soul over-emphasizes one strand, harsh discipline or endless study, and the balance is lost.

excess.text:

Somatic: Excessive tapas damaging body, ascetic injury, neglect of other limbs.

Emotional: Dry intellectualism without devotion, harsh self-discipline without warmth.

Cognitive: Imbalanced practice, missing the integration of three approaches.

Psychic: The soul over-emphasizes one strand, harsh discipline or endless study, and the balance is lost.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Without kriya-yoga, those with rajasic/tamasic minds lack the preparatory practice for samadhi. Without action, vrittis cannot be subdued.
facets.somatic: Undisciplined body, no transformative practice, no preparation.
facets.emotional: Emotional life without devotional dimension, no transformation through practice.
facets.cognitive: Mind unprepared for higher practice, no action-based purification.
facets.psychic: The soul practices none of the three, its aspiration left without a working method.

Emotional: Emotional life without devotional dimension, no transformation through practice.

Cognitive: Mind unprepared for higher practice, no action-based purification.

attributes.title: Viveka-Khyati (Discriminative Discernment)
attributes.aspect: विवेक ख्याति   ·   Sutra 2.26, 2.27, 3.52, 3.54, 4.26 The nature of this aspect. Viveka-khyati is the supreme discriminative discernment—direct experiential realization of the eternal difference between Purusha (pure consciousness) and Prakriti (matter). When this discrimination becomes unbroken (aviplava), it destroys the root klesha (avidya) and leads to kaivalya. It is the cognitive cure for ignorance. Insight. Vyāsa: Viveka-khyati is the seven-stage wisdom that culminates in kaivalya. Vijñānabhikṣu: This is the direct realization of the difference between Purusha and Prakriti—the cure for the root klesha (avidya). Hariharānanda: This discrimination becomes spontaneous and unbroken at higher stages.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: State / faculty (unbroken discriminative discernment) · Valence: The near-goal (the realization that liberates) · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Mechanism / fruit (discerns Puruṣa from Prakṛti) · Key relation: The direct means to Kaivalya; distinguishes seer from seen.
coordinate.key_relation: The direct means to Kaivalya; distinguishes seer from seen

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When viveka-khyati is established and unbroken, the yogi continuously recognizes the difference between Self and not-Self. This is the destroyer of avidya and the path to liberation.
facets.somatic: Body known as not-Self, healthy non-identification, body used as instrument.
facets.emotional: Emotional freedom from identification with feelings as Self, witnessing emotions clearly.
facets.cognitive: Continuous direct discrimination between Self and not-Self, mind serves consciousness, wisdom flows.
facets.psychic: The soul rests in unbroken discernment, directly distinguishing the witnessing awareness from the changing show of nature.

balanced.text:

Somatic: Body known as not-Self, healthy non-identification, body used as instrument.

Emotional: Emotional freedom from identification with feelings as Self, witnessing emotions clearly.

Cognitive: Continuous direct discrimination between Self and not-Self, mind serves consciousness, wisdom flows.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Intellectual claims of viveka without direct realization create spiritual arrogance and dangerous self-deception.
facets.somatic: Body neglect from intellectual claims of transcendence.
facets.emotional: Emotional bypassing through intellectual viveka, false detachment.
facets.cognitive: Intellectual conviction without realization, spiritual arrogance, mistaking theory for direct knowing.
facets.psychic: The soul intellectualizes discrimination, the idea standing in for the lived discernment.

excess.text:

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Without viveka, the fundamental confusion of Self with not-Self continues. All other kleshas flourish on this foundation.
facets.somatic: Complete identification with body as Self, all body-anxieties.
facets.emotional: Complete identification with emotions as Self, all emotional suffering.
facets.cognitive: Complete identification with mind as Self, foundational ignorance, all kleshas active.
facets.psychic: The soul cannot tell seer from seen, wholly merged with the play of nature.

attributes.title: Pratipaksha-Bhavana (Cultivating Opposite Thoughts)
attributes.aspect: प्रतिपक्ष भावन   ·   Sutra 2.33, 2.34 The nature of this aspect. Pratipaksha-bhavana is the powerful technique of consciously cultivating thoughts opposite to disturbing ones. When thoughts of violence (himsa) arise, cultivate non-violence; when greed arises, cultivate generosity. This active mental practice transforms the citta's tendencies. Insight. Vyāsa: When the mind goes toward thoughts contrary to the yamas (like violence), one should think their opposite. Vijñānabhikṣu: Replace each negative thought with its corresponding positive thought consciously. Hariharānanda: This is one of the most practical techniques for transforming the mind.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental · Ontic mode: Practice (a corrective mental technique) · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Mechanism (redirects a harmful thought toward its opposite) · Key relation: A method supporting the yamas and niyamas.
coordinate.causal_status: Mechanism (redirects a harmful thought toward its opposite)
coordinate.key_relation: A method supporting the yamas and niyamas

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When pratipaksha-bhavana is practiced wisely, the yogi transforms harmful tendencies into their opposites through active mental cultivation.
facets.somatic: Body increasingly oriented toward virtuous actions, healthy patterns developing.
facets.emotional: Emotional patterns shifting from negative to positive, transformation of feeling-states.
facets.cognitive: Active transformation of thought patterns, conscious replacement of harmful thoughts, mind becomes virtuous.
facets.psychic: The soul meets a harmful thought by deliberately cultivating its opposite, redirecting the mind toward the good.

balanced.text:

Somatic: Body increasingly oriented toward virtuous actions, healthy patterns developing.

Emotional: Emotional patterns shifting from negative to positive, transformation of feeling-states.

Cognitive: Active transformation of thought patterns, conscious replacement of harmful thoughts, mind becomes virtuous.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Mechanical pratipaksha-bhavana without insight becomes mere thought-suppression that doesn't address root causes.
facets.somatic: Forced behavioral change without inner transformation.
facets.emotional: Suppressing emotions through thought replacement rather than truly transforming.
facets.cognitive: Mental gymnastics without insight, forced opposite thinking that suppresses rather than transforms.
facets.psychic: The soul forces positive thoughts over real feeling, a suppression masquerading as practice.

Psychic: The soul forces positive thoughts over real feeling, a suppression masquerading as practice.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Without pratipaksha-bhavana, harmful thoughts run unchecked, creating samskaras and karma. The mind cannot be reformed.
facets.somatic: Body driven by unchecked harmful impulses.
facets.emotional: Negative emotions run wild without conscious transformation.
facets.cognitive: Mind dominated by harmful thoughts, no active cultivation of virtue, samskaras of vice strengthen.
facets.psychic: The soul lets harmful thoughts run unopposed, swept along by every negative current.

attributes.title: Ahimsa (Non-Violence) [Yoga Sutra Context]
attributes.aspect: अहिंसा   ·   Sutra 2.30, 2.35 The nature of this aspect. In the Yoga Sutras, Ahimsa is the first and foundational Yama. It is non-harming in thought, word, and deed toward all beings. Patanjali states that when Ahimsa is firmly established, all hostility ceases in the presence of the yogi (Sutra 2.35).

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Virtue — a yama (non-harming) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate (the foundational restraint) · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Foundation (the first and root yama) · Key relation: A yama (part of the Yama limb); the ground of all the others.
coordinate.key_relation: A yama (part of the Yama limb); the ground of all the others

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When Ahimsa is firmly established, the yogi radiates such peaceful presence that hostility cannot exist around them. There is profound non-harming in thoughts, words, and actions.
facets.somatic: Calm and peaceful physical presence, healthy nervous system, balanced energy that doesn't provoke others, naturally non-aggressive body language, healthy biology from non-stressful living.
facets.emotional: Deep emotional peace and compassion for all beings, freedom from anger and aggression, emotional warmth that disarms hostility, ability to remain peaceful under provocation.
facets.cognitive: Mind free from violent thoughts, mental peace radiates outward, freedom from internal hostility, wisdom about interconnectedness, thoughts that cause no harm.
facets.psychic: The soul establishes non-harming in thought, word, and deed, and a deep harmlessness radiates from it.

balanced.text:
Overview: When Ahimsa is firmly established, the yogi radiates such peaceful presence that hostility cannot exist around them. There is profound non-harming in thoughts, words, and actions.

Somatic: Calm and peaceful physical presence, healthy nervous system, balanced energy that doesn't provoke others, naturally non-aggressive body language, healthy biology from non-stressful living.

Emotional: Deep emotional peace and compassion for all beings, freedom from anger and aggression, emotional warmth that disarms hostility, ability to remain peaceful under provocation.

Cognitive: Mind free from violent thoughts, mental peace radiates outward, freedom from internal hostility, wisdom about interconnectedness, thoughts that cause no harm.

Psychic: The soul establishes non-harming in thought, word, and deed, and a deep harmlessness radiates from it.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Misunderstood Ahimsa becomes passive submission to harm, inability to set protective boundaries, allowing destruction in name of non-violence.
facets.somatic: Allowing physical harm to oneself or others, inability to take defensive action when needed, weakness disguised as non-violence.
facets.emotional: Emotional passivity in face of injustice, allowing emotional harm, codependent acceptance of toxic relationships.
facets.cognitive: Mental confusion about appropriate response to harm, inability to discriminate when action is needed, false peace masking enabling behavior.
facets.psychic: Non-harming becomes scrupulous paralysis, the soul afraid any act might injure.

Cognitive: Mental confusion about appropriate response to harm, inability to discriminate when action is needed, false peace masking enabling behavior.

Psychic: Non-harming becomes scrupulous paralysis, the soul afraid any act might injure.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Absence of Ahimsa creates violent thoughts, words, actions—the yogi cannot make progress as their consciousness is contaminated by harm.
facets.somatic: Aggressive physical behavior, violence toward others or self, stress-related illness from internal aggression, harmful actions.
facets.emotional: Anger, hatred, hostility, emotional cruelty, vindictiveness, lack of compassion, emotional violence in relationships.
facets.cognitive: Violent thoughts, mental cruelty, planning harm, mental aggression that contaminates consciousness, obstruction to all yogic progress.
facets.psychic: The soul harms freely in word and deed, the foundational restraint unobserved.

attributes.title: Satya (Truthfulness) [Yoga Sutra Context]
attributes.aspect: सत्य   ·   Sutra 2.30, 2.36 The nature of this aspect. Satya is truthfulness in thought, word, and deed. Patanjali states that when established in truth, action and its fruits become subservient to the yogi (Sutra 2.36)—meaning their words become powerful and creative.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Vital · Ontic mode: Virtue — a yama (truthfulness) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Foundation (a root restraint) · Key relation: A yama (part of the Yama limb); kin to Ārjavam (Dim 10/24).
coordinate.key_relation: A yama (part of the Yama limb); kin to Ārjavam (Dim 10/24)

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When Satya is established, a person's words and thoughts align with reality. Their pronouncements become true and creative; they can literally manifest what they speak.
facets.somatic: Body language consistent with words, calm nervous system from authenticity, voice that carries truth-power, healthy biology from non-stressful authenticity.
facets.emotional: Emotional authenticity and clarity, freedom from emotional deception, integrity in feelings, peace from authentic living.
facets.cognitive: Clear honest thinking, mind aligned with reality, freedom from self-deception, mental power that comes from truth-alignment.
facets.psychic: The soul establishes truthfulness, its word and reality so aligned that what it says comes to pass.

balanced.text:

Somatic: Body language consistent with words, calm nervous system from authenticity, voice that carries truth-power, healthy biology from non-stressful authenticity.

Emotional: Emotional authenticity and clarity, freedom from emotional deception, integrity in feelings, peace from authentic living.

Cognitive: Clear honest thinking, mind aligned with reality, freedom from self-deception, mental power that comes from truth-alignment.

Psychic: The soul establishes truthfulness, its word and reality so aligned that what it says comes to pass.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Excessive Satya without wisdom becomes harmful truth-telling, brutal honesty without compassion, inappropriate disclosure that damages.
facets.somatic: Harsh physical expression of truth, body language that wounds, inappropriate physical disclosure.
facets.emotional: Truth weaponized to hurt others, emotional brutality disguised as honesty, lack of compassion in disclosure.
facets.cognitive: Rigid insistence on stating all truth regardless of impact, mental harshness, inability to recognize when discretion serves higher truth.
facets.psychic: Truth is wielded harshly, honesty becoming a blunt instrument that wounds.

excess.text:

Cognitive: Rigid insistence on stating all truth regardless of impact, mental harshness, inability to recognize when discretion serves higher truth.

Psychic: Truth is wielded harshly, honesty becoming a blunt instrument that wounds.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Absence of Satya creates web of lies, self-deception, manipulation, fragmented consciousness, and powerlessness in speech.
facets.somatic: Body language contradicting words, stress symptoms from constant pretense, exhausted nervous system, somatic manifestations of deception.
facets.emotional: Emotional dishonesty, inability to feel authentic, emotional manipulation, disconnection from real feelings.
facets.cognitive: Mental fragmentation from lies, rationalization, self-deception, weak and ineffective speech (words have no power), mental confusion.
facets.psychic: The soul lives in falsehood, its words unmoored from any reality.

attributes.title: Asteya (Non-Stealing)
attributes.aspect: अस्तेय   ·   Sutra 2.30, 2.37 The nature of this aspect. Asteya is non-stealing in thought, word, and deed—not taking what is not freely given. It extends to time, energy, credit, and ideas. Patanjali states that when established in Asteya, all jewels come to the yogi (Sutra 2.37).

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Virtue — a yama (non-stealing) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Foundation (a root restraint) · Key relation: A yama (part of the Yama limb).
coordinate.key_relation: A yama (part of the Yama limb)

balanced.label: Balanced expression

facets.overview: Overview: When Asteya is established, abundance flows to the yogi naturally. There is contentment with what one has and integrity in interactions with what belongs to others.
facets.somatic: Physical respect for others' belongings and space, healthy boundaries, abundance manifesting in physical reality, body free from anxiety of acquisition.
facets.emotional: Emotional contentment and integrity, freedom from envy and grasping, peace with what is one's own, emotional abundance.
facets.cognitive: Honest thinking about what belongs to whom, freedom from coveting, mental abundance, wisdom about true ownership, integrity in ideas and attribution.
facets.psychic: The soul takes only what is freely given, and in non-stealing finds a strange abundance flowing to it.

balanced.text:
Overview: When Asteya is established, abundance flows to the yogi naturally. There is contentment with what one has and integrity in interactions with what belongs to others.

Somatic: Physical respect for others' belongings and space, healthy boundaries, abundance manifesting in physical reality, body free from anxiety of acquisition.

Emotional: Emotional contentment and integrity, freedom from envy and grasping, peace with what is one's own, emotional abundance.

Cognitive: Honest thinking about what belongs to whom, freedom from coveting, mental abundance, wisdom about true ownership, integrity in ideas and attribution.

Psychic: The soul takes only what is freely given, and in non-stealing finds a strange abundance flowing to it.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Overview: Excessive Asteya can become excessive scrupulosity, paranoid concern about taking anything, inability to receive gifts or help, false purity.
facets.somatic: Refusing legitimate help or gifts, physical hoarding from fear of taking, anxiety about all possessions.
facets.emotional: Emotional rigidity about receiving, inability to accept emotional support, false saintliness about possessions, emotional anxiety.
facets.cognitive: Obsessive thinking about ownership, scrupulosity that paralyzes, false purity about taking, inability to engage normally.
facets.psychic: Non-stealing becomes anxious scrupulosity over every borrowed thing.

excess.text:

Emotional: Emotional rigidity about receiving, inability to accept emotional support, false saintliness about possessions, emotional anxiety.

Cognitive: Obsessive thinking about ownership, scrupulosity that paralyzes, false purity about taking, inability to engage normally.

Psychic: Non-stealing becomes anxious scrupulosity over every borrowed thing.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Overview: Absence of Asteya creates stealing in many forms—material, intellectual (plagiarism), emotional (stealing credit), time (lateness), energy (vampirism). Inner poverty results.
facets.somatic: Taking what isn't given, stealing physical objects, body that takes more than its share, depletion from unfair acquisition.
facets.emotional: Emotional stealing (claiming others' achievements), envy and coveting, taking emotional energy from others, emotional poverty despite acquisition.
facets.cognitive: Plagiarism of ideas, mental coveting, dishonest thinking about belongings, mental poverty (always needing more), inner emptiness driving acquisition.
facets.psychic: The soul takes what is not given — goods, credit, attention — grasping at what is not its own.
