deficient.label: Deficiency: Saturnine Collapse (“Too Little Saturn”)

facets.overview: Vignette: The Sāḍe Sāti Season Scenario A: The Binding Path (Low Epistemic Integrity) Context: Deepak, 50, has entered Sāḍe Sāti—Saturn’s seven-and-a-half-year transit through his natal Moon sign. In the past eighteen months: his mother has died, his eldest child has left for university, and his company has been restructured, leaving him in a diminished role. Internal Landscape: Deepak wakes each morning to a heaviness that sits on his chest like wet stone. His joints ache. The world appears in greyscale. “This is punishment. I must have done something terrible in a past life. The stars are against me.” He externalizes Saturn’s influence as persecution rather than pedagogy. He consults an astrologer who prescribes a blue sapphire ring—but Deepak wears it as a talisman against fate rather than a tool for attunement. Conduct: Deepak withdraws. He stops calling friends. He works mechanically, without investment. He sits in front of the television for hours, numbed. His wife’s attempts at warmth are rebuffed: “You don’t understand.” The Sāḍe Sāti period becomes a long, grey tunnel that he endures rather than engages. Outcome: The Saṃskāra of passive victimhood hardens. The period ends, as all transits do, but Deepak emerges from it diminished rather than deepened. The karmic curriculum was presented; he did not enroll. The same lessons will return in another form. Scenario B: The Liberating Path (High Epistemic Integrity) Internal Landscape: Deepak feels the same heaviness. But he has been studying his chart with a wise counsellor who framed Sāḍe Sāti not as punishment but as purification. “This is Saturn’s classroom. The curriculum is loss, limitation, and the discovery of what remains when everything non-essential is stripped away.” He does not deny the grief. He sits with it, names it, and asks: “What is Saturn asking me to become?” Conduct: Deepak begins a practice of disciplined simplicity. He rises at 5 a.m. and walks for one hour in silence—Saturn’s medicine of slow, sustained effort. He volunteers at a hospice (Saturn governs the elderly and the dying). He fasts on Saturdays, not as superstitious observance but as a conscious meeting with limitation. He begins to journal—recording not what he has lost, but what each loss has revealed about what he truly values. He lights a sesame oil lamp on Saturday evenings and chants: “Om Śanaiścaraya Namaḥ.” Outcome: The Saṃskāra of victimhood is met with disciplined engagement. The grief does not disappear, but it transmutes into depth. Deepak emerges from Sāḍe Sāti leaner, quieter, and more honest. His relationships, while fewer, are now founded on genuine presence rather than social performance. Saturn’s lesson has been received: the discovery that one can lose everything that can be lost and still stand.
facets.somatic: The skeletal structure is weak. Bones are fragile; teeth deteriorate early. There is a lack of physical endurance—the body tires quickly and recovers slowly. Posture is poor, not from emotional collapse (as with weak Sun) but from structural inadequacy. The body lacks the “frame” upon which health depends.
facets.emotional: Discipline is absent. Commitments are made and broken casually. Responsibility is avoided. The emotional life is governed by the pleasure principle: whatever feels good now is pursued, regardless of long-term consequences. There is an emotional adolescence—a refusal to accept the constraints of adult life. Relationships lack depth because they lack endurance.
facets.cognitive: Thinking is short-sighted and undisciplined. Plans are never completed because the next shiny idea replaces the current one. The internal narrative avoids any mention of consequences: “Why worry? It’ll work out.” Hard truths are evaded. Deadlines are missed. The mind has no scaffolding upon which to build sustained intellectual achievement.
facets.psychic: The soul avoids its karmic curriculum. The lessons that Saturn presents—patience, humility, accountability—are refused or postponed. The individual may be spiritually sincere but spiritually unstructured: they read many books but complete no practice; they begin many paths but walk none to its end. The lesson is commitment: choosing one practice, one discipline, one path, and staying on it through boredom, difficulty, and the temptation to flee.

deficient.text:
Vignette: The Sāḍe Sāti Season Scenario A: The Binding Path (Low Epistemic Integrity) Context: Deepak, 50, has entered Sāḍe Sāti—Saturn’s seven-and-a-half-year transit through his natal Moon sign. In the past eighteen months: his mother has died, his eldest child has left for university, and his company has been restructured, leaving him in a diminished role. Internal Landscape: Deepak wakes each morning to a heaviness that sits on his chest like wet stone. His joints ache. The world appears in greyscale. “This is punishment. I must have done something terrible in a past life. The stars are against me.” He externalizes Saturn’s influence as persecution rather than pedagogy. He consults an astrologer who prescribes a blue sapphire ring—but Deepak wears it as a talisman against fate rather than a tool for attunement. Conduct: Deepak withdraws. He stops calling friends. He works mechanically, without investment. He sits in front of the television for hours, numbed. His wife’s attempts at warmth are rebuffed: “You don’t understand.” The Sāḍe Sāti period becomes a long, grey tunnel that he endures rather than engages. Outcome: The Saṃskāra of passive victimhood hardens. The period ends, as all transits do, but Deepak emerges from it diminished rather than deepened. The karmic curriculum was presented; he did not enroll. The same lessons will return in another form. Scenario B: The Liberating Path (High Epistemic Integrity) Internal Landscape: Deepak feels the same heaviness. But he has been studying his chart with a wise counsellor who framed Sāḍe Sāti not as punishment but as purification. “This is Saturn’s classroom. The curriculum is loss, limitation, and the discovery of what remains when everything non-essential is stripped away.” He does not deny the grief. He sits with it, names it, and asks: “What is Saturn asking me to become?” Conduct: Deepak begins a practice of disciplined simplicity. He rises at 5 a.m. and walks for one hour in silence—Saturn’s medicine of slow, sustained effort. He volunteers at a hospice (Saturn governs the elderly and the dying). He fasts on Saturdays, not as superstitious observance but as a conscious meeting with limitation. He begins to journal—recording not what he has lost, but what each loss has revealed about what he truly values. He lights a sesame oil lamp on Saturday evenings and chants: “Om Śanaiścaraya Namaḥ.” Outcome: The Saṃskāra of victimhood is met with disciplined engagement. The grief does not disappear, but it transmutes into depth. Deepak emerges from Sāḍe Sāti leaner, quieter, and more honest. His relationships, while fewer, are now founded on genuine presence rather than social performance. Saturn’s lesson has been received: the discovery that one can lose everything that can be lost and still stand.

Somatic: The skeletal structure is weak. Bones are fragile; teeth deteriorate early. There is a lack of physical endurance—the body tires quickly and recovers slowly. Posture is poor, not from emotional collapse (as with weak Sun) but from structural inadequacy. The body lacks the “frame” upon which health depends.

Emotional: Discipline is absent. Commitments are made and broken casually. Responsibility is avoided. The emotional life is governed by the pleasure principle: whatever feels good now is pursued, regardless of long-term consequences. There is an emotional adolescence—a refusal to accept the constraints of adult life. Relationships lack depth because they lack endurance.

Cognitive: Thinking is short-sighted and undisciplined. Plans are never completed because the next shiny idea replaces the current one. The internal narrative avoids any mention of consequences: “Why worry? It’ll work out.” Hard truths are evaded. Deadlines are missed. The mind has no scaffolding upon which to build sustained intellectual achievement.

Psychic: The soul avoids its karmic curriculum. The lessons that Saturn presents—patience, humility, accountability—are refused or postponed. The individual may be spiritually sincere but spiritually unstructured: they read many books but complete no practice; they begin many paths but walk none to its end. The lesson is commitment: choosing one practice, one discipline, one path, and staying on it through boredom, difficulty, and the temptation to flee.

additional_info.heading: Chapter 5: The Chāyā Grahas — Rāhu and Ketu
additional_info.text:
Rāhu and Ketu are the most enigmatic members of the Navagraha. They are not physical planets but mathematical points—the ascending and descending nodes of the Moon’s orbit, where it intersects the ecliptic plane. In mythology, they are the head (Rāhu) and tail (Ketu) of a demon who drank the nectar of immortality and was severed by Viṣṇu’s discus. This mythological image encodes a phenomenological truth: Rāhu and Ketu represent the two faces of karmic compulsion—the insatiable hunger for what one has never had (Rāhu) and the painful release of what one has already mastered (Ketu).

Together they form the karmic axis of the birth chart, defining the essential tension of the soul’s evolutionary journey in this lifetime. Rāhu points toward the unfamiliar territory the soul must explore; Ketu points toward the familiar territory the soul must release.

attributes.title: Rāhu (The North Node): The Principle of Worldly Obsession and Karmic Hunger
attributes.aspect: Rāhu represents the point of maximum worldly desire, amplification, and illusion. It has no body—only a head—and therefore its appetite is literally insatiable: it consumes but can never be filled. In the psyche, Rāhu manifests as compulsive ambition, fascination with the foreign or unconventional, technological obsession, and the pursuit of experiences that one has never had before. Rāhu’s Daśā period is eighteen years.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental (obsession, ambition, illusion) · Ontic mode: Chāyā graha — a shadow / nodal influence · Valence: Neutral force tending to affliction (amplifies craving) · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Cosmic (Ādhidaivika), lived individually · Causal status: Conditioning influence (amplifies worldly hunger and obsession) · Key relation: One of the navagraha (a chāyā / shadow graha); antagonist-complement to Ketu (its opposite node).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Chāyā graha — a shadow / nodal influence
coordinate.valence: Neutral force tending to affliction (amplifies craving)
coordinate.causal_status: Conditioning influence (amplifies worldly hunger and obsession)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the navagraha (a chāyā / shadow graha); antagonist-complement to Ketu (its opposite node)

balanced.label: Equilibrium: Rāhu as Evolutionary Driver

facets.somatic: When Rāhu’s energy is well-channelled, it manifests as a heightened sensitivity to environmental stimuli—a capacity to pick up on subtle cues that others miss. The nervous system is alert without being frayed. There is an adaptability in the body that allows one to thrive in unfamiliar settings—new countries, new climates, new physical demands.
facets.emotional: There is an excitement about the unknown that is not manic but genuinely curious. The individual can engage with foreign cultures, unconventional ideas, and taboo subjects without being consumed by them. There is a healthy ambition—a desire to grow beyond one’s current station—that is tempered by awareness of its source in karmic need rather than genuine necessity.
facets.cognitive: Thinking is innovative and boundary-breaking. The individual questions conventions and sees possibilities that others cannot. There is a natural affinity for technology, research, and paradigm-shifting ideas. The cognitive style is synthetic—combining elements from different domains in novel ways.
facets.psychic: The soul understands Rāhu as a teacher of expansion—a force that pushes consciousness into unfamiliar territory precisely because comfort zones have become spiritual stagnation points. The hunger is honoured, but not obeyed blindly.

balanced.text:

The nervous system is alert without being frayed. There is an adaptability in the body that allows one to thrive in unfamiliar settings—new countries, new climates, new physical demands.

Emotional: There is an excitement about the unknown that is not manic but genuinely curious. The individual can engage with foreign cultures, unconventional ideas, and taboo subjects without being consumed by them. There is a healthy ambition—a desire to grow beyond one’s current station—that is tempered by awareness of its source in karmic need rather than genuine necessity.

Cognitive: Thinking is innovative and boundary-breaking. The individual questions conventions and sees possibilities that others cannot. There is a natural affinity for technology, research, and paradigm-shifting ideas. The cognitive style is synthetic—combining elements from different domains in novel ways.

Psychic: The soul understands Rāhu as a teacher of expansion—a force that pushes consciousness into unfamiliar territory precisely because comfort zones have become spiritual stagnation points. The hunger is honoured, but not obeyed blindly.

excess.label: Excess: Rāhu as Obsessive Compulsion

facets.somatic: The nervous system is over-stimulated and dysregulated. Unusual or undiagnosed symptoms arise—strange pains, hypersensitivities, phantom sensations. The body feels as though it is being driven by an engine with no off-switch. Addictive patterns emerge: substances, screens, or compulsive behaviours that provide temporary relief from the relentless inner hunger. Sleep is disturbed by vivid, sometimes disturbing dreams.
facets.emotional: Desire becomes obsession. The individual fixates on a particular person, goal, or experience with an intensity that is disproportionate to its actual value. There is a quality of “spell”—a feeling of being under the influence of a force one cannot control. Anxiety is chronic and free-floating, attaching itself to whatever object is nearest. There is a sense that something is desperately wrong, but it cannot be named.
facets.cognitive: Thinking becomes conspiratorial and paranoid. The mind weaves elaborate narratives to justify its obsessions. Illusion is mistaken for insight. The individual may become fixated on conspiracy theories, occult practices without grounding, or grandiose visions of world-changing significance. The internal narrative: “Only I can see the truth. Everyone else is asleep.”
facets.psychic: The soul is lost in Māyā—the cosmic illusion. Rāhu’s shadow has eclipsed the inner light, and the individual cannot distinguish genuine spiritual prompting from karmic compulsion. The lesson is devastating and simple: what you most desperately want is not what you most deeply need.

deficient.label: Deficiency: Rāhu Suppressed

facets.somatic: The body feels paralyzed in its comfort zone. There is an inability to adapt to new environments, new foods, new climates. The nervous system is under-responsive to novelty—dull, sluggish, and resistant to change. Chronic conditions of stagnation may develop.
facets.emotional: Fear of the unknown dominates. Anything foreign, unconventional, or challenging is avoided. The individual clings to the familiar with a rigidity that prevents growth. Relationships are limited to a very small circle, and any disruption to the routine triggers disproportionate distress.
facets.cognitive: Thinking is provincial and closed. New ideas are rejected without examination. The cognitive style is purely reproductive—repeating what has already been learned rather than generating new understanding. Innovation is impossible because the mind has walled itself off from the discomfort that innovation requires.
facets.psychic: The soul refuses its own evolutionary curriculum. The growth that Rāhu demands is denied. Life becomes a repetition of patterns that were mastered long ago, and the resulting existential boredom slowly poisons every domain.

attributes.title: Ketu (The South Node): The Principle of Detachment and Spiritual Liberation
attributes.aspect: Ketu is Rāhu’s counterpart and complement. Where Rāhu reaches forward into the unknown, Ketu points backward to what has already been completed. It represents past-life mastery, spiritual insight, the capacity for detachment, and the dissolution of worldly attachment. In the body, Ketu is associated with the lower limbs and with mysterious, untraceable ailments. In the psyche, it represents mysticism, psychic sensitivity, liberation, and the willingness to let go. Ketu’s Daśā period is seven years.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic (detachment, past-life mastery, liberation) · Ontic mode: Chāyā graha — a shadow / nodal influence · Valence: Neutral force (dissolving — liberating or disorienting) · Guṇa: Tamas–Sattva (dissolution toward liberation) · Scale: Cosmic (Ādhidaivika), lived individually · Causal status: Conditioning influence (dissolves attachment; turns toward liberation) · Key relation: One of the navagraha (a chāyā / shadow graha); antagonist-complement to Rāhu (its opposite node).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Chāyā graha — a shadow / nodal influence
coordinate.causal_status: Conditioning influence (dissolves attachment; turns toward liberation)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the navagraha (a chāyā / shadow graha); antagonist-complement to Rāhu (its opposite node)

balanced.label: Equilibrium: Ketu as Spiritual Wisdom

facets.somatic: When Ketu’s energy is integrated, there is a lightness to the body that reflects the absence of excessive attachment. The individual does not cling to physical comfort; they can fast, sit in stillness, or endure austerity without suffering. There is an intuitive body-awareness that does not require external tools—the body “knows” what it needs without elaborate analysis.
facets.emotional: There is a serene detachment that is not coldness but clarity. The individual can witness the arising and passing of emotions without being destabilized by them. There is no fear of loss because there is a deep understanding that nothing real can be lost. Relationships are held lightly—with genuine love but without grasping.
facets.cognitive: Thinking is penetrative and non-verbal. The individual understands through direct perception rather than elaborate reasoning. There is an affinity for the mystical, the paradoxical, and the ineffable. The internal narrative is sparse: much is understood that cannot be articulated, and there is peace with that limitation.
facets.psychic: The soul is in contact with its own deepest nature. The layers of identification—with body, mind, emotion, role—are transparent. There is a lived sense of what the Śāstras describe as “the witness”—the consciousness that observes experience without being identical to it. Ketu at its highest is Mokṣa itself: liberation from the cycle of becoming.

The individual does not cling to physical comfort; they can fast, sit in stillness, or endure austerity without suffering. There is an intuitive body-awareness that does not require external tools—the body “knows” what it needs without elaborate analysis.

Emotional: There is a serene detachment that is not coldness but clarity. The individual can witness the arising and passing of emotions without being destabilized by them. There is no fear of loss because there is a deep understanding that nothing real can be lost. Relationships are held lightly—with genuine love but without grasping.

There is a lived sense of what the Śāstras describe as “the witness”—the consciousness that observes experience without being identical to it. Ketu at its highest is Mokṣa itself: liberation from the cycle of becoming.

excess.label: Excess: Ketu as Dissociation

facets.somatic: The body feels unreal—as though one is observing it from outside. Dissociation manifests physically as numbness, clumsiness, or a strange disconnect between intention and movement. There may be mysterious symptoms—pains without physical cause, sensitivities without explanation—that reflect Ketu’s fundamental nature as a headless body groping in the dark.
facets.emotional: Detachment becomes indifference. The individual cannot engage emotionally with life—not because they have transcended emotion, but because they have disengaged from it prematurely. Relationships wither from neglect. There is a ghostly quality—present in body but absent in spirit. The person seems to be always somewhere else.
facets.cognitive: Thinking becomes nebulous and unfocused. Mysticism replaces clear reasoning. The individual may claim profound spiritual insight while being unable to function in daily life—unable to pay bills, maintain a schedule, or complete ordinary tasks. The internal narrative: “The material world doesn’t matter. I am beyond all this.” But “beyond” is really “in retreat from.”
facets.psychic: The soul uses spirituality as an escape from incarnation rather than as a path through it. Ketu’s gift of non-attachment has been perverted into avoidance. The lesson is that Mokṣa is not achieved by refusing to engage with life, but by engaging so fully and so consciously that attachment naturally dissolves.

deficient.label: Deficiency: Ketu Suppressed

facets.overview: Vignette: The Career Pivot at Forty-Five Scenario A: The Binding Path (Low Epistemic Integrity) Context: Sunita, 45, is a successful corporate lawyer who has entered her Ketu Daśā. For the first time in her career, her work feels hollow. She wins a major case and feels nothing. Internal Landscape: Sunita is bewildered. “This is what I worked twenty years for. Why do I feel empty?” Rather than recognizing the Ketu Daśā as an invitation to explore what lies beyond professional identity, she panics. She mislabels the detachment as depression and the hollowness as burnout. She decides what she needs is more —a bigger case, a higher-profile client, a book deal. Conduct: Sunita doubles down on work. She takes on pro bono cases not out of service but out of a desperate need to feel the old surge of purpose. She starts a podcast about legal strategy, hoping public visibility will fill the void. The emptiness deepens. Outcome: The Saṃskāra of identity-through-achievement hardens even as Ketu systematically undermines its foundations. The Vāsanā for professional validation becomes more frantic as its returns diminish. Sunita is running faster on a treadmill that is slowing down. Scenario B: The Liberating Path (High Epistemic Integrity) Internal Landscape: Sunita notices the emptiness and, instead of fleeing, sits down inside it. “This isn’t depression. This is Ketu. This is the soul saying: you’ve completed this lesson. The things that gave you identity—the titles, the wins, the recognition—are being gently removed not as punishment, but as preparation. Something else is trying to emerge.” Conduct: Sunita does not quit her job impulsively. But she begins to create space. She takes a three-month sabbatical. She visits an ashram—not to “find herself” in a consumerist sense, but to sit in the silence that Ketu demands. She begins a daily practice of Viśpassanā meditation—the practice most aligned with Ketu’s energy, which involves observing the arising and passing of all phenomena without attachment. She donates anonymously to causes she cares about, releasing the need for recognition (a Ketu practice). Outcome: The Saṃskāra of identity-through-achievement is met with conscious dissolution. The Eṣaṇā for recognition loosens. Sunita does not become a “spiritual person” in the performative sense; she becomes someone for whom the question “Who am I?” is no longer terrifying but generative. When she returns to law, she practises differently—with less ego and more service. The title remains; the attachment to it dissolves.
facets.somatic: The body is entirely identified with worldly pursuit—health is maintained for productivity, appearance is curated for social advantage, and physical discipline serves ambition rather than awareness. There is no capacity for stillness; meditation is impossible not because the mind is restless but because the body demands constant stimulation.
facets.emotional: There is no access to the contemplative dimension of emotional life. Every feeling is channelled into action. Grief is “processed” through work. Loneliness is “solved” through socializing. The deeper currents—the existential longing for meaning, the intuition of something beyond the visible—are walled off entirely.
facets.cognitive: Thinking is entirely materialistic and empirical. The invisible dimension of experience is denied or mocked. Spirituality is dismissed as superstition. The internal narrative: “Only what I can see and measure is real.” This is not philosophical empiricism but an existential flinch—a refusal to confront the mystery at the heart of being.
facets.psychic: The soul is fully identified with its worldly roles—professional, familial, social—and has forgotten that it is, at its core, something that cannot be defined by any role. The lesson is the first encounter with genuine emptiness: the moment when all external identifiers fail and the question “Who am I really?” can no longer be evaded.

attributes.title: Avidyā — The Root: Mistaking the Self
attributes.aspect: अविद्या   ·   Yoga Sūtra II.3–II.5 The nature of this affliction. Avidyā is the root affliction — the structural error of mistaking the impermanent for permanent, the impure for pure, the painful for pleasant, and the not-Self for the Self. Patañjali names it the field in which all the other afflictions grow. It is not stupidity and not lack of education; it is a misperception so basic that it usually never becomes an object of attention at all.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Mental (the root misperception of self) · Ontic mode: Affliction — the root kleśa (primal ignorance) · Valence: Affliction (the root to see through) · Guṇa: Tamas-dominant (obscuration) · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Root cause (the soil from which the other four grow) · Key relation: Whole / root — the ground of Asmitā, Rāga, Dveṣa, and Abhiniveśa; mistakes the eternal Self for the transient.
coordinate.causal_status: Root cause (the soil from which the other four grow)
coordinate.key_relation: Whole / root — the ground of Asmitā, Rāga, Dveṣa, and Abhiniveśa; mistakes the eternal Self for the transient

balanced.label: In the refined state — avidyā seen through

facets.overview: When Avidya is removed through Viveka (discrimination), reality is seen as it is. The yogi knows the difference between Self and non-Self, real and unreal.
facets.somatic: Body known as instrument not Self, physical clarity about temporary nature of body, healthy non-identification.
facets.emotional: Emotional freedom from misidentification, peace from knowing true Self, freedom from suffering of confusion.
facets.cognitive: Clear discrimination, freedom from fundamental error, wisdom about reality, all other kleshas dissolved.
facets.psychic: The soul rests in its own light; the lesson — that the witness was never the witnessed — has been touched at the very root of all veiling.

balanced.text:

Somatic: Body known as instrument not Self, physical clarity about temporary nature of body, healthy non-identification.

excess.label: In excess — ignorance disguised as attainment

facets.overview: Cannot have excessive ignorance in a yogic sense; however, intellectual claims of removed ignorance without realization create spiritual arrogance.
facets.somatic: Body neglect from false transcendence claims, ungrounded physical state.
facets.emotional: Emotional bypassing claiming Avidya is removed, false transcendence, spiritual arrogance.
facets.cognitive: Intellectual conviction without realization, spiritual pride, false claims of awakening, dangerous self-deception.
facets.psychic: The soul is subtly betrayed by the mind’s claim to have arrived; the lesson at play is humility — that realization cannot be performed or asserted, only lived.

excess.text:
Cannot have excessive ignorance in a yogic sense; however, intellectual claims of removed ignorance without realization create spiritual arrogance.

Somatic: Body neglect from false transcendence claims, ungrounded physical state.

Emotional: Emotional bypassing claiming Avidya is removed, false transcendence, spiritual arrogance.

Cognitive: Intellectual conviction without realization, spiritual pride, false claims of awakening, dangerous self-deception.

Psychic: The soul is subtly betrayed by the mind’s claim to have arrived; the lesson at play is humility — that realization cannot be performed or asserted, only lived.

deficient.label: Fully active — avidyā as the architecture of suffering

facets.overview: Avidya in its fullest form creates the entire structure of suffering—all other kleshas arise from it. The person mistakes everything fundamental.
facets.somatic: Identification with body as Self, fear of death, body anxiety, illness from misperception of nature.
facets.emotional: All emotional suffering arises here—from misidentification with the impermanent, emotional turmoil.
facets.cognitive: Fundamental confusion about reality, mistaking impermanent for permanent, all mental suffering, foundation of all kleshas.
facets.psychic: The soul is wholly veiled from itself, taking the perishable for its own being; the lesson waiting beneath all others is that one is simply not what one has mistaken oneself to be.

deficient.text:
Avidya in its fullest form creates the entire structure of suffering—all other kleshas arise from it. The person mistakes everything fundamental.

Somatic: Identification with body as Self, fear of death, body anxiety, illness from misperception of nature.

Emotional: All emotional suffering arises here—from misidentification with the impermanent, emotional turmoil.

Cognitive: Fundamental confusion about reality, mistaking impermanent for permanent, all mental suffering, foundation of all kleshas.

Psychic: The soul is wholly veiled from itself, taking the perishable for its own being; the lesson waiting beneath all others is that one is simply not what one has mistaken oneself to be.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: In conduct, a person living from refined viveka holds roles and possessions lightly: they can lose a title or a relationship and grieve cleanly without an identity collapse, because they never confused the role with the self. Where avidyā is full-grown, every threat to the body, the reputation, or the self-image registers as a threat to existence itself — so small slights become unbearable, and ordinary change feels like a small death.

additional_info.heading: A day in the life
additional_info.text: Maya, 41, is passed over for a promotion she expected. In the full-grown grip of avidyā, the thought is not ‘I did not get the role’ but ‘I am a failure’ — the event and the self fuse, and a sleepless, churning night follows in which her whole worth feels revoked. On a clearer morning weeks later, the same memory returns, but now there is a hairline gap between the fact and the self: the role was wanted, the role was not given, and the one who wanted it remains, untouched, watching. Nothing about the event changed. The coloring did.

attributes.title: Asmitā — The Separate ‘I’
attributes.aspect: अस्मिता   ·   Yoga Sūtra II.3, II.6 The nature of this affliction. Asmitā is the confusion of the seer with the instrument of seeing — when pure awareness and the mind through which it looks appear as one and the same thing. Patañjali defines it as the apparent identity of the power of consciousness and the power of the mental faculty. Practically, it is the felt sense of being a separate, bounded someone: the ‘I’ that must be defended, enlarged, and confirmed.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Mental (the ego-sense) · Ontic mode: Affliction — a kleśa (‘I-am-ness’) · Valence: Affliction (to attenuate; a working ‘I’ held lightly is its refined form) · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: First branch (the first growth from Avidyā) · Key relation: Branch of Avidyā; kin to Ahaṁkāra (Dim 3); parent to Rāga and Dveṣa.
coordinate.valence: Affliction (to attenuate; a working ‘I’ held lightly is its refined form)
coordinate.causal_status: First branch (the first growth from Avidyā)
coordinate.key_relation: Branch of Avidyā; kin to Ahaṁkāra (Dim 3); parent to Rāga and Dveṣa

balanced.label: In the refined state — a working ‘I’ held lightly

facets.overview: When Asmita is dissolved, consciousness recognizes itself as separate from its instruments. There is clear distinction between awareness and the contents of awareness.
facets.somatic: Body recognized as instrument, no identity-attachment, healthy use of body without identification, clarity in physical existence.
facets.emotional: Emotional freedom from defending an ego-identity, peace from non-identification, emotional liberation.
facets.cognitive: Pure awareness recognizing itself, freedom from mental identification, witness consciousness established.
facets.psychic: The soul wears the ‘I’ as a useful garment without being bound by it; the lesson — that the seer is not the instrument of seeing — is held in living balance.

balanced.text:
When Asmita is dissolved, consciousness recognizes itself as separate from its instruments. There is clear distinction between awareness and the contents of awareness.

Somatic: Body recognized as instrument, no identity-attachment, healthy use of body without identification, clarity in physical existence.

Emotional: Emotional freedom from defending an ego-identity, peace from non-identification, emotional liberation.

Cognitive: Pure awareness recognizing itself, freedom from mental identification, witness consciousness established.

Psychic: The soul wears the ‘I’ as a useful garment without being bound by it; the lesson — that the seer is not the instrument of seeing — is held in living balance.

excess.label: In excess — inflation, self-reference, the swollen self

facets.overview: Excessive Asmita is dominant ego-identification—the person is completely identified with personality, body, status, achievements. All other kleshas reinforce it.
facets.somatic: Body completely identified with as self, physical attachment to appearance and abilities, anxiety about body changes.
facets.emotional: Emotional defensive of ego, constant emotional reactivity to ego threats, suffering from ego-bruising.
facets.cognitive: Complete mental identification with personality, no witness awareness, all suffering reinforced by ego.
facets.psychic: The soul is eclipsed by the swollen self; the lesson at play is the quiet deflation of the separate ‘I,’ the recognition that what it defends so fiercely is not finally real.

excess.text:
Excessive Asmita is dominant ego-identification—the person is completely identified with personality, body, status, achievements. All other kleshas reinforce it.

Somatic: Body completely identified with as self, physical attachment to appearance and abilities, anxiety about body changes.

Emotional: Emotional defensive of ego, constant emotional reactivity to ego threats, suffering from ego-bruising.

Cognitive: Complete mental identification with personality, no witness awareness, all suffering reinforced by ego.

Psychic: The soul is eclipsed by the swollen self; the lesson at play is the quiet deflation of the separate ‘I,’ the recognition that what it defends so fiercely is not finally real.

deficient.label: Over-corrected — the functional ego dissolved before realization, undermining practical living

facets.overview: Healthy ego-function is necessary for practical living. Complete dissolution before realization is unstable; healthy ego serves discrimination.
facets.somatic: Inability to maintain healthy boundaries, physical vulnerability, inability to act in world.
facets.emotional: Identity confusion, inability to maintain healthy emotional boundaries, vulnerability to manipulation.
facets.cognitive: Confusion about self-identity, inability to function in world, premature dissolution without realization.
facets.psychic: The soul’s lesson is mis-applied — the working self undone before its time; the invitation is to a maturity that can hold a functioning ‘I’ even while knowing it is not ultimate.

deficient.text:
Healthy ego-function is necessary for practical living. Complete dissolution before realization is unstable; healthy ego serves discrimination.

Somatic: Inability to maintain healthy boundaries, physical vulnerability, inability to act in world.

Emotional: Identity confusion, inability to maintain healthy emotional boundaries, vulnerability to manipulation.

Cognitive: Confusion about self-identity, inability to function in world, premature dissolution without realization.

Psychic: The soul’s lesson is mis-applied — the working self undone before its time; the invitation is to a maturity that can hold a functioning ‘I’ even while knowing it is not ultimate.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: A refined asmitā still uses the word ‘I’ — it signs emails, keeps appointments, takes responsibility — but the sense of self is functional rather than absolute, a costume worn knowingly. In its inflated form it shows as the constant subtle scorekeeping of comparison, the need to be right, the hunger for recognition. In its over-corrected form — the working self prematurely talked away before it is genuinely transcended — it shows as an inability to hold healthy boundaries, make decisions, or function practically, the ordinary ego undermined before anything stable has taken its place.

additional_info.heading: A day in the life
additional_info.text: In a team meeting, Maya’s idea is improved upon by a colleague. Through inflated asmitā, the improvement lands as a wound — a flush of heat, a rehearsed rebuttal, the silent insistence that her version was better. Through refined asmitā, the same improvement lands as simply better, and there is an odd lightness in saying so out loud: the idea was never her, so its improvement costs her nothing.

attributes.title: Rāga — The Pull Toward Pleasure
attributes.aspect: राग   ·   Yoga Sūtra II.3, II.7 The nature of this affliction. Rāga is attachment that follows pleasure — the dwelling on past enjoyments and the leaning-forward to repeat them. It is not the enjoyment itself, which is innocent, but the residue enjoyment leaves: a track laid down in memory that pulls the mind back toward the source and quietly organizes future behavior around getting more.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital (attraction, craving) · Ontic mode: Affliction — a kleśa (attachment) · Valence: Affliction (craving to attenuate) · Guṇa: Rajas-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Branch (grows from Asmitā / Avidyā) · Key relation: Branch of Avidyā; antagonist-pair with Dveṣa; drives the pleasant pole of Valence (Dim 7).
coordinate.key_relation: Branch of Avidyā; antagonist-pair with Dveṣa; drives the pleasant pole of Valence (Dim 7)

balanced.label: In the refined state — enjoyment without the hook

facets.overview: When Raga is overcome, the yogi can experience pleasure without compulsive attachment to repeat it. There is engagement without bondage.
facets.somatic: Body can experience pleasure without compulsion, healthy enjoyment, freedom from sensory addiction, balanced engagement.
facets.emotional: Emotional freedom from compulsive seeking of pleasant feelings, ability to feel without grasping, peace.
facets.cognitive: Mental freedom from dwelling on pleasures, no compulsion to repeat experiences, clear thinking.
facets.psychic: The soul enjoys the play of the world as the play of the one Self, taking what comes without grasping; the lesson — that fullness is one’s nature, not a thing to be acquired — is being lived.

balanced.text:
When Raga is overcome, the yogi can experience pleasure without compulsive attachment to repeat it. There is engagement without bondage.

Somatic: Body can experience pleasure without compulsion, healthy enjoyment, freedom from sensory addiction, balanced engagement.

Emotional: Emotional freedom from compulsive seeking of pleasant feelings, ability to feel without grasping, peace.

Cognitive: Mental freedom from dwelling on pleasures, no compulsion to repeat experiences, clear thinking.

Psychic: The soul enjoys the play of the world as the play of the one Self, taking what comes without grasping; the lesson — that fullness is one’s nature, not a thing to be acquired — is being lived.

excess.label: In excess — craving, grasping, the never-enough

facets.overview: Excessive Raga creates addiction, compulsive seeking of pleasure, inability to stop pursuing what brought pleasure once, slavery to enjoyment.
facets.somatic: Addiction to pleasurable substances or activities, physical bondage to pleasure-objects, health damaged by pursuit.
facets.emotional: Emotional slavery to pleasurable feelings, addiction to emotional highs, emotional suffering from unrealized desire.
facets.cognitive: Obsessive mental focus on objects of pleasure, inability to think of anything else, mental bondage.
facets.psychic: The soul forgets its own completeness and seeks itself in objects; the lesson at play is the discovery that no outer thing can fill an inner fullness that was never actually missing.

excess.text:
Excessive Raga creates addiction, compulsive seeking of pleasure, inability to stop pursuing what brought pleasure once, slavery to enjoyment.

Somatic: Addiction to pleasurable substances or activities, physical bondage to pleasure-objects, health damaged by pursuit.

Emotional: Emotional slavery to pleasurable feelings, addiction to emotional highs, emotional suffering from unrealized desire.

Cognitive: Obsessive mental focus on objects of pleasure, inability to think of anything else, mental bondage.

Psychic: The soul forgets its own completeness and seeks itself in objects; the lesson at play is the discovery that no outer thing can fill an inner fullness that was never actually missing.

deficient.label: Over-suppressed — pleasure forcibly denied, hardening into joyless asceticism

facets.overview: Forced suppression of all attraction without wisdom creates harsh asceticism, denial of natural responses, eventual explosive release.
facets.somatic: Physical suppression of natural responses, body in conflict with itself, illness from forced denial.
facets.emotional: Emotional suppression and pretense of non-attachment, eventual emotional eruption, false coldness.
facets.cognitive: Mental suppression of natural attraction, eventual breakdown, distorted thinking from forced denial.
facets.psychic: The soul’s lesson is forced rather than understood; the invitation is to a dispassion that flowers from clear seeing, not from the violent denial of a still-active wanting.

deficient.text:
Forced suppression of all attraction without wisdom creates harsh asceticism, denial of natural responses, eventual explosive release.

Somatic: Physical suppression of natural responses, body in conflict with itself, illness from forced denial.

Emotional: Emotional suppression and pretense of non-attachment, eventual emotional eruption, false coldness.

Cognitive: Mental suppression of natural attraction, eventual breakdown, distorted thinking from forced denial.

Psychic: The soul’s lesson is forced rather than understood; the invitation is to a dispassion that flowers from clear seeing, not from the violent denial of a still-active wanting.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: With refined rāga, a person enjoys the good meal, the warm praise, the comfortable evening — fully — and then lets it pass without needing to bank it against future lack. In excess, the same pleasures become demands: the second drink, the next acquisition, the compulsive return to the thing that once delivered relief, each repetition delivering a little less while the reaching grows. Daily life narrows around the maintenance of supply.

additional_info.heading: A day in the life
additional_info.text: After the disappointing week, Maya orders the dress she has been eyeing. Through full-grown rāga, the click of ‘buy’ brings a real lift — followed within an hour by a flat, hungry feeling and the browser already open to the next thing; the pleasure was real and the relief did not last, and the gap is filled by reaching again. On a steadier day she buys the same dress, enjoys it plainly, and notices — without drama — that the wanting and the having were two different states, and that she is, underneath both, already alright.

attributes.title: Dveṣa — The Recoil from Pain
attributes.aspect: द्वेष   ·   Yoga Sūtra II.3, II.8 The nature of this affliction. Dveṣa is aversion that follows pain — the mirror image of rāga. It is the residue of past painful experience hardening into recoil: the dwelling on what hurt and the active avoidance of anything that resembles it. Where rāga reaches toward, dveṣa pushes away; together they form the two arms of the same grasping reflex.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital (aversion, recoil) · Ontic mode: Affliction — a kleśa (aversion) · Valence: Affliction (aversion to attenuate) · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Branch (grows from Asmitā / Avidyā) · Key relation: Branch of Avidyā; antagonist-pair with Rāga; drives the unpleasant pole of Valence (Dim 7).
coordinate.key_relation: Branch of Avidyā; antagonist-pair with Rāga; drives the unpleasant pole of Valence (Dim 7)

balanced.label: In the refined state — clear preference without contraction

facets.overview: When Dvesha is overcome, the yogi can encounter unpleasant experiences without reactive aversion. There is acceptance without compulsive avoidance.
facets.somatic: Body that can tolerate discomfort without dramatic reaction, healthy nervous system in face of unpleasantness.
facets.emotional: Emotional freedom from compulsive avoidance, acceptance of unpleasant feelings, emotional resilience.
facets.cognitive: Mental freedom from dwelling on past pain, no compulsion to avoid all discomfort, clear thinking about challenges.
facets.psychic: The soul meets the unwelcome without contraction, declining what does not serve while wishing harm to nothing; the lesson — that aversion is craving turned inside out — has been seen.

balanced.text:
When Dvesha is overcome, the yogi can encounter unpleasant experiences without reactive aversion. There is acceptance without compulsive avoidance.

Somatic: Body that can tolerate discomfort without dramatic reaction, healthy nervous system in face of unpleasantness.

Emotional: Emotional freedom from compulsive avoidance, acceptance of unpleasant feelings, emotional resilience.

Cognitive: Mental freedom from dwelling on past pain, no compulsion to avoid all discomfort, clear thinking about challenges.

Psychic: The soul meets the unwelcome without contraction, declining what does not serve while wishing harm to nothing; the lesson — that aversion is craving turned inside out — has been seen.

excess.label: In excess — hatred, blame, the hardened ‘no’

facets.overview: Excessive Dvesha creates chronic aversion, hatred, avoidance of growth-producing challenges, obsessive flight from unpleasantness.
facets.somatic: Avoidance of necessary physical challenges, stress from constant aversion, illness from chronic resistance.
facets.emotional: Chronic emotional aversion, hatred patterns, emotional suffering from inability to face difficulties.
facets.cognitive: Obsessive avoidance thinking, hatred and rejection patterns, mental contraction from aversion.
facets.psychic: The soul is bound to what it hates as tightly as to what it craves; the lesson at play is forgiveness — the release of the self from the prison of its own recoil.

excess.text:
Excessive Dvesha creates chronic aversion, hatred, avoidance of growth-producing challenges, obsessive flight from unpleasantness.

Somatic: Avoidance of necessary physical challenges, stress from constant aversion, illness from chronic resistance.

Emotional: Chronic emotional aversion, hatred patterns, emotional suffering from inability to face difficulties.

Cognitive: Obsessive avoidance thinking, hatred and rejection patterns, mental contraction from aversion.

Psychic: The soul is bound to what it hates as tightly as to what it craves; the lesson at play is forgiveness — the release of the self from the prison of its own recoil.

deficient.label: Over-corrected — no healthy aversion; unable to recognize or refuse genuine harm

facets.overview: Complete absence of aversion can lead to inability to recognize harm, accepting toxic conditions, no protective discrimination.
facets.somatic: Accepting physical harm without protective response, inability to recognize physical danger.
facets.emotional: Emotional inability to feel appropriate concern, accepting toxic relationships, no protective response.
facets.cognitive: Inability to discriminate against harmful conditions, mental acceptance of what should be avoided.
facets.psychic: The soul’s discernment is lost in a false, flattened equanimity; the invitation is to a clarity that can still say a clean ‘no’ to what genuinely harms.

deficient.text:
Complete absence of aversion can lead to inability to recognize harm, accepting toxic conditions, no protective discrimination.

Somatic: Accepting physical harm without protective response, inability to recognize physical danger.

Emotional: Emotional inability to feel appropriate concern, accepting toxic relationships, no protective response.

Cognitive: Inability to discriminate against harmful conditions, mental acceptance of what should be avoided.

Psychic: The soul’s discernment is lost in a false, flattened equanimity; the invitation is to a clarity that can still say a clean ‘no’ to what genuinely harms.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: Refined dveṣa allows a clean ‘no, thank you’ — a clear declining of what does not serve, without the heat of grievance. In excess it becomes resentment, fault-finding, the rehearsing of injuries, the conviction that certain people or experiences are simply wrong, and an avoidance that quietly shrinks the world. Over-corrected in the other direction — aversion suppressed entirely — a person loses the healthy ‘no’ altogether: unable to recognize what genuinely harms them, they tolerate toxic situations and people they would be wise to move away from.

additional_info.heading: A day in the life
additional_info.text: Maya runs into the colleague who got the promotion. Through full-grown dveṣa, her body tightens before a word is spoken — jaw, shoulders, a cool clipped voice — and the rest of the afternoon is spent silently re-litigating the unfairness. Through refined dveṣa, she registers the flare of dislike as a passing weather, greets him plainly, and finds the charge has already drained by the time she sits back down; the aversion arose and was simply not fed.

attributes.title: Abhiniveśa — The Clinging to Life
attributes.aspect: अभिनिवेश   ·   Yoga Sūtra II.3, II.9 The nature of this affliction. Abhiniveśa is the deep, instinctive clinging to continued existence — the will to live and the flinch from annihilation. Patañjali notes that it is firmly rooted even in the wise, and arises by its own nature. It is the most subtle and persistent of the afflictions because it underlies every other fear: every smaller dread is, at bottom, a rehearsal of this one.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Vital (the survival instinct, fear of death) · Ontic mode: Affliction — a kleśa (clinging to life) · Valence: Affliction (the death-grip to attenuate) · Guṇa: Tamas-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Deepest branch (the most tenacious, rooted even in the wise) · Key relation: Branch of Avidyā; kin to Jīveśana (Dim 12); the last kleśa to dissolve.
coordinate.seat: Physical–Vital (the survival instinct, fear of death)
coordinate.causal_status: Deepest branch (the most tenacious, rooted even in the wise)
coordinate.key_relation: Branch of Avidyā; kin to Jīveśana (Dim 12); the last kleśa to dissolve

balanced.label: In the refined state — life held without the death-grip

facets.overview: When Abhinivesha is dissolved through Self-realization, fear of death disappears because the Self is known to be deathless. There is profound peace with the prospect of death.
facets.somatic: Body without chronic death-fear, healthy nervous system, peace with mortality, freedom from anxiety about physical end.
facets.emotional: Profound peace with death, freedom from death-anxiety, emotional liberation from clinging.
facets.cognitive: Mind at peace with death, knowing Self as immortal, freedom from this most subtle klesha, deep stillness.
facets.psychic: The soul rests in what does not die, and so can hold the body’s mortality with grace; the lesson — that the witness was never born and cannot die — is ripening into realization.

balanced.text:
When Abhinivesha is dissolved through Self-realization, fear of death disappears because the Self is known to be deathless. There is profound peace with the prospect of death.

Somatic: Body without chronic death-fear, healthy nervous system, peace with mortality, freedom from anxiety about physical end.

Emotional: Profound peace with death, freedom from death-anxiety, emotional liberation from clinging.

Cognitive: Mind at peace with death, knowing Self as immortal, freedom from this most subtle klesha, deep stillness.

Psychic: The soul rests in what does not die, and so can hold the body’s mortality with grace; the lesson — that the witness was never born and cannot die — is ripening into realization.

excess.label: In excess — frantic self-preservation, control, the armored life

facets.overview: Extreme Abhinivesha creates phobic fear of death, anxiety disorders, hypochondria, obsessive concern with safety and life extension.
facets.somatic: Health anxiety, hypochondria, body in chronic fear-state, stress-related illness from death-fear.
facets.emotional: Chronic anxiety about death, phobic fear, emotional suffering from clinging, anxious gripping at life.
facets.cognitive: Obsessive thoughts about death and survival, mental contraction from fear, anxious planning to avoid death.
facets.psychic: The soul clings to the form it must one day release; the lesson at play is the deepest surrender, the loosening of the very root from which every smaller fear grows.

excess.text:
Extreme Abhinivesha creates phobic fear of death, anxiety disorders, hypochondria, obsessive concern with safety and life extension.

Somatic: Health anxiety, hypochondria, body in chronic fear-state, stress-related illness from death-fear.

Emotional: Chronic anxiety about death, phobic fear, emotional suffering from clinging, anxious gripping at life.

Cognitive: Obsessive thoughts about death and survival, mental contraction from fear, anxious planning to avoid death.

Psychic: The soul clings to the form it must one day release; the lesson at play is the deepest surrender, the loosening of the very root from which every smaller fear grows.

deficient.label: Over-corrected — the healthy life-instinct lost; recklessness and disregard for safety

facets.overview: False or premature absence of life-instinct can be suicidal ideation, reckless behavior, depression with death-wish—not genuine transcendence but pathology.
facets.somatic: Reckless physical behavior, inability to protect physical body, neglect of survival needs.
facets.emotional: Death-wish from depression, suicidal feelings (pathological not transcendent), emotional emptiness.
facets.cognitive: Reckless thinking, suicidal ideation, mental disregard for life, inappropriate detachment from survival.
facets.psychic: The soul’s lesson is counterfeited — the flight from fear mistaken for freedom from it; the invitation is to meet the fear of death consciously rather than to override the body’s own wisdom.

deficient.text:
False or premature absence of life-instinct can be suicidal ideation, reckless behavior, depression with death-wish—not genuine transcendence but pathology.

Somatic: Reckless physical behavior, inability to protect physical body, neglect of survival needs.

Emotional: Death-wish from depression, suicidal feelings (pathological not transcendent), emotional emptiness.

Cognitive: Reckless thinking, suicidal ideation, mental disregard for life, inappropriate detachment from survival.

Psychic: The soul’s lesson is counterfeited — the flight from fear mistaken for freedom from it; the invitation is to meet the fear of death consciously rather than to override the body’s own wisdom.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: Where abhiniveśa is loosened, a person can take real risks, speak uncomfortable truths, age and change and let go of phases of life with grace, because survival is not silently at stake in every choice. In excess it shows as over-control — of health, of money, of the future, of other people — a life slowly armored against a threat that can never be fully secured against, often with a low background hum of dread driving the compulsive checking. Over-corrected in the other direction — the healthy life-instinct itself suppressed or lost — it can show as recklessness, a careless disregard for one’s own safety, even the dark pull toward self-destruction.

additional_info.heading: A day in the life
additional_info.text: A routine health scare — an ambiguous test result, a week’s wait. Through full-grown abhiniveśa, Maya cannot sleep; the mind runs every catastrophe, the body floods with adrenaline at 3 a.m., and the waiting feels like standing at an edge. Through a more refined relationship to it, the fear still comes — it is, after all, rooted even in the wise — but she can feel it as a wave that rises and passes, can hold the genuine uncertainty without being swept off her feet, and can notice, in the quiet after the wave, an awareness that was never in danger at all.

attributes.title: Pramāṇa — Valid Cognition
attributes.aspect: प्रमाण   ·   Sutra 1.6, 1.7 The nature of this aspect. Pramana is right cognition—correct knowledge through direct perception, inference, or authoritative testimony. It is one of the five types of mental modifications (vrittis) that yoga seeks to still.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental (the mind’s valid knowing) · Ontic mode: Vṛtti — a mental modification (valid cognition) · Valence: Neutral faculty (useful; to be stilled, not scorned) · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant (clarity) · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Manifestation (perception, inference, and testimony) · Key relation: One of the five vṛttis — the sattvic mode; stilled in Citta-Vṛtti-Nirodha (Dim 11).
coordinate.valence: Neutral faculty (useful; to be stilled, not scorned)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the five vṛttis — the sattvic mode; stilled in Citta-Vṛtti-Nirodha (Dim 11)

balanced.label: Balanced — clear, accurate knowing

facets.overview: When Pramana operates clearly, the mind accurately knows what it perceives, infers, or learns from valid sources. Knowledge corresponds to reality.
facets.somatic: Accurate sensory perception, healthy sensory function, body that responds to actual reality, clear seeing.
facets.emotional: Emotional clarity from accurate perception, feelings based on reality not distortion, emotional truth.
facets.cognitive: Clear correct knowledge, accurate perception, sound reasoning, ability to learn from valid sources, mental clarity.
facets.psychic: The soul is well served by a mind that sees truly; valid knowing becomes a clear window onto the real, and discernment ripens toward viveka.

balanced.text:
When Pramana operates clearly, the mind accurately knows what it perceives, infers, or learns from valid sources. Knowledge corresponds to reality.

Somatic: Accurate sensory perception, healthy sensory function, body that responds to actual reality, clear seeing.

Emotional: Emotional clarity from accurate perception, feelings based on reality not distortion, emotional truth.

Cognitive: Clear correct knowledge, accurate perception, sound reasoning, ability to learn from valid sources, mental clarity.

Psychic: The soul is well served by a mind that sees truly; valid knowing becomes a clear window onto the real, and discernment ripens toward viveka.

excess.label: In excess — over-analysis, the tyranny of proof

facets.overview: Over-reliance on Pramana without spiritual practice can become intellectual pride, scientism, dismissal of subtler realities.
facets.somatic: Over-reliance on physical evidence, dismissal of subtle perception, materialist limitation.
facets.emotional: Cold intellectual approach, dismissal of emotional knowing, emotional coldness from rationalism.
facets.cognitive: Intellectual arrogance, dismissal of intuitive knowledge, scientism, mental rigidity about what's real.
facets.psychic: The faculty meant to serve the soul crowds it out — knowing hardened into the compulsion to be certain, the mind mistaking accumulated proof for wisdom.

excess.text:

Somatic: Over-reliance on physical evidence, dismissal of subtle perception, materialist limitation.

Emotional: Cold intellectual approach, dismissal of emotional knowing, emotional coldness from rationalism.

Cognitive: Intellectual arrogance, dismissal of intuitive knowledge, scientism, mental rigidity about what's real.

Psychic: The faculty meant to serve the soul crowds it out — knowing hardened into the compulsion to be certain, the mind mistaking accumulated proof for wisdom.

deficient.label: Deficient — poor reality-testing, credulity

facets.overview: Lack of valid knowledge means living based on misperception, fantasy, or unreliable sources. The other vrittis (especially Viparyaya - wrong knowledge) dominate.
facets.somatic: Acting on inaccurate perception, physical consequences of misperception, accidents.
facets.emotional: Feelings based on distortion, emotional suffering from incorrect knowing.
facets.cognitive: Living in wrong knowledge, mental confusion, susceptibility to deception, no foundation for wisdom.
facets.psychic: The soul is left without reliable footing; when valid cognition fails, trust in one’s own seeing erodes and the inner life drifts on fantasy and hearsay.

deficient.text:
Lack of valid knowledge means living based on misperception, fantasy, or unreliable sources. The other vrittis (especially Viparyaya - wrong knowledge) dominate.

Somatic: Acting on inaccurate perception, physical consequences of misperception, accidents.

Emotional: Feelings based on distortion, emotional suffering from incorrect knowing.

Cognitive: Living in wrong knowledge, mental confusion, susceptibility to deception, no foundation for wisdom.

Psychic: The soul is left without reliable footing; when valid cognition fails, trust in one’s own seeing erodes and the inner life drifts on fantasy and hearsay.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: In balance, pramāṇa shows as good judgment: a person who checks before believing, distinguishes what they actually know from what they assume, and updates when the evidence changes. In excess, it hardens into a need to verify everything, an inability to trust intuition or another person’s word, a life spent demanding proof until spontaneity and warmth are squeezed out. In deficiency, it collapses into credulity — believing too quickly, unable to tell a sound source from a flattering one, swept by whatever was said most confidently.

additional_info.heading: A day in the life
additional_info.text: A friend forwards Maya a startling health claim. Through over-active pramāṇa she cannot let it rest until she has read six studies, and the evening dissolves into tabs and counter-tabs. Through deficient pramāṇa she would simply forward it on, alarmed and convinced. In balance she notes the claim, registers that she doesn’t yet know, makes one quick check of a reliable source, and moves on — holding the question lightly instead of being held by it.

attributes.title: Viparyaya — Erroneous Cognition
attributes.aspect: विपर्यय   ·   Sutra 1.6, 1.8 The nature of this aspect. Viparyaya is wrong cognition—false knowledge not based on actual reality. Patanjali defines it as knowledge that is not founded on the true nature of its object.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental (the mind’s false knowing) · Ontic mode: Vṛtti — a mental modification (erroneous cognition) · Valence: Neutral faculty tending to affliction (misperception) · Guṇa: Tamas–Rajas (distortion) · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Manifestation (knowing that does not match the real) · Key relation: One of the five vṛttis; antagonist to Pramāṇa; the ground on which the kleśas (Dim 19) build.
coordinate.causal_status: Manifestation (knowing that does not match the real)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the five vṛttis; antagonist to Pramāṇa; the ground on which the kleśas (Dim 19) build

balanced.label: Balanced — error caught and corrected

facets.overview: Viparyaya is never positively desired. The balanced state is its absence—replacement by Pramana (correct knowledge) and discrimination.
facets.somatic: Accurate perception of physical reality, body acting on truth, healthy seeing.
facets.emotional: Emotional response to actual reality, freedom from misperception-based emotions.
facets.cognitive: Mind freed from false knowledge, replaced by correct cognition, clarity.
facets.psychic: The soul matures through the humility of catching its own errors; misperception seen and corrected becomes the very mechanism by which discrimination is born.

balanced.text:
Viparyaya is never positively desired. The balanced state is its absence—replacement by Pramana (correct knowledge) and discrimination.

Somatic: Accurate perception of physical reality, body acting on truth, healthy seeing.

Emotional: Emotional response to actual reality, freedom from misperception-based emotions.

Cognitive: Mind freed from false knowledge, replaced by correct cognition, clarity.

Psychic: The soul matures through the humility of catching its own errors; misperception seen and corrected becomes the very mechanism by which discrimination is born.

excess.label: In excess — confident, pervasive misperception

facets.overview: Excessive Viparyaya creates a life lived in fundamental error—conclusions drawn from misperceptions, mistakes compounding mistakes.
facets.somatic: Physical actions based on misperception, accidents, harm from wrong knowledge, body in confusion.
facets.emotional: Emotional suffering from wrong knowledge, feelings based on falsehoods, painful misunderstandings.
facets.cognitive: Mind entirely dominated by error, false beliefs, mental confusion, life lived in fundamental delusion.
facets.psychic: The soul lives inside a self-built distortion — here is the whole spiritual predicament in miniature: bondage is error mistaken for truth, the rope seen as a snake.

excess.text:
Excessive Viparyaya creates a life lived in fundamental error—conclusions drawn from misperceptions, mistakes compounding mistakes.

Somatic: Physical actions based on misperception, accidents, harm from wrong knowledge, body in confusion.

Emotional: Emotional suffering from wrong knowledge, feelings based on falsehoods, painful misunderstandings.

Cognitive: Mind entirely dominated by error, false beliefs, mental confusion, life lived in fundamental delusion.

Psychic: The soul lives inside a self-built distortion — here is the whole spiritual predicament in miniature: bondage is error mistaken for truth, the rope seen as a snake.

deficient.label: Deficient — (the rare clarity in which error seldom takes hold)

facets.overview: Absence of Viparyaya is desirable. The deficient state would be hypothetical—occurs when wisdom replaces error.
facets.somatic: Body responds to actual reality, no actions based on error.
facets.emotional: Emotions correspond to real conditions, no suffering from misperception.
facets.cognitive: Mind operating on truth, no false knowledge.
facets.psychic: The soul is paralyzed by the fear of being wrong, unable to trust any perception — discrimination collapsing into endless second-guessing.

deficient.text:
Absence of Viparyaya is desirable. The deficient state would be hypothetical—occurs when wisdom replaces error.

Somatic: Body responds to actual reality, no actions based on error.

Emotional: Emotions correspond to real conditions, no suffering from misperception.

Cognitive: Mind operating on truth, no false knowledge.

Psychic: The soul is paralyzed by the fear of being wrong, unable to trust any perception — discrimination collapsing into endless second-guessing.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: Viparyaya is the only vṛtti that is, by definition, a distortion; its ‘balanced’ state is therefore the swift recognition and correction of error rather than its absence. A person near balance notices the mistaken read — ‘I assumed she was angry; she was just tired’ — and revises. In excess, viparyaya runs the show: the rope is a snake, the neutral email is an attack, the silence is contempt, and the person acts on the misreading as if it were fact, often setting in motion exactly the conflict they imagined. Its rare deficiency is the seasoned clarity in which the mind so reliably checks itself that gross error finds little purchase.

additional_info.heading: A day in the life
additional_info.text: Maya’s manager passes her in the hall without a word. Through full-grown viparyaya the mind instantly knows: ‘He’s decided against me; this is the cold shoulder.’ The whole afternoon is lived inside that conclusion — the curt replies, the bracing — until she learns he was simply absorbed in a family emergency. The snake was always a rope. The cost was real anyway, paid in an afternoon of suffering built on nothing.

attributes.title: Vikalpa — Conceptual Imagination
attributes.aspect: विकल्प   ·   Sutra 1.6, 1.9 The nature of this aspect. Vikalpa is verbal construction/imagination—mental activity with no corresponding reality, based purely on words and concepts. It is fantasy and conceptual imagining.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental (verbal and conceptual construction) · Ontic mode: Vṛtti — a mental modification (imagination on words without an object) · Valence: Neutral faculty (language and vision; empty abstraction in excess) · Guṇa: Rajas–Sattva · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Manifestation (the mind building on words alone) · Key relation: One of the five vṛttis — the faculty of language, planning, and fantasy.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Vṛtti — a mental modification (imagination on words without an object)
coordinate.valence: Neutral faculty (language and vision; empty abstraction in excess)
coordinate.causal_status: Manifestation (the mind building on words alone)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the five vṛttis — the faculty of language, planning, and fantasy

balanced.label: Balanced — language, planning, creativity in service

facets.overview: When Vikalpa is recognized, the yogi can use imagination creatively without being deceived by it. There is clear awareness of what is real vs. imagined.
facets.somatic: Body free from acting on imagined fears or desires, present-reality response.
facets.emotional: Freedom from emotions based on imagination, emotions based on reality, healthy use of imagination for creativity.
facets.cognitive: Conscious use of imagination, clear distinction between concepts and reality, creative thinking when appropriate.
facets.psychic: The soul is served by imagination held lightly — language, planning, and creativity used as instruments without being mistaken for the real.

balanced.text:
When Vikalpa is recognized, the yogi can use imagination creatively without being deceived by it. There is clear awareness of what is real vs. imagined.

Somatic: Body free from acting on imagined fears or desires, present-reality response.

Emotional: Freedom from emotions based on imagination, emotions based on reality, healthy use of imagination for creativity.

Cognitive: Conscious use of imagination, clear distinction between concepts and reality, creative thinking when appropriate.

Psychic: The soul is served by imagination held lightly — language, planning, and creativity used as instruments without being mistaken for the real.

excess.label: In excess — rumination, fantasy, living in words

facets.overview: Excessive Vikalpa creates a life lived in fantasy—imagining experiences that haven't occurred, suffering or rejoicing in pure mental constructs, disconnection from reality.
facets.somatic: Physical responses to imagined situations (anxiety from imagined fears, etc.), body in fantasy-driven states.
facets.emotional: Emotional suffering from imagined scenarios, emotional life dominated by fantasy, anxiety about imagined futures.
facets.cognitive: Mind lost in imagination, inability to distinguish real from imagined, mental fantasy substituting for engagement.
facets.psychic: The soul is lost among words and concepts about reality rather than reality itself; the lesson is to notice when the map has quietly replaced the territory.

excess.text:
Excessive Vikalpa creates a life lived in fantasy—imagining experiences that haven't occurred, suffering or rejoicing in pure mental constructs, disconnection from reality.

Somatic: Physical responses to imagined situations (anxiety from imagined fears, etc.), body in fantasy-driven states.

Emotional: Emotional suffering from imagined scenarios, emotional life dominated by fantasy, anxiety about imagined futures.

Cognitive: Mind lost in imagination, inability to distinguish real from imagined, mental fantasy substituting for engagement.

Psychic: The soul is lost among words and concepts about reality rather than reality itself; the lesson is to notice when the map has quietly replaced the territory.

deficient.label: Deficient — concreteness without abstraction or vision

facets.overview: Complete absence of imagination would eliminate creative capacity, mental flexibility, planning ability. Some Vikalpa serves practical and creative life.
facets.somatic: Inability to envision physical possibilities, lack of physical creativity.
facets.emotional: Lack of emotional imagination, inability to envision possibilities, emotional flatness.
facets.cognitive: Lack of creative thinking, inability to plan or envision, rigid concrete thinking, no imagination.
facets.psychic: The soul’s capacity for vision and meaning is starved; without the play of conception, hope, planning, and creativity wither.

deficient.text:
Complete absence of imagination would eliminate creative capacity, mental flexibility, planning ability. Some Vikalpa serves practical and creative life.

Somatic: Inability to envision physical possibilities, lack of physical creativity.

Emotional: Lack of emotional imagination, inability to envision possibilities, emotional flatness.

Cognitive: Lack of creative thinking, inability to plan or envision, rigid concrete thinking, no imagination.

Psychic: The soul’s capacity for vision and meaning is starved; without the play of conception, hope, planning, and creativity wither.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: Vikalpa is the faculty of word-built mind — and it is not inherently a fault, for all planning, abstraction, art, and language run on it. In balance it is creativity and foresight: the ability to model a future, draft a plan, imagine a possibility. In excess it becomes the runaway commentary of rumination and fantasy — rehearsing conversations that will never happen, building elaborate catastrophes or daydreams with no object, mistaking the vividness of a thought for the reality of a thing. In deficiency, the capacity for abstraction thins: a person can be doggedly concrete, literal, and present, but without vision, metaphor, or the ability to plan beyond the visible.

additional_info.heading: A day in the life
additional_info.text: Lying awake, Maya conducts a flawless imaginary argument with her colleague — every line of his answered, every point won. The body tenses and the heart rate climbs as if the fight were happening; it is not, and it never will. This is vikalpa in excess: a whirl of pure words generating real adrenaline. In balance, she would catch the spin — ‘this is a conversation with no one’ — and let the imagined courtroom dissolve, freeing the same faculty to picture, instead, how she might simply ask him a real question tomorrow.

attributes.title: Nidrā — Sleep
attributes.aspect: निद्रा   ·   Sutra 1.6, 1.10 The nature of this aspect. Nidra is sleep—a mental modification of the absence of consciousness of objects. Patanjali defines it specifically as a state where the mind is supported by the cause of non-being.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Mental (the mode of dreamless rest) · Ontic mode: Vṛtti — a mental modification (sleep, a content of absence) · Valence: Neutral faculty (restorative; escape in excess) · Guṇa: Tamas-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Manifestation (the mind’s mode of dreamless rest) · Key relation: One of the five vṛttis — as a vṛtti (a mode of mind), distinct from Nidrā the transient wave (Dim 9) and the sleep state (Dim 4).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Vṛtti — a mental modification (sleep, a content of absence)
coordinate.causal_status: Manifestation (the mind’s mode of dreamless rest)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the five vṛttis — as a vṛtti (a mode of mind), distinct from Nidrā the transient wave (Dim 9) and the sleep state (Dim 4)

balanced.label: Balanced — deep, restorative rest

facets.overview: Balanced Nidra provides healthy restorative sleep. The yogi may also enter conscious sleep states (yogic sleep/yoga nidra) maintaining awareness through deep rest.
facets.somatic: Restorative sleep, healthy sleep cycles, body restored through sleep, balanced energy.
facets.emotional: Emotional restoration through sleep, freedom from sleep disturbance, emotional balance from rest.
facets.cognitive: Healthy sleep that restores mind, freedom from insomnia, mental refreshment, potentially conscious sleep states.
facets.psychic: In deep sleep the soul touches its own causal ground, returning to the formless rest from which renewal is drawn; being continues even in oblivion.

balanced.text:
Balanced Nidra provides healthy restorative sleep. The yogi may also enter conscious sleep states (yogic sleep/yoga nidra) maintaining awareness through deep rest.

Somatic: Restorative sleep, healthy sleep cycles, body restored through sleep, balanced energy.

Emotional: Emotional restoration through sleep, freedom from sleep disturbance, emotional balance from rest.

Cognitive: Healthy sleep that restores mind, freedom from insomnia, mental refreshment, potentially conscious sleep states.

Psychic: In deep sleep the soul touches its own causal ground, returning to the formless rest from which renewal is drawn; being continues even in oblivion.

excess.label: In excess — oversleep, dullness, escape into unconsciousness

facets.overview: Excessive Nidra (tamasic) creates oversleeping, lethargy, dullness, mental fog, sluggishness, escape into unconsciousness.
facets.somatic: Excessive sleep damaging health, lethargy, body weighed down, sluggish metabolism, inability to be active.
