facets.emotional: Emotional dullness, depression manifesting as oversleep, emotional escape into unconsciousness.
facets.cognitive: Mental dullness, fog, escape from thinking, mind dominated by tamas, no clarity.
facets.psychic: The soul hides in unconsciousness — sleep as escape, the tamasic retreat from the labor of waking awareness.

excess.text:
Excessive Nidra (tamasic) creates oversleeping, lethargy, dullness, mental fog, sluggishness, escape into unconsciousness.

Somatic: Excessive sleep damaging health, lethargy, body weighed down, sluggish metabolism, inability to be active.

Emotional: Emotional dullness, depression manifesting as oversleep, emotional escape into unconsciousness.

Cognitive: Mental dullness, fog, escape from thinking, mind dominated by tamas, no clarity.

Psychic: The soul hides in unconsciousness — sleep as escape, the tamasic retreat from the labor of waking awareness.

deficient.label: Deficient — insomnia, depletion, the mind that cannot let go

facets.overview: Insufficient Nidra (often from excessive rajas) creates insomnia, exhaustion, mental and physical breakdown, inability to function.
facets.somatic: Insomnia, exhaustion, body unable to repair, stress-related illness, accidents from fatigue, breakdown.
facets.emotional: Emotional reactivity from sleep deprivation, irritability, inability to regulate emotions.
facets.cognitive: Mental exhaustion, inability to think clearly, cognitive impairment, dangerous mental states from no rest.
facets.psychic: The soul is denied its nightly return to source; starved of rest, the whole system frays and the inner light dims.

deficient.text:
Insufficient Nidra (often from excessive rajas) creates insomnia, exhaustion, mental and physical breakdown, inability to function.

Somatic: Insomnia, exhaustion, body unable to repair, stress-related illness, accidents from fatigue, breakdown.

Emotional: Emotional reactivity from sleep deprivation, irritability, inability to regulate emotions.

Cognitive: Mental exhaustion, inability to think clearly, cognitive impairment, dangerous mental states from no rest.

Psychic: The soul is denied its nightly return to source; starved of rest, the whole system frays and the inner light dims.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: Patañjali’s subtle point is that sleep is itself a vṛtti — a modification whose object is the absence of objects — which is why we can remember having slept well. In balance, nidrā is healthy rest that restores body and mind and even, for the yogi, becomes a doorway to studying consciousness itself. In excess, it slides into oversleeping, daytime heaviness, and the use of unconsciousness as escape — the nap that is really an avoidance, the tamasic fog. In deficiency, it fails: insomnia, the exhausted body that cannot power down, the mind too gripped by the day’s other vṛttis to release into rest.

additional_info.heading: A day in the life
additional_info.text: After the imaginary argument, Maya cannot sleep — deficient nidrā, the mind’s other ripples too active to let the lake go still. On a different week, low and discouraged, she sleeps ten hours and still wakes heavy, using the dark as a place to not-be — nidrā in excess. In balance, sleep simply comes when the body is tired and the mind has set its commentary down, and she wakes restored, with the faint clean sense of having been, for a while, gone.

attributes.title: Smṛti — Memory
attributes.aspect: स्मृति   ·   Sutra 1.6, 1.11 The nature of this aspect. Smriti is memory—the retention of experienced objects. As a vritti, it is the mental modification that brings forth past experiences. In yoga, it has both ordinary and yogic dimensions.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental (the return of past experience) · Ontic mode: Vṛtti — a mental modification (memory) · Valence: Neutral faculty (recall; rumination in excess) · Guṇa: Sattva (clear recall), Rajas (rumination) · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Manifestation (the retention and return of experience) · Key relation: One of the five vṛttis — the same faculty as Smṛti the wave (Dim 9); the vehicle of saṁskāra-recall.
coordinate.causal_status: Manifestation (the retention and return of experience)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the five vṛttis — the same faculty as Smṛti the wave (Dim 9); the vehicle of saṁskāra-recall

balanced.label: Balanced — recall in service of learning and continuity

facets.overview: Healthy Smriti supports learning, wisdom, and practice. Patanjali also uses Smriti as a key practice—holding the object steadily in awareness, which is foundational to meditation.
facets.somatic: Healthy memory function, body memory supporting skills, sensory memory aiding life.
facets.emotional: Useful emotional memory aiding wisdom, healthy retention of experiences, learning from past.
facets.cognitive: Excellent memory function, learning capacity, accumulated wisdom, mental ability to hold objects in attention.
facets.psychic: The soul is served by memory that carries learning forward without binding; remembrance becomes the thread of a coherent life and, refined, the recollection of one’s own deeper nature.

balanced.text:
Healthy Smriti supports learning, wisdom, and practice. Patanjali also uses Smriti as a key practice—holding the object steadily in awareness, which is foundational to meditation.

Somatic: Healthy memory function, body memory supporting skills, sensory memory aiding life.

Emotional: Useful emotional memory aiding wisdom, healthy retention of experiences, learning from past.

Cognitive: Excellent memory function, learning capacity, accumulated wisdom, mental ability to hold objects in attention.

Psychic: The soul is served by memory that carries learning forward without binding; remembrance becomes the thread of a coherent life and, refined, the recollection of one’s own deeper nature.

excess.label: In excess — the past replaying, rumination over old wounds

facets.overview: Excessive Smriti becomes dwelling in past, inability to be present, traumatic memory loops, obsessive recall of negative experiences.
facets.somatic: Body holding trauma memories, somatic re-experiencing, physical responses to memory.
facets.emotional: Emotional dwelling in past pain or pleasure, trauma loops, inability to release past, emotional bondage to memory.
facets.cognitive: Obsessive mental rumination, mind trapped in past, inability to be present, memory-based suffering.
facets.psychic: The soul is held captive by the past — memory as rumination, the replaying of what was, samskaras grooving ever deeper.

excess.text:
Excessive Smriti becomes dwelling in past, inability to be present, traumatic memory loops, obsessive recall of negative experiences.

Somatic: Body holding trauma memories, somatic re-experiencing, physical responses to memory.

Emotional: Emotional dwelling in past pain or pleasure, trauma loops, inability to release past, emotional bondage to memory.

Cognitive: Obsessive mental rumination, mind trapped in past, inability to be present, memory-based suffering.

Psychic: The soul is held captive by the past — memory as rumination, the replaying of what was, samskaras grooving ever deeper.

deficient.label: Deficient — forgetfulness, lost continuity, lost lessons
Deficient Smriti creates inability to learn from experience, forgetfulness, no accumulated wisdom, repeated mistakes.

facets.somatic: Memory dysfunction, inability to remember physical skills or routines, dementia.
facets.emotional: Inability to learn from emotional experience, repeating emotional mistakes, no emotional wisdom.
facets.cognitive: Forgetfulness, inability to retain learning, no wisdom accumulation, cognitive impairment.
facets.psychic: The soul loses its thread of continuity; without memory’s service, learning cannot accumulate and identity itself frays.

deficient.text:
Somatic: Memory dysfunction, inability to remember physical skills or routines, dementia.

Emotional: Inability to learn from emotional experience, repeating emotional mistakes, no emotional wisdom.

Cognitive: Forgetfulness, inability to retain learning, no wisdom accumulation, cognitive impairment.

Psychic: The soul loses its thread of continuity; without memory’s service, learning cannot accumulate and identity itself frays.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: Smṛti is the re-presentation of past impressions — the faculty that gives life continuity and makes learning possible. In balance, it serves: a lesson recalled at the right moment, a face remembered, a skill retained, and — crucially for the yogi — the steady remembering of one’s own deepest intention. In excess, memory becomes a loop: old slights, regrets, and griefs replayed until the present is lived through the filter of the past, the wound rehearsed until it never heals. In deficiency, continuity frays — forgetfulness, the lost thread, lessons that do not stick, the same mistake made again because its cost was not retained.

additional_info.heading: A day in the life
additional_info.text: The colleague’s promotion stirs an older memory — a time years ago when Maya was overlooked — and through excess smṛti the two fuse, the present disappointment swelling with the full charge of the past, so that she is no longer responding to today but to a decade of accumulated slight. In balance, the old memory arises, is recognized as old, and is even useful — ‘I have survived this before’ — informing the present without flooding it. Same memory; in one case a chain, in the other a teacher.

attributes.title: Vyādhi — Disease
attributes.aspect: व्याधि   ·   Sutra 1.30 The nature of this aspect. Vyadhi is disease or illness—the first of nine obstacles Patanjali names that distract the mind from yoga. Physical illness disturbs mental focus and impedes practice.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical (bodily distress) · Ontic mode: Obstacle — an antarāya (disease) · Valence: Affliction / obstacle (to remedy) · Guṇa: Tamas-tinged · Scale: Individual, with environmental cause · Causal status: Obstruction (bodily distress that stalls practice) · Key relation: One of the nine antarāyas; same as Vyādhi the wave (Dim 9); undermines the Annamaya ground.
coordinate.key_relation: One of the nine antarāyas; same as Vyādhi the wave (Dim 9); undermines the Annamaya ground

balanced.label: Balanced — health as the ground of practice

facets.overview: When the body is healthy, it serves as a fit vehicle for yoga practice. Vyadhi is overcome through proper care, lifestyle, and yogic practices.
facets.somatic: Healthy disease-free body, strong immune function, vitality, balanced physiology, body fit for practice.
facets.emotional: Emotional stability supported by physical health, freedom from disease-related distress.
facets.cognitive: Mental clarity from healthy body, focus undisturbed by physical complaints, capacity for practice.
facets.psychic: The soul has a fit vehicle; bodily health, neither clung to nor neglected, becomes a quiet support for the inner work rather than a distraction from it.

balanced.text:
When the body is healthy, it serves as a fit vehicle for yoga practice. Vyadhi is overcome through proper care, lifestyle, and yogic practices.

Somatic: Healthy disease-free body, strong immune function, vitality, balanced physiology, body fit for practice.

Emotional: Emotional stability supported by physical health, freedom from disease-related distress.

Cognitive: Mental clarity from healthy body, focus undisturbed by physical complaints, capacity for practice.

Psychic: The soul has a fit vehicle; bodily health, neither clung to nor neglected, becomes a quiet support for the inner work rather than a distraction from it.

excess.label: In excess — health-anxiety and hypochondria; identification with symptoms

facets.overview: Obsession with illness (excessive focus) becomes hypochondria, health anxiety, identification with sickness, life dominated by illness narrative.
facets.somatic: Hypochondria, exaggerated physical symptoms, somatization, illness identity becoming dominant.
facets.emotional: Emotional identification with illness, victim mentality around disease, anxiety about health.
facets.cognitive: Obsessive mental focus on health, illness anxiety, mental dominance by disease concerns.
facets.psychic: The soul is eclipsed by preoccupation with the body; health-anxiety turns the instrument into the obsession, the fixation itself a subtle bondage.

excess.text:
Obsession with illness (excessive focus) becomes hypochondria, health anxiety, identification with sickness, life dominated by illness narrative.

Somatic: Hypochondria, exaggerated physical symptoms, somatization, illness identity becoming dominant.

Emotional: Emotional identification with illness, victim mentality around disease, anxiety about health.

Cognitive: Obsessive mental focus on health, illness anxiety, mental dominance by disease concerns.

Psychic: The soul is eclipsed by preoccupation with the body; health-anxiety turns the instrument into the obsession, the fixation itself a subtle bondage.

deficient.label: When present — actual disease disrupting practice and focus

facets.overview: Vyadhi as obstacle means disease is interfering with practice. When fully present, it creates inability to do yoga, distraction, suffering.
facets.somatic: Active disease, body unable to practice, physical suffering, illness disrupting daily life.
facets.emotional: Emotional distress from illness, depression from sickness, frustration with body.
facets.cognitive: Mental distraction by physical suffering, inability to focus on practice, mind dominated by symptoms.
facets.psychic: The soul is asked to find its ground beyond the body precisely when the body fails; illness becomes a wall or a teacher depending on whether one identifies with the ailing form or witnesses it.

deficient.text:
Vyadhi as obstacle means disease is interfering with practice. When fully present, it creates inability to do yoga, distraction, suffering.

Somatic: Active disease, body unable to practice, physical suffering, illness disrupting daily life.

Emotional: Emotional distress from illness, depression from sickness, frustration with body.

Cognitive: Mental distraction by physical suffering, inability to focus on practice, mind dominated by symptoms.

Psychic: The soul is asked to find its ground beyond the body precisely when the body fails; illness becomes a wall or a teacher depending on whether one identifies with the ailing form or witnesses it.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: A balanced relationship to vyādhi treats the body as the vehicle of all practice — tended, rested, fed, and moved well enough to be forgotten. When illness genuinely strikes, it disturbs focus directly; and a mind already caught in obstacle can also become preoccupied with the body, magnifying small symptoms into a full-time concern. Its neglect-pole is the practitioner who pushes the body past its limits in the name of discipline until it breaks down and stops practice altogether.

additional_info.heading: A day in the life
additional_info.text: A bad cold arrives the week Maya finally established a morning sit. The congestion, the broken sleep, the low-grade ache make the cushion feel impossible, and within days the habit is gone — not abandoned by choice but dissolved by the body’s disturbance. Seeing it as vyādhi rather than as personal failure, she lets the practice shrink to two honest minutes a day until the body returns.

attributes.title: Styāna — Mental Dullness
attributes.aspect: स्त्यान   ·   Sutra 1.30 The nature of this aspect. Styana is mental dullness or apathy—a state of mental sluggishness where there is no enthusiasm for practice. It is a tamasic obstruction.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental (heaviness of mind) · Ontic mode: Obstacle — an antarāya (mental dullness) · Valence: Affliction / obstacle · Guṇa: Tamas-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Obstruction (dullness that saps the will to practice) · Key relation: One of the nine antarāyas; kin to Ālasya and the tamasic vṛttis.
coordinate.causal_status: Obstruction (dullness that saps the will to practice)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the nine antarāyas; kin to Ālasya and the tamasic vṛttis

balanced.label: Balanced — a clear, willing, alert mind

facets.overview: When Styana is overcome, the mind is alert and enthusiastic for practice. There is mental vitality and engagement.
facets.somatic: Alert physical presence, vital energy, body that supports active engagement, healthy nervous system.
facets.emotional: Enthusiasm and emotional engagement, joy in practice, emotional vitality.
facets.cognitive: Mental alertness, enthusiasm for yoga, sharp clarity, engaged thinking.
facets.psychic: The soul’s light reaches the surface through a clear, willing mind; enthusiasm for practice is itself a sign that the inner fire still burns.

balanced.text:
When Styana is overcome, the mind is alert and enthusiastic for practice. There is mental vitality and engagement.

Somatic: Alert physical presence, vital energy, body that supports active engagement, healthy nervous system.

Emotional: Enthusiasm and emotional engagement, joy in practice, emotional vitality.

Cognitive: Mental alertness, enthusiasm for yoga, sharp clarity, engaged thinking.

Psychic: The soul’s light reaches the surface through a clear, willing mind; enthusiasm for practice is itself a sign that the inner fire still burns.

excess.label: In excess — fog, apathy, no enthusiasm for practice

facets.overview: Excessive (and prolonged) Styana becomes chronic apathy, depression-like state, complete loss of motivation for practice or life.
facets.somatic: Severe lethargy, body weighed down by inertia, lack of physical energy, depression-like physical state.
facets.emotional: Emotional flatness, depression, loss of all enthusiasm, emotional dullness, no joy.
facets.cognitive: Severe mental fog, complete apathy, inability to think clearly, dulled cognition.
facets.psychic: The soul is smothered under tamas; chronic dullness is the heaviness that makes even the longing for freedom feel impossibly distant.

excess.text:
Excessive (and prolonged) Styana becomes chronic apathy, depression-like state, complete loss of motivation for practice or life.

Somatic: Severe lethargy, body weighed down by inertia, lack of physical energy, depression-like physical state.

Emotional: Emotional flatness, depression, loss of all enthusiasm, emotional dullness, no joy.

Cognitive: Severe mental fog, complete apathy, inability to think clearly, dulled cognition.

Psychic: The soul is smothered under tamas; chronic dullness is the heaviness that makes even the longing for freedom feel impossibly distant.

deficient.label: Deficient — (the keen alertness in which dullness finds no foothold)

facets.overview: Styana as obstacle means dullness present. When absent, the mind is alert—this is the desired state.
facets.somatic: Alert body, vital energy, healthy responsiveness.
facets.emotional: Engaged emotions, enthusiasm, joy.
facets.cognitive: Sharp clear mind, alert thinking, enthusiastic engagement.
facets.psychic: The dullness lifts entirely — the keen, awake mind in which apathy finds no foothold, which is precisely the clarity practice exists to restore.

deficient.text:
Styana as obstacle means dullness present. When absent, the mind is alert—this is the desired state.

Somatic: Alert body, vital energy, healthy responsiveness.

Emotional: Engaged emotions, enthusiasm, joy.

Cognitive: Sharp clear mind, alert thinking, enthusiastic engagement.

Psychic: The dullness lifts entirely — the keen, awake mind in which apathy finds no foothold, which is precisely the clarity practice exists to restore.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: Styāna is a dullness specifically of the mind — the mental enthusiasm gone flat, the sense that one simply cannot be bothered to engage, even where the body is willing. It is a tamasic fog over the intellect. In its grip, practice feels pointless not because of any argument against it but because the very appetite for it has drained away. The remedy is rarely more force; it is usually a change of energy — light, breath, movement — to lift the fog enough for willingness to return.

additional_info.heading: A day in the life
additional_info.text: Maya sits down to meditate and finds nothing there — no resistance exactly, just a grey flatness, a mind like wet wool. Five minutes feel like a chore with no flavor. This is styāna: the dullness that makes the most worthwhile thing feel like the most tedious. She opens a window, takes ten brisker breaths, and the fog thins enough that one real minute of attention becomes possible.

attributes.title: Saṁśaya — Corrosive Doubt
attributes.aspect: संशय   ·   Sutra 1.30 The nature of this aspect. Samshaya is doubt that undermines practice. Not healthy questioning, but corrosive doubt that prevents commitment and progress on the path.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental (wavering conviction) · Ontic mode: Obstacle — an antarāya (corrosive doubt) · Valence: Affliction / obstacle · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Obstruction (doubt that undermines commitment) · Key relation: One of the nine antarāyas; the corrosive form of Vitarka’s deliberation (Dim 9); dissolved by resolve and faith.
coordinate.key_relation: One of the nine antarāyas; the corrosive form of Vitarka’s deliberation (Dim 9); dissolved by resolve and faith

balanced.label: Balanced — honest questioning that seeks truth

facets.overview: When Samshaya is overcome through faith (shraddha) and experience, the yogi practices with conviction. Healthy questioning remains, but corrosive doubt is gone.
facets.somatic: Body practiced with conviction, healthy commitment to practice, physical confidence.
facets.emotional: Emotional confidence in path, faith from experience, freedom from corrosive doubt.
facets.cognitive: Mental conviction supporting practice, clarity about path, healthy critical thinking without paralysis.
facets.psychic: The soul questions honestly and then trusts; doubt resolved into conviction frees the energy that endless skepticism would consume.

balanced.text:
When Samshaya is overcome through faith (shraddha) and experience, the yogi practices with conviction. Healthy questioning remains, but corrosive doubt is gone.

Somatic: Body practiced with conviction, healthy commitment to practice, physical confidence.

Emotional: Emotional confidence in path, faith from experience, freedom from corrosive doubt.

Cognitive: Mental conviction supporting practice, clarity about path, healthy critical thinking without paralysis.

Psychic: The soul questions honestly and then trusts; doubt resolved into conviction frees the energy that endless skepticism would consume.

excess.label: In excess — doubt that paralyzes commitment

facets.overview: Excessive Samshaya becomes chronic doubt that undermines all action, paralysis through skepticism, inability to commit to anything.
facets.somatic: Inability to sustain practice, body in inconsistent action, no commitment to physical routines.
facets.emotional: Emotional instability from doubt, inability to trust feelings, anxiety from uncertainty.
facets.cognitive: Paralyzing skepticism, mental inability to commit, constant questioning that prevents action.
facets.psychic: The soul is paralyzed at the threshold; chronic doubt undermines every step, the faculty meant to discern truth refusing to let it land.

excess.text:
Excessive Samshaya becomes chronic doubt that undermines all action, paralysis through skepticism, inability to commit to anything.

Somatic: Inability to sustain practice, body in inconsistent action, no commitment to physical routines.

Emotional: Emotional instability from doubt, inability to trust feelings, anxiety from uncertainty.

Cognitive: Paralyzing skepticism, mental inability to commit, constant questioning that prevents action.

Psychic: The soul is paralyzed at the threshold; chronic doubt undermines every step, the faculty meant to discern truth refusing to let it land.

deficient.label: Deficient — credulous certainty that never examines

facets.overview: Complete absence of all doubt becomes blind faith, gullibility, inability to discriminate, vulnerability to false teachers and ideas.
facets.somatic: Blind following of harmful practices, physical danger from no discrimination.
facets.emotional: Emotional gullibility, vulnerability to manipulation, blind devotion to wrong figures.
facets.cognitive: Blind faith, gullibility, absence of healthy critical thinking, vulnerability to deception.
facets.psychic: Doubt collapses into blind credulity; without the healthy check of discernment, the soul becomes prey to whatever certainty is offered it.

deficient.text:
Complete absence of all doubt becomes blind faith, gullibility, inability to discriminate, vulnerability to false teachers and ideas.

Somatic: Blind following of harmful practices, physical danger from no discrimination.

Emotional: Emotional gullibility, vulnerability to manipulation, blind devotion to wrong figures.

Cognitive: Blind faith, gullibility, absence of healthy critical thinking, vulnerability to deception.

Psychic: Doubt collapses into blind credulity; without the healthy check of discernment, the soul becomes prey to whatever certainty is offered it.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: Patañjali’s saṁśaya is not the healthy inquiry that strengthens understanding — that is welcome — but the corrosive doubt that undermines commitment: ‘Is this even working? Is this path real? Am I wasting my time?’ asked not to find an answer but in a way that quietly dissolves the will to continue. In balance, doubt is a question one actually pursues to resolution. In excess, it is a permanent hover that never lets practice take root. Its opposite pole, deficient doubt, is the credulity that swallows any teaching whole and never tests it.

additional_info.heading: A day in the life
additional_info.text: Three weeks into the new habit, with no dramatic results, the thought arrives: ‘This probably doesn’t do anything anyway.’ It is reasonable-sounding and quietly fatal — and the next morning’s sit is skipped on its authority. Recognizing it as saṁśaya, Maya neither believes it nor argues with it; she simply notes ‘doubt is present’ and keeps the appointment with herself anyway, letting results, if any, accumulate before she judges.

attributes.title: Pramāda — Carelessness
attributes.aspect: प्रमाद   ·   Sutra 1.30 The nature of this aspect. Pramada is carelessness or negligence—lack of awareness and attention in practice. It is a moment-to-moment forgetting of yogic awareness.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Vital (heedlessness) · Ontic mode: Obstacle — an antarāya (carelessness) · Valence: Affliction / obstacle · Guṇa: Tamas–Rajas · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Obstruction (inattention that lets practice lapse) · Key relation: One of the nine antarāyas; antagonist to the vigilance of mindful attention.
coordinate.key_relation: One of the nine antarāyas; antagonist to the vigilance of mindful attention

balanced.label: Balanced — sustained, vigilant attention

facets.overview: When Pramada is overcome, the yogi maintains constant mindful awareness. There is steady attention without forgetting one's purpose and practice.
facets.somatic: Mindful physical presence, careful attention to body, healthy alertness, careful actions.
facets.emotional: Emotional mindfulness, careful attention to feelings, freedom from emotional carelessness.
facets.cognitive: Constant mental awareness, careful attention, mindfulness maintained, no negligence.
facets.psychic: The soul keeps its thread; steady mindful attention is the continuity through which all practice deepens.

balanced.text:
When Pramada is overcome, the yogi maintains constant mindful awareness. There is steady attention without forgetting one's purpose and practice.

Somatic: Mindful physical presence, careful attention to body, healthy alertness, careful actions.

Emotional: Emotional mindfulness, careful attention to feelings, freedom from emotional carelessness.

Cognitive: Constant mental awareness, careful attention, mindfulness maintained, no negligence.

Psychic: The soul keeps its thread; steady mindful attention is the continuity through which all practice deepens.

excess.label: In excess — chronic inattention, the dropped thread

facets.overview: Excessive Pramada is habitual carelessness—forgetting practice constantly, never coming back to awareness, mindlessness as a way of life.
facets.somatic: Careless physical action, accidents, neglect of body, mindless living.
facets.emotional: Emotional carelessness, hurting others through negligence, emotional mindlessness.
facets.cognitive: Chronic mindlessness, no attention, mind always wandering, no return to awareness.
facets.psychic: The soul keeps forgetting itself; carelessness lets hard-won awareness slip again and again, the thread dropped almost as soon as it is picked up.

excess.text:
Excessive Pramada is habitual carelessness—forgetting practice constantly, never coming back to awareness, mindlessness as a way of life.

Somatic: Careless physical action, accidents, neglect of body, mindless living.

Emotional: Emotional carelessness, hurting others through negligence, emotional mindlessness.

Cognitive: Chronic mindlessness, no attention, mind always wandering, no return to awareness.

Psychic: The soul keeps forgetting itself; carelessness lets hard-won awareness slip again and again, the thread dropped almost as soon as it is picked up.

deficient.label: Over-corrected — hypervigilance and anxiety from relentless self-monitoring, the inability to ever relax

facets.overview: Excessive vigilance without rest becomes hypervigilance, anxiety, inability to ever relax. Some natural attention/inattention rhythm is healthy.
facets.somatic: Hypervigilance creating tension, inability to relax, stress-related illness.
facets.emotional: Anxious over-attention, inability to be at ease, emotional rigidity from constant vigilance.
facets.cognitive: Hypervigilant mind, anxiety, inability to rest mental attention, exhaustion from constant focus.
facets.psychic: Vigilance hardens into hypervigilance; terrified of slipping, the soul monitors itself so relentlessly that it can never rest in the ease that is the point.

deficient.text:
Excessive vigilance without rest becomes hypervigilance, anxiety, inability to ever relax. Some natural attention/inattention rhythm is healthy.

Somatic: Hypervigilance creating tension, inability to relax, stress-related illness.

Emotional: Anxious over-attention, inability to be at ease, emotional rigidity from constant vigilance.

Cognitive: Hypervigilant mind, anxiety, inability to rest mental attention, exhaustion from constant focus.

Psychic: Vigilance hardens into hypervigilance; terrified of slipping, the soul monitors itself so relentlessly that it can never rest in the ease that is the point.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: Pramāda is the moment-to-moment lapse of awareness — not a grand failure but the small, repeated forgetting of one’s own intention. It is autopilot: the meal eaten without tasting, the resolution forgotten by mid-morning, the practice that drifts because attention simply wandered off and was not brought back. In balance, attention is sustained and self-recovering; the mind wanders and returns. In excess, the thread is dropped and not picked up, so that whole stretches of life and practice pass unwitnessed.

additional_info.heading: A day in the life
additional_info.text: Maya resolves, sincerely, to pause and breathe before reacting to her colleague. By the time the colleague speaks, the resolution has vanished entirely — she is three sentences into a sharp reply before she even remembers she meant to pause. This is pramāda: not malice, just the dropped thread. Naming it, she treats each forgetting not as defeat but as the very moment to begin again — which is, in fact, the whole of the practice.

attributes.title: Ālasya — Sloth
attributes.aspect: आलस्य   ·   Sutra 1.30 The nature of this aspect. Alasya is physical and mental laziness—lack of effort, sloth, postponing practice. It is a tamasic obstacle related to but distinct from Styana (mental dullness specifically).

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Vital (inertia, disinclination to effort) · Ontic mode: Obstacle — an antarāya (sloth) · Valence: Affliction / obstacle · Guṇa: Tamas-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Obstruction (inertia that resists effort) · Key relation: One of the nine antarāyas; same as Ālasya the wave (Dim 9); kin to Styāna.
coordinate.key_relation: One of the nine antarāyas; same as Ālasya the wave (Dim 9); kin to Styāna

balanced.label: Balanced — willing, ready energy for what matters

facets.overview: When Alasya is overcome, the yogi practices with appropriate effort and consistency. Energy is available for sustained engagement.
facets.somatic: Available energy for practice, body that responds to effort, vitality, sustained physical engagement.
facets.emotional: Emotional motivation, willingness to make effort, enthusiasm.
facets.cognitive: Mental discipline, ability to exert effort, sustained focus through challenges.
facets.psychic: The soul’s energy is available for what matters; willing, ready effort is itself a form of inner aliveness.

balanced.text:
When Alasya is overcome, the yogi practices with appropriate effort and consistency. Energy is available for sustained engagement.

Somatic: Available energy for practice, body that responds to effort, vitality, sustained physical engagement.

Emotional: Emotional motivation, willingness to make effort, enthusiasm.

Cognitive: Mental discipline, ability to exert effort, sustained focus through challenges.

Psychic: The soul’s energy is available for what matters; willing, ready effort is itself a form of inner aliveness.

excess.label: In excess — heaviness, postponement, the will that won’t move
Excessive Alasya is chronic laziness, inability to act, postponement of everything, life of inactivity, sloth.

facets.somatic: Severe physical laziness, body weighed down, refusing all effort, deteriorating from inactivity.
facets.emotional: Emotional unwillingness to do anything, motivational paralysis, apathy.
facets.cognitive: Mental laziness, refusing to think, postponing all mental effort, chronic procrastination.
facets.psychic: The soul sinks into heaviness; sloth is the will that knows the good and cannot move toward it, the postponement that quietly becomes a life.

excess.text:
Somatic: Severe physical laziness, body weighed down, refusing all effort, deteriorating from inactivity.

Emotional: Emotional unwillingness to do anything, motivational paralysis, apathy.

Cognitive: Mental laziness, refusing to think, postponing all mental effort, chronic procrastination.

Psychic: The soul sinks into heaviness; sloth is the will that knows the good and cannot move toward it, the postponement that quietly becomes a life.

deficient.label: Deficient — restless over-activity that cannot rest

facets.overview: Total absence of any rest (false absence of laziness) becomes burnout, overwork, inability to honor body's need for rest.
facets.somatic: Burnout from no rest, exhaustion, overwork damaging body.
facets.emotional: Emotional exhaustion from no break, anxiety about resting, emotional overload.
facets.cognitive: Mental burnout, inability to rest mind, exhaustion from constant effort.
facets.psychic: The pendulum swings to driven restlessness; refusing all rest, the soul burns itself out and mistakes exhaustion for diligence.

deficient.text:
Total absence of any rest (false absence of laziness) becomes burnout, overwork, inability to honor body's need for rest.

Somatic: Burnout from no rest, exhaustion, overwork damaging body.

Emotional: Emotional exhaustion from no break, anxiety about resting, emotional overload.

Cognitive: Mental burnout, inability to rest mind, exhaustion from constant effort.

Psychic: The pendulum swings to driven restlessness; refusing all rest, the soul burns itself out and mistakes exhaustion for diligence.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: Ālasya is the heaviness of body and will — the sloth that postpones, that finds the cushion too far and the morning too early, that always intends to begin tomorrow. Where styāna is a dullness of mind, ālasya is a leadenness of will and limb. In balance, energy is available for what matters and rest for what doesn’t. In excess, everything worthwhile is deferred. Its opposite pole, a restless inability to rest at all, drives a person into constant motion that is its own kind of avoidance.

additional_info.heading: A day in the life
additional_info.text: The alarm is set for the morning sit, and when it sounds the bed is warm and the practice can, surely, happen later. ‘Later’ becomes evening becomes never. This is ālasya — not a decision against practice but a body-and-will too heavy to move toward it. Maya makes the obstacle’s job harder: she lays out the cushion the night before and shrinks the commitment to something almost too small to resist, so that the heaviness has nothing large to refuse.

attributes.title: Avirati — Sensual Craving
attributes.aspect: अविरति   ·   Sutra 1.30 The nature of this aspect. Avirati is craving for sense objects—the inability to turn away from sensory pleasures. It pulls the mind away from yoga practice toward sensory engagement.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital (sensual pull) · Ontic mode: Obstacle — an antarāya (sensual craving / non-restraint) · Valence: Affliction / obstacle · Guṇa: Rajas-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Obstruction (craving that pulls attention outward) · Key relation: One of the nine antarāyas; kin to Rāga (Dim 19) and Kāma-vāsanā (Dim 12); antagonist to Brahmacharya.
coordinate.key_relation: One of the nine antarāyas; kin to Rāga (Dim 19) and Kāma-vāsanā (Dim 12); antagonist to Brahmacharya

balanced.label: Balanced — senses enjoyed without being ruled

facets.overview: When Avirati is overcome through Vairagya (dispassion), the senses no longer dominate. The yogi can engage with senses consciously without being dragged.
facets.somatic: Healthy relationship with sensory pleasure, body free from sensory addiction, balanced engagement.
facets.emotional: Emotional freedom from sensory craving, contentment, peace with sensory experience.
facets.cognitive: Mental freedom from sensory obsession, focus available for practice, no slavery to senses.
facets.psychic: The soul enjoys the senses without being ruled by them; dispassion here is not coldness but freedom, the senses placed at the service of a larger aim.

balanced.text:
When Avirati is overcome through Vairagya (dispassion), the senses no longer dominate. The yogi can engage with senses consciously without being dragged.

Somatic: Healthy relationship with sensory pleasure, body free from sensory addiction, balanced engagement.

Emotional: Emotional freedom from sensory craving, contentment, peace with sensory experience.

Cognitive: Mental freedom from sensory obsession, focus available for practice, no slavery to senses.

Psychic: The soul enjoys the senses without being ruled by them; dispassion here is not coldness but freedom, the senses placed at the service of a larger aim.

excess.label: In excess — the constant outward pull toward stimulation

facets.overview: Excessive Avirati is total slavery to senses—addiction, hedonism, inability to focus on anything but sensory gratification.
facets.somatic: Sensory addiction, body controlled by sense-cravings, health damaged by excess indulgence, depleted from sensory pursuit.
facets.emotional: Emotional slavery to sensory pleasures, compulsive seeking, emotional volatility from sense-driven life.
facets.cognitive: Mind enslaved by senses, no mental focus possible, scattered attention chasing sensory objects.
facets.psychic: The soul is pulled ceaselessly outward; enslaved to stimulation, it cannot turn inward long enough for anything subtle to register.

excess.text:
Excessive Avirati is total slavery to senses—addiction, hedonism, inability to focus on anything but sensory gratification.

Somatic: Sensory addiction, body controlled by sense-cravings, health damaged by excess indulgence, depleted from sensory pursuit.

Emotional: Emotional slavery to sensory pleasures, compulsive seeking, emotional volatility from sense-driven life.

Cognitive: Mind enslaved by senses, no mental focus possible, scattered attention chasing sensory objects.

Psychic: The soul is pulled ceaselessly outward; enslaved to stimulation, it cannot turn inward long enough for anything subtle to register.

deficient.label: Deficient — joyless aversion to the senses, forced asceticism

facets.overview: Forced suppression of all sensory engagement creates harsh asceticism, denial of natural function, eventual breakdown of suppression.
facets.somatic: Sensory deprivation, atrophied function, harm from extreme denial.
facets.emotional: Emotional suppression of natural pleasure, harsh denial, eventual eruption.
facets.cognitive: Mental suppression with eventual breakdown, distorted thinking from forced denial.
facets.psychic: Renunciation forced rather than ripened becomes its own affliction; joyless aversion to the senses splits the soul against its own embodiment.

deficient.text:
Forced suppression of all sensory engagement creates harsh asceticism, denial of natural function, eventual breakdown of suppression.

Somatic: Sensory deprivation, atrophied function, harm from extreme denial.

Emotional: Emotional suppression of natural pleasure, harsh denial, eventual eruption.

Cognitive: Mental suppression with eventual breakdown, distorted thinking from forced denial.

Psychic: Renunciation forced rather than ripened becomes its own affliction; joyless aversion to the senses splits the soul against its own embodiment.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: Avirati is the mind’s outward pull toward sense-pleasure — the inability to turn the attention inward because the next pleasant thing is always more inviting than the quiet. It is the scroll, the snack, the notification, the small hit of stimulation that the mind reaches for in any pause. In balance, the senses are enjoyed without governing the day. In excess, every gap is immediately filled, and stillness becomes intolerable. Forced against its nature, it can swing to the deficient pole — a joyless, white-knuckled rejection of all pleasure that is its own distortion.

additional_info.heading: A day in the life
additional_info.text: Maya sits to meditate and within thirty seconds the hand reaches, almost on its own, for the phone — just to check, just for a second. The pull is not toward anything in particular; it is the sheer discomfort of an unfilled moment. This is avirati. She does not fight it with contempt; she simply puts the phone in another room before sitting, removing the easy object, and lets the craving rise and pass in the empty space it was reaching to fill.

attributes.title: Bhrāntidarśana — False Perception
attributes.aspect: भ्रान्तिदर्शन   ·   Sutra 1.30 The nature of this aspect. Bhrantidarshana is false perception or erroneous view—wrong understanding of practice, philosophy, or experience. Mistaking progress, misidentifying experiences.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental (mistaken understanding) · Ontic mode: Obstacle — an antarāya (false perception) · Valence: Affliction / obstacle · Guṇa: Tamas–Rajas · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Obstruction (wrong view that misdirects practice) · Key relation: One of the nine antarāyas; kin to Viparyaya (Dim 20) and Avidyā (Dim 19).
coordinate.key_relation: One of the nine antarāyas; kin to Viparyaya (Dim 20) and Avidyā (Dim 19)

balanced.label: Balanced — accurate understanding of the path and one’s place on it

facets.overview: When Bhrantidarshana is overcome, the yogi has correct understanding of practice, experiences, and reality. There is clear discrimination about progress.
facets.somatic: Accurate perception of body's signals, healthy embodied understanding.
facets.emotional: Accurate emotional understanding, no false self-assessment of feelings.
facets.cognitive: Correct understanding of practice and experiences, clear discrimination, accurate progress assessment.
facets.psychic: The soul sees the path and its own place on it accurately; right understanding keeps the whole journey honest.

balanced.text:
When Bhrantidarshana is overcome, the yogi has correct understanding of practice, experiences, and reality. There is clear discrimination about progress.

Somatic: Accurate perception of body's signals, healthy embodied understanding.

Emotional: Accurate emotional understanding, no false self-assessment of feelings.

Cognitive: Correct understanding of practice and experiences, clear discrimination, accurate progress assessment.

Psychic: The soul sees the path and its own place on it accurately; right understanding keeps the whole journey honest.

excess.label: In excess — spiritual self-deception, mistaking the map

facets.overview: Severe Bhrantidarshana creates deeply false views, mistaking progress, spiritual delusions, fixed wrong understandings that prevent correction.
facets.somatic: Misreading body's signals consistently, harmful practices believed beneficial.
facets.emotional: Mistaking emotional states (calling agitation peace), emotional self-deception.
facets.cognitive: Deep false views about reality, spiritual delusions, mistaking experiences, fundamental misunderstanding of path.
facets.psychic: The soul wanders a self-built map; spiritual delusion — mistaking imagination for attainment — is among the most dangerous obstacles precisely because it feels like progress.

excess.text:
Severe Bhrantidarshana creates deeply false views, mistaking progress, spiritual delusions, fixed wrong understandings that prevent correction.

Somatic: Misreading body's signals consistently, harmful practices believed beneficial.

Emotional: Mistaking emotional states (calling agitation peace), emotional self-deception.

Cognitive: Deep false views about reality, spiritual delusions, mistaking experiences, fundamental misunderstanding of path.

Psychic: The soul wanders a self-built map; spiritual delusion — mistaking imagination for attainment — is among the most dangerous obstacles precisely because it feels like progress.

deficient.label: A subtler form — the false confidence of believing one’s perception is now wholly free of error

facets.overview: Absence of Bhrantidarshana is the desired state. The deficient version (false beliefs about having no false beliefs) is itself a form of Bhrantidarshana.
facets.somatic: Accurate physical perception.
facets.emotional: Accurate emotional awareness.
facets.cognitive: Clear understanding without delusion.
facets.psychic: A subtler trap: the soul grows certain it is now beyond all misperception, and that very confidence becomes the last and most invisible distortion.

deficient.text:
Absence of Bhrantidarshana is the desired state. The deficient version (false beliefs about having no false beliefs) is itself a form of Bhrantidarshana.

Somatic: Accurate physical perception.

Emotional: Accurate emotional awareness.

Cognitive: Clear understanding without delusion.

Psychic: A subtler trap: the soul grows certain it is now beyond all misperception, and that very confidence becomes the last and most invisible distortion.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: Bhrāntidarśana is erroneous understanding applied to the path itself — mistaking a pleasant experience for awakening, misjudging one’s own progress, following a wrong map with full conviction, or interpreting genuine experiences through a distorting frame. It is the most dangerous obstacle for the sincere because it disguises itself as attainment. In balance, a practitioner holds an accurate, humble picture of where they actually are. In excess, they are sincerely convinced of a position they have not reached, and the conviction itself bars further movement.

additional_info.heading: A day in the life
additional_info.text: After a few unusually calm sessions, the thought forms: ‘I’ve really gotten somewhere now — I’m past the basics.’ Subtly, practice loosens; the calm was pleasant and has been mistaken for arrival. This is bhrāntidarśana, and its symptom is precisely the slackening it produces. Recognizing it, Maya treats every good session as weather rather than as a summit reached, and keeps beginning as if she knows nothing — which, the tradition would say, is itself the more accurate view.

attributes.title: Alabdha-bhūmikatva — Failure to Gain Ground
attributes.aspect: अलब्धभूमिकत्व   ·   Sutra 1.30 The nature of this aspect. Alabdha-bhumikatva is the inability to reach the higher states of yoga despite practice. There is effort but no fruit—failure to gain ground.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic (the plateau) · Ontic mode: Obstacle — an antarāya (failure to gain ground) · Valence: Affliction / obstacle · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Obstruction (stalled progress) · Key relation: One of the nine antarāyas; the felt failure to advance through the stages (Dim 11).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Obstacle — an antarāya (failure to gain ground)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the nine antarāyas; the felt failure to advance through the stages (Dim 11)

balanced.label: Balanced — steady progress in due season

facets.overview: When Alabdha-bhumikatva is overcome, the yogi makes progress and attains higher states. Practice bears fruit, advancement occurs.
facets.somatic: Body shows progress in practice, gaining capacity, fruit of effort visible in body.
facets.emotional: Emotional advancement, transformation visible, fruits of practice felt.
facets.cognitive: Mental progress, higher states attained, advancement on path, gaining new ground.
facets.psychic: The soul ripens in its own season; steady progress, trusted without demand, is its own quiet reassurance.

balanced.text:
When Alabdha-bhumikatva is overcome, the yogi makes progress and attains higher states. Practice bears fruit, advancement occurs.

Somatic: Body shows progress in practice, gaining capacity, fruit of effort visible in body.

Emotional: Emotional advancement, transformation visible, fruits of practice felt.

Cognitive: Mental progress, higher states attained, advancement on path, gaining new ground.

Psychic: The soul ripens in its own season; steady progress, trusted without demand, is its own quiet reassurance.

excess.label: In excess — over-attachment to attainment; spiritual materialism, grasping for higher states

facets.overview: Excessive focus on reaching higher states becomes attachment to attainment, spiritual materialism, frustrated grasping for experiences.
facets.somatic: Forcing physical attainment, harm from over-effort, body strained by grasping for states.
facets.emotional: Emotional frustration with progress, spiritual materialism, attachment to experiences.
facets.cognitive: Mental obsession with attainment, grasping for higher states, anxiety about progress.
facets.psychic: The soul grasps at the very states it seeks; attachment to attainment turns practice into spiritual materialism, the wanting itself blocking the door.

excess.text:
Excessive focus on reaching higher states becomes attachment to attainment, spiritual materialism, frustrated grasping for experiences.

Somatic: Forcing physical attainment, harm from over-effort, body strained by grasping for states.

Emotional: Emotional frustration with progress, spiritual materialism, attachment to experiences.

Cognitive: Mental obsession with attainment, grasping for higher states, anxiety about progress.

Psychic: The soul grasps at the very states it seeks; attachment to attainment turns practice into spiritual materialism, the wanting itself blocking the door.

deficient.label: When dominant — sustained effort yielding no ground; the stalled plateau

facets.overview: When Alabdha-bhumikatva is the dominant state—much practice but no progress, ground not gained, advancement blocked.
facets.somatic: Body not responding to practice, no physical advancement, plateau or regression.
facets.emotional: Frustration from no emotional progress, despair about path, emotional stagnation.
facets.cognitive: Mental discouragement, no breakthrough states, no insight despite effort, stuck.
facets.psychic: The plateau holds; sincere effort yields no ground, and the gap between labor and fruit tests whether the soul can continue without the reward it expected.

deficient.text:
When Alabdha-bhumikatva is the dominant state—much practice but no progress, ground not gained, advancement blocked.

Somatic: Body not responding to practice, no physical advancement, plateau or regression.

Emotional: Frustration from no emotional progress, despair about path, emotional stagnation.

Cognitive: Mental discouragement, no breakthrough states, no insight despite effort, stuck.

Psychic: The plateau holds; sincere effort yields no ground, and the gap between labor and fruit tests whether the soul can continue without the reward it expected.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: Alabdha-bhūmikatva is the obstacle of the plateau — sincere, sustained effort that nonetheless seems to yield no higher ground. The practice is done; the fruit does not come; and the gap between effort and result breeds discouragement, which is itself the obstacle deepening. In balance, a practitioner trusts the slow ripening and continues without demanding a timeline. When the obstacle dominates, the failure to advance becomes proof, to the doubting mind, that advance is impossible — and the practice is abandoned just short of where it might have turned.

additional_info.heading: A day in the life
additional_info.text: Months in, Maya feels she is exactly where she started — same restlessness, same wandering mind, no breakthrough. The lack of visible progress feels like a verdict. This is alabdha-bhūmikatva, and its danger is that the plateau is often the last stretch before the ground gives way to something new. She holds the long view, lowers her demand for results, and keeps practicing on the plateau itself, which is where the real work quietly happens.

attributes.title: Anavasthitatva — Instability
attributes.aspect: अनवस्थितत्व   ·   Sutra 1.30 The nature of this aspect. Anavasthitatva is the inability to maintain a state once attained—instability where ground is gained then lost. The mind cannot stabilize in higher states.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic (loss of a gained level) · Ontic mode: Obstacle — an antarāya (instability) · Valence: Affliction / obstacle · Guṇa: Rajas-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Obstruction (the loss of an attained ground) · Key relation: One of the nine antarāyas; counterpart to Alabdha-bhūmikatva — losing rather than failing to reach.
coordinate.causal_status: Obstruction (the loss of an attained ground)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the nine antarāyas; counterpart to Alabdha-bhūmikatva — losing rather than failing to reach

balanced.label: Balanced — ground gained and held

facets.overview: When Anavasthitatva is overcome, the yogi stabilizes attained states. Progress is maintained, ground is held, advancement consolidates.
facets.somatic: Maintained physical practice, stabilized practice routines, body's gains preserved.
facets.emotional: Emotional stability in attained states, maintained transformation, lasting changes.
facets.cognitive: Mental stabilization in higher states, sustained meditation, consolidated wisdom.
facets.psychic: The soul consolidates what it gains; a state once reached becomes stable ground for the next, and progress stops leaking away.

balanced.text:
When Anavasthitatva is overcome, the yogi stabilizes attained states. Progress is maintained, ground is held, advancement consolidates.

Somatic: Maintained physical practice, stabilized practice routines, body's gains preserved.

Emotional: Emotional stability in attained states, maintained transformation, lasting changes.

Cognitive: Mental stabilization in higher states, sustained meditation, consolidated wisdom.

Psychic: The soul consolidates what it gains; a state once reached becomes stable ground for the next, and progress stops leaking away.

excess.label: In excess — rigid clinging to attained states; attachment to specific experiences

facets.overview: Excessive rigidity in trying to maintain states becomes attachment to specific experiences, refusing natural fluctuations, gripping at attainment.
facets.somatic: Rigid attempt to maintain physical states, inability to flow with body's natural changes.
facets.emotional: Anxious gripping at emotional states, fear of losing peace, rigid emotional control.
facets.cognitive: Rigid attempt to maintain meditation states, frustration with natural mental fluctuations.
facets.psychic: The soul clings rigidly to particular experiences; attachment to a remembered state prevents the fluid movement that real growth requires.

excess.text:
Excessive rigidity in trying to maintain states becomes attachment to specific experiences, refusing natural fluctuations, gripping at attainment.

Somatic: Rigid attempt to maintain physical states, inability to flow with body's natural changes.

Emotional: Anxious gripping at emotional states, fear of losing peace, rigid emotional control.

Cognitive: Rigid attempt to maintain meditation states, frustration with natural mental fluctuations.

Psychic: The soul clings rigidly to particular experiences; attachment to a remembered state prevents the fluid movement that real growth requires.

deficient.label: When dominant — ground gained then immediately lost; chronic instability

facets.overview: When Anavasthitatva is dominant—states are reached but immediately lost, fluctuation prevents consolidation, ground constantly slipping away.
facets.somatic: Inability to maintain physical practice, body gains lost quickly, inconsistent body.
facets.emotional: Emotional gains lost rapidly, instability, peace coming and going without consolidation.
facets.cognitive: Meditation states lost immediately, mental gains slipping away, inability to stabilize.
facets.psychic: The hardest obstacle of the path: ground is gained and instantly lost, the calm of Tuesday gone by Thursday — until the soul learns to treat this fluctuation as the ordinary texture of consolidation rather than as failure.

deficient.text:
When Anavasthitatva is dominant—states are reached but immediately lost, fluctuation prevents consolidation, ground constantly slipping away.

Somatic: Inability to maintain physical practice, body gains lost quickly, inconsistent body.

Emotional: Emotional gains lost rapidly, instability, peace coming and going without consolidation.

Cognitive: Meditation states lost immediately, mental gains slipping away, inability to stabilize.

Psychic: The hardest obstacle of the path: ground is gained and instantly lost, the calm of Tuesday gone by Thursday — until the soul learns to treat this fluctuation as the ordinary texture of consolidation rather than as failure.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: Anavasthitatva is the inability to hold a state once it has been attained — the calm reached on Tuesday gone by Thursday, the insight that fades, the ground gained and then lost again and again. It is the final obstacle of the list precisely because it troubles those who have begun to succeed. In balance, a state once reached becomes a stable platform for the next. When the obstacle dominates, the mind cannot settle into any gain, and the repeated loss of hard-won ground is exhausting and demoralizing — unless it is understood as the ordinary texture of consolidation rather than as failure.

additional_info.heading: A day in the life
additional_info.text: For one beautiful week the morning sits are deep and the days are calmer; then, with no clear cause, it all evaporates, and Maya is back to a scattered, resistant mind, mourning the steadiness she briefly had. This is anavasthitatva. Rather than read the loss as proof that the gain was illusory, she treats stabilization as its own long phase of practice — expecting the tide to go in and out many times before the ground is truly held.

attributes.title: Maitrī — Friendliness Toward the Happy
attributes.aspect: मैत्री   ·   Sutra 1.33 The nature of this aspect. Maitri is friendliness or loving-kindness toward those who are happy and successful. It dissolves envy and creates serene mental clarity. One of the four locks/keys to mental peace.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital (the heart’s warmth) · Ontic mode: Virtue — a brahmavihāra (loving-kindness) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate (near enemy: attachment; far enemy: ill-will) · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Condition (warms the heart toward all beings) · Key relation: One of the four brahmavihāras — the key for those who are happy; antidote to Dveṣa (Dim 19).
coordinate.valence: Virtue to cultivate (near enemy: attachment; far enemy: ill-will)
coordinate.causal_status: Condition (warms the heart toward all beings)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the four brahmavihāras — the key for those who are happy; antidote to Dveṣa (Dim 19)

balanced.label: Balanced — genuine gladness at another’s good fortune

facets.overview: When Maitri is established, a person feels genuine warmth and friendliness toward happy and successful people. Envy dissolves, mental serenity arises.
facets.somatic: Open warm physical presence with happy others, healthy nervous system, body that doesn't tense around success.
facets.emotional: Genuine joy at others' happiness, warmth in relationships, freedom from envy, emotional abundance.
facets.cognitive: Mental serenity, freedom from comparison thinking, clarity from non-envious mind, peaceful thinking.
facets.psychic: The soul recognizes itself in another’s happiness; gladness at others’ good fortune dissolves the boundary between self and other, which is the secret the brahmavihāras exist to teach.

balanced.text:
When Maitri is established, a person feels genuine warmth and friendliness toward happy and successful people. Envy dissolves, mental serenity arises.

Somatic: Open warm physical presence with happy others, healthy nervous system, body that doesn't tense around success.

Emotional: Genuine joy at others' happiness, warmth in relationships, freedom from envy, emotional abundance.

Cognitive: Mental serenity, freedom from comparison thinking, clarity from non-envious mind, peaceful thinking.

Psychic: The soul recognizes itself in another’s happiness; gladness at others’ good fortune dissolves the boundary between self and other, which is the secret the brahmavihāras exist to teach.

excess.label: Excessive Maitri without discernment becomes inability to address others' faults, false agreeableness, sycophancy.

facets.somatic: Physical inability to take stands, weakness in face of harm to happy others.
facets.emotional: Emotional sycophancy, false agreeableness, inability to give honest feedback.
facets.cognitive: Mental inability to discriminate, avoiding necessary critical assessment, false harmony.
facets.psychic: The soul loses itself in the need to be liked; friendliness curdles into people-pleasing, and the self disappears into approval rather than into love.

excess.text:
Somatic: Physical inability to take stands, weakness in face of harm to happy others.

Emotional: Emotional sycophancy, false agreeableness, inability to give honest feedback.

Cognitive: Mental inability to discriminate, avoiding necessary critical assessment, false harmony.

Psychic: The soul loses itself in the need to be liked; friendliness curdles into people-pleasing, and the self disappears into approval rather than into love.

deficient.label: Deficient — envy, comparison, the soured response to others’ joy

facets.overview: Absence of Maitri creates envy, jealousy of successful people, bitter comparison, suffering at others' joy.
facets.somatic: Tension in presence of successful others, stress from envy, physical signs of jealousy.
facets.emotional: Envy and jealousy, bitterness, emotional suffering at others' success, inability to feel for happy others.
facets.cognitive: Comparison thinking, mental bitterness, distorted perception of success, agitation from envy.
facets.psychic: The soul contracts into the prison of comparison; envy at another’s joy is the exact measure of how separate one still feels.

deficient.text:
Absence of Maitri creates envy, jealousy of successful people, bitter comparison, suffering at others' joy.

Somatic: Tension in presence of successful others, stress from envy, physical signs of jealousy.

Emotional: Envy and jealousy, bitterness, emotional suffering at others' success, inability to feel for happy others.

Cognitive: Comparison thinking, mental bitterness, distorted perception of success, agitation from envy.

Psychic: The soul contracts into the prison of comparison; envy at another’s joy is the exact measure of how separate one still feels.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: Maitrī is the warm, open friendliness that meets another’s happiness with gladness rather than comparison. In balance, it lets you celebrate a friend’s success cleanly, without the small inner subtraction that asks ‘what about me.’ In excess, friendliness can curdle into ingratiation — an over-attachment to being liked, a people-pleasing that loses its own ground. In deficiency, its absence is envy: the happiness of others lands as a wound, the social feed becomes a daily measuring of oneself against everyone’s highlight reel, and other people’s good news quietly diminishes one’s own day.

additional_info.heading: A day in the life
additional_info.text: A close friend announces a wonderful new job. Through deficient maitrī, Maya’s congratulations are warm on the surface and cold underneath — a flush of ‘why is it always them,’ a comparison that sours the rest of her evening. Through balanced maitrī, the friend’s joy is simply contagious; there is room in her for it, and the gladness she feels for them turns out to leave her own mind lighter, not heavier — which is exactly the clarifying effect Patañjali promised.

attributes.title: Karuṇā — Compassion Toward the Suffering
attributes.aspect: करुणा   ·   Sutra 1.33 The nature of this aspect. Karuna is compassion toward those who are suffering. It dissolves cruelty and indifference, creating mental purity and serenity.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital (tenderness toward pain) · Ontic mode: Virtue — a brahmavihāra (compassion) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate (near enemy: pity / grief; far enemy: cruelty) · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Condition (opens the heart to another’s suffering) · Key relation: One of the four brahmavihāras — the key for those who suffer; the ripened form of Śoka’s Karuṇā Rasa (Dim 8).
coordinate.valence: Virtue to cultivate (near enemy: pity / grief; far enemy: cruelty)
coordinate.causal_status: Condition (opens the heart to another’s suffering)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the four brahmavihāras — the key for those who suffer; the ripened form of Śoka’s Karuṇā Rasa (Dim 8)

balanced.label: Balanced — warm, steady compassion that can act

facets.overview: When Karuna is established, genuine compassion arises in face of suffering. The yogi feels for others without absorption, helps wisely.
facets.somatic: Open heart center, warm physical presence with suffering others, healthy compassionate engagement.
facets.emotional: Genuine compassion and warmth toward suffering, ability to help from balance, emotional generosity.
facets.cognitive: Wisdom about how to alleviate suffering, clear thinking compassion, mental purity from caring.
facets.psychic: The soul meets suffering with a compassion that can act — the heart open and the ground steady, mirroring the boundless mercy it is learning to embody.

balanced.text:
When Karuna is established, genuine compassion arises in face of suffering. The yogi feels for others without absorption, helps wisely.

Somatic: Open heart center, warm physical presence with suffering others, healthy compassionate engagement.

Emotional: Genuine compassion and warmth toward suffering, ability to help from balance, emotional generosity.

Cognitive: Wisdom about how to alleviate suffering, clear thinking compassion, mental purity from caring.

Psychic: The soul meets suffering with a compassion that can act — the heart open and the ground steady, mirroring the boundless mercy it is learning to embody.

excess.label: In excess — over-identification, burnout, drowning in another’s pain

facets.overview: Excessive Karuna becomes emotional absorption in others' pain, codependent rescuing, overwhelm from compassion, burnout.
facets.somatic: Compassion fatigue, physical exhaustion from over-helping, body absorbing others' tension.
facets.emotional: Emotional overwhelm by others' suffering, codependent rescuing, emotional drowning.
facets.cognitive: Mental confusion about helping vs enabling, inability to maintain perspective.
facets.psychic: The soul drowns in another’s pain, losing the steadiness from which real help flows; compassion without equanimity exhausts itself.

excess.text:
Excessive Karuna becomes emotional absorption in others' pain, codependent rescuing, overwhelm from compassion, burnout.

Somatic: Compassion fatigue, physical exhaustion from over-helping, body absorbing others' tension.

Emotional: Emotional overwhelm by others' suffering, codependent rescuing, emotional drowning.

Cognitive: Mental confusion about helping vs enabling, inability to maintain perspective.

Psychic: The soul drowns in another’s pain, losing the steadiness from which real help flows; compassion without equanimity exhausts itself.

deficient.label: Deficient — cruelty, cold indifference, the turned-away face

facets.overview: Absence of Karuna creates cruelty, indifference to suffering, hardness of heart, inability to care.
facets.somatic: Closed body in presence of suffering, physical coldness, hard presence.
facets.emotional: Emotional coldness, cruelty, inability to feel for sufferers, hardness of heart.
facets.cognitive: Mental dismissal of others' suffering, callous thinking, rationalization for not caring.
facets.psychic: The soul hardens and turns away; the closed heart is the surest sign of a self still defended against the world’s pain.

deficient.text:
Absence of Karuna creates cruelty, indifference to suffering, hardness of heart, inability to care.

Somatic: Closed body in presence of suffering, physical coldness, hard presence.

Emotional: Emotional coldness, cruelty, inability to feel for sufferers, hardness of heart.

Cognitive: Mental dismissal of others' suffering, callous thinking, rationalization for not caring.

Psychic: The soul hardens and turns away; the closed heart is the surest sign of a self still defended against the world’s pain.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: Karuṇā is compassion toward those who suffer — the open-hearted wish to relieve pain, steady enough to remain present to it. In balance, it lets you stay with someone’s grief without either fleeing or collapsing, and to help where help is possible. In excess, compassion loses its footing and becomes over-identification: taking on another’s pain as one’s own until one is flooded, exhausted, and finally burned out — a common fate of caregivers who feel everything and cannot put it down. In deficiency, it hardens into indifference or even cruelty: the suffering of others met with avoidance, contempt, or a wall.

additional_info.heading: A day in the life
additional_info.text: A colleague breaks down in tears over a personal loss. Through deficient karuṇā, Maya feels only awkwardness and the urge to escape the room. Through excess karuṇā, she absorbs the grief so completely that she carries it home and cannot sleep, no use to anyone. Through balanced karuṇā, she stays — present, warm, unhurried — feeling the pain without becoming it, able to offer the simple steadiness that actually helps, and able, afterward, to set it down.

attributes.title: Muditā — Joy at the Virtuous
attributes.aspect: मुदिता   ·   Sutra 1.33 The nature of this aspect. Mudita is joy at others' virtue or happiness, particularly the gladness at seeing meritorious actions. It dissolves competitive envy regarding spiritual or moral accomplishment.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital (gladness at another’s good) · Ontic mode: Virtue — a brahmavihāra (sympathetic joy) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate (near enemy: comparison / flattery; far enemy: envy) · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Condition (rejoices in another’s good fortune) · Key relation: One of the four brahmavihāras — the key for the fortunate and virtuous; antidote to Asūyā (envy, Dim 9).
coordinate.valence: Virtue to cultivate (near enemy: comparison / flattery; far enemy: envy)
coordinate.key_relation: One of the four brahmavihāras — the key for the fortunate and virtuous; antidote to Asūyā (envy, Dim 9)

balanced.label: Balanced — sympathetic joy at goodness and merit

facets.overview: When Mudita is established, the yogi feels genuine joy at others' virtue and spiritual progress. This dissolves competitive spiritual envy.
facets.somatic: Body that lights up at others' goodness, healthy joyful response, warm presence.
facets.emotional: Genuine emotional joy at virtue, freedom from spiritual envy, warm celebration of goodness.
facets.cognitive: Mental celebration of virtue, freedom from comparison about progress, joyful thinking.
facets.psychic: The soul delights in goodness wherever it appears, taking joy in another’s virtue as its own; this gladness quietly erodes the comparing, competing ego.

balanced.text:
When Mudita is established, the yogi feels genuine joy at others' virtue and spiritual progress. This dissolves competitive spiritual envy.

Somatic: Body that lights up at others' goodness, healthy joyful response, warm presence.

Emotional: Genuine emotional joy at virtue, freedom from spiritual envy, warm celebration of goodness.

Cognitive: Mental celebration of virtue, freedom from comparison about progress, joyful thinking.

Psychic: The soul delights in goodness wherever it appears, taking joy in another’s virtue as its own; this gladness quietly erodes the comparing, competing ego.

excess.label: In excess — idealization, hero-worship that loses discernment
Excessive Mudita can become overlooking faults, refusing to recognize problems, false positivity about everything.

facets.somatic: Inappropriate positive response to harmful situations.
facets.emotional: False positivity, inability to feel appropriate concern, emotional bypassing of legitimate issues.
facets.cognitive: Inability to recognize problems, distorted positive thinking, denial of legitimate concerns.
facets.psychic: The soul loses discernment in idealization; admiration becomes hero-worship, projecting onto another the light it has not yet claimed in itself.

excess.text:
Somatic: Inappropriate positive response to harmful situations.

Emotional: False positivity, inability to feel appropriate concern, emotional bypassing of legitimate issues.

Cognitive: Inability to recognize problems, distorted positive thinking, denial of legitimate concerns.

Psychic: The soul loses discernment in idealization; admiration becomes hero-worship, projecting onto another the light it has not yet claimed in itself.

deficient.label: Deficient — competitive resentment, the begrudging of others’ goodness

facets.overview: Absence of Mudita creates jealousy of virtue, resentment of others' spiritual progress, competitive comparison on the path.
facets.somatic: Physical tension around virtuous others, stress from spiritual comparison.
facets.emotional: Jealousy of virtue, resentment of others' progress, spiritual envy, bitter comparison.
facets.cognitive: Mental competition in spirituality, distorted thinking about progress, agitation from comparison.
facets.psychic: The soul begrudges what it cannot yet celebrate; resentment at another’s goodness reveals the ego measuring itself against everyone.

deficient.text:
Absence of Mudita creates jealousy of virtue, resentment of others' spiritual progress, competitive comparison on the path.

Somatic: Physical tension around virtuous others, stress from spiritual comparison.

Emotional: Jealousy of virtue, resentment of others' progress, spiritual envy, bitter comparison.

Cognitive: Mental competition in spirituality, distorted thinking about progress, agitation from comparison.

Psychic: The soul begrudges what it cannot yet celebrate; resentment at another’s goodness reveals the ego measuring itself against everyone.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: Muditā is gladness at another’s virtue, merit, and good fortune — particularly the appreciative joy at seeing someone do genuine good. It is the specific antidote to the subtle, corrosive envy that can arise even on the spiritual path, where another’s evident progress or kindness provokes not admiration but a competitive ache. In balance, the goodness of others is a delight and even an inspiration. In excess, appreciation tips into idealization or hero-worship that loses discernment. In deficiency, it sours into the begrudging response that cannot let another’s goodness simply be good — that must diminish it, doubt its motives, or measure itself against it.

additional_info.heading: A day in the life
additional_info.text: Maya watches a peer give freely of their time to a struggling newcomer, patient and generous in a way she knows she rarely manages. Through deficient muditā, the sight stings — a competitive ‘they’re just doing it to look good’ that protects her from feeling smaller. Through balanced muditā, the same scene simply gladdens her, and the gladness carries a clean inspiration: not ‘I should compete’ but ‘how good that this exists,’ and a quiet wish to be more like that herself.

attributes.title: Upekṣā — Equanimity Toward the Wicked
attributes.aspect: उपेक्षा   ·   Sutra 1.33 The nature of this aspect. Upeksha is equanimity (sometimes translated as 'indifference') toward those who are unvirtuous or wicked. Not approval, but freedom from being disturbed by their vice. It preserves mental peace.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Mental (the steady, even heart) · Ontic mode: Virtue — a brahmavihāra (equanimity) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate (near enemy: indifference; far enemy: agitation / partiality) · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond the guṇas · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika), grounded in the individual · Causal status: Condition (holds the heart even amid what cannot be changed) · Key relation: One of the four brahmavihāras — the key for the difficult and beyond-help; kin to Śama (Dim 8) and Udāsīnatā (Dim 10).
coordinate.valence: Virtue to cultivate (near enemy: indifference; far enemy: agitation / partiality)
coordinate.causal_status: Condition (holds the heart even amid what cannot be changed)
