Cognitive: Constant self-criticism, mental shame, inability to act for fear of being seen, paralysis from concern about judgment, distorted self-image.

Psychic: Modesty collapses into crippling self-effacement; the soul shrinks from its own legitimate light, mistaking smallness for virtue.

deficient.label: Deficient — shamelessness, the loss of conscience

facets.overview: Absence of Hrih manifests as shamelessness, inability to feel proper restraint, crude behavior, and lack of inner moral compass.
facets.somatic: Crude physical behavior, lack of physical boundaries, inappropriate physical conduct, disrespect for own and others' physical dignity.
facets.emotional: Shamelessness and crudeness, inability to feel appropriate shame, lack of moral feeling, emotional coarseness.
facets.cognitive: No internal sense of right action, mental rationalizations for any behavior, inability to discriminate dignified from undignified, missing conscience.
facets.psychic: The soul loses its inner compass; shamelessness is the inability to feel the quiet ‘no’ of conscience before wrong.

deficient.text:
Absence of Hrih manifests as shamelessness, inability to feel proper restraint, crude behavior, and lack of inner moral compass.

Somatic: Crude physical behavior, lack of physical boundaries, inappropriate physical conduct, disrespect for own and others' physical dignity.

Emotional: Shamelessness and crudeness, inability to feel appropriate shame, lack of moral feeling, emotional coarseness.

Cognitive: No internal sense of right action, mental rationalizations for any behavior, inability to discriminate dignified from undignified, missing conscience.

Psychic: The soul loses its inner compass; shamelessness is the inability to feel the quiet ‘no’ of conscience before wrong.

attributes.title: Mārdavam — Gentleness
attributes.aspect: मार्दवम्   ·   Chapter 16 The nature of this aspect. Märdavam is gentleness—softness in conduct, manner, and approach. It is the quality of being kind in interaction with others, not harsh or rough. It reflects refinement and compassion in expression.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Virtue — a daivī quality (gentleness / softness) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Condition (a soft, non-harsh disposition) · Key relation: A daivī quality; direct antagonist to Pāruṣyam (harshness, 4.3).
coordinate.key_relation: A daivī quality; direct antagonist to Pāruṣyam (harshness, 4.3)

balanced.label: Balanced — strength expressed as gentleness

facets.overview: When Märdavam is present, a person interacts with others gently and kindly while maintaining strength. There is softness without weakness, gentleness without timidity.
facets.somatic: Gentle physical touch and presence, soft body language, peaceful physical demeanor, healthy nervous system from peaceful engagement.
facets.emotional: Emotional softness and warmth, kind emotional expression, gentle approach to others, emotional tenderness combined with strength.
facets.cognitive: Gentle thinking patterns, mental refinement, sensitive consideration of others, thoughtful and considerate mental engagement.
facets.psychic: The soul meets the world with a softness that is not weakness; gentleness is strength that has no need to harden.

balanced.text:
When Märdavam is present, a person interacts with others gently and kindly while maintaining strength. There is softness without weakness, gentleness without timidity.

Somatic: Gentle physical touch and presence, soft body language, peaceful physical demeanor, healthy nervous system from peaceful engagement.

Emotional: Emotional softness and warmth, kind emotional expression, gentle approach to others, emotional tenderness combined with strength.

Cognitive: Gentle thinking patterns, mental refinement, sensitive consideration of others, thoughtful and considerate mental engagement.

Psychic: The soul meets the world with a softness that is not weakness; gentleness is strength that has no need to harden.

excess.label: In excess — softness without spine, conflict-avoidance

facets.overview: Excessive Märdavam can become weak softness, inability to take firm action when needed, over-sensitivity, and ineffective gentleness in face of harshness.
facets.somatic: Physical weakness and inability to assert, lack of physical strength when needed, over-yielding to others' physical assertions.
facets.emotional: Emotional weakness disguised as gentleness, inability to be firm, over-sensitivity, lack of healthy assertiveness.
facets.cognitive: Mental weakness and inability to take strong positions, over-accommodating thinking, lack of mental firmness when needed.
facets.psychic: Gentleness slides into spinelessness; the soul cannot hold a needed edge and is shaped by whoever pushes hardest.

excess.text:
Excessive Märdavam can become weak softness, inability to take firm action when needed, over-sensitivity, and ineffective gentleness in face of harshness.

Somatic: Physical weakness and inability to assert, lack of physical strength when needed, over-yielding to others' physical assertions.

Emotional: Emotional weakness disguised as gentleness, inability to be firm, over-sensitivity, lack of healthy assertiveness.

Cognitive: Mental weakness and inability to take strong positions, over-accommodating thinking, lack of mental firmness when needed.

Psychic: Gentleness slides into spinelessness; the soul cannot hold a needed edge and is shaped by whoever pushes hardest.

deficient.label: Deficient — hardness, abrasiveness, the bruising manner

facets.overview: Absence of Märdavam creates harshness, roughness in conduct, hurtful manner of interaction, and inability to soften when appropriate.
facets.somatic: Harsh physical contact, rough body language, abrasive physical presence, ungentle touch.
facets.emotional: Emotional harshness and roughness, inability to soften emotionally, emotional brusqueness, lack of warmth.
facets.cognitive: Harsh thinking patterns, rough mental engagement, inability to consider sensitivities, blunt and unrefined mental approach.
facets.psychic: The soul roughens; harshness in manner and speech is the surface sign of an inner brittleness.

deficient.text:
Absence of Märdavam creates harshness, roughness in conduct, hurtful manner of interaction, and inability to soften when appropriate.

Somatic: Harsh physical contact, rough body language, abrasive physical presence, ungentle touch.

Emotional: Emotional harshness and roughness, inability to soften emotionally, emotional brusqueness, lack of warmth.

Cognitive: Harsh thinking patterns, rough mental engagement, inability to consider sensitivities, blunt and unrefined mental approach.

Psychic: The soul roughens; harshness in manner and speech is the surface sign of an inner brittleness.

attributes.title: Satya — Truthfulness
attributes.aspect: सत्य   ·   Chapter 16, Chapter 17 The nature of this aspect. Satya is truthfulness in thought, speech, and action. It is alignment with what is real and right, expressed with love (Satyam priyahitamchayat — truth spoken with love or be silent). It includes both factual truth and the deeper truth of Self.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Vital · Ontic mode: Virtue — a daivī quality (truthfulness) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Condition (alignment of word, thought, and deed) · Key relation: A daivī quality; same as Satya the yama (Dim 11); kin to Ārjavam (Dim 10).
coordinate.causal_status: Condition (alignment of word, thought, and deed)
coordinate.key_relation: A daivī quality; same as Satya the yama (Dim 11); kin to Ārjavam (Dim 10)

balanced.label: Balanced — honesty aligned with kindness

facets.overview: When Satya is present, a person speaks and lives in accordance with truth while exercising wisdom about how and when to express it. Truth is delivered with kindness and care.
facets.somatic: Body language consistent with words, nervous system at ease from lack of pretense, healthy physical presence from authenticity, no need to hide.
facets.emotional: Emotional honesty with self and others, freedom from emotional pretense, emotional integrity, authentic expression of feelings, peace from authenticity.
facets.cognitive: Clear honest thinking, mental integrity, freedom from rationalization and self-deception, wisdom in expression, mental clarity from authenticity.
facets.psychic: The soul aligns word, thought, and deed; truthfulness is less a rule than the transparency of a being with nothing to hide.

balanced.text:
When Satya is present, a person speaks and lives in accordance with truth while exercising wisdom about how and when to express it. Truth is delivered with kindness and care.

Somatic: Body language consistent with words, nervous system at ease from lack of pretense, healthy physical presence from authenticity, no need to hide.

Emotional: Emotional honesty with self and others, freedom from emotional pretense, emotional integrity, authentic expression of feelings, peace from authenticity.

Cognitive: Clear honest thinking, mental integrity, freedom from rationalization and self-deception, wisdom in expression, mental clarity from authenticity.

Psychic: The soul aligns word, thought, and deed; truthfulness is less a rule than the transparency of a being with nothing to hide.

excess.label: In excess — brutal honesty, truth as a weapon

facets.overview: Excessive Satya without wisdom becomes brutal honesty, hurtful truth-telling, inappropriate disclosure, and harsh truth delivered without compassion.
facets.somatic: Harsh physical presence, blunt body language, inappropriate physical disclosure.
facets.emotional: Emotional brutality disguised as honesty, hurtful truth-telling, inability to be tactful, harshness in disclosure.
facets.cognitive: Rigid insistence on truth-telling without context, inability to recognize when discretion serves higher truth, mental harshness about facts.
facets.psychic: Truth wielded without compassion becomes a weapon; the soul mistakes brutal candor for honesty and wounds in the name of the real.

excess.text:
Excessive Satya without wisdom becomes brutal honesty, hurtful truth-telling, inappropriate disclosure, and harsh truth delivered without compassion.

Somatic: Harsh physical presence, blunt body language, inappropriate physical disclosure.

Emotional: Emotional brutality disguised as honesty, hurtful truth-telling, inability to be tactful, harshness in disclosure.

Cognitive: Rigid insistence on truth-telling without context, inability to recognize when discretion serves higher truth, mental harshness about facts.

Psychic: Truth wielded without compassion becomes a weapon; the soul mistakes brutal candor for honesty and wounds in the name of the real.

deficient.label: Deficient — deceit, evasion, the divided self

facets.overview: Absence of Satya manifests as habitual lying, deception, self-deception, manipulation, and disconnection from reality. It creates fragmentation and suffering.
facets.somatic: Body language that contradicts words, physical signs of stress from constant lies, exhausted nervous system from maintaining pretense, somatic symptoms.
facets.emotional: Emotional deception and self-deception, inability to feel authentically, emotional disconnection from reality, exhaustion from emotional facades.
facets.cognitive: Habitual lying and rationalization, mental disconnection from truth, fragmented thinking, deceptive mental patterns, self-deception.
facets.psychic: The soul fragments itself through deceit; each lie divides what is from what is shown, and self-deception is the deepest division of all.

deficient.text:
Absence of Satya manifests as habitual lying, deception, self-deception, manipulation, and disconnection from reality. It creates fragmentation and suffering.

Somatic: Body language that contradicts words, physical signs of stress from constant lies, exhausted nervous system from maintaining pretense, somatic symptoms.

Emotional: Emotional deception and self-deception, inability to feel authentically, emotional disconnection from reality, exhaustion from emotional facades.

Cognitive: Habitual lying and rationalization, mental disconnection from truth, fragmented thinking, deceptive mental patterns, self-deception.

Psychic: The soul fragments itself through deceit; each lie divides what is from what is shown, and self-deception is the deepest division of all.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: Truthfulness in balance is the alignment of word, thought, and deed — honesty held together with care, so that it serves rather than wounds. In excess it becomes the ‘brutal honesty’ that uses truth as a weapon and calls cruelty candor. In deficiency it is the deceit and evasion that split a person into a presented self and a hidden one, a division that is exhausting to maintain and corrosive to the soul.

additional_info.heading: A day in the life
additional_info.text: Asked his opinion of a friend’s flawed plan, the deficient response flatters falsely; the excess response demolishes it with relish. Balanced satya tells the truth the plan needs — clearly, and in a way the friend can actually use — because honesty and kindness are, here, the same movement.

attributes.title: Shaucham — Purity
attributes.aspect: शौचम्   ·   Chapter 13, Chapter 16 The nature of this aspect. Shaucham is purity—both external (physical cleanliness, hygiene) and internal (purity of mind, motives, emotions). It is the foundation that allows clarity and spiritual progress.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Mental · Ontic mode: Virtue — a daivī quality (purity, inner and outer) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Condition (cleanliness of body and mind) · Key relation: A daivī quality; same as Śaucha the niyama (Dim 11).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Virtue — a daivī quality (purity, inner and outer)
coordinate.key_relation: A daivī quality; same as Śaucha the niyama (Dim 11)

balanced.label: Balanced — cleanliness of body, speech, and intent

facets.overview: When Shaucham is present, a person maintains appropriate hygiene and cleanliness while cultivating inner purity of mind and motive. There is natural cleanliness without obsession.
facets.somatic: Clean and well-maintained body, healthy hygiene practices, clean environment, body that radiates wellness, healthy elimination.
facets.emotional: Pure motives and emotional clarity, freedom from corrupting emotions, emotional cleanness, absence of toxic feelings.
facets.cognitive: Pure thoughts and clean mental patterns, freedom from corrupted thinking, mental clarity, ability to think clean thoughts.
facets.psychic: Outer and inner cleanliness mirror each other; purity is the soul keeping its instrument clear enough to reflect the real.

balanced.text:
When Shaucham is present, a person maintains appropriate hygiene and cleanliness while cultivating inner purity of mind and motive. There is natural cleanliness without obsession.

Somatic: Clean and well-maintained body, healthy hygiene practices, clean environment, body that radiates wellness, healthy elimination.

Emotional: Pure motives and emotional clarity, freedom from corrupting emotions, emotional cleanness, absence of toxic feelings.

Cognitive: Pure thoughts and clean mental patterns, freedom from corrupted thinking, mental clarity, ability to think clean thoughts.

Psychic: Outer and inner cleanliness mirror each other; purity is the soul keeping its instrument clear enough to reflect the real.

excess.label: In excess — obsessive purity, scrupulosity

facets.overview: Excessive Shaucham becomes obsessive cleanliness, germphobia, perfectionism about purity, and rigid judgment of others' impurity.
facets.somatic: OCD-like cleaning behaviors, skin damage from over-washing, anxiety about contamination, inability to engage with normal world due to cleanliness obsession.
facets.emotional: Disgust at any imperfection, emotional rigidity about purity, anxiety about contamination, judgment of others.
facets.cognitive: Obsessive thinking about cleanliness, inability to engage with messy reality, perfectionism, mental rigidity about purity standards.
facets.psychic: Purity becomes obsession; the soul’s hunger for cleanness turns into anxious ritual and contempt for ordinary embodiment.

excess.text:
Excessive Shaucham becomes obsessive cleanliness, germphobia, perfectionism about purity, and rigid judgment of others' impurity.

Somatic: OCD-like cleaning behaviors, skin damage from over-washing, anxiety about contamination, inability to engage with normal world due to cleanliness obsession.

Emotional: Disgust at any imperfection, emotional rigidity about purity, anxiety about contamination, judgment of others.

Cognitive: Obsessive thinking about cleanliness, inability to engage with messy reality, perfectionism, mental rigidity about purity standards.

Psychic: Purity becomes obsession; the soul’s hunger for cleanness turns into anxious ritual and contempt for ordinary embodiment.

deficient.label: Deficient — inner and outer impurity, the muddied life

facets.overview: Absence of Shaucham creates physical uncleanliness, neglect of hygiene, internal moral pollution, corrupt motives, and toxic mental states.
facets.somatic: Poor hygiene, dirty body and environment, disease from uncleanliness, physical deterioration, neglect of body.
facets.emotional: Corrupt motives, toxic emotional patterns, emotional contamination, allowing destructive feelings, emotional uncleanness.
facets.cognitive: Corrupt and impure thinking, toxic mental patterns, allowing harmful thoughts, mental contamination.
facets.psychic: Neglect dulls the instrument; impurity of body and mind clouds the very clarity through which the soul would see.

deficient.text:
Absence of Shaucham creates physical uncleanliness, neglect of hygiene, internal moral pollution, corrupt motives, and toxic mental states.

Somatic: Poor hygiene, dirty body and environment, disease from uncleanliness, physical deterioration, neglect of body.

Emotional: Corrupt motives, toxic emotional patterns, emotional contamination, allowing destructive feelings, emotional uncleanness.

Cognitive: Corrupt and impure thinking, toxic mental patterns, allowing harmful thoughts, mental contamination.

Psychic: Neglect dulls the instrument; impurity of body and mind clouds the very clarity through which the soul would see.

attributes.title: Damaḥ — Self-Control
attributes.aspect: दमः   ·   Chapter 10, Chapter 16, Chapter 18 The nature of this aspect. Damah is the disciplined control of the senses—the ability to direct attention and engagement of sight, hearing, taste, touch, and smell consciously rather than being driven by sensory cravings.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Vital · Ontic mode: Virtue — a daivī quality (self-control) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Condition (mastery of the sense-organs) · Key relation: A daivī quality; same as Dama the treasure (Dim 23); kin to Pratyāhāra (Dim 11).
coordinate.key_relation: A daivī quality; same as Dama the treasure (Dim 23); kin to Pratyāhāra (Dim 11)

balanced.label: Balanced — mastered senses serving a chosen life

facets.overview: When Damah is present, a person enjoys sensory experiences without being controlled by them. There is conscious choice in engagement with sense objects.
facets.somatic: Healthy sense organs functioning naturally, appropriate sensory engagement, body that responds to conscious direction, vitality from balanced sensory life.
facets.emotional: Emotional freedom from sensory compulsion, ability to enjoy pleasure without enslavement, contentment regardless of sensory input.
facets.cognitive: Mental capacity to direct senses consciously, freedom from sensory obsession, wisdom about appropriate sensory engagement, focused attention.
facets.psychic: The soul governs the senses wisely; self-control is not war on the senses but their right ordering under a higher aim.

balanced.text:
When Damah is present, a person enjoys sensory experiences without being controlled by them. There is conscious choice in engagement with sense objects.

Somatic: Healthy sense organs functioning naturally, appropriate sensory engagement, body that responds to conscious direction, vitality from balanced sensory life.

Emotional: Emotional freedom from sensory compulsion, ability to enjoy pleasure without enslavement, contentment regardless of sensory input.

Cognitive: Mental capacity to direct senses consciously, freedom from sensory obsession, wisdom about appropriate sensory engagement, focused attention.

Psychic: The soul governs the senses wisely; self-control is not war on the senses but their right ordering under a higher aim.

excess.label: In excess — rigid repression, joyless control

facets.overview: Excessive Damah becomes harsh sensory denial, rejection of legitimate pleasures, sensory deprivation, and rigid suppression of natural senses.
facets.somatic: Sensory deprivation, atrophied sensory function from disuse, physical rigidity, harsh denial of physical pleasures, ascetic excess.
facets.emotional: Emotional suppression of natural pleasure, harsh inner discipline, joylessness, emotional rigidity around pleasure.
facets.cognitive: Rigid mental control that suppresses rather than transforms, harsh judgment of sensory engagement, inability to enjoy normal life.
facets.psychic: Control tightens into mortification; the soul punishes its own instrument and mistakes suppression for mastery.

excess.text:
Excessive Damah becomes harsh sensory denial, rejection of legitimate pleasures, sensory deprivation, and rigid suppression of natural senses.

Somatic: Sensory deprivation, atrophied sensory function from disuse, physical rigidity, harsh denial of physical pleasures, ascetic excess.

Emotional: Emotional suppression of natural pleasure, harsh inner discipline, joylessness, emotional rigidity around pleasure.

Cognitive: Rigid mental control that suppresses rather than transforms, harsh judgment of sensory engagement, inability to enjoy normal life.

Psychic: Control tightens into mortification; the soul punishes its own instrument and mistakes suppression for mastery.

deficient.label: Deficient — senses as masters, the ungoverned life

facets.overview: Absence of Damah creates slavery to senses, inability to control sensory cravings, addiction, hedonistic excess, and unconscious living.
facets.somatic: Physical addiction and excess, sensory overload, body controlled by cravings, health damaged by sensory indulgence, exhausted nervous system.
facets.emotional: Emotional slavery to sensory pleasures, inability to resist temptations, emotional volatility based on sensory input, compulsive seeking.
facets.cognitive: Mental obsession with sensory objects, inability to direct attention, scattered mind controlled by senses, addiction patterns.
facets.psychic: The soul is enslaved by its own senses; without restraint it is pulled wherever stimulation leads.

deficient.text:
Absence of Damah creates slavery to senses, inability to control sensory cravings, addiction, hedonistic excess, and unconscious living.

Somatic: Physical addiction and excess, sensory overload, body controlled by cravings, health damaged by sensory indulgence, exhausted nervous system.

Emotional: Emotional slavery to sensory pleasures, inability to resist temptations, emotional volatility based on sensory input, compulsive seeking.

Cognitive: Mental obsession with sensory objects, inability to direct attention, scattered mind controlled by senses, addiction patterns.

Psychic: The soul is enslaved by its own senses; without restraint it is pulled wherever stimulation leads.

attributes.title: Ahiṁsā — Non-Violence
attributes.aspect: अहिंसा   ·   Chapter 16 (implied through divine qualities) The nature of this aspect. Ahimsa is the principle of non-harm and non-violence, extending to all living beings. It encompasses harmlessness in thought, word, and deed, and reflects respect for all life.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Virtue — a daivī quality (non-harming) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate (the foundational restraint) · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Foundation (the root of the ethical life) · Key relation: A daivī quality; same as Ahiṁsā the yama (Dim 11), the ground of all restraints.
coordinate.causal_status: Foundation (the root of the ethical life)
coordinate.key_relation: A daivī quality; same as Ahiṁsā the yama (Dim 11), the ground of all restraints

balanced.label: Balanced — harmlessness that still protects what is right

facets.overview: When Ahimsa is balanced, a person avoids unnecessary harm, acts with compassion, stands up for what is right without cruelty, protects the vulnerable, and lives peacefully without aggression.
facets.somatic: Gentle and non-aggressive body language, peaceful physical presence, absence of violence or aggression, good physical health from peaceful living, kind and caring touch.
facets.emotional: Compassion and caring for all beings, emotional warmth and kindness, emotional peace and non-reactivity, ability to set boundaries without violence, emotional strength combined with gentleness.
facets.cognitive: Thinking that harms no one, mindful and conscious speech, awareness of impact of words and thoughts, wisdom in addressing injustice without harm, mental peace from non-harming.
facets.psychic: The soul wishes harm to nothing; non-violence is the conduct of one who has begun to see the same Self everywhere.

balanced.text:
When Ahimsa is balanced, a person avoids unnecessary harm, acts with compassion, stands up for what is right without cruelty, protects the vulnerable, and lives peacefully without aggression.

Somatic: Gentle and non-aggressive body language, peaceful physical presence, absence of violence or aggression, good physical health from peaceful living, kind and caring touch.

Emotional: Compassion and caring for all beings, emotional warmth and kindness, emotional peace and non-reactivity, ability to set boundaries without violence, emotional strength combined with gentleness.

Cognitive: Thinking that harms no one, mindful and conscious speech, awareness of impact of words and thoughts, wisdom in addressing injustice without harm, mental peace from non-harming.

Psychic: The soul wishes harm to nothing; non-violence is the conduct of one who has begun to see the same Self everywhere.

excess.label: In excess — passivity that permits harm by inaction

facets.overview: Excessive Ahimsa can lead to passivity, inability to establish boundaries, allowing oneself to be harmed, and inability to take necessary protective action.
facets.somatic: Weakness and inability to defend, poor health from allowing harm, passive body language, physical vulnerability, inability to take protective action.
facets.emotional: Emotional doormat quality, inability to feel appropriate anger at injustice, emotional suppression and victimhood, emotional weakness and ineffectualness, emotional suffering from passivity.
facets.cognitive: Passive thinking and lack of discernment, inability to recognize injustice, mental weakness and indecision, inability to protect others even when necessary, mental paralysis in face of harm.
facets.psychic: Non-violence taken to extremity paralyzes action; the soul, afraid of all harm, cannot perform even necessary duty.

excess.text:
Excessive Ahimsa can lead to passivity, inability to establish boundaries, allowing oneself to be harmed, and inability to take necessary protective action.

Somatic: Weakness and inability to defend, poor health from allowing harm, passive body language, physical vulnerability, inability to take protective action.

Emotional: Emotional doormat quality, inability to feel appropriate anger at injustice, emotional suppression and victimhood, emotional weakness and ineffectualness, emotional suffering from passivity.

Cognitive: Passive thinking and lack of discernment, inability to recognize injustice, mental weakness and indecision, inability to protect others even when necessary, mental paralysis in face of harm.

Psychic: Non-violence taken to extremity paralyzes action; the soul, afraid of all harm, cannot perform even necessary duty.

deficient.label: Deficient — harm in thought, word, and deed

facets.overview: Lack of Ahimsa results in violence, cruelty, harm to others, aggressive behavior, disrespect for life, and inner conflict and guilt.
facets.somatic: Aggressive and violent physical behavior, poor health from violent living, tension and aggression in body, disease from harmful karma, physical harm to others.
facets.emotional: Cruelty and lack of compassion, anger and aggression, emotional hardness, guilt and shame from harming others, emotional isolation from damaged relationships, inner conflict.
facets.cognitive: Violent and harmful thinking patterns, disrespect for life, lack of conscience, inability to consider impact of actions, mental disturbance from causing harm, low consciousness.
facets.psychic: Harm flows freely from a soul that still sees others as separate; violence in thought, word, or deed is separateness in action.

deficient.text:
Lack of Ahimsa results in violence, cruelty, harm to others, aggressive behavior, disrespect for life, and inner conflict and guilt.

Somatic: Aggressive and violent physical behavior, poor health from violent living, tension and aggression in body, disease from harmful karma, physical harm to others.

Emotional: Cruelty and lack of compassion, anger and aggression, emotional hardness, guilt and shame from harming others, emotional isolation from damaged relationships, inner conflict.

Cognitive: Violent and harmful thinking patterns, disrespect for life, lack of conscience, inability to consider impact of actions, mental disturbance from causing harm, low consciousness.

Psychic: Harm flows freely from a soul that still sees others as separate; violence in thought, word, or deed is separateness in action.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: Ahiṁsā in balance is the deep disposition of harmlessness — in thought, word, and deed — that nonetheless does not collapse into passivity where genuine protection is needed. In excess, a misread non-violence permits harm through inaction, refusing the firm response a situation requires. In deficiency, it is simply absent: the casual cruelty of speech, the harm done without a second thought.

additional_info.heading: 4. The Demoniacal Endowment (Āsurī Sampat)
additional_info.text: The qualities below are the recognizable patterns of the ego closed around its own wanting — the textures of a life that binds itself. Because these are vices rather than neutral qualities, their three states read in a particular way: the balanced state is the vice transformed or reduced to its minimal presence; the excess state is its full destructive expression; and the third, over-corrected state is what arises when the vice is forcibly or unwisely suppressed rather than genuinely transformed — a suppression that produces its own distortions, such as ineffectual softness, social ineptitude, or buried resentment.

attributes.title: Dambha — Hypocrisy
attributes.aspect: दम्भ   ·   Chapter 16, Chapter 17, Chapter 18 The nature of this aspect. Dambha is hypocrisy—the false display of virtues one does not actually possess. It is putting on a religious or moral facade for show, while inner reality contradicts the outer presentation.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Affliction — an āsurī quality (hypocrisy / ostentation) · Valence: Affliction (to see through and drop) · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Distortion (a false show of virtue) · Key relation: An āsurī quality; antagonist to Hrī (modesty) and Satya (truthfulness).
coordinate.key_relation: An āsurī quality; antagonist to Hrī (modesty) and Satya (truthfulness)

balanced.label: Balanced — transparency; image and reality aligned

facets.overview: When Dambha is absent and replaced with Arjavam (straightforwardness), a person's inner reality matches their outer presentation. There is integrity without false display.
facets.somatic: Natural and authentic physical presence, body that matches inner state, healthy nervous system from integration, no exhaustion from maintaining facades.
facets.emotional: Emotional authenticity, freedom from emotional pretense, genuine feelings expressed appropriately, integrity in emotional expression.
facets.cognitive: Mental honesty, freedom from self-deception, wisdom about appropriate display versus hiding, integrated thinking aligned with action.
facets.psychic: The soul lets image and reality coincide; with nothing to perform, it can simply be what it is.

balanced.text:
When Dambha is absent and replaced with Arjavam (straightforwardness), a person's inner reality matches their outer presentation. There is integrity without false display.

Somatic: Natural and authentic physical presence, body that matches inner state, healthy nervous system from integration, no exhaustion from maintaining facades.

Emotional: Emotional authenticity, freedom from emotional pretense, genuine feelings expressed appropriately, integrity in emotional expression.

Cognitive: Mental honesty, freedom from self-deception, wisdom about appropriate display versus hiding, integrated thinking aligned with action.

Psychic: The soul lets image and reality coincide; with nothing to perform, it can simply be what it is.

excess.label: In excess — full performance, the curated false self

facets.overview: Severe Dambha creates a life of constant performance—every action is for show. The inner self becomes lost in the maintained facade. This is the excessive/dominant state.
facets.somatic: Constant exhaustion from performing, body language always for show, stress-related illness from pretense, disconnect between body's actual needs and presented state.
facets.emotional: Emotional performance rather than feeling, loss of authentic emotional life, exhaustion from emotional theater, emotional emptiness beneath display.
facets.cognitive: Mental energy consumed by maintaining appearances, sophisticated rationalizations for hypocrisy, distorted self-perception, mental compartmentalization.
facets.psychic: The soul disappears into its own performance; every act staged for an audience, the inner self starving beneath the mask.

excess.text:
Severe Dambha creates a life of constant performance—every action is for show. The inner self becomes lost in the maintained facade. This is the excessive/dominant state.

Somatic: Constant exhaustion from performing, body language always for show, stress-related illness from pretense, disconnect between body's actual needs and presented state.

Emotional: Emotional performance rather than feeling, loss of authentic emotional life, exhaustion from emotional theater, emotional emptiness beneath display.

Cognitive: Mental energy consumed by maintaining appearances, sophisticated rationalizations for hypocrisy, distorted self-perception, mental compartmentalization.

Psychic: The soul disappears into its own performance; every act staged for an audience, the inner self starving beneath the mask.

deficient.label: Over-corrected — social ineptitude; unable to observe ordinary social convention

facets.overview: Complete absence of any social performance might manifest as inability to navigate social conventions, naive disclosure inappropriate to context.
facets.somatic: Inability to maintain even appropriate social composure, raw physical expression in inappropriate contexts.
facets.emotional: Inappropriate emotional disclosure, inability to maintain composure when needed, raw expression of all feelings regardless of context.
facets.cognitive: Inability to filter expression appropriately, no awareness of social context, mental rigidity about full disclosure.
facets.psychic: The pendulum overshoots into social ineptitude; unable to observe even ordinary convention, the soul mistakes awkward bluntness for authenticity.

deficient.text:
Complete absence of any social performance might manifest as inability to navigate social conventions, naive disclosure inappropriate to context.

Somatic: Inability to maintain even appropriate social composure, raw physical expression in inappropriate contexts.

Emotional: Inappropriate emotional disclosure, inability to maintain composure when needed, raw expression of all feelings regardless of context.

Cognitive: Inability to filter expression appropriately, no awareness of social context, mental rigidity about full disclosure.

Psychic: The pendulum overshoots into social ineptitude; unable to observe even ordinary convention, the soul mistakes awkward bluntness for authenticity.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: Dambha is the gap between the self one performs and the self one is — the curated image, the spiritual or moral display put on for others. Its remedy is not better performance but transparency: the willingness to be seen as one actually is. In its full form it organizes an entire life around appearance, until the performer loses track of who they are beneath the act.

additional_info.heading: A day in the life
additional_info.text: Maya catches herself narrating a small kindness to others before she has even finished doing it — the act half for the person, half for the audience in her mind. Seeing the dambha, she does the next kind thing and tells no one, letting it be real precisely by letting it be unwitnessed.

attributes.title: Krodha — Anger
attributes.aspect: क्रोध   ·   Chapter 2, Chapter 3, Chapter 16 The nature of this aspect. Krodha is anger that arises when desires are frustrated. The Gita describes the chain: contemplation of objects → attachment → desire → anger when desire is thwarted → delusion → loss of memory → loss of discrimination → destruction.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Affliction — an āsurī quality (anger) · Valence: Affliction (to transmute) · Guṇa: Rajas-dominant · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Distortion (destructive reactivity) · Key relation: An āsurī quality; the same Krodha as the sthāyibhāva (Dim 8), here as a binding endowment; antagonist to Akrodha (3.3).
coordinate.key_relation: An āsurī quality; the same Krodha as the sthāyibhāva (Dim 8), here as a binding endowment; antagonist to Akrodha (3.3)

balanced.label: Balanced — the energy of anger transmuted into clear action

facets.overview: When Krodha is transcended, a person responds to frustration with wisdom rather than reactive anger. There is capacity for righteous response without being consumed by rage.
facets.somatic: Calm nervous system even when frustrated, healthy stress response, body in equilibrium, no chronic tension from suppressed anger.
facets.emotional: Emotional equanimity in face of frustration, ability to feel disappointment without rage, healthy boundaries without aggression, emotional maturity.
facets.cognitive: Clear thinking when frustrated, freedom from revenge thinking, wisdom about appropriate response, mental peace despite obstacles.
facets.psychic: The soul transmutes the heat of anger into clear, righteous energy; the fire warms and lights rather than destroys.

balanced.text:
When Krodha is transcended, a person responds to frustration with wisdom rather than reactive anger. There is capacity for righteous response without being consumed by rage.

Somatic: Calm nervous system even when frustrated, healthy stress response, body in equilibrium, no chronic tension from suppressed anger.

Emotional: Emotional equanimity in face of frustration, ability to feel disappointment without rage, healthy boundaries without aggression, emotional maturity.

Cognitive: Clear thinking when frustrated, freedom from revenge thinking, wisdom about appropriate response, mental peace despite obstacles.

Psychic: The soul transmutes the heat of anger into clear, righteous energy; the fire warms and lights rather than destroys.

excess.label: In excess — rage, the flooded and destructive mind

facets.overview: Excessive Krodha is rage that destroys discrimination and consciousness. According to Gita, it directly leads to delusion, loss of memory, and ultimately destruction of intellect.
facets.somatic: Frequent rage states, high blood pressure, cardiovascular damage, stress-related illness, muscular tension, depleted adrenals, physical aggression.
facets.emotional: Emotional rage and hostility, inability to control emotional reactions, bitterness, emotional volatility, emotional cruelty when angry.
facets.cognitive: Loss of mental discrimination during anger, distorted thinking, vengeful planning, inability to think clearly, mental destruction from rage cycles.
facets.psychic: Rage drowns discrimination; in its grip the soul loses the very consciousness through which it might choose — the road the Gita calls ruin.

excess.text:
Excessive Krodha is rage that destroys discrimination and consciousness. According to Gita, it directly leads to delusion, loss of memory, and ultimately destruction of intellect.

Somatic: Frequent rage states, high blood pressure, cardiovascular damage, stress-related illness, muscular tension, depleted adrenals, physical aggression.

Emotional: Emotional rage and hostility, inability to control emotional reactions, bitterness, emotional volatility, emotional cruelty when angry.

Cognitive: Loss of mental discrimination during anger, distorted thinking, vengeful planning, inability to think clearly, mental destruction from rage cycles.

Psychic: Rage drowns discrimination; in its grip the soul loses the very consciousness through which it might choose — the road the Gita calls ruin.

deficient.label: Over-suppressed — anger denied until it curdles into resentment and the inability to register genuine wrong

facets.overview: Complete suppression of all anger (rather than transcendence) leads to inability to recognize wrongs, enabling injustice, suppressed rage erupting, and lack of legitimate self-protection.
facets.somatic: Tension from suppressed anger, eventual explosive release, stress-related illness from holding emotions, somatic symptoms.
facets.emotional: Suppressed anger creating bitterness, inability to feel legitimate frustration, emotional dishonesty, inability to set protective boundaries.
facets.cognitive: Denial of valid grievances, mental suppression of legitimate concerns, eventual mental breakdown from accumulated suppression.
facets.psychic: Anger denied festers into resentment; the soul that cannot feel honest indignation also loses the power to recognize and name genuine wrong.

deficient.text:
Complete suppression of all anger (rather than transcendence) leads to inability to recognize wrongs, enabling injustice, suppressed rage erupting, and lack of legitimate self-protection.

Somatic: Tension from suppressed anger, eventual explosive release, stress-related illness from holding emotions, somatic symptoms.

Emotional: Suppressed anger creating bitterness, inability to feel legitimate frustration, emotional dishonesty, inability to set protective boundaries.

Cognitive: Denial of valid grievances, mental suppression of legitimate concerns, eventual mental breakdown from accumulated suppression.

Psychic: Anger denied festers into resentment; the soul that cannot feel honest indignation also loses the power to recognize and name genuine wrong.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: Krodha is anger — which the Gītā names, with kāma, as a gateway to bondage. Its transformation is not suppression (which curdles into resentment) but the channeling of its considerable energy into clear, firm, non-destructive action. In excess it floods the mind, narrows perception to a tunnel, and drives words and acts that outlast the heat by years.

additional_info.heading: A day in the life
additional_info.text: Cut off in traffic, the surge of krodha arrives — heat, tightening, the rehearsed insult. In excess it would own the next twenty minutes. Recognized, the same energy is felt as a wave, allowed to crest, and spent on nothing; she arrives where she is going with the charge already gone.

attributes.title: Pāruṣyam — Harshness
attributes.aspect: पारुष्यम्   ·   Chapter 16 The nature of this aspect. Pärusyam is harshness, particularly in speech but also in manner. It is the rough, hurtful, cruel way of communicating that damages others and reflects an inner state of aggression.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Affliction — an āsurī quality (harshness of speech and manner) · Valence: Affliction (to transmute) · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Distortion (cutting, cruel expression) · Key relation: An āsurī quality; kin to Ugratā (Dim 9); direct antagonist to Mārdavam (gentleness, 3.6).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Affliction — an āsurī quality (harshness of speech and manner)
coordinate.key_relation: An āsurī quality; kin to Ugratā (Dim 9); direct antagonist to Mārdavam (gentleness, 3.6)

balanced.label: Balanced — speech that is firm but never cruel

facets.overview: When Pärusyam is absent and Anudvegakaram (non-hurtful speech) is present, a person speaks truth without harshness. Communication is firm when needed but never cruel.
facets.somatic: Calm physical demeanor, gentle physical presence, healthy nervous system, no chronic tension from aggressive interactions.
facets.emotional: Emotional warmth and gentleness in communication, ability to be firm without cruelty, emotional regulation in speech, peaceful relationships.
facets.cognitive: Mental refinement in expression, thoughtful word choice, wisdom about impact of speech, considerate communication.
facets.psychic: The soul speaks truth that does not wound; firm and kind at once, its words carry weight without leaving injury.

balanced.text:
When Pärusyam is absent and Anudvegakaram (non-hurtful speech) is present, a person speaks truth without harshness. Communication is firm when needed but never cruel.

Somatic: Calm physical demeanor, gentle physical presence, healthy nervous system, no chronic tension from aggressive interactions.

Emotional: Emotional warmth and gentleness in communication, ability to be firm without cruelty, emotional regulation in speech, peaceful relationships.

Cognitive: Mental refinement in expression, thoughtful word choice, wisdom about impact of speech, considerate communication.

Psychic: The soul speaks truth that does not wound; firm and kind at once, its words carry weight without leaving injury.

excess.label: In excess — cutting, contemptuous, wounding speech

facets.overview: Excessive Pärusyam manifests as habitual cruelty in speech, rough manner, hurtful communication patterns, and verbal aggression that damages relationships.
facets.somatic: Aggressive body language, harsh facial expressions, tense nervous system from constant verbal aggression, physical signs of inner aggression.
facets.emotional: Emotional cruelty in expression, inability to communicate without hurting, damaged relationships, emotional aggression, hostile communication patterns.
facets.cognitive: Harsh thinking patterns, cruel mental engagement, inability to consider impact of words, aggressive mental approach to others, contempt.
facets.psychic: The soul cuts with its tongue; cruel speech reveals a hardness within that wounds others and, finally, the speaker.

excess.text:
Excessive Pärusyam manifests as habitual cruelty in speech, rough manner, hurtful communication patterns, and verbal aggression that damages relationships.

Somatic: Aggressive body language, harsh facial expressions, tense nervous system from constant verbal aggression, physical signs of inner aggression.

Emotional: Emotional cruelty in expression, inability to communicate without hurting, damaged relationships, emotional aggression, hostile communication patterns.

Cognitive: Harsh thinking patterns, cruel mental engagement, inability to consider impact of words, aggressive mental approach to others, contempt.

Psychic: The soul cuts with its tongue; cruel speech reveals a hardness within that wounds others and, finally, the speaker.

deficient.label: Over-corrected — ineffectual softness; unable to speak with the firmness a situation needs

facets.overview: Complete absence of any firmness in speech becomes ineffectual softness, inability to communicate hard truths, and weakness disguised as kindness.
facets.somatic: Physical inability to assert through voice and presence, weakness in communication, lack of conviction in speech.
facets.emotional: Emotional inability to express hard truths, false sweetness, inability to be firm when needed, emotional cowardice.
facets.cognitive: Mental inability to express direct positions, evasive communication, lack of clarity in expression.
facets.psychic: Softness becomes ineffectual; unable to speak the firm word a moment requires, the soul lets necessary truths go unsaid.

deficient.text:
Complete absence of any firmness in speech becomes ineffectual softness, inability to communicate hard truths, and weakness disguised as kindness.

Somatic: Physical inability to assert through voice and presence, weakness in communication, lack of conviction in speech.

Emotional: Emotional inability to express hard truths, false sweetness, inability to be firm when needed, emotional cowardice.

Cognitive: Mental inability to express direct positions, evasive communication, lack of clarity in expression.

Psychic: Softness becomes ineffectual; unable to speak the firm word a moment requires, the soul lets necessary truths go unsaid.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: Pāruṣyam is harshness, especially of speech — the cutting remark, the contemptuous tone, the words chosen to wound. Its opposite, which the tradition prizes, is anudvegakara vākya, speech that does not agitate. The cure is the small discipline of pausing before the cutting word and choosing the firm-but-kind one instead.

attributes.title: Kāma — Binding Desire
attributes.aspect: काम   ·   Chapter 3, Chapter 16 The nature of this aspect. Kama is desire, longing, and the drive for pleasure and gratification. It is a natural force that motivates action but can also lead to suffering if uncontrolled. Kama includes sensory pleasures and emotional desires.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Affliction — an āsurī quality (binding, insatiable desire) · Valence: Affliction (to transmute) · Guṇa: Rajas-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Root distortion (the insatiable craving the Gītā names, with Krodha, the gate of hell) · Key relation: An āsurī quality; kin to Rāga (Dim 19) and Kāma-vāsanā (Dim 12); root of the demonic trajectory.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Affliction — an āsurī quality (binding, insatiable desire)
coordinate.causal_status: Root distortion (the insatiable craving the Gītā names, with Krodha, the gate of hell)
coordinate.key_relation: An āsurī quality; kin to Rāga (Dim 19) and Kāma-vāsanā (Dim 12); root of the demonic trajectory

balanced.label: Balanced — desire directed toward the worthy

facets.overview: When Kama is balanced and acknowledged, a person can pursue legitimate pleasures and desires without being enslaved by them. Desires are fulfilled appropriately while maintaining spiritual focus.
facets.somatic: Good sensory enjoyment and appreciation, healthy appetite and sexuality, pleasure in food, movement, and physical experiences, vitality from enjoying life, good health from balanced satisfaction.
facets.emotional: Joy and pleasure in appropriate measure, emotional satisfaction from fulfilling desires, emotional richness and engagement, emotional freedom and spontaneity, balanced attachments.
facets.cognitive: Clear understanding of desires and their nature, wisdom in pursuing desires, ability to enjoy without obsession, mental clarity about what truly satisfies, intentional choice of desires to pursue.
facets.psychic: Desire directed toward the worthy becomes fuel; the soul lets longing pull it toward what genuinely nourishes.

balanced.text:
When Kama is balanced and acknowledged, a person can pursue legitimate pleasures and desires without being enslaved by them. Desires are fulfilled appropriately while maintaining spiritual focus.

Somatic: Good sensory enjoyment and appreciation, healthy appetite and sexuality, pleasure in food, movement, and physical experiences, vitality from enjoying life, good health from balanced satisfaction.

Emotional: Joy and pleasure in appropriate measure, emotional satisfaction from fulfilling desires, emotional richness and engagement, emotional freedom and spontaneity, balanced attachments.

Cognitive: Clear understanding of desires and their nature, wisdom in pursuing desires, ability to enjoy without obsession, mental clarity about what truly satisfies, intentional choice of desires to pursue.

Psychic: Desire directed toward the worthy becomes fuel; the soul lets longing pull it toward what genuinely nourishes.

excess.label: In excess — insatiable craving, the never-enough

facets.overview: Excessive Kama leads to addiction, obsession with pleasure, loss of self-control, damaged relationships, financial ruin, and health problems.
facets.somatic: Addiction to sensory pleasures, poor health from excess (obesity, disease), depleted energy, addiction to alcohol/drugs/sex, physical deterioration, chronic illness from excess.
facets.emotional: Emotional reactivity and obsession, emotional instability and mood swings, emotional neediness and dependence, damaged relationships from excess, emotional emptiness beneath pursuit of pleasure.
facets.cognitive: Obsessive thinking about desired objects, mental slavery to desires, poor judgment and decision-making, inability to think of anything else, loss of self-control, diminished mental clarity.
facets.psychic: The soul is consumed by insatiable craving; the Gita names kāma the eternal enemy, the fire no fuel can satisfy.

excess.text:
Excessive Kama leads to addiction, obsession with pleasure, loss of self-control, damaged relationships, financial ruin, and health problems.

Somatic: Addiction to sensory pleasures, poor health from excess (obesity, disease), depleted energy, addiction to alcohol/drugs/sex, physical deterioration, chronic illness from excess.

Emotional: Emotional reactivity and obsession, emotional instability and mood swings, emotional neediness and dependence, damaged relationships from excess, emotional emptiness beneath pursuit of pleasure.

Cognitive: Obsessive thinking about desired objects, mental slavery to desires, poor judgment and decision-making, inability to think of anything else, loss of self-control, diminished mental clarity.

Psychic: The soul is consumed by insatiable craving; the Gita names kāma the eternal enemy, the fire no fuel can satisfy.

deficient.label: Over-suppressed — desire forcibly repressed, breeding frustration, resentment, and inner conflict

facets.overview: Suppression of Kama without understanding leads to frustration, resentment, inner conflict, and eventual explosive release.
facets.somatic: Lack of vitality from suppressed desires, physical tension and rigidity, sexual dysfunction or issues, poor appetite, weak energy, eventual health problems from repression.
facets.emotional: Emotional frustration and resentment, suppressed emotions seeking outlet, emotional coldness and disconnection, emotional explosiveness when barriers break, emotional emptiness and joylessness.
facets.cognitive: Mental preoccupation with suppressed desires, mental rigidity and control, intrusive thoughts breaking through barriers, mental anxiety and tension, eventual mental breakdown or explosion.
facets.psychic: Desire forcibly repressed turns inward as frustration; the soul at war with its own wanting finds the denied energy curdling into resentment.

deficient.text:
Suppression of Kama without understanding leads to frustration, resentment, inner conflict, and eventual explosive release.

Somatic: Lack of vitality from suppressed desires, physical tension and rigidity, sexual dysfunction or issues, poor appetite, weak energy, eventual health problems from repression.

Emotional: Emotional frustration and resentment, suppressed emotions seeking outlet, emotional coldness and disconnection, emotional explosiveness when barriers break, emotional emptiness and joylessness.

Cognitive: Mental preoccupation with suppressed desires, mental rigidity and control, intrusive thoughts breaking through barriers, mental anxiety and tension, eventual mental breakdown or explosion.

Psychic: Desire forcibly repressed turns inward as frustration; the soul at war with its own wanting finds the denied energy curdling into resentment.

additional_info.heading: External conduct
additional_info.text: Kāma is desire in its binding form — named in the Gītā, with anger, as the great enemy and the gateway to ruin. It is not the innocent enjoyment of life but the insatiable craving that organizes a self around the next acquisition and is never filled. Its transformation is not the killing of all desire but its redirection — the same longing turned from outward grasping toward what actually frees.

additional_info.heading: A day in the life
additional_info.text: The familiar reach for the next purchase, the next pleasure, the next hit of stimulation rises in Maya on a restless evening. In excess it would drive the whole night, each acquisition fading into the next wanting. Seen clearly, the craving is recognized as the outward movement of a mind that has briefly forgotten it is already whole — and, unfed, it passes.

attributes.attribute: The Lived Experience of Upāsanā Itself
attributes.title: The Lived Experience of Upāsanā Itself
attributes.aspect: Before turning to the individual meditations, it is worth examining what the practice of upāsanā feels like in its three states — for the practice, like any other, can be balanced, excessive, or deficient, quite apart from which particular vidyā one takes up.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Mental · Ontic mode: Practice — upāsanā (devoted meditation / re-identification) · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual, toward the chosen object · Causal status: Means (the general practice of meditative re-identification) · Key relation: Genus — the practice of which all the specific vidyās are species.
coordinate.causal_status: Means (the general practice of meditative re-identification)
coordinate.key_relation: Genus — the practice of which all the specific vidyās are species

balanced.label: Balanced — fruitful contemplation that transforms living

facets.overview: In its balanced state, upāsanā does exactly what the texts promise: it relocates the meditator’s felt center of gravity. The person still works, relates, eats, and grieves — fully engaged with the finite world — but the engagement is held within a consciousness that rests, increasingly, in something larger. The result is not withdrawal but a particular lightness: the same events, lived from a self that is no longer purely small, lose their power to devastate. The body is at ease, the breath settled, the emotions present but not overwhelming, and the mind has a stable place to return to.

balanced.text: In its balanced state, upāsanā does exactly what the texts promise: it relocates the meditator’s felt center of gravity. The person still works, relates, eats, and grieves — fully engaged with the finite world — but the engagement is held within a consciousness that rests, increasingly, in something larger. The result is not withdrawal but a particular lightness: the same events, lived from a self that is no longer purely small, lose their power to devastate. The body is at ease, the breath settled, the emotions present but not overwhelming, and the mind has a stable place to return to.

excess.label: In excess — inflation, literalism, escapism

facets.overview: Upāsanā in excess turns the medicine into a poison in two characteristic ways. The first is spiritual inflation: the meditator takes the identification literally and prematurely — ‘I am the infinite’ becomes a claim of the ego rather than a dissolving of it, breeding subtle grandiosity. The second is escapism: the relocation into the larger self becomes an excuse to neglect the finite responsibilities that remain — the relationships, the work, the body — dismissed as ‘merely mortal.’ In both, the practice has been hijacked by the very self it was meant to loosen.

excess.text: Upāsanā in excess turns the medicine into a poison in two characteristic ways. The first is spiritual inflation: the meditator takes the identification literally and prematurely — ‘I am the infinite’ becomes a claim of the ego rather than a dissolving of it, breeding subtle grandiosity. The second is escapism: the relocation into the larger self becomes an excuse to neglect the finite responsibilities that remain — the relationships, the work, the body — dismissed as ‘merely mortal.’ In both, the practice has been hijacked by the very self it was meant to loosen.

deficient.label: Deficient — dry ritual, or no contemplative ground at all

facets.overview: Where upāsanā is deficient, one of two things is true. Either the forms are performed mechanically — the meditation done as dry ritual, the words repeated without the inward relocation that gives them life — or there is no contemplative ground at all, and the person lives wholly identified with the small self, chasing finite satisfactions that never satisfy. This deficiency is the ordinary modern condition: a life of endless finite pursuit — the next acquisition, the next achievement — in which the infinite is never recognized as the only thing that was ever actually wanted.

deficient.text: Where upāsanā is deficient, one of two things is true. Either the forms are performed mechanically — the meditation done as dry ritual, the words repeated without the inward relocation that gives them life — or there is no contemplative ground at all, and the person lives wholly identified with the small self, chasing finite satisfactions that never satisfy. This deficiency is the ordinary modern condition: a life of endless finite pursuit — the next acquisition, the next achievement — in which the infinite is never recognized as the only thing that was ever actually wanted.

additional_info.heading: 4. The Highest Advaitic Meditations
additional_info.text: These are the meditations that go directly to the unconditioned — pure being, the infinite, the imperishable, the light of lights — without an intermediate symbol. They are the most demanding and the most direct, suited to a mind already steadied and discriminating. The flagship is Bhūma Vidyā, which Sanatkumāra gave to Nārada and which the tradition calls the greatest of all the vidyās, summing up the whole of Vedāntic inquiry.

attributes.title: Sat-Vidya (Meditation on Pure Existence)
attributes.aspect: सत् विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. In the beginning was mere Existence, one without a second. From non-existence, existence cannot arise. Meditation on the reality of pure Sat (existence). The contemplative asserts the reality of the Sat aspect of the Absolute whose essential nature is Chit (consciousness) and Ananda (bliss). Establishes the foundation for Tat Tvam Asi realization.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: Practice — a vidyā (meditation on pure Being) · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Individual, toward the Absolute · Causal status: Means (re-identifies the self with pure Existence) · Key relation: A highest Advaitic meditation; object = Sat (pure Being).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Practice — a vidyā (meditation on pure Being)
coordinate.causal_status: Means (re-identifies the self with pure Existence)
coordinate.key_relation: A highest Advaitic meditation; object = Sat (pure Being)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Recognition that pure existence is the foundation of all manifestation. Direct contact with 'is-ness' as one's deepest nature.
facets.somatic: Body experienced as expression of pure existence, grounded sense of being.
facets.emotional: Emotional grounding in the simple fact of being, peace from existence-contact.
facets.cognitive: Mind anchored in 'I am' before all qualities, recognition of pure existence.
facets.psychic: The soul rests in pure Being — Existence one without a second — recognizing the same ‘is’ underlying every passing form.

balanced.text:
Recognition that pure existence is the foundation of all manifestation. Direct contact with 'is-ness' as one's deepest nature.

Somatic: Body experienced as expression of pure existence, grounded sense of being.

Emotional: Emotional grounding in the simple fact of being, peace from existence-contact.

Cognitive: Mind anchored in 'I am' before all qualities, recognition of pure existence.

Psychic: The soul rests in pure Being — Existence one without a second — recognizing the same ‘is’ underlying every passing form.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Static fixation on 'I exist' as ego-identity, missing the creative dynamism of Sat.
facets.somatic: Body fixed in stagnant 'being' posture.
facets.emotional: Emotional flatness mistaken for being.
facets.cognitive: Mental rigidity around eternal-self concept.
facets.psychic: The soul abstracts ‘Existence’ into a concept to contemplate, losing the immediacy of the being it already is.

excess.text:
Static fixation on 'I exist' as ego-identity, missing the creative dynamism of Sat.

Somatic: Body fixed in stagnant 'being' posture.

Emotional: Emotional flatness mistaken for being.

Cognitive: Mental rigidity around eternal-self concept.

Psychic: The soul abstracts ‘Existence’ into a concept to contemplate, losing the immediacy of the being it already is.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Existence taken for granted, never inquired into. The miracle of being is missed; nihilistic confusion possible.
facets.somatic: Body taken as obvious without wonder.
facets.emotional: Emotional flatness about being.
facets.cognitive: Mind doesn't recognize Sat as foundation, may slip into 'all is non-existence' confusion.
facets.psychic: The soul takes only forms and names as real, never noticing the bare Existence in which they all appear.

deficient.text:
Existence taken for granted, never inquired into. The miracle of being is missed; nihilistic confusion possible.

Somatic: Body taken as obvious without wonder.

Emotional: Emotional flatness about being.

Cognitive: Mind doesn't recognize Sat as foundation, may slip into 'all is non-existence' confusion.

Psychic: The soul takes only forms and names as real, never noticing the bare Existence in which they all appear.

attributes.title: Maitreyi Vidya (Self as What All is Dear)
attributes.aspect: मैत्रेयी विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. Not for the love of all this, this all is dear, but for the love of the Self. The Self is to be seen, heard, reflected upon, meditated upon. Recognition that what we love in everything is actually the Self. The four-fold practice: Sravana (hearing), Manana (reflection), Nididhyasana (deep meditation), and Sakshatkara (realization). Where one's Self alone is everywhere, through what can one know what?

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Vital · Ontic mode: Practice — a vidyā · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual, toward the Self · Causal status: Means (sees the Self as the love behind all loves) · Key relation: A highest Advaitic meditation; object = the Self for whose sake all is dear (Bṛhadāraṇyaka).
coordinate.causal_status: Means (sees the Self as the love behind all loves)
coordinate.key_relation: A highest Advaitic meditation; object = the Self for whose sake all is dear (Bṛhadāraṇyaka)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: All love recognized as ultimately Self-love at the deepest sense. Relationships deepened through this recognition.
facets.somatic: Body relationships understood as Self-meetings.
facets.emotional: Emotional love deepened through recognition of underlying Self.
facets.cognitive: Mind sees the Self as the ultimate beloved in all loves.
facets.psychic: The soul discovers that everything it loves, it loves for the sake of the Self; love is traced home to its true source.

balanced.text:
All love recognized as ultimately Self-love at the deepest sense. Relationships deepened through this recognition.

Somatic: Body relationships understood as Self-meetings.

Emotional: Emotional love deepened through recognition of underlying Self.

Cognitive: Mind sees the Self as the ultimate beloved in all loves.

Psychic: The soul discovers that everything it loves, it loves for the sake of the Self; love is traced home to its true source.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Selfish 'Self-love' as ego-justification—using doctrine to justify self-centered behavior.
facets.somatic: Body in selfish pursuits justified spiritually.
facets.emotional: Emotional manipulation under guise of Self-love.
facets.cognitive: Doctrine misused to justify ego-centrism.
facets.psychic: The soul turns this into a justification for self-absorption, using ‘all for the Self’ to excuse withdrawal of real care.

excess.text:
Selfish 'Self-love' as ego-justification—using doctrine to justify self-centered behavior.

Somatic: Body in selfish pursuits justified spiritually.

Emotional: Emotional manipulation under guise of Self-love.

Cognitive: Doctrine misused to justify ego-centrism.

Psychic: The soul turns this into a justification for self-absorption, using ‘all for the Self’ to excuse withdrawal of real care.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Loving objects as objects—no recognition of the Self-dimension that makes them dear.
facets.somatic: Body in possessive relationships.
facets.emotional: Emotional possessiveness, dependency.
facets.cognitive: Mind misses the Self-dimension in all loves.
facets.psychic: The soul loves only outer objects for their own sake, never tracing the love back to the Self that all things serve.

deficient.text:
Loving objects as objects—no recognition of the Self-dimension that makes them dear.

Somatic: Body in possessive relationships.

Emotional: Emotional possessiveness, dependency.

Cognitive: Mind misses the Self-dimension in all loves.

Psychic: The soul loves only outer objects for their own sake, never tracing the love back to the Self that all things serve.

attributes.title: Akshara Vidya (The Imperishable)
attributes.aspect: अक्षर विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. Brahmanas call this the Imperishable. Not coarse, not fine, not short, not long, not glowing, not adhesive, not shadowy, not dark, not airy, not space, not sticky, odourless, tasteless, eye-less, ear-less... It eats nothing. No one eats it. Corollary of the neti-neti method. All phenomenality and relatedness are denied their validity. After negating every conceptual attribute, what remains is That which IS—the Eternal, Akshara. The most rigorous negation-meditation.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: Practice — a vidyā · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Individual, toward the imperishable Absolute · Causal status: Means (meditation on the Imperishable) · Key relation: A highest Advaitic meditation; object = Akṣara (the imperishable Brahman).

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Systematic negation reveals the Imperishable. What cannot be negated is realized as one's own deepest nature.
facets.somatic: Body negated as Self while still maintained—neti-neti practice in lived form.
facets.emotional: Emotions negated as Self while still felt.
facets.cognitive: Mind negates all conceivable attributes; what remains is Akshara.
facets.psychic: The soul approaches the Imperishable by negation — not coarse, not fine, not long, not short — resting at last in what no quality can touch.

facets.psychic: The soul makes negation a stance of mere denial, arriving at a barren blank rather than the fullness beyond qualities.

attributes.title: Akasa Vidya (Space/Ether Meditation)
attributes.aspect: आकाश विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. Akasa (space/ether) is the highest. From Akasa all things arise; into Akasa all return. The space inside the heart is the same as space outside. Meditation on Akasa as Brahman. Recognition that inner and outer space are one. Foundational practice for transcending solid-body identification. All things come from and return to space.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Practice — a vidyā · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual, toward the all-pervading · Causal status: Means (space as symbol of the all-pervading Self) · Key relation: A highest Advaitic meditation; object = Ākāśa (space) as Brahman; corresponds to the Space element (Dim 1).

facets.cognitive: Mind recognizes itself as space-like consciousness.
facets.psychic: The soul contemplates space as the nearest emblem of Brahman — that from which all arises and into which all returns — and learns spaciousness as a way of being.

attributes.title: Uddalaka-Aruni Vidya (Self-Brahman Identity)
attributes.aspect: उद्दालक-आरुणि विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. Meditation on the absolute identity of the Self and Brahman—the Inner Controller (Antaryami) in all elements, senses, mind. Meditation on the absolute identity of the Self and Brahman through the Antaryami teaching. He who inhabits all but is not known by any—is your Self, the inner controller, the immortal.

facets.cognitive: Mind realizes the Inner Controller as Self-Brahman.
facets.psychic: The soul recognizes the one Inner Controller threading through all beings as its very own Self — the antaryāmin who rules from within.

attributes.title: Jyotisham Jyotir-Vidya (Light of Lights)
attributes.aspect: ज्योतिषां ज्योति विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. He is the Light of Lights. The supreme Self-luminosity by which all other lights are perceived as light. The Vidya of the Light of Lights. Meditation on Brahman as the Self-luminous principle that illumines sun, moon, fire, lightning—the original luminosity behind every perception of light.

attributes.title: Satya-Jnana-Ananta Vidya (Truth-Knowledge-Infinity)
attributes.aspect: सत्य ज्ञान अनन्त विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. Satyam, Jnanam, Anantam Brahma—Truth, Knowledge, Infinity is Brahman. Whoever knows this hidden in the cave (heart) and the highest heaven obtains all desires. Meditation on Brahman as Satyam (Truth), Jnanam (Knowledge), Anantam (Infinity) simultaneously. The triadic definition that points beyond conceptual limit. Brahman known in the heart-cave equally with the highest heaven.

facets.somatic: Body recognized as participant in Truth-Knowledge-Infinity.
facets.emotional: Emotional immersion in triadic Brahman.
facets.cognitive: Mind grasps the triadic nature of ultimate reality.
facets.psychic: The soul contemplates Brahman as Truth, Knowledge, and Infinity together, and finds its own depths answering to each.

attributes.title: Bhūma Vidyā — Meditation on the Infinite
attributes.aspect: भूम विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. Bhūma Vidyā is the meditation on the unconditioned Infinite Plenum. Its core recognition, given by Sanatkumāra to a Nārada already vast in learning yet still sorrowful, is devastatingly simple: in the finite there is no happiness; only in the Infinite — where one sees nothing else, hears nothing else, knows nothing else — is there happiness. The knower of this vidyā becomes svarāt, self-ruled, dependent on nothing. The tradition holds it as the summit of all Upanishadic enquiry.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: Practice — a vidyā · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Individual, toward the Infinite · Causal status: Means (meditation on the Infinite as alone joy) · Key relation: A highest Advaitic meditation; object = Bhūma (the Infinite Plenum, Chāndogya).

facets.somatic: Body engaged with finite world while consciousness rests in Infinite.
facets.emotional: Emotional understanding that joy requires the Infinite, finite engagements appropriately contextualized.
facets.cognitive: Mind oriented toward Bhuma as the only source of lasting happiness.
facets.psychic: The soul tastes the Infinite (bhūmā) in which alone there is happiness; resting in the boundless, it stops seeking fullness in the finite.

additional_info.heading: 5. The Heart-Centered Meditations
additional_info.text: These meditations enter through the heart — not the physical organ but the dahara, the ‘small space’ within the heart-cave said to contain the whole of reality. For meditators of a devotional or feeling temperament, the heart is the most natural doorway: warm, intimate, and immediate. The flagship is Dahara Vidyā, paired closely with Śāṇḍilya Vidyā, which gives the heart’s space as the dwelling-place of the Self.

attributes.title: Sandilya Vidya (Heart-Cave Meditation)
attributes.aspect: शाण्डिल्य विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. All this is Brahman. Tranquil, one should worship it as that from which all comes, into which all dissolves, in which all lives. The Self in the heart is Brahman; into Him I enter on departing. Meditation on Self in the heart-cave conceiving it as simultaneously smaller than an atom and bigger than the universe. Contains all karmas, desires, mind, life, smells, tastes. Unspeaking and unconcerned. Suited to both Saguna and Nirguna meditation.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Vital (the heart) · Ontic mode: Practice — a vidyā · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual, toward the indwelling Self · Causal status: Means (meditation on Brahman in the heart) · Key relation: A heart-centered meditation; object = the Self in the heart-cave (Śāṇḍilya).

facets.cognitive: Mind absorbed in heart's paradoxical infinity.
facets.psychic: The soul holds ‘all this is Brahman’ as a living conviction, meeting each moment as the One and resting in the heart’s tranquil Self.

attributes.title: Akshi Vidya (Eye-Self Meditation)
attributes.aspect: अक्षि विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. The Person seen in the eye is the Atman, immortal, fearless. This is Brahman. To this Brahman, even when ghee or water is poured, it goes to its source—the eye Purusha. Meditation on the Purusha (Person) seen reflected in another's eye as the immortal, fearless Atman/Brahman. Recognition of the Self looking through every eye. Knower attains the Saguna Brahman beyond waking state and accrues all sought after.

facets.cognitive: Mind sees the one Self looking through every pair of eyes.
facets.psychic: The soul recognizes the immortal, fearless Person seen in the eye as its own true Self, deathless behind the looking.
