attributes.title: Bhrigu-Varuni Vidya (Progressive Inquiry to Ananda)
attributes.aspect: भृगु-वारुणि विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. Bhrigu progressively realizes Brahman as: food, prana, mind, intellect, bliss. 'That from which beings are born, by which they live, into which they return—desire to know That.' Final realization: Ananda is Brahman. Aims at the innermost seat of Bliss, on a small part of which other creatures are living. Progressive penetration through five sheaths via tapas (focused practice). Each provisional identification surpassed until ananda (bliss-sheath) is reached.

facets.emotional: Emotional layers progressively transcended toward ananda.
facets.cognitive: Mind systematically penetrates from gross to subtle to bliss.
facets.psychic: The soul, like Bhṛgu, climbs from food to breath to mind to bliss, discovering Brahman at ever-subtler depths until it rests in ānanda.

attributes.title: Ushasta-Kahola Vidya (Self as Witness in Senses)
attributes.aspect: उषस्त-कहोल विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. The Self is the inner controller, the witness, that which sees through every act of seeing, hears through every act of hearing. It cannot be made an object. Meditation on the Self as the unwitnessable witness—seeing through every act of perception, present in every sense-engagement, but never itself becoming an object.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Mental · Ontic mode: Practice — a vidyā · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual, toward the inner witness · Causal status: Means (the Self as the witness within all functions) · Key relation: A heart-centered meditation; object = the immediate inner Self / witness (Bṛhadāraṇyaka).

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.somatic: Body's perceptions all witnessed by the same unmoved Seer.
facets.emotional: Emotional witnessing without identification.
facets.cognitive: Mind recognizes that the witness can never become the witnessed.
facets.psychic: The soul recognizes the witness that sees through every sense and cannot itself be seen — the inner controller that is its own Self.

attributes.title: Dahara Vidyā — The Small Space in the Heart
attributes.aspect: दहर विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. Dahara Vidyā is the meditation on the daharākāśa, the small space within the lotus of the heart — a space the Chāndogya declares to be as vast within as the whole external cosmos, containing earth and sky, sun and stars, and all that one has and has not yet attained. To dwell upon this inner space is to discover that what one had taken to be a tiny interior is, in truth, the dwelling-place of the infinite Self. The heart becomes not a private chamber but the doorway to everything.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Vital (the heart) · Ontic mode: Practice — a vidyā · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual, toward the infinite-in-the-heart · Causal status: Means (the tiny space in the heart holding the infinite) · Key relation: A heart-centered meditation; object = the dahara-ākāśa (small space) in the heart (Chāndogya).

facets.cognitive: Mind enters heart-space and discovers infinity within.
facets.psychic: The soul discovers the vast space within the ‘small’ cave of the heart, where the whole cosmos is found within; outer seeking turns inward and home.

additional_info.heading: 6. The Cosmic Identity Meditations
additional_info.text: These meditations identify the personal self with a cosmic body — the universe seen as the very form of the Self, so that what one had taken to be ‘out there’ is recognized as one’s own larger body. Vaiśvānara Vidyā meditates on the cosmic person whose limbs are the regions of the world; Madhu Vidyā, the Honey Doctrine, sees all beings as honey to one another, each the sweet sustenance and product of all the rest, bound in radiant mutual dependence.

attributes.title: Vaisvanara Vidya (Universal Self/Cosmic Body Meditation)
attributes.aspect: वैश्वानर विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. Each seeker had partial conception (heaven, sun, air, ether, water, earth). King teaches: the whole cosmos is the Universal Self. Mouth=heaven, eyes=sun, breath=air, body=ether, bladder=water, feet=earth. Meditation on cosmic Self as one's expanded body. To meditate on a part is to miss the whole. The realization effected is proportional to the extensiveness or inclusiveness of conception. Body extended to cosmic dimensions in awareness.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Physical (the cosmic body) · Ontic mode: Practice — a vidyā · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual, toward the cosmic person (Ādhidaivika) · Causal status: Means (the Self as the universal, cosmic person) · Key relation: A cosmic-identity meditation; object = Vaiśvānara; same as Vaishvanara Vidya (Dim 4).

attributes.title: Upakosala Vidya (Three Fires Teaching)
attributes.aspect: उपकोसल विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. Prana is Brahman. Happiness is Brahman. Ether is Brahman. Happiness and Ether are one. The Garhapatya, Dakshinagni, and Ahavaniya fires teach. The three sacred fires instruct: they are Purushas in sun, moon, lightning. Collective meditation on these leads through Devayana (path of gods). The Centre of Joy reached through subjective (not objective) conception of these elemental Purushas.

attributes.title: Antaraditya Vidya (Sun-Within Meditation)
attributes.aspect: अन्तरादित्य विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. The Purusha within the sun and the Purusha within the eye are the same. The cosmic representation of inner truth. Counterpart of Akshi Vidya. Cosmic representation through the same insight. Meditation on the Purusha within the Sun as identical with the Purusha within the eye. Comprehensive identification of subjective and objective.

attributes.title: Aditya Vidya (Sun as Liberator)
attributes.aspect: आदित्य विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. One who realises the Aditya-Purusha through this Vidya goes above sin and reaches Saguna Brahman. Specific meditation on the Sun as Aditya-Purusha—the personal Purusha within the solar disc. Realization through this Vidya: one transcends sin and reaches Saguna Brahman.

attributes.title: Satyakama Vidya (Four-Fold Brahman)
attributes.aspect: सत्यकाम विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. Four nature-teachers each give one quarter of Brahman. The meditator becomes shining, endless, dazzling, with an abode/support. Meditation taught by nature itself: bull, fire, swan, diver-bird each reveal one quarter of Brahman. The Vidya makes one 'shining, endless, dazzling and with an abode or support.' Recognition that nature itself is teacher when one is ready.

facets.psychic: The soul learns from the whole world as teacher — directions, fire, sun, breath each giving a quarter of Brahman — and humility before creation becomes the path.

attributes.title: Madhu Vidyā — The Honey Doctrine of Cosmic Sweetness
attributes.aspect: मधु विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. Madhu Vidyā, the Honey Doctrine, is the meditation on the radiant mutual interdependence of all things. Just as honey is gathered by bees from countless flowers and becomes a single sweetness in which no individual flower can be distinguished, so each being is the honey of all other beings, and all beings are the honey of each — the sun the honey of all creatures, all creatures the honey of the sun. To dwell upon this is to dissolve the felt boundary between self and world into a single, mutually nourishing whole, luminous and sweet.

Psychic: The soul tastes the mutual sweetness (madhu) by which each being is honey to every other; the world is seen as one web of reciprocal nourishment.

additional_info.heading: 7. The Cosmic Process Meditations
additional_info.text: These meditations contemplate the great processes — death, rebirth, the journey of the soul, the cosmic ‘fires’ through which life is offered and reborn. The flagship, Pañcāgni Vidyā, the Doctrine of the Five Fires, traces how the offering of life passes through five cosmic fires (heaven, rain-cloud, earth, man, woman) in an endless round, and contrasts the path of return (devayāna) with the path of recurrence (pitṛyāna). To meditate here is to place one’s small life within the vast rhythm of the cosmos.

attributes.title: Course After Death (Devayana Path)
attributes.aspect: ·   Chapter 8, Section 6 The nature of this aspect. The Devayana (Path of the Gods) is the after-death trajectory for those who have prepared through practice. Each stage represents progressively subtle dimensions: flame→day→bright fortnight→northern course→year→sun→moon→lightning→Amanava Purusha→Brahmaloka. From this path there is no return.

attributes.title: Svetaketu Vidya (Five-Fire/Devayana Knowledge)
attributes.aspect: श्वेतकेतु विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. Through the experiments (salt in water, banyan seed) and ninefold repetition: 'That subtle essence which is the Self of all this—That you are' (Tat Tvam Asi). The teaching that culminates in Tat Tvam Asi realization. Through experiments revealing the subtle essence pervading all. Repeated nine times to drive in the identity of individual Self with universal Self.

attributes.title: Shodasakala Vidya (Sixteen-Part Person)
attributes.aspect: षोडशकला विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. The Purusha has sixteen kalas (parts): prana, faith, space, air, fire, water, earth, senses, mind, food, virility, austerity, mantras, karma, worlds, name. When realized, all sixteen merge into the Self. Meditation recognizing the sixteen parts that constitute the personal Purusha. Each is a temporary manifestation; upon realization all sixteen merge back into the Self. Map of human constitution and dissolution.

attributes.title: Pañcāgni Vidyā — The Doctrine of the Five Fires
attributes.aspect: पञ्चाग्नि विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. Pañcāgni Vidyā contemplates the five cosmic fires through which the offering of life circulates — the way the libation of faith rises to become the moon, descends as rain, becomes food, becomes seed, and is reborn as a being — an endless round of offering and renewal. Knowing it, the meditator distinguishes the two great courses after death: the path of light, the devayāna, that leads on toward Brahman and does not return, and the path of recurrence that comes back again. To dwell on this is to see one’s life as a single moment in a vast, ordered cosmic process.

additional_info.heading: 8. The Sound, Sacrifice, and Journey Meditations
additional_info.text: Three further meditations complete the families: one that enters through sacred sound, one that consecrates the whole arc of a life, and one that orients the soul toward its ultimate journey.

attributes.title: Udgitha Vidya (Sacred Singing of OM)
attributes.aspect: उद्गीथ विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. Speech=Rik, Prana=Saman, OM=Udgitha. The gods conquered demons through prana-as-Udgitha alone—the only one demons couldn't pierce with evil. Udgitha is singing of OM—the sacred syllable as essence of speech and prana. 'Ut'=above, 'gi'=lifted, 'tha'=established. Both Saguna and Nirguna realizations possible. Sound becomes transformation practice.

facets.cognitive: Mind unified through Udgitha practice, lifted upward.
facets.psychic: The soul takes up the sacred chant of OM as the udgītha, and through that vibration gathers its scattered powers toward the divine.

attributes.title: Purusha Vidya (Life as Sacrifice - 116 Years)
attributes.aspect: पुरुष विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. The person is a sacrifice. First 24 years = morning oblation. Next 44 years = midday oblation. Next 48 years (till 116) = third oblation. Mahidasa lived 116 years, declaring sickness could not destroy him. Meditation on one's own life as a continuous sacrifice—the three life stages (study, household, spiritual practice) as three oblations. Knower of this Vidya lives 116 years without sickness. The universalisation of life-conception leads to immediate liberation.

attributes.title: Paryanka Vidya (Couch of Brahma)
attributes.aspect: पर्यङ्क विद्या   · The nature of this aspect. The desireless soul passes beyond moon to lightning, meets Guru, says 'I am thyself.' Passes through Agni, Vayu, Varuna, Indra, Prajapati, Brahma. To Brahma says 'You are everything, and I am yourself.' Meditation on the soul's post-mortem journey through cosmic worlds toward Hiranyagarbha's couch (paryanka). The throne Vichakshana in hall Vibhu-Pramita is of immeasurable splendor. One reaching this gets power of creating/preserving/destroying worlds if in tune with the One on the throne.

attributes.title: Saptanga Yoga (Ghatastha Yoga)
attributes.aspect: Saptanga Yoga (Ghatastha Yoga)   · The nature of this aspect. Gheranda's distinctive Seven-Limb Yoga: Purification, Firmness, Steadiness, Calmness, Lightness, Direct Perception, and Liberation — achieved respectively through Shatkarma, Asana, Mudra, Pratyahara, Pranayama, Dhyana, and Samadhi. Don't skip to advanced practices. The 7 stages each cure a different problem; treat life-development as systematic, not random.

Psychic: The soul follows the sevenfold ascent of the body-vessel, each stage preparing the next toward liberation.

attributes.title: Ghata (The Body-Vessel)
attributes.aspect: Ghata (The Body-Vessel)   · The nature of this aspect. The body is a 'pot' (ghata) — like an unbaked clay pot in water, it decays unless baked in the fire of yogic training. Karma and rebirth turn like a Persian wheel until the body is purified by sadhana. Your body is not a separate problem from your spiritual life — it is the vessel that holds it. Care for it as you would care for the most sacred object you own.

attributes.title: Abhyasa (Sustained Practice)
attributes.aspect: Abhyasa (Sustained Practice)   · The nature of this aspect. Gheranda's opening: 'As by learning the alphabets one masters all sciences through practice, so by practising the physical training, one acquires Knowledge of the True.' Knowledge is alphabet; practice is fluency. Reading about anything—yoga, sourdough, language, instrument—gives only the alphabet. Daily practice is what teaches fluency. Without it, all is theory.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Practice — sustained, devoted repetition (abhyāsa) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Rajas refined to Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Mechanism (repetition that establishes the ground) · Key relation: With Vairāgya, one of the two wings of yoga (Yoga Sūtra 1.12); the engine of all the practices.

additional_info.heading: 3. The Foundation: Ethics, Diet, and the Conditions of Progress
additional_info.text: Before any technique, the Pradīpikā lays an ethical and lifestyle foundation — the same yamas and niyamas that underlie all yoga, with a characteristic Haṭha emphasis on the body. Ahiṁsā, satya, and brahmacharya (the wise stewardship of vital energy) are named, and to them is added mitāhāra — moderate, wholesome diet — which Haṭha treats as nearly a technique in itself, since the body it is refining is built, quite literally, from what one eats. Santoṣa, contentment, anchors the niyamas.

attributes.title: Ahimsa
attributes.aspect: Ahimsa   · The nature of this aspect. Non-injury to living beings in thought, word, and deed; the first of ten yamas listed by Svatmarama. Pause before reactive speech, harsh judgment, or unconscious harm; choose the response that does the least damage.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Virtue — a yama (non-harming) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Foundation (the ethical ground) · Key relation: Same as Ahiṁsā the yama (Dim 11) and the daivī quality (Dim 24); the ethical precondition of haṭha.
coordinate.key_relation: Same as Ahiṁsā the yama (Dim 11) and the daivī quality (Dim 24); the ethical precondition of haṭha

facets.psychic: The soul builds its practice on harmlessness; the body refined for yoga is never refined by harming another or oneself.

Six ‘destroyers’ (ṣaḍ-doṣa) ruin yoga — overeating, overexertion, over-talking, rigid rule-following, restless company, and instability — while six ‘enablers’ (ṣaṭ-sampatti) secure it: enthusiasm, perseverance, discrimination, faith, courage, and the avoidance of idle company.

attributes.title: Shad-Dosha (Six Destroyers)
attributes.aspect: Shad-Dosha (Six Destroyers)   · The nature of this aspect. Six causes that destroy yogic progress: atyahara (overeating), prayasa (over-exertion), prajalpa (idle talk), niyama-graha (rigid rule-bound-ness), jana-sanga (entanglement with people), laulya (fickleness/restlessness). Audit your day weekly: where am I overeating, overworking, gossiping, rigidly clinging, over-socializing, or flitting? Fix the one biggest leak.

attributes.title: Shat-Sampatti (Six Enablers)
attributes.aspect: Shat-Sampatti (Six Enablers)   · The nature of this aspect. Six qualities that bring success: utsaha (enthusiasm), sahasa (courage/daring), dhairya (perseverance), tattva-jnana (discrimination), nishchaya (resolute conviction), jana-sanga-parityaga (avoiding wrong company). When facing any worthwhile pursuit, check: do I have enthusiasm, courage, perseverance, understanding, resolve, and the right company? If one is missing, start there.

additional_info.heading: 4. Purification (Śodhana / Ṣaṭkarma)
additional_info.text: The first active work is cleansing. The ṣaṭkarmas — six purificatory actions — remove the physical impurities and energetic blockages that make the body restless and the breath uneven. The Gheraṇḍa adds tongue-cleansing (jihvā śodhana) and shares with the Pradīpikā the practice of trāṭaka, steady gazing, which cleanses and concentrates at once. Their daily-life value is that a clean, light body simply feels different to inhabit — clearer, steadier, less driven by the dullness and agitation that impurity breeds.

attributes.title: Trataka
attributes.aspect: Trataka   · The nature of this aspect. Steady gaze on a small object until tears flow; cleanses eyes, removes sloth, and develops concentration. Daily, look steadily at one point (candle flame, dot on wall) for 3-5 minutes. This single practice radically strengthens focus.

attributes.title: Jihva Shodhana (Tongue Cleansing)
attributes.aspect: Jihva Shodhana (Tongue Cleansing)   · The nature of this aspect. Specific daily cleansing of the tongue — pulling it gently with three fingers, then massaging with butter; said to remove aging, disease, and death. The tongue is the gateway to digestion and prana. Scrape your tongue every morning. The coating that accumulates overnight is removed toxins; not removing it means re-ingesting them. A 30-second daily habit.

attributes.title: Ṣaṭkarma — The Six Cleansings
attributes.aspect: Shatkarma   · The nature of this aspect. The ṣaṭkarmas are the six purificatory techniques — working on the digestive tract, the nasal passages, the abdomen, the eyes, the breath, and the skull — by which Haṭha removes the gross impurities that obstruct the free flow of prāṇa and keep the body restless. They are recommended especially for those in whom the elements are unbalanced, as a preparation that makes the subtler practices possible. Their spirit is not punishment of the body but its careful, respectful preparation.

additional_info.heading: 5. Firmness (Dṛḍhatā / Āsana)
additional_info.text: In Haṭha, āsana means primarily a steady, comfortable seat — a body that can be held still and at ease for long periods, so that breath and mind can settle. The Pradīpikā prizes Siddhāsana, the ‘accomplished pose,’ above almost all others; Śavāsana and the Gheraṇḍa’s Mṛtāsana (corpse pose) cultivate the deep relaxation that is its complement. The point of posture here is not athletic display but the firmness (dṛḍhatā) that makes the body a reliable, quiet platform.

attributes.title: Siddhasana
attributes.aspect: Siddhasana   · The nature of this aspect. The 'Adept's Pose' — Svatmarama calls it chief among all 84 asanas; left heel at perineum, right above genitals, chin lock, gaze at brow center; cleanses the 72,000 nadis. Choose ONE seated posture you can hold for 30+ minutes, and practice it daily. Stability of body is the door to stability of mind.

attributes.title: Asana for Dridhata
attributes.aspect: Asana for Dridhata   · The nature of this aspect. Of 84 lakh asanas described by Shiva, 32 are useful for humans, 4 are essential. Asanas cultivate Dridhata — firmness/strength — the second limb. Posture builds physical capacity for sustained practice. Strength isn't optional in spiritual life. A weak body cannot sit for meditation, cannot withstand austerity, cannot hold its center when life applies force. Build it daily.

When utterly spent—physically or emotionally—10 minutes lying perfectly still on the floor (not bed) restores more than 30 minutes of distracted rest.

attributes.title: Āsana — The Steady Seat
attributes.aspect: Asana   · The nature of this aspect. Āsana in the Haṭha sense is the cultivation of a posture that is both steady and comfortable — a body that can be held still long enough, and at ease enough, that it stops clamoring for attention and lets the breath and mind come to rest. Firmness of body is sought not for its own sake but as the foundation on which everything subtler is built; an unsteady, complaining body cannot support meditation.

additional_info.heading: 6. The Locks and the Seals (Bandhas & Mudrās)
additional_info.text: With a clean, steady body, Haṭha turns to the direct management of energy. The bandhas — the root lock (mūla bandha), the abdominal lock (uḍḍiyāna), and the throat lock (jālandhara) — are muscular-energetic ‘locks’ that contain and redirect prāṇa, drawing it into the central channel. The mudrās — ‘seals’ such as Khecharī (the tongue turned back) and Śāmbhavī (the inward gaze) — are subtler still, sealing and concentrating energy and attention. These are the most powerful and the most easily misused of the Haṭha tools, and the texts surround them with cautions about proper guidance.

attributes.title: Uddiyana Bandha
attributes.aspect: Uddiyana Bandha   · The nature of this aspect. Upward-flying lock—after exhale, drawing belly up and back under ribs; said to lead to youth and immortality. Even gently engaging the lower belly in throughout the day strengthens core, supports digestion, and creates upward energy.

attributes.title: Khechari Mudra
attributes.aspect: Khechari Mudra   · The nature of this aspect. Tongue rolled back into nasopharynx; sips inner nectar (amrita) said to flow from soma center; brings perfection in samadhi. Even the simple version—tongue tip touching the upper palate during meditation—stills the mind faster than effort to think less.

attributes.title: Shambhavi Mudra
attributes.aspect: Shambhavi Mudra   · The nature of this aspect. Inner-aimed, outer-gazing—gaze externally fixed (often at brow point) while attention is wholly internal; called the most secret of mudras. While interacting with the world, keep one strand of attention turned inward to the witness. Engagement and presence both intact.

attributes.title: Nabho Mudra
attributes.aspect: Nabho Mudra   · The nature of this aspect. 'Whatever business a yogi may be engaged in, wherever he may be, let him keep his tongue turned upward (toward soft palate) and restrain the breath.' A continuous, portable mudra integrating practice into all activity. Throughout the day, gently place the tongue tip on the upper palate. This single subtle gesture quiets mental chatter and steadies breath — and you can do it in meetings.

attributes.title: Ashwini Mudra
attributes.aspect: Ashwini Mudra   · The nature of this aspect. Repeated contraction and relaxation of the anal sphincter ('Ashwini' = horse, named for the horse's reflex). Awakens shakti, destroys rectal disease, gives strength, prevents premature death. A few rounds of conscious pelvic-floor contraction-release throughout the day strengthens core, improves digestion and pelvic health, and creates an upward energetic flow.

attributes.title: Pancha Dharana (Five Element Concentrations)
attributes.aspect: Pancha Dharana (Five Element Concentrations)   · The nature of this aspect. Five meditations on the five elements — Parthivi (earth/yellow/stability), Ambhasi (water/white/flow), Agneyi (fire/red/transformation), Vayavi (air/black/movement), Akashi (ether/sea-blue/space). Each held for 5 ghatikas with Pranayama. When unbalanced, you can diagnose by element: Need stability? Earth. Stuck? Water. Cold? Fire. Stagnant? Air. Trapped? Space. Match the practice to the imbalance.

attributes.title: Tadagi Mudra
attributes.aspect: Tadagi Mudra   · The nature of this aspect. 'Tank' or 'pond' mudra — sitting in Paschimottanasana, hollow the stomach inward and upward, making the belly like a tank. Destroys decay and death. A few rounds of belly-hollowing on the exhale, several times a day, tones digestive organs, lifts energy, and reverses the slumped 'screen posture' habit.

attributes.title: Mūla Bandha — The Root Lock
attributes.aspect: Mula Bandha   · The nature of this aspect. Mūla bandha, the root lock, is the contraction and lifting of the pelvic floor by which the downward-moving apāna energy is reversed and drawn upward to meet the prāṇa, kindling the inner fire and rousing the dormant kuṇḍalinī. It is a cornerstone of Haṭha energetics — a small, precise physical action with large subtle consequences. Like all the bandhas, it is to be learned under guidance and applied with care, for it works directly on the vital energies.

additional_info.heading: 7. Withdrawal, Meditation, and Realization
additional_info.text: The upper rungs of the ladder turn inward. The Gheraṇḍa’s pratyāhāra (withdrawal of the senses, including a striking ‘withdrawal from praise and censure’) calms the outward pull; its three forms of dhyāna — gross, light, and subtle — lead perception inward; and both texts converge on the awakening of kuṇḍalinī and the absorption in inner sound (nāda anusandhāna) that culminate in manonmanī (the mind beyond mind), the sahaja avasthā (the natural spontaneous state), and finally samādhi.

attributes.title: Pratyahara
attributes.aspect: Pratyahara   · The nature of this aspect. Withdrawing the mind from sense-attractions: 'Whenever the mind wanders, draw it back and bring it under control.' Applied separately to sight, sound, smell, taste — and to praise and censure. When a sense pulls your mind — beautiful sight, irritating sound, tempting smell, sharp criticism — practice the pause. Notice the pull, then choose whether to engage. Don't react automatically.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Stage / practice — pratyāhāra (sense-withdrawal) · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Condition (gathers the attention inward) · Key relation: Same as Pratyāhāra the 5th limb (Dim 11).

attributes.title: Pratyahara of Praise & Censure
attributes.aspect: Pratyahara of Praise & Censure   · The nature of this aspect. Specifically named: when praise or censure, sweet speech or terrible speech reaches the mind, withdraw the mind and bring it under control of the Self. The social-emotional dimension of sense-restraint. Notice how much energy you spend chasing approval or defending against criticism. Both are sense-objects. The mature response: receive, but don't be moved.

attributes.title: Sthula Dhyana (Gross Meditation)
attributes.aspect: Sthula Dhyana (Gross Meditation)   · The nature of this aspect. Contemplation of a specific form — visualizing a luminous island of jewels with kalpa tree, throne, and the Ishta Devata (chosen deity) or one's Guru. Detailed sensory imagery as concentration object. Beginners' meditation works better with form than with formlessness. Visualize a vivid scene daily — your guru, deity, or even a serene place. Detail breeds concentration.

attributes.title: Jyotir Dhyana (Light Meditation)
attributes.aspect: Jyotir Dhyana (Light Meditation)   · The nature of this aspect. Contemplation of Brahma as luminous flame — either as Kundalini-flame at Muladhara or as Om-light between the eyebrows. 'A hundred times superior to gross meditation.' Once form-meditation is steady, shift to contemplating just light — a candle-flame internalized at the heart or between brows. Light is more subtle than form.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: Practice — meditation on light · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Means (concentration on inner light) · Key relation: The second of three dhyānas (luminous); between gross and subtle.
coordinate.key_relation: The second of three dhyānas (luminous); between gross and subtle

attributes.title: Sukshma Dhyana (Subtle Meditation)
attributes.aspect: Sukshma Dhyana (Subtle Meditation)   · The nature of this aspect. When Kundalini joins Atma and leaves the body through the eyes, walking the 'royal road' (astral light). Attained via Shambhavi Mudra — gazing fixedly without winking. 'A hundred thousand times superior to Jyotir Dhyana.' The deepest meditation feels like nothing-special — formless, lightless, just open awareness. Don't chase experiences; rest as awareness itself.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: Practice — subtle meditation · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Means (meditation on the formless subtle) · Key relation: The third of three dhyānas (subtle); the threshold of samādhi.

attributes.title: Manonmani
attributes.aspect: Manonmani   · The nature of this aspect. 'Mind-no-mind' state — when prana enters sushumna, mind stops; equated with samadhi, raja yoga, the highest goal. Brief experiences of true silence (between thoughts, in the breath pause, in awe) are not nothing — they are seeds of manonmani. Cherish them.

attributes.title: Vasana-Prana Connection
attributes.aspect: Vasana-Prana Connection   · The nature of this aspect. Two causes of mental activity: vasana (latent desire) and prana (breath); destroying one destroys both. The deepest practical teaching of the text. When triggered, you can work either end: breathe consciously OR examine the underlying desire/aversion. Both work.

attributes.title: Sahaja Avastha
attributes.aspect: Sahaja Avastha   · The nature of this aspect. Spontaneous, natural state of samadhi—not as effortful achievement but as natural condition when mind, prana, and shakti unite without effort. The goal isn't a special state someday—it's your most natural state, available now beneath the noise. Practice is removing what obscures, not adding what's needed.

attributes.title: Six Forms of Samadhi
attributes.aspect: Six Forms of Samadhi   · The nature of this aspect. Gheranda's unique six-fold classification: Dhyana-Samadhi (via Shambhavi), Nada-Samadhi (via Khechari), Rasananda-Samadhi (via Bhramari), Laya-Samadhi (via Yoni mudra), Bhakti-Samadhi (via devotion), Raja-Yoga Samadhi (via Manomurchha). Different temperaments need different paths. Devotional? Bhakti samadhi. Analytical? Dhyana. Musical? Nada. Pleasure-prone? Rasananda. Heart-led? Laya. Use your nature, don't fight it.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: State-set — six modes of absorption · Valence: Fruit / attained states · Guṇa: Sattva, beyond the guṇas · Scale: Individual, transcendent · Causal status: Fruit (six doorways to absorption) · Key relation: Whole — six forms of samādhi in the haṭha tradition; kin to the samādhis of Dim 11.

attributes.title: Bhakti Yoga Samadhi
attributes.aspect: Bhakti Yoga Samadhi   · The nature of this aspect. Contemplate the chosen deity within the heart with such devotion that tears of joy flow and ecstasy arises. By this contemplation, Samadhi and Manonmani arise. Distinctive in Gheranda — emotional/devotional path explicitly named. If your nature is loving, lean into that. Daily contemplation of what you love — divine, person, principle — with full feeling can liberate as surely as any austerity.

attributes.title: Sarvam Brahmamayam (All is Brahman)
attributes.aspect: Sarvam Brahmamayam (All is Brahman)   · The nature of this aspect. Final teaching: 'Vishnu is in water, in earth, on mountains, in fire and flames — the whole universe is full of Vishnu.' All living beings, trees, oceans, mountains — know all to be Brahman. See all in Atma. The final yogic vision isn't escape — it's recognition. The same divine reality is in your morning coffee, your difficult colleague, your own breath. Practice seeing it.

attributes.title: Manas-Atma Union
attributes.aspect: Manas-Atma Union   · The nature of this aspect. Samadhi is defined as: 'Separate the manas (mind) from the body, and unite it with the Paramatma.' Raja-Yoga Samadhi = Unmani = Sahaja Avastha — all synonyms for this union. The goal isn't somewhere else. It's mind dissolving into the awareness that watches it — available in any moment of true stillness.

attributes.title: Nāda Anusandhāna — Absorption in Inner Sound
attributes.aspect: Nada Anusandhana   · The nature of this aspect. Nāda anusandhāna is the practice of listening, with closed ears, to the subtle inner sounds — progressing from gross resonances to ever-finer tones — until the mind, captivated and absorbed by the sound as a deer is by music, dissolves into stillness. The Pradīpikā prizes it as one of the surest gateways to laya, the dissolution of the mind, and to manonmanī, the state beyond mind. It is the gentle culmination toward which the forceful disciplines have been clearing the way.

additional_info.heading: 8. The Whole Point: Haṭha-Rāja Integration
additional_info.text: The Pradīpikā is emphatic that all of this is means, not end. Haṭha without Rāja is mere gymnastics; Rāja without the Haṭha preparation is, for most, an unreachable ideal. The two are wings of one bird. Three concepts secure the integration: the explicit teaching that Haṭha exists for the sake of Rāja; abhyāsa, the sustained, patient, long-term practice without which nothing takes root; and guru-upadeśa, the guidance of a teacher, held as essential precisely because the powerful energetic tools can harm as easily as heal without it.

attributes.title: Abhyasa (Practice)
attributes.aspect: Abhyasa (Practice)   · The nature of this aspect. Practice is the means—'Whether young or old, sick or weak, success comes to whomever practices.' Knowledge of texts alone does not bring siddhi. Don't wait to feel like it. Don't wait for perfect conditions. Imperfect practice today beats perfect intentions later. Daily, no exceptions.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Practice — sustained practice (abhyāsa), restated at the summit · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Rajas refined to Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Mechanism (the persistence that carries haṭha into rāja) · Key relation: The same principle as the earlier Abhyāsa; the thread running the whole ladder.

Sustained transformation needs human transmission.

attributes.title: Haṭha-Rāja Yoga — The Ladder and Its Summit
attributes.aspect: Hatha-Raja Yoga   · The nature of this aspect. The integration of Haṭha and Rāja is the recognition that the forceful disciplines of the body and breath exist entirely to make possible the effortless absorption of the mind — the ladder and the height it reaches. To hold this rightly is to practice the body-work wholeheartedly while never mistaking it for the goal, so that purification, steadiness, and energetic mastery all flow naturally into meditation rather than becoming ends in themselves.

attributes.attribute: The Lived Experience of Holding the Vision
attributes.title: The Lived Experience of Holding the Vision
attributes.aspect: Before the individual themes, it is essential to examine the three states of the vision itself — for a non-dual understanding can be held in a way that frees, a way that subtly inflates and evades, and it can be wholly absent. This is the single most important practical distinction in the whole dimension.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Mental · Ontic mode: State / practice — sustaining the non-dual view in daily life · Valence: Cultivated stance / the fruit lived · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Ground held moment to moment · Key relation: Genus — the lived holding of which all the pointers below are expressions.
coordinate.ontic_mode: State / practice — sustaining the non-dual view in daily life
coordinate.key_relation: Genus — the lived holding of which all the pointers below are expressions

balanced.label: Balanced — the vision as a lived, grounding recognition

facets.overview: Held in balance, the non-dual vision functions as a quiet, stabilizing ground beneath ordinary life. The person fully engages the relative world — works, loves, grieves, takes responsibility — while some steady part of awareness rests in the recognition that they are, most deeply, the unshaken witness in which all of it appears. The fruit is not detachment from life but a particular fearlessness within it: losses are felt cleanly without becoming identity-collapse, because the one who would be annihilated was never the whole story. The body is at ease, the emotions flow without flooding, and the mind has a ground that does not move.

balanced.text: Held in balance, the non-dual vision functions as a quiet, stabilizing ground beneath ordinary life. The person fully engages the relative world — works, loves, grieves, takes responsibility — while some steady part of awareness rests in the recognition that they are, most deeply, the unshaken witness in which all of it appears. The fruit is not detachment from life but a particular fearlessness within it: losses are felt cleanly without becoming identity-collapse, because the one who would be annihilated was never the whole story. The body is at ease, the emotions flow without flooding, and the mind has a ground that does not move.

excess.label: In excess — the bypass: ‘it’s all One, so nothing matters’

facets.overview: The most dangerous distortion of the non-dual vision is what is sometimes called neo-advaita or spiritual bypassing: the vision is grasped intellectually and used by the ego to evade rather than dissolve. ‘There is no one to suffer; it’s all just appearance; the body and the relationship and the responsibility are unreal’ — the words are technically the tradition’s own, but in this mouth they become a sophisticated avoidance of grief, intimacy, and duty. The vision becomes a hiding-place for an ego that has learned to call its own numbness ‘liberation.’ The texts guard against this precisely: advaita is transcendence, they insist, not negation; harmonization, not the contemptuous dismissal of the relative.

excess.text: The most dangerous distortion of the non-dual vision is what is sometimes called neo-advaita or spiritual bypassing: the vision is grasped intellectually and used by the ego to evade rather than dissolve. ‘There is no one to suffer; it’s all just appearance; the body and the relationship and the responsibility are unreal’ — the words are technically the tradition’s own, but in this mouth they become a sophisticated avoidance of grief, intimacy, and duty. The vision becomes a hiding-place for an ego that has learned to call its own numbness ‘liberation.’ The texts guard against this precisely: advaita is transcendence, they insist, not negation; harmonization, not the contemptuous dismissal of the relative.

deficient.label: Deficient — the contraction: the small self as the whole story

facets.overview: Where the vision is wholly absent, a person lives entirely identified with the separate body-mind. There is no felt ground beneath the small self, no sense of any reality larger than the personal, and so the whole weight of existence rests on a fragile, temporary individual who must be defended, enlarged, and kept safe against a world that will eventually take everything. This is the ordinary condition the tradition diagnoses as suffering at its root: not this or that misfortune, but the structural insecurity of a self that has mistaken a wave for the whole ocean.

additional_info.heading: 4. The Great Sayings (Mahāvākyas)
additional_info.text: The four great sayings are the most direct pointers, each from one of the four Vedas, each asserting the identity of the individual self with the Absolute from a different angle — by instruction (‘thou art That’), by realization (‘I am Brahman’), by definition (‘consciousness is Brahman’), and by recognition (‘this Self is Brahman’).

attributes.title: I Am Shiva (Aham Shivasmi)
attributes.aspect: ·   Byrom's chapter title: 'I Am Shiva' The nature of this aspect. The final chapter culminates in Janaka's recognition 'I am Shiva'—not the deity but the auspicious pure consciousness behind all phenomena. Shiva means the all-pervading auspicious Self that is everyone's true nature. To say 'I am Shiva' is to recognize one's identity with the ground of all being. This is the destination of Ashtavakra Gita.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: Pointer — a non-dual realization · Valence: Neutral — the truth to be seen · Guṇa: Beyond the guṇas · Scale: Individual, as the Absolute (transcendent) · Causal status: Ground / realization · Key relation: mahāvākya ‘Aham Shivasmi’ — the Self as pure auspicious awareness.

facets.psychic: The soul declares its identity with Śiva, the auspicious pure awareness, and lives from that recognition rather than the small self.

attributes.title: Prajnana Brahma Lived (Aitareya Mahavakya)
attributes.aspect: ·   Aitareya Mahavakya The nature of this aspect. Prajnana Brahma is one of the four Mahavakyas, traditionally from Aitareya. Krishnananda emphasizes its lived dimension: the very consciousness in you—right now—is Brahman. Not abstract concept but immediate recognition. The shift from 'I am conscious of things' to 'My consciousness IS Brahman' transforms experience.

attributes.title: Tat Tvam Asi — Thou Art That
attributes.aspect: तत् त्वम् असि   ·   6.8.7 (Uddalaka to Shvetaketu) The nature of this aspect. Tat tvam asi, ‘thou art That,’ is the instruction-saying given by Uddālaka to his son Śvetaketu nine times over, each time stripping away another layer of what the boy took himself to be. Its claim is the heart of the whole vision: the innermost reality of the individual (tvam, thou) is not different from the ultimate ground of all existence (tat, That). It is not asking the small self to become Brahman; it is pointing out that the small self was never the real subject — that the awareness reading these words is already the one reality, momentarily mistaking itself for a person.

deficient.label: Deficient — total identification with the separate self; the saying as mere words

facets.overview: Complete separation between 'I' and 'That'—maximum dualistic suffering, no recognition of underlying unity.
facets.somatic: Body as alien from cosmos.
facets.emotional: Emotional separation from all.
facets.cognitive: Mind cannot conceive of identity with absolute.
facets.psychic: Maximum separation reigns; the soul experiences itself as utterly cut off from the divine, an island of ‘I’ in a foreign world.

additional_info.heading: 5. The Nature of the Self (Ātman)
additional_info.text: If the great sayings assert the identity, these visions describe what that innermost Self actually is — and the descriptions are consistent across the Upaniṣads: the Atman is unborn and undying, subtler than the subtlest, self-luminous (needing no other light to be known), imperishable, and the very light by which all else is known. It is not a thing among things but the awareness in which all things appear.

attributes.title: Kutastha (Unmoving Meditation Point)
attributes.aspect: ·   Throughout Lahiri's Gita commentaries The nature of this aspect. Kutastha is the 'unmoving' point—the meditation focal point at the spiritual eye (between eyebrows). It is where individual consciousness meets cosmic consciousness; where inner light is seen; where Pranava is heard. Lahiri Mahasay's interpretation: Krishna in the Bhagavad Gita IS Kutastha—the silent witnessing core that does not move while everything else does.

attributes.title: Sarvabhuteshu Atmanam (Self in All Beings)
attributes.aspect: सर्वभूतेषु आत्मानं   ·   Verse 6 The nature of this aspect. He who perceives all beings in the Self and the Self in all beings entertains no hatred. The realization of one Self in all destroys the basis for hatred, delusion, grief.

facets.cognitive: Mind sees the One in many, dissolution of fundamental otherness.
facets.psychic: The soul sees its own Self in all beings and all beings in the Self, and so meets the world without enmity.

attributes.title: Yajnavalkya-Maitreyi: Atman is What All is Dear
attributes.aspect: आत्मा कामाय   ·   2.4.5 (Yajnavalkya to Maitreyi) The nature of this aspect. Yajnavalkya tells his wife Maitreyi: 'Not for the sake of the husband is the husband dear, but for the sake of the Self.' Not for the sake of objects are they dear, but for the sake of the Self. The Self must be seen, heard, reflected upon, meditated upon.

attributes.title: Atman as Self-Luminous (Svayamprakasha)
attributes.aspect: स्वयंप्रकाश आत्मा   ·   4.3.6 The nature of this aspect. Yajnavalkya to King Janaka: 'When the sun has set, when the moon has set, when fire is out, what light does a person have here?' 'The Self alone is one's light.' By the light of the Atman alone one acts. The Self is the light of lights, self-luminous beyond all external lights.

attributes.title: Akshara (The Imperishable)
attributes.aspect: अक्षरम्   ·   3.8.8-11 (Yajnavalkya's reply to Gargi) The nature of this aspect. By the command of the Akshara (Imperishable) sun and moon stand in their places, heaven and earth, moments and hours, rivers flow, etc. It is neither gross nor subtle, neither short nor long, has no smell, taste, sight, hearing, mind. It is unseen seer, unheard hearer, unthought thinker, ununderstood understander. It is the inner ruler.

attributes.title: The Self-Luminous Atman — the Light of Lights
attributes.aspect: स्वयं ज्योतिः   ·   2.2.15 The nature of this aspect. The Self is svayam-prakāśa, self-luminous — it is not illumined by anything else, but is itself the light by which everything else is known. The sun, the moon, the fire, the mind: all are known by this awareness, but this awareness is known by nothing other than itself. It is, as the Upaniṣad says, the light of lights. To recognize this is to stop searching for the Self as an object — a special experience, a state to be attained — and to notice instead the ever-present knowing in which every experience, ordinary or exalted, is already appearing.

additional_info.heading: 6. The Fourth State and the Substratum (Turīya)
additional_info.text: The Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad maps consciousness through four states: waking (vaiśvānara), dream (taijasa), deep sleep (prājña), and turīya, the ‘fourth’ — which is not a fourth state alongside the others but the awareness that underlies and pervades all three, the unchanging substratum in which waking, dreaming, and sleeping come and go. To recognize turīya is to find the changeless ground that was present even in dreamless sleep, the continuity of pure awareness behind the discontinuity of experience.

attributes.attribute: Awake in Sleep, Dreaming Without Dreaming
attributes.title: Awake in Sleep, Dreaming Without Dreaming
attributes.aspect: ·   XVIII.94 The nature of this aspect. Ashtavakra's profound description of the Jivanmukta's state across all three states of consciousness. In sleep, he's not really asleep (witness remains). In dream, he's not identified with the dreamer. In waking, he's not entangled with objects like ordinary people. This is the Turiya-state operative through Jagrat, Svapna, Sushupti—the fourth that is the substratum.

attributes.title: Quietude (Shanti) — Already-Here State
attributes.aspect: ·   Chapters 10, 13, 18 The nature of this aspect. Ashtavakra's distinctive teaching on Quietude: it is not produced, not earned, not achieved through effort. It is the natural state of consciousness; we only disturb it through false identification. When false identification with body-mind drops, quietude is recognized as one's nature. Not a goal but a return.

attributes.title: Sahaja Samādhi — the Natural, Already-Here State
attributes.aspect: ·   Chapter XVIII The nature of this aspect. Sahaja samādhi is the natural, spontaneous absorption — not a special trance entered and exited, but the recognition of the awareness that is always already present, in which one can rest in the midst of any activity. It is turīya lived: the discovery that the peace one was seeking through effort is the very ground from which the seeking arose, a quietude (śānti) that is not produced but uncovered, already here beneath the noise. Its mark is that it does not depend on conditions — it is as available in the marketplace as on the cushion.

additional_info.heading: 7. World, Māyā, and Apparent Reality
additional_info.text: The non-dual vision is often caricatured as world-denial, but the texts are careful. The famous formula — brahma satyam jagan-mithyā — says Brahman is real and the world is mithyā, but mithyā does not mean ‘illusion’ in the sense of ‘nonexistent’; it means dependent, apparent, real-as-appearance but not real-in-itself, the way a wave is real water but not a separate thing. Hence the tradition’s insistence: advaita is transcendence, not negation; harmonization, not contempt for the world.

attributes.attribute: World Exists On Account of You
attributes.title: World Exists On Account of You
attributes.aspect: ·   1.5 (Atmananda's commentary) The nature of this aspect. Atmananda's radical commentary on Ashtavakra: the world exists because YOU witness it. Without consciousness, no world is. The world is in consciousness; consciousness is not in the world. This reverses the ordinary view that places the observer in the observed-world. The observer (you) is the foundation; the world is appearance.

attributes.title: Advaita as Harmonization (Not Negation)
attributes.aspect: ·   Talk on Sankara Jayanti 1977 The nature of this aspect. Krishnananda's deep teaching from Sankara: Advaita is misunderstood as 'plurality is unreal, only One is real.' Actually, Advaita is HARMONIZATION—unity does not abolish plurality but integrates it. The senses, reason, intuition are not negated but integrated, each in its proper place. Higher transcends lower without rejecting it.

attributes.title: Transcendence Not Negation
attributes.aspect: ·   Talk on Sankara Jayanti 1977 The nature of this aspect. Krishnananda's profound teaching: 'There is no such thing as criticism or condemnation of values, but only a living touch given to existent conditions for the purpose of a growth into a higher state of being.' attributes.title: Brahma-Satyam Jagan-Mithyā — the Real and the Apparent
attributes.aspect: ·   Core Advaitic teaching The nature of this aspect. This formula — Brahman is real, the world is apparent — is the precise statement of the non-dual relationship between the Absolute and the world. The world is not denied; it is correctly located, as a dependent appearance arising in and inseparable from the one reality, like images in a mirror or waves in the ocean. To hold this rightly is to engage the world fully while no longer mistaking it for the final ground — to invest in the wave while knowing oneself the water. The error to avoid is the contempt that calls the world ‘mere illusion’ and uses the doctrine to dismiss life’s genuine claims.

additional_info.heading: 8. The Self in All / The Inner Controller
additional_info.text: If there is one Self, it is the Self of all — and the Upaniṣads draw the staggering consequence: the same awareness that looks out of your eyes looks out of every pair of eyes; the one indweller (antaryāmin) controls and inhabits all beings from within; all this is pervaded by the Lord (īśāvāsyam idaṁ sarvam). The image of the two birds on one tree — one eating the fruits, one simply watching — captures the relationship of the active personal self and the witnessing universal Self, both present in every heart.

attributes.title: Boundless Ocean (Cosmic Identity)
attributes.aspect: ·   Byrom's chapter title: 'The Boundless Ocean' The nature of this aspect. The boundless ocean is Ashtavakra's metaphor: phenomena (thoughts, emotions, body, world) are waves that rise and fall. The Self is the ocean itself—the depth that does not change as waves come and go. To realize oneself as the ocean rather than a wave is to find true rest. The wave-identity is the source of all suffering; the ocean-identity is liberation.

attributes.title: Antaryami (Inner Controller)
attributes.aspect: अन्तर्यामी   ·   Implicit throughout 3.12-3.14 The nature of this aspect. The Self residing in the heart is the inner controller (antaryami) of all functions—heart-beat, breath, mind-activity. Smaller than a grain of rice yet vaster than all worlds, this Self silently directs everything from within.

attributes.title: Antaryami (Inner Controller of the Universe)
attributes.aspect: अन्तर्यामी   ·   3.7.1-3.7.23 (Uddalaka-Yajnavalkya) The nature of this aspect. Uddalaka asks Yajnavalkya about the inner controller. attributes.title: Two Birds on One Tree
attributes.aspect: द्वा सुपर्णा   ·   3.1.1-2 The nature of this aspect. The Muṇḍaka and Śvetāśvatara image: two birds, close companions, perch on the same tree. One eats the sweet and bitter fruits; the other looks on without eating. The first is the jīva, the personal self caught in enjoyment and suffering; the second is the witnessing Self, the unaffected awareness. The vision’s liberating turn is the recognition that one has always been, in truth, the watching bird — that the eating, suffering self is something witnessed, not the witness. To live from the watching bird is to be in the midst of life’s sweet and bitter fruits without being consumed by them.

additional_info.heading: 9. Liberation as Already-Present
additional_info.text: The boldest move of the non-dual vision — carried furthest in the Aṣṭāvakra Gītā and the direct-path teachings — is that liberation is not an attainment in the future but a recognition available now, because what one seeks one already is. There is no distance to travel, only a misidentification to drop. Hence the talk of ‘instant’ liberation, the ‘direct method’ of separating awareness from the body-mind, and jīvanmukti, liberation while still living — not as a reward earned but as a fact uncovered.

attributes.title: Quantum Flight to the Absolute (Instantaneous Liberation)
attributes.aspect: ·   Ashtavakra Gita I.4 The nature of this aspect. Ashtavakra's revolutionary claim: liberation is not a gradual achievement but an instantaneous quantum flight. The seeker is here in the phenomenal world; in the next moment, established in the Absolute. Both the world and the 'I' dissolve into nothingness. This contradicts all gradualist paths—instantaneous awakening is the central claim.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: State — an already-present condition · Valence: The fruit / goal · Guṇa: Beyond the guṇas · Scale: Individual, transcendent · Causal status: Fruit / ground · Key relation: instantaneous liberation — the immediate leap to the Absolute (no gradual path).

attributes.title: Repose in Self (Atma-Vishrama)
attributes.aspect: ·   Atmananda Chapter 19: 'Repose in Self' The nature of this aspect. Atma-vishrama is repose in one's own Self—the final cessation of seeking. The seeker has come home; the wanderer has reached the destination. This is not metaphorical rest but actual cessation of all spiritual ambition, all becoming, all striving. The Self is its own resting place. Whoever rests in Self rests in everything.

Emotional: Emotions as final reality.

Cognitive: Mind as ultimate self.

Psychic: The soul is fused without remainder to body and mind, unable even to conceive of itself apart from them.

attributes.title: Jīvanmukti — Liberation as Already-Realized
attributes.aspect: ·   Chapter 20: 'Repose in Self / Liberation in Life' The nature of this aspect. Jīvanmukti is liberation while still embodied — and in its most radical Upanishadic and Aṣṭāvakra form, it is presented not as a distant goal but as one’s already-existing nature, merely unrecognized. The seeker is not building freedom but discovering that the awareness they already are was never bound. This is the vision’s most liberating and most easily counterfeited claim: rightly held, it ends the endless postponement of peace; wrongly held, it lets the ego declare itself already free and excuse the work that recognition actually requires.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: State — an already-present condition · Valence: The fruit / goal · Guṇa: Beyond the guṇas · Scale: Individual, transcendent · Causal status: Fruit / ground · Key relation: liberation-while-living — freedom already realized in this body.
coordinate.key_relation: liberation-while-living — freedom already realized in this body

balanced.label: Balanced — freedom recognized as present nature, expressed in lived equanimity

facets.overview: Living recognition of one's eternal liberation. Activities continue without identification with doership.
facets.somatic: Body lives out its karmic momentum.
facets.emotional: Emotional life unfolds without binding the Self.
facets.cognitive: Mind operates while consciousness rests in its always-free nature.
facets.psychic: The soul knows freedom as already its nature, here and now; liberation is not postponed to after death but lived in this very body.

balanced.text:

facets.cognitive: Mental performance of liberation.

facets.emotional: Emotional life of postponed liberation.
facets.cognitive: Mind never conceives liberation in living.
facets.psychic: The soul believes freedom is forever distant — only after death, or only for others — and so never looks for what is already present.

additional_info.heading: 10. The Creative Word and the Cosmogonic Visions
additional_info.text: Finally, a cluster of visions sees the one reality as creatively self-expressing. OM (sarvam aum) is taken as the sound-form of the whole, and vāc, speech, as a genuine creative power by which the unmanifest becomes manifest. The cosmogonic meditations — Prajāpati’s hunger creating food, death (mṛtyu) as a creative principle, the honey-doctrine (madhu vidyā) of universal mutual sustenance — present creation not as a one-time event but as the ongoing self-display of the one reality. Held as lived experience, these visions infuse ordinary perception with a sense of the sacred ordinariness of things: the world not as dead matter but as the very body and utterance of the Self.

attributes.title: Vac (Speech as Creative Power)
attributes.aspect: ·   Various sections on Vac The nature of this aspect. Vac is the cosmic creative speech-principle. Beyond mere human language, it is the Word-vibration that creates worlds. The Vedas are crystallisations of Vac; human speech a tiny fraction. Recognizing Vac transforms one's relationship with words: each word becomes a creative act, each speech an exercise of cosmic power.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework — a cosmogonic principle · Valence: Neutral — a creative truth · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Cosmic (Ādhidaivika), realized individually · Causal status: Cause / ground · Key relation: speech (Vāc) as creative power — the word that brings forth worlds.
coordinate.key_relation: speech (Vāc) as creative power — the word that brings forth worlds

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.somatic: Voice used reverently, recognized as cosmic instrument.
facets.emotional: Emotional dimension of speech respected, no careless words.
facets.cognitive: Mind orchestrates speech with awareness of its creative power.
facets.psychic: The soul reveres the creative power of the word, recognizing speech as a force that shapes worlds.

attributes.title: OM as Everything (Sarvam Aum)
attributes.aspect: सर्वम् ओम्   ·   Verse 1 The nature of this aspect. OM—this syllable is all this. Whatever was, is, will be—all is OM. What is beyond past, present, future—that too is OM. Everything verily is Brahman; this Self is Brahman.

attributes.title: Mrityu (Death as Creative Principle)
attributes.aspect: ·   Chapter 1, Second Brahmana The nature of this aspect. In Brihadaranyaka's cosmology, creation begins with Mrityu (Death) or hunger—the cosmic principle of consumption-desire. This 'hunger' creates more and more objects to consume. The intensity of desire equals the intensity of separation. Recognizing this principle in oneself transforms one's relationship with desire and death.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework — a cosmogonic principle · Valence: Neutral — a creative truth · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Cosmic (Ādhidaivika), realized individually · Causal status: Cause / ground · Key relation: death (Mṛtyu) as a creative principle — the hunger from which creation proceeds.

facets.emotional: Emotional hungers seen as expressions of creative principle.
facets.cognitive: Mind understands desire as creative cosmic force, not personal flaw.
facets.psychic: The soul contemplates how death itself is woven into creation, the great recycler through which life renews.

attributes.title: Prajapati's Hunger Creating Food (Annada)
attributes.aspect: ·   Chapter 1, Fifth Brahmana The nature of this aspect. The cosmic eating-eaten cycle: Prajapati creates beings to be His food, and Himself becomes the food for these beings. Everything that eats is being eaten; everything that is eaten is also eating. This cosmic vision transforms the act of eating into participation in cosmic sacrament.

attributes.title: Madhu Vidya (Honey Doctrine)
attributes.aspect: मधु विद्या   ·   3.1-11 The nature of this aspect. The sun yonder is the honey of the gods. Sky is its hive; rays are its bees. Eastern rays are the eastern honey-cells; Rig verses are the bees; Brahman is the flower. Through this honey, all worlds nourish each other. This is the doctrine of interconnected sweetness—all beings are honey for each other.

attributes.title: Madhu Vidya (Honey Doctrine in Brihadaranyaka)
attributes.aspect: मधु विद्या   ·   2.5.1-2.5.19 The nature of this aspect. This earth is honey for all beings, and all beings are honey for this earth. The waters are honey for all beings, and all beings are honey for the waters. Fire, air, sun, quarters, moon, lightning, thunder, ether, dharma, truth, mankind, Self—each is honey for all others. The infinite, immutable, all-pervading Atman is the honey of all.

additional_info.heading: 10b. Further Foundational Visions
additional_info.text: Beyond the principal themes, the corpus offers a wealth of further pointers — visions of the heart, of one’s own splendor, of the inverted cosmic tree, of the mind-stuff and its tendencies, and of the personal Lord — each a distinct doorway into the same recognition. Each is examined below in its balanced, excessive, and deficient expression.

attributes.title: Awareness as Heart (Hridaya-Bodha)
attributes.aspect: ·   Byrom's framing: 'The Heart of Awareness' The nature of this aspect. Byrom's poetic framing of Ashtavakra Gita: awareness is the single mystery that enfolds all other mysteries, including the secret of the separate self. As a child of seven, Byrom became aware of awareness itself by the sea. The Gita answers: this awareness you noticed is YOU. The heart of everything is awareness recognizing itself.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Vital · Ontic mode: Framework — a non-dual vision · Valence: Neutral — a pointer to be realized · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Individual, toward the Absolute · Causal status: Framework / realization · Key relation: awareness as the Heart (hṛdaya-bodha) — the seat of the Self.
coordinate.valence: Neutral — a pointer to be realized

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.somatic: Body recognized as appearance in awareness-heart.
facets.emotional: Emotional life as movements in awareness.
facets.cognitive: Mind's contents appearing in awareness-heart.
facets.psychic: The soul recognizes awareness itself as the true heart, the seat from which 'I' arises and to which it returns.

attributes.title: My Own Splendor (Sva-Mahima-Bodha)
attributes.aspect: ·   Byrom's chapter title: 'My Own Splendor' The nature of this aspect. Janaka's culminating recognition: the splendor he sought outside was always his own. The Self is not lacking—it is the very splendor that all things shine with. To recognize 'my own splendor' is to stop seeking what is already present as one's nature. This is the ultimate beauty—being radiantly oneself.

attributes.title: Bhakti Washes Karma (Daily Confidence)
attributes.aspect: ·   Bhagavata: 'Bhakti washes away all karma' The nature of this aspect. One of the Bhagavata's central teachings: bhakti washes away karma. This contradicts views that karma is iron-clad fate. Sincere daily devotion progressively cleanses karmic accumulation. In daily life this is enormously freeing: past mistakes, regrets, perceived bad karma are not iron destiny — current devotional practice transforms them. There is always a way forward.

facets.somatic: Body engaged in present devotion regardless of past.

facets.cognitive: Mind doesn't despair about past — bhakti now cleanses.
facets.psychic: The soul trusts that sincere devotion can dissolve the residue of past action, and so moves forward unburdened.

attributes.title: Mandukya as Sole Necessary Upanishad
attributes.aspect: ·   Mandukya entire The nature of this aspect. Mandukya is uniquely positioned as the COMPLETE teaching in 12 verses. Per Muktika Upanishad: if Kaivalya is sought, Mandukya alone is sufficient. The 10 principal, 32 important, and 108 minor Upanishads are progressively for those who don't get it from Mandukya. This places Mandukya as the apex.

facets.cognitive: Mind devoted to penetrating the 12 verses fully.
facets.psychic: The soul finds in the Māṇḍūkya's map of the four states a complete and sufficient guide to its own depths.

attributes.title: Kena Ishitam (Who Directs the Senses?)
attributes.aspect: केन ईशितम्   ·   Verse 1.1 The nature of this aspect. By whom willed and directed does the mind fall on its objects? By whom commanded does prana move? This opening question reverses ordinary perception—asking what lies BEHIND the senses, the mind, the vital force.

facets.somatic: Body's functions traced back to their source, not taken as self-acting.
facets.emotional: Emotional life seen as movement, with awareness of the moved-by behind it.
facets.cognitive: Mind investigates its own source, opens to the consciousness behind.
facets.psychic: The soul turns the great question back upon itself — who is the awareness behind eye and ear? — and inquiry begins.

attributes.title: Tadvanam (Adorable to All Beings)
attributes.aspect: तद्वनम्   ·   Verse 4.6 The nature of this aspect. Brahman is known as Tadvanam—the one to whom all beings pray (consciously or unconsciously). When one knows Brahman as Tadvanam, all beings yearn toward one.

attributes.title: Two Selves in Heart-Cave (Jiva and Ishvara)
attributes.aspect: द्वौ आत्मानौ   ·   1.3.1 The nature of this aspect. Two Selves dwell in the same heart-cave—Ishvara (universal Self, all-knowing, all-powerful) and Jiva (individual self, limited in knowledge and power). They are like original and reflection, light and shadow. The Jiva is enslaved by avidya; Ishvara controls maya. Yet both inhabit the same place.

facets.cognitive: Mind sees the relationship between individual and universal—light and shadow of one source.
facets.psychic: The soul recognizes the two birds in the heart-cave — the individual self and the Lord — and turns toward the silent witness.

attributes.title: Inverted Tree of Life (Urdhva-Mula Ashvattha)
attributes.aspect: ऊर्ध्वमूल अश्वत्थ   ·   2.3.1 The nature of this aspect. Eternal is the Ashvattha tree with roots above (in Brahman) and branches below (manifestation). 'Ashvattha' means 'not the same tomorrow'—ever-changing. The tree appears solid but is in constant transformation. All worlds are in this tree; none can go beyond. The roots (Brahman) is the source; the branches (manifest world) are the expression.

facets.cognitive: Mind grasps the tree-structure of reality, source above, manifestation below.
facets.psychic: The soul perceives existence as a tree rooted above in the Absolute and branching below, and so seeks its source upward.

attributes.title: Bhuma Vidya (The Infinite)
attributes.aspect: भूम विद्या   ·   7.23-26 The nature of this aspect. Narada is taught: in the finite is no happiness; only in the Infinite (Bhuma) is happiness. 'That in which one sees nothing else, hears nothing else, knows nothing else—That is Infinite.' Bhuma is below, above, behind, before, right, left—it is all this.

attributes.title: Sat Vidya (Pure Being Inquiry)
attributes.aspect: सत् विद्या   ·   6.2.1-6.2.4 The nature of this aspect. Uddalaka teaches: 'In the beginning, this was Sat alone, one only without a second. Some say it was Asat (non-existence). But how can existence come from non-existence? Sat alone was; it desired to become many. From Sat, fire was emitted; from fire, water; from water, food. All this has Sat as its root, support, and abode.'

attributes.title: Three Rare Blessings (Manushyatva-Mumukshutva-Mahapurusha-Samshraya)
attributes.aspect: ·   Verse 3 The nature of this aspect. Three exceedingly rare attainments mark the seeker's situation: (1) Manushyatva - human birth itself, (2) Mumukshutva - the burning desire for liberation, (3) Mahapurusha-samshraya - association with a realized teacher. Each is rare in isolation; together they constitute extraordinary good fortune. Wasting them is the supreme tragedy.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Mental · Ontic mode: Framework — the three rare attainments · Valence: Neutral — preconditions to prize · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Condition (human birth, longing for freedom, a great teacher) · Key relation: Whole — the three rarities; Mumukṣutva (Dim 23) is one of them.
coordinate.causal_status: Condition (human birth, longing for freedom, a great teacher)

facets.somatic: Body honored as the rare vehicle of potential liberation.

facets.cognitive: Mind alert to the preciousness of present opportunity for liberation.
facets.psychic: The soul recognizes the rare fortune of human birth, the longing for freedom, and the company of the wise, and uses it well.

facets.cognitive: Mental fixation on time-pressure preventing deeper engagement.
facets.psychic: The soul turns the recognition into spiritual pride, congratulating itself on its rare blessings.

excess.text:
Anxiety-driven striving from sense of urgency. Rushing practice without integration.

Somatic: Body burnt out by anxiety-driven practice.

Emotional: Emotional panic about wasting opportunity.

Cognitive: Mental fixation on time-pressure preventing deeper engagement.

Psychic: The soul turns the recognition into spiritual pride, congratulating itself on its rare blessings.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Casual treatment of human birth as just any existence. Spiritual opportunity squandered in trivia.
facets.somatic: Body used for sense-pursuits only.
facets.emotional: Emotional indifference to spiritual opportunity.
facets.cognitive: Mind unaware of the rare configuration available.
facets.psychic: The soul squanders its rare opportunity, taking human life and the longing for truth for granted.

attributes.title: Citta (Mind-Stuff/Consciousness Vehicle)
attributes.aspect: चित्त   ·   Sutra 1.2, 1.30, 1.33, throughout The nature of this aspect. Citta is the totality of the inner cognitive apparatus—the mind-stuff comprising intelligence (buddhi), ego (ahamkara), and mind proper (manas). It is the field on which all mental activity occurs and the instrument that must be stilled for liberation.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Faculty / constituent — the mind-stuff, vehicle of consciousness · Valence: Neutral faculty · Guṇa: Spans all three · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Instrument (the field on which vṛttis and saṁskāras play) · Key relation: The mind-stuff; kin to the Antaḥkaraṇa (Dim 3); its vṛttis (Dim 20) are to be stilled.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Faculty / constituent — the mind-stuff, vehicle of consciousness
coordinate.causal_status: Instrument (the field on which vṛttis and saṁskāras play)
coordinate.key_relation: The mind-stuff; kin to the Antaḥkaraṇa (Dim 3); its vṛttis (Dim 20) are to be stilled

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When the citta is purified and stilled, it becomes a clear translucent medium that reflects the Self perfectly. It can be the ideal instrument for self-realization.
facets.somatic: Healthy nervous system reflecting purified mind, integrated body-mind, energy flowing harmoniously through the citta-system.
facets.emotional: Emotional clarity from purified citta, healing of emotional patterns, emotional intelligence developed.
facets.cognitive: Clear mind functioning optimally, all three aspects (buddhi, ahamkara, manas) in harmony, mind serves consciousness.
facets.psychic: The soul recognizes the mind-stuff as a vehicle it has, not the self it is, and so is no longer ruled by its movements.

balanced.text:

Somatic: Healthy nervous system reflecting purified mind, integrated body-mind, energy flowing harmoniously through the citta-system.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Over-identification with citta as Self creates confusion between mind and consciousness, leading to mental arrogance or premature dissolution claims.
facets.somatic: Body identified with mental activity, stress from over-mentation, neglect of body for mental development.
facets.emotional: Emotional rigidity around mental states, identification with thoughts as self, emotional suffering from misidentification.
facets.cognitive: Mistaking the citta for the Self, mental pride about psychological development, identification with mental contents.
facets.psychic: The soul over-identifies with its rich inner mental life, mistaking the vehicle for the driver.

excess.text:

Psychic: The soul over-identifies with its rich inner mental life, mistaking the vehicle for the driver.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Damaged or unhealthy citta (through trauma, disease, or affliction) prevents proper yogic functioning. The instrument itself is compromised.
facets.somatic: Physical symptoms from disturbed citta, somatic distress from mental dysfunction.
facets.emotional: Emotional dysregulation from impaired citta, inability to feel coherently, trauma effects.
facets.cognitive: Citta dominated by kleshas, scattered mental functioning, inability to think clearly, mental dysfunction.
facets.psychic: The soul is wholly fused with the citta, taking every thought and mood as simply itself.

attributes.title: Samskaras (Latent Mental Impressions)
attributes.aspect: संस्कार   ·   Sutra 1.18, 1.50, 2.15, 3.9, 3.18, 4.9 The nature of this aspect. Samskaras are subliminal imprints—mental and karmic seeds left in the citta by every experience and action. They are stored dormant and activated when similar circumstances arise, creating habit patterns, personality traits, and karmic destiny. Yoga aims to burn these seeds.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Mental · Ontic mode: Process / latent residue · Valence: Neutral tending to bondage · Guṇa: Takes the action’s coloring · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Mechanism / residue · Key relation: Same as the Saṁskāras (Dim 12); the grooves that condition response.
coordinate.key_relation: Same as the Saṁskāras (Dim 12); the grooves that condition response

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When samskaras are recognized and purified through practice, they lose their compulsive power. The yogi acts freely without being driven by latent patterns. New samskaras from yogic practice support liberation.
facets.somatic: Body free from compulsive habit patterns, healthy physical conditioning from yogic samskaras, no driven physical patterns.
facets.emotional: Emotional freedom from old patterns, healing of emotional samskaras, ability to choose responses consciously.
facets.cognitive: Mental freedom from compulsive thinking patterns, awareness of when samskaras activate, ability to not feed them.
facets.psychic: The soul understands its grooves of habit as conditioned impressions, and so can work to soften rather than obey them.

balanced.text:

Somatic: Body free from compulsive habit patterns, healthy physical conditioning from yogic samskaras, no driven physical patterns.

Emotional: Emotional freedom from old patterns, healing of emotional samskaras, ability to choose responses consciously.

Cognitive: Mental freedom from compulsive thinking patterns, awareness of when samskaras activate, ability to not feed them.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Excessive focus on samskaras becomes obsessive psychoanalysis, endless processing without liberation, getting lost in patterns.
facets.somatic: Body trapped in trauma analysis, somatic re-experiencing without resolution.
facets.emotional: Emotional regression into old patterns through over-analysis, getting stuck in past, trauma re-living.
facets.cognitive: Mental obsession with patterns, endless analysis, intellectual understanding without transformation.
facets.psychic: The soul becomes fatalistic about its conditioning, treating samskāras as an unchangeable identity.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: When samskaras are dominant and unconscious, the person lives entirely controlled by hidden conditioning—compulsive behavior, addictions, personality patterns.
facets.somatic: Body completely controlled by samskara-driven habits, physical compulsions, addictions, automatic patterns.
facets.emotional: Emotions arising compulsively from samskaras without awareness, repetitive emotional patterns, slavery to conditioning.
facets.cognitive: Mind dominated by unconscious patterns, repetitive thinking, no awareness of why one thinks/feels as one does, life on autopilot.
facets.psychic: The soul is run by its grooves unawares, mistaking deep habit for free choice.
