attributes.title: Vasanas (Karmic Tendencies)
attributes.aspect: वासना   ·   Sutra 4.8, 4.9, 4.24 The nature of this aspect. Vasanas are deeper, accumulated currents of karmic tendency built up over many lifetimes. They are clusters of samskaras forming subtle character orientations and propensities. Distinct from individual samskaras, they represent the deeper karmic flow of an individual.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Mental · Ontic mode: Faculty / latent tendency · Valence: Faculty tending to affliction · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Latent cause · Key relation: Same as the Vāsanās (Dim 12); the tendencies born of saṁskāra.
coordinate.key_relation: Same as the Vāsanās (Dim 12); the tendencies born of saṁskāra

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When vasanas are understood and gradually purified through practice, the yogi gains insight into karmic patterns spanning lifetimes. There is wisdom about deep tendencies.
facets.somatic: Body that has accumulated yogic vasanas supporting practice, healthy long-term physical orientation toward liberation.
facets.emotional: Emotional patterns oriented toward spiritual progress, accumulated wisdom-vasanas guiding feelings.
facets.cognitive: Mind oriented toward liberation by accumulated yogic vasanas, deeper tendencies toward Self-realization.
facets.psychic: The soul recognizes its driving tendencies as vāsanās to be gradually exhausted, not commands to be obeyed.

balanced.text:

Cognitive: Mind oriented toward liberation by accumulated yogic vasanas, deeper tendencies toward Self-realization.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Strong unbroken vasanas of any kind (even spiritual) can become limiting—identification with personality traits, refusal to evolve beyond accumulated patterns.
facets.somatic: Body locked into vasana-driven patterns, inability to change physical habits, limited by deep conditioning.
facets.emotional: Emotional patterns running on auto-pilot, deep tendencies that resist transformation.
facets.cognitive: Mind locked into accumulated patterns, personality as prison, inability to transcend deep conditioning.
facets.psychic: The soul indulges its tendencies in the name of authenticity, deepening the very grooves that bind it.

attributes.title: Ishvara (The Lord/Personal God)
attributes.aspect: ईश्वर   ·   Sutra 1.23-29, 2.1, 2.32, 2.45 The nature of this aspect. Ishvara is the special Purusha—a unique eternally free consciousness, the supreme being—distinct from individual liberated souls. Patanjali presents devotion to Ishvara as one path to samadhi (an alternative to discrimination-based path), and identifies the symbol of Ishvara as Pranava (Om).

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework / principle — the personal God (saguṇa Brahman) · Valence: Neutral / object of devotion · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant (pure saguṇa) · Scale: Cosmic (Ādhidaivika), approached individually · Causal status: Ground (the Absolute with attributes, the Lord) · Key relation: Saguṇa Brahman (Dim 2’s Two Forms); the inner controller and object of Bhakti.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Framework / principle — the personal God (saguṇa Brahman)
coordinate.causal_status: Ground (the Absolute with attributes, the Lord)
coordinate.key_relation: Saguṇa Brahman (Dim 2’s Two Forms); the inner controller and object of Bhakti

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When Ishvara is wisely engaged as object of devotion and meditation, profound spiritual progress occurs. Bhakti-yoga path through Ishvara-pranidhana brings samadhi.
facets.somatic: Body surrendered to higher reality, healthy effects of devotional practice, body as instrument of divine.
facets.emotional: Devotional love, emotional warmth and connection, peace from surrender, divine relationship.
facets.cognitive: Mind united with Ishvara through Pranava and meditation, surrendered intellect, devotional intelligence.
facets.psychic: The soul relates to the Lord as the personal face of the absolute, a focus for devotion and surrender.

facets.somatic: Physical exhaustion from extreme devotional practices, body neglect, ritualism over realization.
facets.emotional: Emotional dependency on divine figure as crutch, codependent spirituality, emotional manipulation.
facets.cognitive: Mental dependency on external authority, fundamentalist thinking, projection rather than direct realization.
facets.psychic: The soul anthropomorphizes and bargains, reducing Ishvara to a cosmic functionary for its wishes.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Without any orientation toward Ishvara (or higher reality), the path of yoga is harder—lacks devotional support, requires harder discrimination path alone.
facets.somatic: Body lacks devotional supports for practice, harder physical discipline without bhakti.
facets.emotional: Emotional life without devotional dimension, drier practice, lack of heart-warmth.
facets.cognitive: Pure intellectual approach without bhakti support, harder progress, lacks devotional aid.
facets.psychic: The soul has no felt relationship to a higher reality, and faces existence as if utterly alone.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: State / practice — knowing as lived · Valence: Neutral · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Ground · Key relation: Genus — the lived act of knowing of which the distinctions below are refinements.
coordinate.key_relation: Genus — the lived act of knowing of which the distinctions below are refinements

balanced.label: Balanced — discriminating knowledge held with humility

facets.overview: Held in balance, the way of knowing combines sharp discrimination with deep humility. The person can tell the difference between an idea they hold and a truth they have realized, and is honest about which is which. They use the intellect fully — to inquire, to discern, to navigate — while knowing its limits before the ultimate. They tend to choose the good over the merely pleasant at the small daily forks, and they hold even their best understanding lightly, as a finger pointing at the moon rather than the moon itself. The fruit is a kind of clear-eyed groundedness: confident where confidence is warranted, humble where it is not.

balanced.text: Held in balance, the way of knowing combines sharp discrimination with deep humility. The person can tell the difference between an idea they hold and a truth they have realized, and is honest about which is which. They use the intellect fully — to inquire, to discern, to navigate — while knowing its limits before the ultimate. They tend to choose the good over the merely pleasant at the small daily forks, and they hold even their best understanding lightly, as a finger pointing at the moon rather than the moon itself. The fruit is a kind of clear-eyed groundedness: confident where confidence is warranted, humble where it is not.

excess.label: In excess — the pride of ‘knowing’; intellectualism as bypass

facets.overview: The characteristic distortion here is intellectual spiritual pride: a head richly furnished with concepts, mistaken for actual transformation. The person can discourse brilliantly on non-duality and remains entirely run by their reactions; they have collected realizations as information and confused the menu for the meal. The Kena’s warning lands precisely on them — the one who thinks he knows, knows not. There is a subtler form too: using analysis itself as an evasion, retreating into the safety of conceptualizing whenever direct feeling or honest not-knowing threatens.

excess.text: The characteristic distortion here is intellectual spiritual pride: a head richly furnished with concepts, mistaken for actual transformation. The person can discourse brilliantly on non-duality and remains entirely run by their reactions; they have collected realizations as information and confused the menu for the meal. The Kena’s warning lands precisely on them — the one who thinks he knows, knows not. There is a subtler form too: using analysis itself as an evasion, retreating into the safety of conceptualizing whenever direct feeling or honest not-knowing threatens.

deficient.label: Deficient — credulity, or no discernment at all

facets.overview: At the other pole is the absence of discrimination: a person who cannot tell the pleasant from the good, the sound source from the flattering one, the realized teacher from the charlatan. They believe too easily or chase whatever gratifies, swept along by preyas at every fork, with no inner compass of viveka to steer by. Where the excess pole knows too proudly, the deficient pole does not really know at all — and both, in their different ways, keep a person bound.

Where the excess pole knows too proudly, the deficient pole does not really know at all — and both, in their different ways, keep a person bound.

additional_info.heading: 4. Higher and Lower Knowledge
additional_info.text: The Muṇḍaka Upaniṣad opens with the foundational distinction: there are two kinds of knowledge to be known, the lower (aparā vidyā) and the higher (parā vidyā). The lower includes everything that can be learned and stored — even the scriptures themselves, when held merely as information. The higher is that by which the imperishable is directly realized. The point is not that the lower is worthless but that it is not enough: one can master every text and remain unfree, because liberation is a different order of knowing altogether.

attributes.title: Knowledge vs Knowing (Information vs Realization)
attributes.aspect: ज्ञान विवेक   ·   Chapter 42 The nature of this aspect. The crucial distinction between accumulated information (knowledge) and direct realization (knowing). Knowledge is parasitic, can grow infinitely without transformation. Knowing requires personal alchemical change—you must become what you would know.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework — an epistemic distinction · Valence: Neutral — a discernment to make · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Framework (clarifies how one knows) · Key relation: information (about) vs realization (being) — the core distinction of this dimension.
coordinate.key_relation: information (about) vs realization (being) — the core distinction of this dimension

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Both used wisely—knowledge for practical life and social function, knowing for spiritual realization. Not confusing one for the other.
facets.somatic: Body engaged in both worldly knowledge and embodied knowing.
facets.emotional: Heart open to knowing while head functions for knowledge.
facets.cognitive: Mind clear that knowledge and knowing are different dimensions, both honored.
facets.psychic: The soul values realization over information, knowing that data about truth and the living taste of it are different orders.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Knowledge accumulated as ego-asset—becoming 'man of knowledge' while being empty inside. Or rejecting knowledge claiming only knowing matters.
facets.somatic: Either body burdened with study or rejecting all learning.
facets.emotional: Pride in knowledge or contempt for learning, both ego-states.
facets.cognitive: Confusing knowledge for knowing or vice versa.
facets.psychic: The soul mistakes accumulated knowledge for attainment, a library of concepts standing in for the missing realization.

facets.cognitive: Mind crowded with information, no realization.
facets.psychic: The soul knows neither, neither informed nor awakened, drifting without orientation.

attributes.title: Veda vs Vedanta (Knowledge for Ignorant vs Knower)
attributes.aspect: वेद-वेदान्त   ·   Chapter 50 The nature of this aspect. Two layers of teaching—one provisional for those still identified, one ultimate for the realized. The ignorant cannot hear the ultimate teaching directly; the provisional teaching breaks identification. When identification breaks, the ultimate teaching is naturally realized.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework — an epistemic distinction · Valence: Neutral — a discernment to make · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Framework (clarifies how one knows) · Key relation: ritual knowledge for the ignorant vs the knowledge of the knower.

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Understanding which teaching applies to which state. Provisional teachings used skillfully to break identification; ultimate teachings recognized when consciousness has matured.
facets.somatic: Body engaged in appropriate practices for current state.
facets.emotional: Emotional engagement matched to capacity.
facets.cognitive: Mind receiving teaching appropriate to its current level.
facets.psychic: The soul honors ritual knowledge as preparation while pressing on toward the knowledge that liberates.

facets.cognitive: Mental theft of ultimate teaching without authentic level.

facets.cognitive: Mind limited to first-layer understanding.
facets.psychic: The soul has neither the discipline of the path nor its culmination, ungrounded at both ends.

attributes.title: Vijnana (Understanding Beyond Information)
attributes.aspect: ·   Chapter 7, Section 7 The nature of this aspect. Vijnana is the discriminating intelligence that distinguishes between what is real and what is apparent. Beyond mere knowledge (jnana), it is the applied wisdom that knows what matters and what doesn't, what is eternal and what is passing. Higher Vijnana directly perceives the Self.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Faculty — higher, realized understanding (vijñāna) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Mechanism (realized, not merely informational, knowing) · Key relation: Higher knowledge; kin to the Vijñānamaya kośa (Dim 5) and Jñāna (Dim 10).
coordinate.key_relation: Higher knowledge; kin to the Vijñānamaya kośa (Dim 5) and Jñāna (Dim 10)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Active discriminating wisdom in every encounter. Seeing through appearances to essences.
facets.somatic: Body's choices guided by discrimination.
facets.emotional: Emotional discrimination—real love vs. attachment, real joy vs. excitement.
facets.cognitive: Mind continuously discriminates real from apparent.
facets.psychic: The soul reaches understanding that has ripened past mere information into lived comprehension.

facets.emotional: Emotional disengagement disguised as discrimination.
facets.cognitive: Mental analysis as escape.
facets.psychic: The soul prides itself on subtle understanding, the intellect inflating where humility was the lesson.

attributes.title: Para-Apara Vidya Distinction (Krishnananda's Deep Treatment)
attributes.aspect: ·   Mundaka 1.1 The nature of this aspect. Krishnananda emphasizes that even Vedas, sacred texts, philosophy, sciences are 'apara' (lower) because they remain object-knowledge. 'Para' (higher) is only the knowledge BY WHICH the Imperishable is realized—which is direct knower-knowledge, not knower-knowing-objects.

facets.psychic: The soul holds clearly the difference between knowledge that informs and knowledge that transforms, seeking the higher without despising the lower.

attributes.title: Vidya and Avidya (Knowledge and Action Combined)
attributes.aspect: विद्या-अविद्या   ·   Verses 9-11 The nature of this aspect. Those who worship only action (Avidya/karma) go to dark worlds; those who worship only knowledge (Vidya) go to greater darkness. He who knows both together transcends mortality through action and reaches immortality through knowledge.

attributes.title: Parā and Aparā Vidyā — The Two Knowledges
attributes.aspect: परा-अपरा विद्या   ·   1.1.4-5 The nature of this aspect. The two knowledges are the higher and the lower: aparā, the knowledge of everything that can be learned, named, and stored — including scripture-as-information; and parā, the knowledge by which the imperishable Self is directly realized. The crucial teaching is that no quantity of the lower ever adds up to the higher — they are different in kind, not degree. A library of spiritual books is aparā; a single moment of direct recognition is parā. The whole danger of the educated seeker is to mistake an abundance of the first for the second.

additional_info.heading: 5. How the Absolute Is Known — and Not
additional_info.text: If the highest knowledge is direct realization, how is the Absolute approached at all by a mind that can only grasp objects? The tradition’s answer is subtle: the ultimate cannot be defined, only indicated. Every positive description fails, because to describe is to make an object, and the Absolute is the very subject that does the knowing. So the great method is negative — neti, neti, ‘not this, not this’ — stripping away everything the Self is not until what cannot be stripped away remains, known not as an object but as one’s own being.

attributes.title: Impersonal Divine (TAT vs THOU)
attributes.aspect: तत्   ·   Chapter 43 The nature of this aspect. The divine is not a person—personhood is itself illusion. Therefore no I-Thou relationship is possible. The divine is 'That'—pure existence without personality. One cannot be related TO the divine; one can only become ONE with it.

attributes.title: Naming the Divine (Indication, not Definition)
attributes.aspect: नाम   ·   Chapter 25 The nature of this aspect. Naming the divine is necessary for relating, but every name is artificial. The Upanishads use negative theology (neti-neti) to avoid creating positive limitations. To say 'He is one' creates the conflict 'we say He is all.' To say 'He is not two' includes both possibilities.

attributes.title: Symptoms vs Disease (Sankara's Diagnostic)
attributes.aspect: ·   Sankara's diagnostic principle The nature of this aspect. Critical diagnostic insight: anxiety, depression, addiction, restlessness are SYMPTOMS, not the disease. The actual disease is mis-identification (taking non-Self as Self). Treating symptoms relieves temporarily; treating mis-identification cures. Therapy stabilizes personality; Vedanta dissolves mis-identity. They operate at different depths.

attributes.title: Neti-Neti — Knowing by Negation
attributes.aspect: नेति-नेति   ·   Chapter 29 The nature of this aspect. Neti-neti, ‘not this, not this,’ is the via negativa of the Upaniṣads — the method of approaching the Self by systematically negating everything it is not. Not the body, not the senses, not the breath, not the mind, not any object that can be known — for all of these are things the Self knows, and the knower can never be among the known. What remains when every ‘this’ has been set aside is not a final object but the objectless awareness itself, recognized as one’s own nature. It is knowing by subtraction, the only knowing adequate to what cannot be made an object.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Practice / method — knowing by negation (‘not this, not this’) · Valence: Cultivated method · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Mechanism (negates all objects to reveal the Self) · Key relation: The via negativa of the Upaniṣads; kin to Viveka (Dim 23).

additional_info.heading: 6. Discrimination and the Turn Inward
additional_info.text: The Kaṭha Upaniṣad gives the most consequential of the daily distinctions: at every fork stand two roads, the preyas, the pleasant, and the śreyas, the good. The pleasant gratifies now; the good truly serves. The wise, says Death to Nachiketa, choose the good; the foolish choose the pleasant. And the whole turn of the spiritual life is figured as a reversal of direction — from the outward vision of senses chasing objects to the inward vision of awareness seeking its own source. Subjectivity, the tradition says strikingly, is spirituality: the turn toward the knowing subject is the turn toward the real.

attributes.title: Choicelessness (Mind as Choice-Maker)
attributes.aspect: अनिर्वचनीयता   ·   Implicit throughout The nature of this aspect. Mind functions through choice—creating dichotomies that don't exist in reality. Choosing one side means choosing the other (love implies hate). Either embrace both, or remain choiceless—both dissolve the mind.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: State / stance — awareness prior to the choosing mind · Valence: The fruit / cultivated stance · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Fruit (awareness before choice) · Key relation: Choiceless awareness; the mind seen as the choice-maker to transcend.

facets.somatic: Body relaxed without internal conflict, no fighting one half of life against the other.
facets.emotional: Emotional acceptance of all polarities, no internal civil war.
facets.cognitive: Mind beyond duality, choiceless witness, peace beyond preference.
facets.psychic: The soul rests in choiceless awareness, watching the mind's preferences arise and pass without being driven by them.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Forced choicelessness—pretending to have no preferences while still divided inside. Or claiming acceptance of all while suppressing real choices.
facets.somatic: Body's natural preferences suppressed in name of choicelessness.
facets.emotional: False neutrality covering real feelings, suppression masquerading as wisdom.
facets.cognitive: Mind pretending unity while still divided, intellectual concept of choicelessness.
facets.psychic: The soul makes 'choicelessness' an excuse for passivity, refusing necessary decisions in the name of non-doing.

attributes.title: Subjectivity as Spirituality
attributes.aspect: अन्तर्मुखता   ·   Chapter 32 The nature of this aspect. The divine cannot be objectified—it is pure subjectivity. To know it, one must become it. Religion is not knowledge ABOUT the divine but living AS the divine. Subjectivity is spirituality; objectivity is science.

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.somatic: Body becomes vehicle of subjective realization, alive with presence.
facets.emotional: Emotions infused with the felt presence of the divine.
facets.cognitive: Mind drops object-orientation, becomes pure subject—the divine itself.
facets.psychic: The soul turns toward the subject — the awareness that knows — recognizing the spiritual quest as inward, not a hunt among objects.

attributes.title: Outward Vision vs Inward Vision
attributes.aspect: ·   Lahiri's foreword The nature of this aspect. Lahiri makes a crucial distinction: outward vision (scholarly understanding of scripture) and inward vision (direct experiential revelation through Kriya). Scholarly study comes first—necessary preparation. But it does not produce inward vision automatically. Inward vision is revealed only through Guru-initiation in the lineage. Both are necessary; outward without inward is barren scholarship.

facets.cognitive: Mind cultivating both scholarly understanding and direct revelation.

attributes.title: Preyas and Śreyas — The Pleasant and the Good
attributes.aspect: प्रेयस्-श्रेयस्   ·   1.2.1-2 The nature of this aspect. At every choice point, the Kaṭha Upaniṣad teaches, two paths present themselves: the preyas, the pleasant, which gratifies the senses and the ego in the moment, and the śreyas, the good, which genuinely serves one’s deepest welfare though it may cost something now. They rarely coincide. The whole quality of a life is set by which one is habitually chosen — and the discriminating intelligence that can tell them apart, and the strength to choose the good over the merely pleasant, is the very faculty the path is built to develop.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework — an epistemic distinction · Valence: Neutral — a discernment to make · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Framework (clarifies how one knows) · Key relation: the pleasant (preyas) vs the good (śreyas) — the fork the Katha names.

balanced.label: Balanced — the good discerned and chosen over the merely pleasant

facets.somatic: Body chooses long-term wellbeing over short-term indulgence.
facets.emotional: Emotional choices favor depth over surface pleasure.
facets.cognitive: Mind discriminates wisely between preyas and shreyas.
facets.psychic: At each fork the soul can tell the merely pleasant (preyas) from the truly good (śreyas), and has the strength to choose the good.

additional_info.heading: 7. The Limits of the Knower
additional_info.text: Crowning the dimension is its most humbling teaching: the strict limit of the knowing mind before the ultimate. The Kena declares that the Self is the ‘ear of the ear, the eye of the eye’ — that by which all knowing happens, and therefore never itself an object of knowing. Gārgī, in the Bṛhadāraṇyaka, presses Yājñavalkya question after question, ‘and what is that warp woven on?’ — until she reaches the imperishable beyond which no further question can go. And the Kena states the paradox outright: the one who thinks he knows, knows not. This is not anti-intellectualism; it is the intellect’s own honest recognition of its boundary.

attributes.title: Sky/Space Metaphor (Inner Suchness)
attributes.aspect: आकाश-तथता   ·   Chapter 48 The nature of this aspect. Sky/space is the perfect metaphor for the Self. Sky contains everything, is touched by nothing. The inner self has the same quality—all experiences happen IN it but never touch it. Recognition of this inner sky-nature is jivanmukti.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework — a vision / pointer · Valence: Neutral — a pointer to be realized · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Individual, toward the Absolute · Causal status: Framework / realization · Key relation: space / sky as the image of inner suchness; kin to Ākāśa (Dim 1 / 25).

attributes.title: Shrotrasya Shrotram (Ear of the Ear)
attributes.aspect: श्रोत्रस्य श्रोत्रम्   ·   Verse 1.2 The nature of this aspect. Brahman is the ear of the ear, the mind of the mind, the breath of the breath, the eye of the eye. The senses are organs—but what enables them to function as senses is the deeper consciousness.

attributes.title: Gargi's Inquiry (Beyond the Beyond)
attributes.aspect: गार्गी जिज्ञासा   ·   3.6.1, 3.8.1-12 The nature of this aspect. Gargi Vachaknavi asks Yajnavalkya progressive questions: 'On what is the earth woven, warp and woof?' Each answer leads to another question 'on what is THAT woven?' Through worlds of water, air, sky, sun, moon, stars, gods, Indra-world, Prajapati-world, Brahma-world. Finally Yajnavalkya: 'Don't ask too much, Gargi, lest your head fall off.' She finds the Akshara (Imperishable) on which all is woven.

attributes.title: Yo Manyate Na Veda — He Who Thinks He Knows, Knows Not
attributes.aspect: यो मन्यते न वेद   ·   Verses 2.1-2.3 The nature of this aspect. The Kena’s great paradox: the one who claims to comprehend the Absolute thereby reveals that he has reduced it to an object and so missed it; the one who realizes that it cannot be comprehended as an object has come nearer the truth. ‘It is unknown to those who know and known to those who do not know.’ This is the epistemic humility at the heart of the tradition — not the false modesty of ‘I might be wrong,’ but the structural recognition that the knower can never be made into the known, and that the deepest knowing wears the face of a luminous not-knowing.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework — the paradox of knowing · Valence: Neutral · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Framework (he who thinks he knows, knows not) · Key relation: The Kena paradox — Brahman is unknown to the one who claims to know it.

facets.emotional: Emotional comfort with not-knowing, sacred mystery.
facets.cognitive: Mind holds the paradox, neither claiming nor denying knowledge.
facets.psychic: The soul holds its knowing in humility; sensing that the Absolute exceeds every thought, it stays open and unfinished before the mystery.

additional_info.heading: 8. Maps and Further Frameworks
additional_info.text: Around these core distinctions the tradition arranges a number of further cognitive maps. Satchidānanda — existence-consciousness-bliss — names the Absolute not as three attributes but as the single nature of reality, the framework within which the highest knowing orients itself. The three layers of matter, energy, and consciousness order the levels of reality the knower moves through. The doctrine of adhikāri-bheda — the differentiation of qualified seekers, figured as four kinds of horse — recognizes that different people are ready for different teachings, so that knowledge must be matched to the knower. The buddhi, the discerning intellect, is honored as the highest instrument within the mind, the faculty of viveka itself. And the recognition that maps are maps — that even the finest framework, like satchidānanda or the levels of reality, is a pointer and not the territory — returns the whole dimension to its founding humility.

attributes.title: Satchidananda (Existence-Consciousness-Bliss)
attributes.aspect: सच्चिदानन्द   ·   Chapter 43 The nature of this aspect. The three inseparable attributes of ultimate reality. To realize any one is to realize all three—they are not three separate things but one reality named from three angles. Existence is conscious, consciousness is blissful, bliss is existence.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework — Being-Consciousness-Bliss (sat-cit-ānanda) · Valence: Neutral — the nature of Brahman · Guṇa: Beyond the guṇas · Scale: Transcendent · Causal status: Ground · Key relation: The threefold nature of Brahman; kin to Satya-Jnana-Ananta (Dim 25).
coordinate.key_relation: The threefold nature of Brahman; kin to Satya-Jnana-Ananta (Dim 25)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.emotional: Emotional sense of permanence beneath constant change.
facets.cognitive: Mind discovers the unchanging witness through changing states.
facets.psychic: The soul recognizes its own nature as existence-consciousness-bliss, and rests in the fullness it had sought outside.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Intellectual grasp of Satchidananda without realization—endlessly quoting the formula without inner contact.
facets.somatic: Body unchanged by intellectual concept.
facets.emotional: No emotional contact with the bliss aspect.
facets.cognitive: Mind playing with words, missing reality.
facets.psychic: The soul turns the formula into a mantra of identity, asserting 'I am bliss' while suffering, the words outrunning realization.

excess.text:
Intellectual grasp of Satchidananda without realization—endlessly quoting the formula without inner contact.

Somatic: Body unchanged by intellectual concept.

Emotional: No emotional contact with the bliss aspect.

Cognitive: Mind playing with words, missing reality.

Psychic: The soul turns the formula into a mantra of identity, asserting 'I am bliss' while suffering, the words outrunning realization.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Identification only with changing states—'I am child/young/old.' No awareness of the unchanging 'I am' beneath all changes.
facets.somatic: Body identified with as self through all changes—each change is identity crisis.
facets.emotional: Emotions fully identified with—I am angry/happy/sad.
facets.cognitive: Mind identified with all its contents, no witness of unchanging existence.
facets.psychic: The soul knows itself only as a needy, transient creature, blind to the being-awareness-joy at its core.

deficient.text:
Identification only with changing states—'I am child/young/old.' No awareness of the unchanging 'I am' beneath all changes.

Somatic: Body identified with as self through all changes—each change is identity crisis.

Emotional: Emotions fully identified with—I am angry/happy/sad.

Cognitive: Mind identified with all its contents, no witness of unchanging existence.

Psychic: The soul knows itself only as a needy, transient creature, blind to the being-awareness-joy at its core.

attributes.title: Three Layers: Matter, Energy, Consciousness
attributes.aspect: त्रिस्तर   ·   Chapter 50 The nature of this aspect. Three layers of reality—matter (gross), energy (subtle), consciousness (causal). Science is moving from matter to energy; Vedanta knows the deeper layer of consciousness. In your body, these three layers correspond. Penetrating to consciousness reveals the whole world as conscious.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework — a vision / pointer · Valence: Neutral — a pointer to be realized · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Individual, toward the Absolute · Causal status: Framework / realization · Key relation: the graded map of reality as matter, energy, and consciousness.
coordinate.valence: Neutral — a pointer to be realized
coordinate.key_relation: the graded map of reality as matter, energy, and consciousness

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Penetration through all three layers—matter to energy to consciousness. The world recognized as conscious manifestation, not dead matter.
facets.somatic: Body known as conscious manifestation, not just matter.
facets.emotional: Emotions known as energy-movements within consciousness.
facets.cognitive: Mind known as consciousness in motion, with the unmoved witness behind.
facets.psychic: The soul understands itself as more than matter and energy — as the consciousness in which both appear — and lives from that depth.

balanced.text:
Penetration through all three layers—matter to energy to consciousness. The world recognized as conscious manifestation, not dead matter.

Somatic: Body known as conscious manifestation, not just matter.

Emotional: Emotions known as energy-movements within consciousness.

Cognitive: Mind known as consciousness in motion, with the unmoved witness behind.

Psychic: The soul understands itself as more than matter and energy — as the consciousness in which both appear — and lives from that depth.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Spiritualist denial of physical reality—claiming all is consciousness while ignoring practical material realities.
facets.somatic: Body neglected as 'just consciousness manifestation.'
facets.emotional: Practical emotions dismissed as 'just energy.'
facets.cognitive: Theory without integration.
facets.psychic: The soul over-systematizes the layers into a rigid map, mistaking the diagram for the lived depth.

excess.text:
Spiritualist denial of physical reality—claiming all is consciousness while ignoring practical material realities.

Somatic: Body neglected as 'just consciousness manifestation.'

Emotional: Practical emotions dismissed as 'just energy.'

Cognitive: Theory without integration.

Psychic: The soul over-systematizes the layers into a rigid map, mistaking the diagram for the lived depth.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Materialism stuck at first layer—matter as ultimate reality, energy as derivative, consciousness denied entirely.
facets.somatic: Body as ultimate identity, only material reality.
facets.emotional: Emotions reduced to chemicals.
facets.cognitive: Mind reduced to brain matter, consciousness denied.
facets.psychic: The soul takes itself to be only matter and energy, with no sense of the consciousness that knows them.

deficient.text:
Materialism stuck at first layer—matter as ultimate reality, energy as derivative, consciousness denied entirely.

Somatic: Body as ultimate identity, only material reality.

Emotional: Emotions reduced to chemicals.

Cognitive: Mind reduced to brain matter, consciousness denied.

Psychic: The soul takes itself to be only matter and energy, with no sense of the consciousness that knows them.

attributes.title: Purusha (Witness Consciousness)
attributes.aspect: पुरुष   ·   Chapter 13, Chapter 15 The nature of this aspect. Purusha is the experience of being an objective, unattached observer of your own thoughts, emotions, and physical actions. It is the witness consciousness that remains unchanged while all phenomena arise and pass.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework / principle — witness consciousness (puruṣa) · Valence: Neutral · Guṇa: Beyond the guṇas · Scale: Transcendent · Causal status: Ground (the seer distinct from the seen) · Key relation: The witness; the Sāṁkhya puruṣa, distinguished from prakṛti by Viveka-Khyāti (Dim 11).
coordinate.causal_status: Ground (the seer distinct from the seen)
coordinate.key_relation: The witness; the Sāṁkhya puruṣa, distinguished from prakṛti by Viveka-Khyāti (Dim 11)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When Purusha consciousness is accessed, a person observes thoughts and feelings without identification. There is freedom from emotional reactivity and profound inner peace.
facets.somatic: Physical ease and relaxation from non-identification with body, freedom from physical reactivity, light and free physical presence, body functions naturally.
facets.emotional: Emotional freedom from identification with feelings, ability to observe emotions without being swept away, emotional peace and non-reactivity, emotional freedom.
facets.cognitive: Mental clarity from observing thoughts, freedom from mental identification, mental peace and stillness, clarity about what is real vs. mental construct.
facets.psychic: The soul recognizes the silent witness, distinct from the moving show of nature, and rests as the seer rather than the seen.

balanced.text:
When Purusha consciousness is accessed, a person observes thoughts and feelings without identification. There is freedom from emotional reactivity and profound inner peace.

Somatic: Physical ease and relaxation from non-identification with body, freedom from physical reactivity, light and free physical presence, body functions naturally.

Emotional: Emotional freedom from identification with feelings, ability to observe emotions without being swept away, emotional peace and non-reactivity, emotional freedom.

Cognitive: Mental clarity from observing thoughts, freedom from mental identification, mental peace and stillness, clarity about what is real vs. mental construct.

Psychic: The soul recognizes the silent witness, distinct from the moving show of nature, and rests as the seer rather than the seen.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Over-identification with witness consciousness can lead to dissociation, disconnection from body and emotion, and inability to engage meaningfully with life.
facets.somatic: Physical disconnection and numbness, difficulty functioning in physical world, disembodiment, inability to feel body.
facets.emotional: Emotional detachment and numbness, inability to feel appropriately, disconnection from emotional life, spiritual bypass of emotional work.
facets.cognitive: Mental dissociation, inability to engage with practical concerns, spiritual escape from necessary growth, detachment from meaningful engagement.
facets.psychic: The soul turns 'I am the witness' into a detached posture, watching itself watching.

excess.text:
Over-identification with witness consciousness can lead to dissociation, disconnection from body and emotion, and inability to engage meaningfully with life.

Somatic: Physical disconnection and numbness, difficulty functioning in physical world, disembodiment, inability to feel body.

Emotional: Emotional detachment and numbness, inability to feel appropriately, disconnection from emotional life, spiritual bypass of emotional work.

Cognitive: Mental dissociation, inability to engage with practical concerns, spiritual escape from necessary growth, detachment from meaningful engagement.

Psychic: The soul turns 'I am the witness' into a detached posture, watching itself watching.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Inability to access witness consciousness results in complete identification with thoughts and emotions. There is no freedom, only reactivity and suffering.
facets.somatic: Physical reactivity and tension, identification with bodily sensations, poor physical awareness, reactive physical behavior.
facets.emotional: Complete emotional identification and reactivity, emotional suffering from identification, inability to step back from feelings, emotional slavery.
facets.cognitive: Complete mental identification, inability to observe thoughts, mental reactivity and suffering, mental slavery to thought patterns.
facets.psychic: The soul is wholly absorbed in the play of nature, never noticing the unmoving witness behind it.

deficient.text:
Inability to access witness consciousness results in complete identification with thoughts and emotions. There is no freedom, only reactivity and suffering.

Somatic: Physical reactivity and tension, identification with bodily sensations, poor physical awareness, reactive physical behavior.

Emotional: Complete emotional identification and reactivity, emotional suffering from identification, inability to step back from feelings, emotional slavery.

Cognitive: Complete mental identification, inability to observe thoughts, mental reactivity and suffering, mental slavery to thought patterns.

Psychic: The soul is wholly absorbed in the play of nature, never noticing the unmoving witness behind it.

attributes.title: Order of Progression (10 Levels of Reality)
attributes.aspect: क्रम-आरोहण   ·   1.3.10-11 The nature of this aspect. Beyond the senses are objects, beyond them is mind, beyond mind is intellect, beyond intellect is the Cosmic Mind (Mahat), beyond Mahat is the Unmanifest (Mulaprakriti), beyond which is Purusha (Spirit). Beyond Purusha there is nothing. This is the gradient of reality from gross to subtle to ultimate.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework — a vision / pointer · Valence: Neutral — a pointer to be realized · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Individual, toward the Absolute · Causal status: Framework / realization · Key relation: the ten graded levels of reality — a map of ascent.
coordinate.valence: Neutral — a pointer to be realized
coordinate.key_relation: the ten graded levels of reality — a map of ascent

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Understanding the systematic levels allows progressive practice. One ascends through senses, mind, intellect to cosmic levels.
facets.somatic: Body engaged with senses while aware of higher levels.
facets.emotional: Emotional life understood as movements at one level, contextualized in larger map.
facets.cognitive: Mind has clear map of reality's levels, knows where it stands and where it's going.
facets.psychic: The soul grasps that reality unfolds in graded levels, and meets its own development with patience and a sense of where it stands.

balanced.text:
Understanding the systematic levels allows progressive practice. One ascends through senses, mind, intellect to cosmic levels.

Somatic: Body engaged with senses while aware of higher levels.

Emotional: Emotional life understood as movements at one level, contextualized in larger map.

Cognitive: Mind has clear map of reality's levels, knows where it stands and where it's going.

Psychic: The soul grasps that reality unfolds in graded levels, and meets its own development with patience and a sense of where it stands.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Obsessive mapping of levels as intellectual exercise, missing the direct experience at each.
facets.somatic: Body abandoned in pursuit of higher levels.
facets.emotional: Emotional engagement dismissed as 'lower.'
facets.cognitive: Mental theorizing about levels without genuine ascent.
facets.psychic: The soul fixates on the map of levels, measuring and ranking its progress rather than living it.

excess.text:
Obsessive mapping of levels as intellectual exercise, missing the direct experience at each.

Somatic: Body abandoned in pursuit of higher levels.

Emotional: Emotional engagement dismissed as 'lower.'

Cognitive: Mental theorizing about levels without genuine ascent.

Psychic: The soul fixates on the map of levels, measuring and ranking its progress rather than living it.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Confined to one level (usually senses or mind) with no awareness of higher dimensions of reality.
facets.somatic: Body taken as the only reality.
facets.emotional: Emotional life seen as final dimension.
facets.cognitive: Mind unable to conceive higher levels of reality.
facets.psychic: The soul has no sense of any graded order, and its development proceeds blind and haphazard.

deficient.text:
Confined to one level (usually senses or mind) with no awareness of higher dimensions of reality.

Somatic: Body taken as the only reality.

Emotional: Emotional life seen as final dimension.

Cognitive: Mind unable to conceive higher levels of reality.

Psychic: The soul has no sense of any graded order, and its development proceeds blind and haphazard.

attributes.title: Gravitation vs Grace (Two Cosmic Laws)
attributes.aspect: गुरुत्व-अनुग्रह   ·   Chapter 38 The nature of this aspect. Two fundamental laws govern existence: gravitation (pull toward objects, downward and outward) and grace (pull toward Source, inward and upward). When body-attraction breaks through awareness, grace becomes operative. The fundamental orientation reverses.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework — an epistemic distinction · Valence: Neutral — a discernment to make · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Framework (clarifies how one knows) · Key relation: two cosmic laws — the downward pull of matter vs the upward lift of grace.
coordinate.key_relation: two cosmic laws — the downward pull of matter vs the upward lift of grace

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Available to grace—pulled inward and upward naturally. Not fighting gravitation but transcending its limited domain. Both laws known; one is freely chosen.
facets.somatic: Body responds to subtle grace-pull, sense of being uplifted, lightness.
facets.emotional: Emotional life lifted by grace, joy not dependent on sense-objects.
facets.cognitive: Mind oriented toward inner sky, available to higher pulls.
facets.psychic: The soul senses two forces — the downward pull of conditioning and the upward lift of grace — and consciously aligns with the lift.

balanced.text:
Available to grace—pulled inward and upward naturally. Not fighting gravitation but transcending its limited domain. Both laws known; one is freely chosen.

Somatic: Body responds to subtle grace-pull, sense of being uplifted, lightness.

Emotional: Emotional life lifted by grace, joy not dependent on sense-objects.

Cognitive: Mind oriented toward inner sky, available to higher pulls.

Psychic: The soul senses two forces — the downward pull of conditioning and the upward lift of grace — and consciously aligns with the lift.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Forced spirituality trying to push grace—efforts that themselves block the law of grace which requires receptivity.
facets.somatic: Body strained in spiritual effort.
facets.emotional: Anxious longing for grace, demanding spiritual experience.
facets.cognitive: Mental striving that itself blocks the natural inflow.
facets.psychic: The soul presumes upon grace, expecting to be lifted while doing nothing to cooperate.

excess.text:
Forced spirituality trying to push grace—efforts that themselves block the law of grace which requires receptivity.

Emotional: Anxious longing for grace, demanding spiritual experience.

Cognitive: Mental striving that itself blocks the natural inflow.

Psychic: The soul presumes upon grace, expecting to be lifted while doing nothing to cooperate.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Total gravity-bondage—drawn outward to bodies, hormones, objects. Maximum slavery to biological/chemical pulls.
facets.somatic: Body driven by chemical attractions, addiction, depletion.
facets.emotional: Emotional life ruled by sensual pulls.
facets.cognitive: Mind constantly seeking outer objects, slavery to gravitation.
facets.psychic: The soul knows only gravity, the downward pull of habit, with no sense of any force that could raise it.

deficient.text:
Total gravity-bondage—drawn outward to bodies, hormones, objects. Maximum slavery to biological/chemical pulls.

Somatic: Body driven by chemical attractions, addiction, depletion.

Emotional: Emotional life ruled by sensual pulls.

Cognitive: Mind constantly seeking outer objects, slavery to gravitation.

Psychic: The soul knows only gravity, the downward pull of habit, with no sense of any force that could raise it.

attributes.title: Prarbdha Karma (Past Momentum)
attributes.aspect: प्रारब्ध कर्म   ·   Chapter 48 The nature of this aspect. Prarabdha is the karma that has begun to fructify—it must run its course even after realization. The realized one doesn't generate new karma but the past momentum continues. This explains why even Buddha experienced physical illness, aging, death.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Psychic · Ontic mode: Process / given portion — past momentum (prārabdha) · Valence: Neutral (fixed conditions) · Guṇa: Mixed · Scale: Individual, this lifetime · Causal status: Effect-in-force · Key relation: Same as Prārabdha Karma (Dim 12); the momentum a knower still lives out.
coordinate.key_relation: Same as Prārabdha Karma (Dim 12); the momentum a knower still lives out

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Realization complete but bodily life continues until prarabdha exhausts. The witness watches the unfolding momentum without identification.
facets.somatic: Body continues karmic completion while self remains free.
facets.emotional: Emotional consequences of past completing without new generation.
facets.cognitive: Mind witnesses karmic unfolding without ownership.
facets.psychic: The soul accepts the momentum of past action as the given conditions of this life, meeting them without resentment or fatalism.

balanced.text:
Realization complete but bodily life continues until prarabdha exhausts. The witness watches the unfolding momentum without identification.

Somatic: Body continues karmic completion while self remains free.

Emotional: Emotional consequences of past completing without new generation.

Cognitive: Mind witnesses karmic unfolding without ownership.

Psychic: The soul accepts the momentum of past action as the given conditions of this life, meeting them without resentment or fatalism.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Using prarabdha as excuse for not changing—'My past karma made me do this.' Fatalism disguised as wisdom.
facets.somatic: Body neglected as 'just karma.'
facets.emotional: Emotional irresponsibility hiding behind karma-talk.
facets.cognitive: Mental excuse-making with karma-doctrine.
facets.psychic: The soul over-uses 'it is my prārabdha' as a fatalistic excuse, surrendering all responsibility to destiny.

excess.text:
Using prarabdha as excuse for not changing—'My past karma made me do this.' Fatalism disguised as wisdom.

Somatic: Body neglected as 'just karma.'

Emotional: Emotional irresponsibility hiding behind karma-talk.

Cognitive: Mental excuse-making with karma-doctrine.

Psychic: The soul over-uses 'it is my prārabdha' as a fatalistic excuse, surrendering all responsibility to destiny.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: No awareness of karmic momentum—every effect creates new clinging, identification, more karma. Endless wheel.
facets.somatic: Body fully engaged in karma-creation.
facets.emotional: Emotional reactions creating more karma.
facets.cognitive: Mental engagement with everything creating new bondage.
facets.psychic: The soul rages against its given conditions as if they should not be, exhausting itself fighting what cannot change.

deficient.text:

Psychic: The soul rages against its given conditions as if they should not be, exhausting itself fighting what cannot change.

attributes.title: Adhikari-Bheda (Four Types of Seekers / Four Horses)
attributes.aspect: ·   XV.1 The nature of this aspect. Ashtavakra acknowledges different categories of seekers (adhikari-bheda). Some realize through a single phrase; others need elaborate instruction; others never realize despite a lifetime. The path is not one-size-fits-all. The Quantum Leap path works for the highest-caliber seeker; others may need preparatory work. Recognizing one's category is itself important wisdom.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework — the four types of seekers (the four horses) · Valence: Neutral (a taxonomy of readiness) · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Framework (teaching fitted to fitness) · Key relation: The gradation of seekers; kin to the readiness of Sādhana Chatuṣṭaya (Dim 23).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Framework — the four types of seekers (the four horses)
coordinate.key_relation: The gradation of seekers; kin to the readiness of Sādhana Chatuṣṭaya (Dim 23)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Honest self-assessment of category without false modesty or pride. Appropriate path chosen accordingly.
facets.somatic: Body engaged in path appropriate to one's stage.
facets.emotional: Emotional acceptance of one's actual capacity.
facets.cognitive: Mind chooses appropriate practices for one's level.
facets.psychic: The soul honors that different seekers need different paths, and chooses the practice fitted to its own readiness.

balanced.text:
Honest self-assessment of category without false modesty or pride. Appropriate path chosen accordingly.

Somatic: Body engaged in path appropriate to one's stage.

Emotional: Emotional acceptance of one's actual capacity.

Cognitive: Mind chooses appropriate practices for one's level.

Psychic: The soul honors that different seekers need different paths, and chooses the practice fitted to its own readiness.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Claiming high adhikari status without justification. Skipping necessary preparation.
facets.somatic: Body unprepared but claiming readiness.
facets.emotional: Emotional pride in supposed advancement.
facets.cognitive: Mind takes Quantum Leap path without genuine capacity.
facets.psychic: The soul ranks itself proudly among the 'higher' types, using the typology for spiritual vanity.

excess.text:
Claiming high adhikari status without justification. Skipping necessary preparation.

Somatic: Body unprepared but claiming readiness.

Emotional: Emotional pride in supposed advancement.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Falsely modest about one's actual capacity. Or treating self as the dullest, never trying direct path.
facets.somatic: Body in elementary practices for years without progressing.
facets.emotional: Emotional self-deprecation preventing higher engagement.
facets.cognitive: Mind underestimates own readiness.
facets.psychic: The soul forces itself onto a path unsuited to it, ignoring its actual readiness and stalling.
