attributes.title: Sanga (Attachment/Association)
attributes.aspect: सङ्ग   ·   Chapter 2, Chapter 5 The nature of this aspect. Sanga is the foundational psychological trigger where repeated mental focus on an object breeds attachment. This progressive process leads from interest to attachment to desire to anger. It is the mechanism by which external objects become internally anchored through the mind's habitual association.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Faculty tending to affliction — attachment / association (saṅga) · Valence: Affliction (to loosen) · Guṇa: Rajas · Scale: Interpersonal (Ādhibhautika) · Causal status: Mechanism (the clinging that binds) · Key relation: Kin to Rāga (Dim 19); loosened by Vairāgya (Dim 23).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Faculty tending to affliction — attachment / association (saṅga)
coordinate.key_relation: Kin to Rāga (Dim 19); loosened by Vairāgya (Dim 23)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When Sanga is appropriately balanced, a person can engage with objects and activities without becoming psychologically bound to them. There is healthy interaction without compulsive attachment.
facets.somatic: Free and natural physical engagement with activities, healthy body language in interactions, flexibility in physical response, good energy management without depletion.
facets.emotional: Emotional freedom in engagement, ability to enjoy without grasping, emotional flexibility and spontaneity, healthy emotional connections without codependence.
facets.cognitive: Clear mental awareness of the process of attachment forming, ability to observe associations without being consumed by them, mental freedom and fluidity, mindful awareness of mental patterns.
facets.psychic: The soul chooses its company and attachments wisely, knowing that whom and what one keeps near shapes the inner life.

balanced.text:
When Sanga is appropriately balanced, a person can engage with objects and activities without becoming psychologically bound to them. There is healthy interaction without compulsive attachment.

Somatic: Free and natural physical engagement with activities, healthy body language in interactions, flexibility in physical response, good energy management without depletion.

Emotional: Emotional freedom in engagement, ability to enjoy without grasping, emotional flexibility and spontaneity, healthy emotional connections without codependence.

Cognitive: Clear mental awareness of the process of attachment forming, ability to observe associations without being consumed by them, mental freedom and fluidity, mindful awareness of mental patterns.

Psychic: The soul chooses its company and attachments wisely, knowing that whom and what one keeps near shapes the inner life.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Excessive Sanga creates deep psychological bonds to objects, relationships, outcomes, and desires. This leads to compulsive behavior, obsession, and the inevita descent into craving and anger.
facets.somatic: Physical tension from holding onto attachments, compulsive physical behaviors, seeking physical contact or objects repeatedly, nervous habits, physical depletion from excessive engagement.
facets.emotional: Obsessive emotional attachment, emotional vulnerability to loss, emotional reactivity when attachments are threatened, emotional dependence and neediness, anxiety about maintaining connections.
facets.cognitive: Obsessive thinking about attached objects or people, inability to think of anything else, mental preoccupation and rumination, loss of mental freedom and autonomy, mental suffering from anticipated loss.
facets.psychic: The soul clings to associations and identities, bound by the very attachments it should hold lightly.

excess.text:
Excessive Sanga creates deep psychological bonds to objects, relationships, outcomes, and desires. This leads to compulsive behavior, obsession, and the inevita descent into craving and anger.

Somatic: Physical tension from holding onto attachments, compulsive physical behaviors, seeking physical contact or objects repeatedly, nervous habits, physical depletion from excessive engagement.

Emotional: Obsessive emotional attachment, emotional vulnerability to loss, emotional reactivity when attachments are threatened, emotional dependence and neediness, anxiety about maintaining connections.

Cognitive: Obsessive thinking about attached objects or people, inability to think of anything else, mental preoccupation and rumination, loss of mental freedom and autonomy, mental suffering from anticipated loss.

Psychic: The soul clings to associations and identities, bound by the very attachments it should hold lightly.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Deficiency of Sanga results in inability to engage meaningfully or form healthy connections. There is emotional detachment and indifference that prevents normal human bonding.
facets.somatic: Withdrawn physical presence, lack of physical engagement or interaction, poor body language in relationships, physical numbness or dissociation, inability to engage in physical activities.
facets.emotional: Emotional coldness and detachment, inability to feel connected to others, emotional indifference, difficulty experiencing closeness or intimacy, emotional numbness.
facets.cognitive: Mental disinterest and apathy, inability to focus on meaningful pursuits, lack of motivation to engage, mental distance and dissociation, inability to develop deep understanding through study.
facets.psychic: The soul drifts without discernment into any company, shaped helplessly by whatever surrounds it.

deficient.text:
Deficiency of Sanga results in inability to engage meaningfully or form healthy connections. There is emotional detachment and indifference that prevents normal human bonding.

Somatic: Withdrawn physical presence, lack of physical engagement or interaction, poor body language in relationships, physical numbness or dissociation, inability to engage in physical activities.

Emotional: Emotional coldness and detachment, inability to feel connected to others, emotional indifference, difficulty experiencing closeness or intimacy, emotional numbness.

Cognitive: Mental disinterest and apathy, inability to focus on meaningful pursuits, lack of motivation to engage, mental distance and dissociation, inability to develop deep understanding through study.

Psychic: The soul drifts without discernment into any company, shaped helplessly by whatever surrounds it.

attributes.title: Buddhi (Intellect/Discernment)
attributes.aspect: बुद्धि   ·   Chapter 2, Chapter 18 The nature of this aspect. Buddhi is the faculty of logical determination and discernment. The Gita categorizes it into three states (sattvic, rajasic, tamasic), determining how clearly a human can distinguish right from wrong, eternal from temporary, and truth from illusion.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental · Ontic mode: Faculty — the intellect / discerning function · Valence: Neutral faculty · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Instrument (the faculty of discernment) · Key relation: The charioteer of the psyche (Dim 3); the seat of Viveka.
coordinate.key_relation: The charioteer of the psyche (Dim 3); the seat of Viveka

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When Buddhi is sattvic and balanced, a person possesses clear discriminative intelligence that guides right action. This faculty enables distinction between dharma and adharma, benefit and harm.
facets.somatic: Clear eyes and alert posture, confident physical bearing, steady and purposeful movement, healthy nervous system function, integrated body-mind connection.
facets.emotional: Emotional wisdom and clarity, emotional discernment about true vs. false feelings, emotional maturity, emotional confidence in decisions, peaceful emotional acceptance of wisdom.
facets.cognitive: Crystal clear thinking, ability to distinguish right from wrong effortlessly, penetrating insight, excellent problem-solving ability, memory and retention of learning, ability to see root causes.
facets.psychic: The soul's discerning intellect functions clearly, the charioteer steering the senses toward the good.

balanced.text:
When Buddhi is sattvic and balanced, a person possesses clear discriminative intelligence that guides right action. This faculty enables distinction between dharma and adharma, benefit and harm.

Somatic: Clear eyes and alert posture, confident physical bearing, steady and purposeful movement, healthy nervous system function, integrated body-mind connection.

Emotional: Emotional wisdom and clarity, emotional discernment about true vs. false feelings, emotional maturity, emotional confidence in decisions, peaceful emotional acceptance of wisdom.

Cognitive: Crystal clear thinking, ability to distinguish right from wrong effortlessly, penetrating insight, excellent problem-solving ability, memory and retention of learning, ability to see root causes.

Psychic: The soul's discerning intellect functions clearly, the charioteer steering the senses toward the good.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Excessive Buddhi manifests as intellectual arrogance, over-analysis, and a rigid application of logic that lacks compassion or contextual understanding. The intellect becomes a weapon.
facets.somatic: Physical tension in head and neck, restless physical energy, harsh physical gestures, poor sleep from mental over-stimulation, physical rigidity.
facets.emotional: Emotional coldness and detachment, emotional arrogance and superiority, dismissal of others' feelings, lack of emotional wisdom, emotional judgment and criticism.
facets.cognitive: Analysis paralysis and over-intellectualization, inability to make decisions, overthinking simple matters, excessive mental activity, inability to trust intuition, intellectual rigidity.
facets.psychic: The soul over-trusts the intellect, mistaking sharp analysis for wisdom and arguing itself away from the heart.

excess.text:
Excessive Buddhi manifests as intellectual arrogance, over-analysis, and a rigid application of logic that lacks compassion or contextual understanding. The intellect becomes a weapon.

Somatic: Physical tension in head and neck, restless physical energy, harsh physical gestures, poor sleep from mental over-stimulation, physical rigidity.

Emotional: Emotional coldness and detachment, emotional arrogance and superiority, dismissal of others' feelings, lack of emotional wisdom, emotional judgment and criticism.

Cognitive: Analysis paralysis and over-intellectualization, inability to make decisions, overthinking simple matters, excessive mental activity, inability to trust intuition, intellectual rigidity.

Psychic: The soul over-trusts the intellect, mistaking sharp analysis for wisdom and arguing itself away from the heart.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Deficient Buddhi results in poor discernment, inability to distinguish right from wrong, foolish decision-making, and mental delusion. The person lacks clarity to navigate life wisely.
facets.somatic: Confused and uncoordinated physical presence, poor posture and uncertain movement, poor physical judgment, susceptibility to physical harm, weak nervous system function.
facets.emotional: Emotional gullibility and confusion, inability to discern true vs. false emotions, emotional mistakes in relationships, emotional instability from poor judgment.
facets.cognitive: Mental confusion and fog, inability to think clearly, poor decision-making, vulnerability to delusion and false beliefs, inability to learn from experience, scattered thinking.
facets.psychic: The soul's discernment is dull or overridden, the chariot driven by the runaway horses of the senses.

deficient.text:
Deficient Buddhi results in poor discernment, inability to distinguish right from wrong, foolish decision-making, and mental delusion. The person lacks clarity to navigate life wisely.

Somatic: Confused and uncoordinated physical presence, poor posture and uncertain movement, poor physical judgment, susceptibility to physical harm, weak nervous system function.

Emotional: Emotional gullibility and confusion, inability to discern true vs. false emotions, emotional mistakes in relationships, emotional instability from poor judgment.

Cognitive: Mental confusion and fog, inability to think clearly, poor decision-making, vulnerability to delusion and false beliefs, inability to learn from experience, scattered thinking.

Psychic: The soul's discernment is dull or overridden, the chariot driven by the runaway horses of the senses.

attributes.title: Asuri Sampat (Demoniac Qualities)
attributes.aspect: असुरी सम्पत्   ·   Chapter 16 The nature of this aspect. Asuri Sampat is a detailed psychological profile of toxic human traits including pride, arrogance, harshness, anger, insatiable lust, and cruelty. These qualities bind the mind to suffering and create karmic consequences.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Affliction-set — the demoniac qualities · Valence: Affliction (to transmute) · Guṇa: Rajas–Tamas · Scale: Individual / interpersonal · Causal status: Distortion · Key relation: Same as the Āsurī Sampat (Dim 24); the binding endowment.
coordinate.key_relation: Same as the Āsurī Sampat (Dim 24); the binding endowment

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When asuri qualities are minimized, a person exhibits opposing divine qualities: humility, gentleness, compassion, restraint, and respect for others.
facets.somatic: Gentle and non-aggressive body, open and relaxed posture, kind physical presence, healthy respect for others' space, non-violent physical expression.
facets.emotional: Humility and absence of arrogance, compassion and caring for others, emotional gentleness, emotional restraint without suppression, emotional maturity.
facets.cognitive: Humble and respectful thinking, absence of pride, lack of harsh judgment, mental compassion, recognition of limitations and others' worth.
facets.psychic: The soul recognizes the demoniac tendencies in itself — arrogance, greed, deceit — and works to transform them.

balanced.text:
When asuri qualities are minimized, a person exhibits opposing divine qualities: humility, gentleness, compassion, restraint, and respect for others.

Somatic: Gentle and non-aggressive body, open and relaxed posture, kind physical presence, healthy respect for others' space, non-violent physical expression.

Emotional: Humility and absence of arrogance, compassion and caring for others, emotional gentleness, emotional restraint without suppression, emotional maturity.

Cognitive: Humble and respectful thinking, absence of pride, lack of harsh judgment, mental compassion, recognition of limitations and others' worth.

Psychic: The soul recognizes the demoniac tendencies in itself — arrogance, greed, deceit — and works to transform them.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: When asuri qualities are dominant, there is a constellation of toxic traits: pride, arrogance, harshness, cruelty, lust, anger, and insatiable desire. These create suffering for self and others.
facets.somatic: Harsh and aggressive physical presence, violent or cruel physical behaviors, poor self-control in physical expression, physical harm to others, neglect of physical ethics.
facets.emotional: Arrogance and superiority, lack of compassion, emotional cruelty, inability to empathize, emotional lust and possessiveness, emotional rage and anger, emotional insatiability.
facets.cognitive: Proud and arrogant thinking, disrespect for others, harsh and cruel mental patterns, obsessive and controlling thoughts, inability to consider others' perspectives, insatiable mental desires.
facets.psychic: The soul gives these qualities free rein, mistaking ruthlessness and pride for strength.

excess.text:
When asuri qualities are dominant, there is a constellation of toxic traits: pride, arrogance, harshness, cruelty, lust, anger, and insatiable desire. These create suffering for self and others.

Somatic: Harsh and aggressive physical presence, violent or cruel physical behaviors, poor self-control in physical expression, physical harm to others, neglect of physical ethics.

Emotional: Arrogance and superiority, lack of compassion, emotional cruelty, inability to empathize, emotional lust and possessiveness, emotional rage and anger, emotional insatiability.

Cognitive: Proud and arrogant thinking, disrespect for others, harsh and cruel mental patterns, obsessive and controlling thoughts, inability to consider others' perspectives, insatiable mental desires.

Psychic: The soul gives these qualities free rein, mistaking ruthlessness and pride for strength.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Deficiency of asuri qualities (which is desirable) means the absence of these harmful traits. This is identical to having divine qualities.
facets.somatic: Gentle and peaceful physical presence, non-violent expression, respectful physical boundaries.
facets.emotional: Compassionate and humble emotions, absence of arrogance and cruelty, gentle emotional expression.
facets.cognitive: Respectful and humble thinking, compassionate mental patterns, ethical decision-making.
facets.psychic: The soul denies it has any such tendencies at all, and so can never guard against them.

deficient.text:
Deficiency of asuri qualities (which is desirable) means the absence of these harmful traits. This is identical to having divine qualities.

Somatic: Gentle and peaceful physical presence, non-violent expression, respectful physical boundaries.

Emotional: Compassionate and humble emotions, absence of arrogance and cruelty, gentle emotional expression.

Cognitive: Respectful and humble thinking, compassionate mental patterns, ethical decision-making.

Psychic: The soul denies it has any such tendencies at all, and so can never guard against them.

attributes.title: Daivi Sampat (Divine Qualities)
attributes.aspect: दैवी सम्पत्   ·   Chapter 16 The nature of this aspect. Daivi Sampat is a checklist of twenty-six psychological virtues including fearlessness, purification of mind, charity, absence of anger and malice, truthfulness, and self-control. These qualities elevate human consciousness and create positive karma.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Virtue-set — the divine qualities · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual / interpersonal · Causal status: Condition · Key relation: Same as the Daivī Sampat (Dim 24); the liberating endowment.
coordinate.key_relation: Same as the Daivī Sampat (Dim 24); the liberating endowment

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When divine qualities are cultivated and present, a person exhibits fearlessness, purity, generosity, truthfulness, self-control, compassion, and wisdom. Life becomes an expression of these elevating qualities.
facets.somatic: Radiant and healthy appearance, confident and upright posture, non-aggressive physical presence, disciplined physical habits, generosity in physical actions.
facets.emotional: Fearlessness and courage, purity of heart, generosity and compassion, truthfulness in feelings, emotional self-control, absence of anger and malice, emotional elevation.
facets.cognitive: Fearless thinking and ideas, pure and clear mental patterns, generous and expansive thinking, truthful mental expression, disciplined and focused mind, wisdom and spiritual insight.
facets.psychic: The soul cultivates the divine qualities — fearlessness, purity, compassion — as the natural fruit of right knowing.

balanced.text:
When divine qualities are cultivated and present, a person exhibits fearlessness, purity, generosity, truthfulness, self-control, compassion, and wisdom. Life becomes an expression of these elevating qualities.

Somatic: Radiant and healthy appearance, confident and upright posture, non-aggressive physical presence, disciplined physical habits, generosity in physical actions.

Emotional: Fearlessness and courage, purity of heart, generosity and compassion, truthfulness in feelings, emotional self-control, absence of anger and malice, emotional elevation.

Cognitive: Fearless thinking and ideas, pure and clear mental patterns, generous and expansive thinking, truthful mental expression, disciplined and focused mind, wisdom and spiritual insight.

Psychic: The soul cultivates the divine qualities — fearlessness, purity, compassion — as the natural fruit of right knowing.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: While divine qualities are generally beneficial, excessive manifestation can lead to spiritual pride, self-righteousness, judging others for not having these qualities, and disconnection.
facets.somatic: Over-discipline and rigidity, perfectionism in physical habits, harsh physical discipline, judgment of others' physical habits.
facets.emotional: Spiritual pride and arrogance, judgment of others' lack of qualities, emotional coldness, difficulty with ordinary human emotions, spiritual superiority.
facets.cognitive: Mental rigidity about virtue, judgment and criticism of others, spiritual intellectualism, inability to accept human flaws in self or others.
facets.psychic: The soul performs virtue for show, the divine qualities becoming a costume for the ego.

excess.text:
While divine qualities are generally beneficial, excessive manifestation can lead to spiritual pride, self-righteousness, judging others for not having these qualities, and disconnection.

Somatic: Over-discipline and rigidity, perfectionism in physical habits, harsh physical discipline, judgment of others' physical habits.

Emotional: Spiritual pride and arrogance, judgment of others' lack of qualities, emotional coldness, difficulty with ordinary human emotions, spiritual superiority.

Cognitive: Mental rigidity about virtue, judgment and criticism of others, spiritual intellectualism, inability to accept human flaws in self or others.

Psychic: The soul performs virtue for show, the divine qualities becoming a costume for the ego.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Absence of divine qualities results in the presence of demoniac qualities: fear, impurity, selfishness, deception, lack of self-control, anger, and cruelty.
facets.somatic: Poor health and appearance, cowardly or aggressive posture, selfish and harming physical actions, lack of discipline.
facets.emotional: Fear and cowardice, impurity and corruption, selfishness and greed, anger and malice, lack of compassion, emotional degradation.
facets.cognitive: Fearful and negative thinking, impure and corrupted mind, selfish and cruel thoughts, deceptive thinking, undisciplined mind, spiritual blindness.
facets.psychic: The soul neglects these qualities entirely, and its knowledge stays barren of character.

deficient.text:
Absence of divine qualities results in the presence of demoniac qualities: fear, impurity, selfishness, deception, lack of self-control, anger, and cruelty.

Somatic: Poor health and appearance, cowardly or aggressive posture, selfish and harming physical actions, lack of discipline.

Emotional: Fear and cowardice, impurity and corruption, selfishness and greed, anger and malice, lack of compassion, emotional degradation.

Cognitive: Fearful and negative thinking, impure and corrupted mind, selfish and cruel thoughts, deceptive thinking, undisciplined mind, spiritual blindness.

Psychic: The soul neglects these qualities entirely, and its knowledge stays barren of character.

attributes.title: Yajna (Sacrifice/Reciprocity)
attributes.aspect: यज्ञ   ·   Chapter 3, Chapter 4 The nature of this aspect. Yajna is the law of cosmic interdependency, stating that human survival and well-being rely on continuous, selfless contributions back to nature and society. It is the principle of reciprocity and sacred exchange.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Psychic · Ontic mode: Practice / principle — sacrifice, reciprocity (yajña) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Interpersonal / cosmic · Causal status: Mechanism (the giving that sustains the world) · Key relation: Sacrifice as the law of reciprocity; kin to Karma Yoga (Dim 10) and Purusha Vidya (Dim 25).
coordinate.causal_status: Mechanism (the giving that sustains the world)
coordinate.key_relation: Sacrifice as the law of reciprocity; kin to Karma Yoga (Dim 10) and Purusha Vidya (Dim 25)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When Yajna is practiced appropriately, a person contributes selflessly to the world while allowing themselves to receive. There is balanced giving and taking, gratitude and generosity.
facets.somatic: Physical health from balanced engagement with world, good energy circulation, physical vitality from meaningful contribution, healthy physical sustainability.
facets.emotional: Emotional satisfaction from giving, emotional peace from reciprocal exchange, emotional connection to community and world, emotional fulfillment from contribution.
facets.cognitive: Mental clarity about interdependency, wisdom about responsibility to world, mental peace from right action, mental connection to larger systems.
facets.psychic: The soul lives in the spirit of sacrifice, giving and receiving in the great reciprocity that sustains the world.

balanced.text:
When Yajna is practiced appropriately, a person contributes selflessly to the world while allowing themselves to receive. There is balanced giving and taking, gratitude and generosity.

Somatic: Physical health from balanced engagement with world, good energy circulation, physical vitality from meaningful contribution, healthy physical sustainability.

Emotional: Emotional satisfaction from giving, emotional peace from reciprocal exchange, emotional connection to community and world, emotional fulfillment from contribution.

Cognitive: Mental clarity about interdependency, wisdom about responsibility to world, mental peace from right action, mental connection to larger systems.

Psychic: The soul lives in the spirit of sacrifice, giving and receiving in the great reciprocity that sustains the world.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Excessive Yajna or self-sacrifice can lead to burnout, resentment, enabling others' dependency, and loss of personal well-being.
facets.somatic: Physical exhaustion and burnout from giving, poor self-care, depletion and fatigue, vulnerability to illness.
facets.emotional: Emotional exhaustion and resentment, emotional self-sacrifice without healthy boundaries, emotional emptiness beneath giving, emotional depletion.
facets.cognitive: Mental exhaustion, mental resentment about obligations, loss of personal identity in contribution, mental depletion.
facets.psychic: The soul turns sacrifice into transactional bargaining, offering only in order to get.

excess.text:
Excessive Yajna or self-sacrifice can lead to burnout, resentment, enabling others' dependency, and loss of personal well-being.

Somatic: Physical exhaustion and burnout from giving, poor self-care, depletion and fatigue, vulnerability to illness.

Emotional: Emotional exhaustion and resentment, emotional self-sacrifice without healthy boundaries, emotional emptiness beneath giving, emotional depletion.

Cognitive: Mental exhaustion, mental resentment about obligations, loss of personal identity in contribution, mental depletion.

Psychic: The soul turns sacrifice into transactional bargaining, offering only in order to get.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Lack of Yajna results in taking without giving, living off others' contributions without reciprocal engagement, and karmic debt accumulation.
facets.somatic: Poor long-term physical sustainability, health problems from karmic consequences, physical dependence on others, eventual physical decline.
facets.emotional: Emotional emptiness from lack of meaningful contribution, emotional dependence, emotional guilt from not contributing, emotional isolation.
facets.cognitive: Mental awareness of debt and obligation, mental guilt and shame, mental isolation from community, eventual mental suffering.
facets.psychic: The soul takes without giving, breaking the cycle of reciprocity and starving its own larger life.

deficient.text:
Lack of Yajna results in taking without giving, living off others' contributions without reciprocal engagement, and karmic debt accumulation.

Somatic: Poor long-term physical sustainability, health problems from karmic consequences, physical dependence on others, eventual physical decline.

Emotional: Emotional emptiness from lack of meaningful contribution, emotional dependence, emotional guilt from not contributing, emotional isolation.

Cognitive: Mental awareness of debt and obligation, mental guilt and shame, mental isolation from community, eventual mental suffering.

Psychic: The soul takes without giving, breaking the cycle of reciprocity and starving its own larger life.

attributes.title: Shraddha (Faith/Core Conviction)
attributes.aspect: श्रद्धा   ·   Chapter 3, Chapter 17 The nature of this aspect. Shraddha is the fundamental psychological orientation of a human being. The Gita states that a person is entirely shaped by the nature of their deep-seated beliefs and convictions. It determines thoughts, emotions, and actions.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Psychic · Ontic mode: Virtue — faith, core conviction (śraddhā) · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Condition (the conviction that sustains knowing) · Key relation: Same as Śraddhā the treasure (Dim 23); antidote to Saṁśaya (Dim 21).
coordinate.key_relation: Same as Śraddhā the treasure (Dim 23); antidote to Saṁśaya (Dim 21)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: With balanced Shraddha, a person has sincere, tested faith that is neither blind nor overly skeptical. Beliefs are examined, questioned, and deeply held. This faith shapes positive action.
facets.somatic: Physical health from belief in possibility, good energy and vitality, physical resilience from faith, strength to overcome challenges.
facets.emotional: Emotional confidence and peace from deep conviction, emotional resilience through difficulties, emotional strength from faith, emotional security.
facets.cognitive: Mental clarity from solid beliefs, focus and determination from conviction, mental resilience, ability to persist through challenges.
facets.psychic: The soul holds a functional faith that lets it act before proof, the conviction that carries it across what it cannot yet see.

balanced.text:
With balanced Shraddha, a person has sincere, tested faith that is neither blind nor overly skeptical. Beliefs are examined, questioned, and deeply held. This faith shapes positive action.

Somatic: Physical health from belief in possibility, good energy and vitality, physical resilience from faith, strength to overcome challenges.

Emotional: Emotional confidence and peace from deep conviction, emotional resilience through difficulties, emotional strength from faith, emotional security.

Cognitive: Mental clarity from solid beliefs, focus and determination from conviction, mental resilience, ability to persist through challenges.

Psychic: The soul holds a functional faith that lets it act before proof, the conviction that carries it across what it cannot yet see.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Excessive or blind Shraddha leads to dogmatism, inability to question or adapt, vulnerability to manipulation, and resistance to new understanding.
facets.somatic: Physical rigidity from dogmatic beliefs, poor adaptation to necessary changes, physical vulnerability from false beliefs.
facets.emotional: Emotional rigidity and defensiveness about beliefs, emotional vulnerability to manipulation, inability to question beliefs emotionally.
facets.cognitive: Mental dogmatism and rigidity, refusal to question or examine, vulnerability to false teachings, mental inability to evolve.
facets.psychic: The soul's faith hardens into credulity, swallowing whatever it is told without discernment.

excess.text:
Excessive or blind Shraddha leads to dogmatism, inability to question or adapt, vulnerability to manipulation, and resistance to new understanding.

Somatic: Physical rigidity from dogmatic beliefs, poor adaptation to necessary changes, physical vulnerability from false beliefs.

Emotional: Emotional rigidity and defensiveness about beliefs, emotional vulnerability to manipulation, inability to question beliefs emotionally.

Cognitive: Mental dogmatism and rigidity, refusal to question or examine, vulnerability to false teachings, mental inability to evolve.

Psychic: The soul's faith hardens into credulity, swallowing whatever it is told without discernment.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Lack of Shraddha results in chronic doubt, inability to commit to anything, constant questioning without foundation, and weakness in facing challenges.
facets.somatic: Poor physical health from lack of conviction, weakness in face of challenges, inability to sustain physical practices, lack of resilience.
facets.emotional: Emotional instability and doubt, emotional weakness, inability to feel secure or grounded, emotional vulnerability.
facets.cognitive: Mental doubt and uncertainty, inability to commit to direction, constant questioning without foundation, mental instability, mental weakness.
facets.psychic: The soul's corrosive doubt undermines every step, unable to commit to a path long enough for it to work.

deficient.text:
Lack of Shraddha results in chronic doubt, inability to commit to anything, constant questioning without foundation, and weakness in facing challenges.

Somatic: Poor physical health from lack of conviction, weakness in face of challenges, inability to sustain physical practices, lack of resilience.

Emotional: Emotional instability and doubt, emotional weakness, inability to feel secure or grounded, emotional vulnerability.

Cognitive: Mental doubt and uncertainty, inability to commit to direction, constant questioning without foundation, mental instability, mental weakness.

Psychic: The soul's corrosive doubt undermines every step, unable to commit to a path long enough for it to work.

attributes.title: Maya (Illusion/Misperception)
attributes.aspect: माया   ·   Chapter 7, Chapter 15 The nature of this aspect. Maya is the cognitive overlay that distorts human perception, making the temporary, changing world appear permanent and ultimate. It is the fundamental illusion that clouds reality perception.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Mental · Ontic mode: Framework / principle — illusion, misperception (māyā) · Valence: Affliction-as-veil (to see through) · Guṇa: Tamas–Rajas · Scale: Cosmic (Ādhidaivika), lived individually · Causal status: Mechanism of appearance (the power by which the one appears as many) · Key relation: The veiling-projecting power; kin to the guṇas-as-veils (Dim 2) and Avidyā (Dim 19).
coordinate.causal_status: Mechanism of appearance (the power by which the one appears as many)
coordinate.key_relation: The veiling-projecting power; kin to the guṇas-as-veils (Dim 2) and Avidyā (Dim 19)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When Maya's nature is understood, a person sees through illusions without being deceived. There is clear perception of what is temporary vs. eternal, and wisdom guides action.
facets.somatic: Clear perception of physical reality, freedom from physical delusions, wise physical action based on accurate perception, good judgment about material world.
facets.emotional: Emotional clarity and freedom from delusions, emotional wisdom about temporary nature of feelings, emotional non-attachment from understanding impermanence.
facets.cognitive: Mental clarity and freedom from illusions, ability to perceive reality accurately, wisdom about nature of existence, mental discrimination between real and illusory.
facets.psychic: The soul recognizes māyā as the creative-veiling power, neither denying the world nor being fully deceived by it.

balanced.text:
When Maya's nature is understood, a person sees through illusions without being deceived. There is clear perception of what is temporary vs. eternal, and wisdom guides action.

Somatic: Clear perception of physical reality, freedom from physical delusions, wise physical action based on accurate perception, good judgment about material world.

Emotional: Emotional clarity and freedom from delusions, emotional wisdom about temporary nature of feelings, emotional non-attachment from understanding impermanence.

Cognitive: Mental clarity and freedom from illusions, ability to perceive reality accurately, wisdom about nature of existence, mental discrimination between real and illusory.

Psychic: The soul recognizes māyā as the creative-veiling power, neither denying the world nor being fully deceived by it.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Over-focus on illusion and unreality can lead to nihilism, cynicism, and loss of meaning. Reality becomes meaningless.
facets.somatic: Physical apathy from sense of futility, poor physical engagement, lack of motivation to care for body or world.
facets.emotional: Emotional cynicism and meaninglessness, emotional emptiness, inability to feel significance of anything.
facets.cognitive: Mental nihilism, sense that nothing is real or meaningful, inability to commit to anything, mental despair.
facets.psychic: The soul over-uses 'it is all māyā' to dismiss real life and avoid responsibility.

excess.text:
Over-focus on illusion and unreality can lead to nihilism, cynicism, and loss of meaning. Reality becomes meaningless.

Somatic: Physical apathy from sense of futility, poor physical engagement, lack of motivation to care for body or world.

Emotional: Emotional cynicism and meaninglessness, emotional emptiness, inability to feel significance of anything.

Cognitive: Mental nihilism, sense that nothing is real or meaningful, inability to commit to anything, mental despair.

Psychic: The soul over-uses 'it is all māyā' to dismiss real life and avoid responsibility.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Inability to see through illusions results in complete delusion, mistaking the temporary for the permanent, and attaching to what is false.
facets.somatic: Complete physical delusion about reality, poor judgment and vulnerability to deception, physical attachment to illusions.
facets.emotional: Emotional delusion and attachment, emotional suffering from mistaking illusions for reality, emotional bondage to false appearances.
facets.cognitive: Complete mental delusion, inability to see through appearances, mental attachment to illusions, mental bondage and suffering.
facets.psychic: The soul is wholly taken in by appearances, with no suspicion that the obvious might be a veiling.

deficient.text:
Inability to see through illusions results in complete delusion, mistaking the temporary for the permanent, and attaching to what is false.

Somatic: Complete physical delusion about reality, poor judgment and vulnerability to deception, physical attachment to illusions.

Emotional: Emotional delusion and attachment, emotional suffering from mistaking illusions for reality, emotional bondage to false appearances.

Cognitive: Complete mental delusion, inability to see through appearances, mental attachment to illusions, mental bondage and suffering.

Psychic: The soul is wholly taken in by appearances, with no suspicion that the obvious might be a veiling.

attributes.title: Niscreyasa (The Ultimate Good)
attributes.aspect: निश्रेयस   ·   Chapter 1, Chapter 12 The nature of this aspect. Niscreyasa is the pursuit of long-term spiritual well-being and liberation, contrasted sharply with Preyas (immediate, short-term sensory pleasure). It represents the choice of the wise.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: State / goal — the ultimate good (niḥśreyasa) · Valence: The goal · Guṇa: Beyond the guṇas · Scale: Transcendent · Causal status: Fruit (the highest good, liberation) · Key relation: The summum bonum; the fruit of right knowing.
coordinate.key_relation: The summum bonum; the fruit of right knowing

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When pursuing Niscreyasa, a person makes wise long-term choices even when requiring short-term sacrifice. This leads to lasting peace, liberation, and profound fulfillment.
facets.somatic: Physical health from wise choices, body serves spiritual purpose, good vitality from aligned actions, physical sustainability.
facets.emotional: Emotional peace from alignment with higher purpose, emotional fulfillment that lasts, emotional strength from values, emotional freedom from addiction to quick fixes.
facets.cognitive: Mental clarity about true well-being, mental strength to choose wisely, mental peace from alignment, mental wisdom and discernment.
facets.psychic: The soul orients its whole life toward the highest good, the lasting freedom beyond all transient gains.

balanced.text:
When pursuing Niscreyasa, a person makes wise long-term choices even when requiring short-term sacrifice. This leads to lasting peace, liberation, and profound fulfillment.

Somatic: Physical health from wise choices, body serves spiritual purpose, good vitality from aligned actions, physical sustainability.

Emotional: Emotional peace from alignment with higher purpose, emotional fulfillment that lasts, emotional strength from values, emotional freedom from addiction to quick fixes.

Cognitive: Mental clarity about true well-being, mental strength to choose wisely, mental peace from alignment, mental wisdom and discernment.

Psychic: The soul orients its whole life toward the highest good, the lasting freedom beyond all transient gains.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Over-pursuit of the ultimate good without compassion can lead to harshness, rejection of legitimate pleasures, and spiritual pride.
facets.somatic: Over-discipline and physical rigidity, rejection of healthy pleasures, harsh physical practice.
facets.emotional: Emotional judgment of those seeking pleasure, emotional coldness, difficulty with human warmth.
facets.cognitive: Mental rigidity about what is good, spiritual pride, inability to accept that pleasure has legitimate role.
facets.psychic: The soul becomes grimly single-minded, sacrificing all legitimate human goods in a forced pursuit of the ultimate.

excess.text:
Over-pursuit of the ultimate good without compassion can lead to harshness, rejection of legitimate pleasures, and spiritual pride.

Somatic: Over-discipline and physical rigidity, rejection of healthy pleasures, harsh physical practice.

Emotional: Emotional judgment of those seeking pleasure, emotional coldness, difficulty with human warmth.

Cognitive: Mental rigidity about what is good, spiritual pride, inability to accept that pleasure has legitimate role.

Psychic: The soul becomes grimly single-minded, sacrificing all legitimate human goods in a forced pursuit of the ultimate.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Choosing only immediate Preyas over long-term Niscreyasa leads to short-term satisfaction followed by long-term suffering, addiction, and spiritual emptiness.
facets.somatic: Poor long-term physical health, addiction and indulgence, physical deterioration, lack of lasting vitality.
facets.emotional: Emotional emptiness beneath momentary pleasures, emotional addiction and dependency, emotional suffering and dissatisfaction, emotional instability.
facets.cognitive: Mental focus on immediate gratification, inability to see long-term consequences, mental restlessness and dissatisfaction, mental slavery to impulses.
facets.psychic: The soul never lifts its eyes to any ultimate good, settling for one passing aim after another.

deficient.text:
Choosing only immediate Preyas over long-term Niscreyasa leads to short-term satisfaction followed by long-term suffering, addiction, and spiritual emptiness.

Somatic: Poor long-term physical health, addiction and indulgence, physical deterioration, lack of lasting vitality.

Emotional: Emotional emptiness beneath momentary pleasures, emotional addiction and dependency, emotional suffering and dissatisfaction, emotional instability.

Cognitive: Mental focus on immediate gratification, inability to see long-term consequences, mental restlessness and dissatisfaction, mental slavery to impulses.

Psychic: The soul never lifts its eyes to any ultimate good, settling for one passing aim after another.

attributes.title: Svadharma (Individual Nature-Driven Duty)
attributes.aspect: स्वधर्म   ·   Chapter 3, Chapter 18 The nature of this aspect. Svadharma is the realization that one's true obligations must align with one's inherent psychological makeup (Svabhava) rather than artificial social expectations. It is duty aligned with natural inclination and capacity.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Faculty / calling — one’s own duty (svadharma) · Valence: Virtue to align with · Guṇa: Sattva when aligned · Scale: Individual, engaging the world · Causal status: Means / calling · Key relation: Same as Svadharma (Dim 16); nature-driven duty.
coordinate.key_relation: Same as Svadharma (Dim 16); nature-driven duty

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When living according to Svadharma, a person's actions align with their nature, gifts, and values. There is authenticity, fulfillment, and natural excellence.
facets.somatic: Physical health from work aligned with capacity, good energy from meaningful engagement, natural physical excellence from aligned action.
facets.emotional: Emotional fulfillment from authentic work, emotional peace from alignment with values, emotional strength from living authentically, emotional joy in expression.
facets.cognitive: Mental clarity about natural gifts, mental focus and excellence, mental peace from authenticity, mental fulfillment from aligned action.
facets.psychic: The soul follows its own nature-given duty, finding freedom in the work that fits its constitution.

balanced.text:
When living according to Svadharma, a person's actions align with their nature, gifts, and values. There is authenticity, fulfillment, and natural excellence.

Somatic: Physical health from work aligned with capacity, good energy from meaningful engagement, natural physical excellence from aligned action.

Emotional: Emotional fulfillment from authentic work, emotional peace from alignment with values, emotional strength from living authentically, emotional joy in expression.

Cognitive: Mental clarity about natural gifts, mental focus and excellence, mental peace from authenticity, mental fulfillment from aligned action.

Psychic: The soul follows its own nature-given duty, finding freedom in the work that fits its constitution.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Over-emphasis on personal nature without considering social responsibility can lead to selfishness, narcissism, and harm to others.
facets.somatic: Physical indulgence in personal pursuits without care for others, potentially harmful physical actions.
facets.emotional: Emotional selfishness and narcissism, lack of emotional consideration for others, emotional entitlement.
facets.cognitive: Mental selfishness and lack of social consideration, mental entitlement, inability to consider others' needs.
facets.psychic: The soul rigidly absolutizes its role, mistaking a fixed identity for its living dharma.

excess.text:
Over-emphasis on personal nature without considering social responsibility can lead to selfishness, narcissism, and harm to others.

Somatic: Physical indulgence in personal pursuits without care for others, potentially harmful physical actions.

Emotional: Emotional selfishness and narcissism, lack of emotional consideration for others, emotional entitlement.

Cognitive: Mental selfishness and lack of social consideration, mental entitlement, inability to consider others' needs.

Psychic: The soul rigidly absolutizes its role, mistaking a fixed identity for its living dharma.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Rejection of Svadharma to follow others' expectations leads to inauthenticity, lack of fulfillment, poor performance, and internal conflict.
facets.somatic: Poor physical health from work misaligned with nature, lack of physical vitality, physical mediocrity despite effort.
facets.emotional: Emotional emptiness from inauthenticity, emotional resentment about imposed duties, emotional frustration despite effort.
facets.cognitive: Mental confusion about true purpose, mental frustration and poor performance, mental resentment about imposed paths, mental unfulfillment.
facets.psychic: The soul abandons its own path to imitate another's, and lives in the chronic friction of a borrowed life.

Emotional: Emotional emptiness from inauthenticity, emotional resentment about imposed duties, emotional frustration despite effort.

Cognitive: Mental confusion about true purpose, mental frustration and poor performance, mental resentment about imposed paths, mental unfulfillment.

attributes.title: Bhakti-Jnana-Vairagya Cycle
attributes.aspect: ·   Canto 1, Chapter 2 The nature of this aspect. The Bhagavatam's distinctive cycle: Bhakti (devotion) opens the heart; experienced ananda (joy) is the result; from ananda arises Jnana (awareness/insight); from jnana arises Vairagya (dispassion). In daily life: cultivating any one strengthens the others. Devotional listening produces joy, which produces clarity, which produces detachment. Not a linear path but a self-reinforcing cycle.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework / process — a mutually-reinforcing cycle · Valence: Virtue-cycle to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Mechanism (each deepens the others) · Key relation: The reinforcing loop of Bhakti and Jñāna (Dim 10) and Vairāgya (Dim 23).
coordinate.key_relation: The reinforcing loop of Bhakti and Jñāna (Dim 10) and Vairāgya (Dim 23)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Daily cultivation of any one (usually bhakti) producing all three. The cycle becomes self-sustaining.
facets.somatic: Body engaged in any of three; the others follow.
facets.emotional: Emotional life cycles through devotion-joy-clarity-detachment.
facets.cognitive: Mind in continuous cycle of devotional development.
facets.psychic: The soul lets devotion, knowledge, and dispassion feed one another in a rising spiral, each strengthening the next.

balanced.text:
Daily cultivation of any one (usually bhakti) producing all three. The cycle becomes self-sustaining.

Somatic: Body engaged in any of three; the others follow.

Emotional: Emotional life cycles through devotion-joy-clarity-detachment.

Cognitive: Mind in continuous cycle of devotional development.

Psychic: The soul lets devotion, knowledge, and dispassion feed one another in a rising spiral, each strengthening the next.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Mechanical pursuit of all three as separate practices. Missing the organic interconnection.
facets.somatic: Body in multiple disjointed practices.
facets.emotional: Emotional life fragmented across practices.
facets.cognitive: Mental compartmentalization.
facets.psychic: The soul over-emphasizes one strand — all feeling, or all analysis, or all renunciation — and the balance is lost.

excess.text:

Emotional: Emotional life fragmented across practices.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: None of three cultivated. Daily life without bhakti, without joy, without clarity, without detachment.
facets.somatic: Body without spiritual exercise.
facets.emotional: Emotional life without sacred dimension.
facets.cognitive: Mind without development in any of three.
facets.psychic: The soul cultivates none of the three, and its inner life has neither warmth, light, nor freedom.

None of three cultivated. Daily life without bhakti, without joy, without clarity, without detachment.

attributes.title: Four Types of Dhyana (Mahavira's Classification)
attributes.aspect: चार ध्यान   ·   Chapter 22 The nature of this aspect. Mahavira's revolutionary framework: meditation is not a special activity—everyone meditates. The angry person meditates on anger; the suffering on suffering; the religious on dharma; the highest on pure consciousness. The quality of life depends on the object of meditation.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework — a fourfold classification of meditation · Valence: Neutral (a taxonomy) · Guṇa: Spans (from tamasic to sattvic dhyāna) · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Framework · Key relation: Mahāvīra’s taxonomy of meditative states, from harmful to liberating.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Framework — a fourfold classification of meditation
coordinate.key_relation: Mahāvīra’s taxonomy of meditative states, from harmful to liberating

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Awareness that one is always meditating allows conscious choice. Dharmadhyan and Shukladhyan—dwelling on truth and pure consciousness—transform life.
facets.somatic: Body shaped by dharmic dwelling—health, vitality, peace.
facets.emotional: Emotions colored by chosen meditation—peace, joy, compassion.
facets.cognitive: Mind elevated by sattvic meditation, ultimately moving toward formless awareness.
facets.psychic: The soul discerns wholesome meditation from unwholesome, cultivating the elevating forms and abandoning the brooding ones.

balanced.text:
Awareness that one is always meditating allows conscious choice. Dharmadhyan and Shukladhyan—dwelling on truth and pure consciousness—transform life.

Somatic: Body shaped by dharmic dwelling—health, vitality, peace.

Emotional: Emotions colored by chosen meditation—peace, joy, compassion.

Cognitive: Mind elevated by sattvic meditation, ultimately moving toward formless awareness.

Psychic: The soul discerns wholesome meditation from unwholesome, cultivating the elevating forms and abandoning the brooding ones.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Pride in higher meditation while still unconsciously engaging in raudradhyan or artadhyan—claiming spirituality while being secretly angry or self-pitying.
facets.somatic: Body shows the lower meditations even when one claims higher practice.
facets.emotional: Hidden anger or suffering beneath claimed spirituality.
facets.cognitive: Self-deception about what one is actually meditating on.
facets.psychic: The soul over-classifies meditative states, analyzing types rather than meditating.

excess.text:

Cognitive: Self-deception about what one is actually meditating on.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Unconscious life—not realizing that anger, lust, jealousy are meditations producing their effects. Helpless to be otherwise.
facets.somatic: Body damaged by unconscious negative meditations—illness, stress-related disease.
facets.emotional: Caught in raudradhyan or artadhyan without awareness, repeating patterns.
facets.cognitive: Unconscious mental dwelling shaping life negatively, no awareness of the mechanism.
facets.psychic: The soul's mind broods in the dark forms of meditation — sorrowful and cruel rumination — mistaking them for thought.

attributes.title: Universal Destiny (Beyond Individual Ego)
attributes.aspect: विश्वनियति   ·   Chapter 25 The nature of this aspect. Individual destiny is the ego's illusion. We are part of the universal continuity—every atom, every event in cosmic history has contributed to our existence. When this is realized, individual concerns dissolve into participation in cosmic destiny.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework — a vision / pointer · Valence: Neutral — a pointer to be realized · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Individual, toward the Absolute · Causal status: Framework / realization · Key relation: the movement of all toward the one goal, beyond the individual ego.
coordinate.valence: Neutral — a pointer to be realized
coordinate.key_relation: the movement of all toward the one goal, beyond the individual ego

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When universal destiny is realized, one no longer suffers as a separate individual. The body dies as a part; the universe continues. There is freedom beyond birth and death.
facets.somatic: Body relaxed in trust of cosmic flow, no individual desperate clinging.
facets.emotional: Emotional freedom from personal destiny anxiety, deep peace.
facets.cognitive: Mind aware of universal interconnection, freedom from ego-bound concerns.
facets.psychic: The soul senses its life as part of a universal movement, and its small concerns relax within that larger story.

balanced.text:
When universal destiny is realized, one no longer suffers as a separate individual. The body dies as a part; the universe continues. There is freedom beyond birth and death.

Somatic: Body relaxed in trust of cosmic flow, no individual desperate clinging.

Emotional: Emotional freedom from personal destiny anxiety, deep peace.

Cognitive: Mind aware of universal interconnection, freedom from ego-bound concerns.

Psychic: The soul senses its life as part of a universal movement, and its small concerns relax within that larger story.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Premature identification with universe as escape from personal responsibility—'I am the universe so nothing matters.' Spiritual bypassing of individual ethics.
facets.somatic: Neglect of body using cosmic excuse, lack of practical care.
facets.emotional: Emotional disconnection from human relationships, false transcendence.
facets.cognitive: Bypassing personal work claiming cosmic identity.
facets.psychic: The soul inflates, identifying its ego with cosmic destiny and grandiose mission.

excess.text:

facets.somatic: Body experiencing isolation, no sense of cosmic support, chronic stress.
facets.emotional: Anxiety about individual destiny, fear of death, isolation suffering.
facets.cognitive: Mind trapped in 'I am separate' delusion, all the suffering of individuality.
facets.psychic: The soul sees only its private fate, cut off from any larger meaning, and so feels small and forsaken.

attributes.title: Complementariness of Sin and Virtue
attributes.aspect: पुण्य-पाप अद्वैत   ·   Chapter 33 The nature of this aspect. The Hindu view sees good and evil not as warring opposites but as complementary balance. The lotus needs dirty mud to flower. Sin gives meaning to virtue; they balance the cosmic process. God is the neutral source from which both arise.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework — an epistemic distinction · Valence: Neutral — a discernment to make · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual (ādhyātmika) · Causal status: Framework (clarifies how one knows) · Key relation: sin and virtue as complementary within the larger movement, transcended in liberation.
coordinate.key_relation: sin and virtue as complementary within the larger movement, transcended in liberation

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Seeing sin and virtue as complementary creates inner peace—no civil war between halves of self. The whole is accepted, transcending good/evil dichotomy.
facets.somatic: Body without internal conflict, peace replacing internal Crucifixion-pattern.
facets.emotional: Emotional acceptance of all aspects of self, no rejected shadow.
facets.cognitive: Mind transcending moral dualism, peace beyond good and evil.
facets.psychic: The soul holds good and evil as a complementary pair within the larger whole, neither naive about wrong nor self-righteous.

balanced.text:

Psychic: The soul holds good and evil as a complementary pair within the larger whole, neither naive about wrong nor self-righteous.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Misunderstanding leading to amoralism—'since good and evil are one, anything goes.' Tantric license without tantric discipline.
facets.somatic: Body abused as 'beyond morality,' destructive behavior.
facets.emotional: Emotional license, no compass.
facets.cognitive: False non-dualism justifying anything.
facets.psychic: The soul over-relativizes morality, using 'beyond good and evil' to excuse genuine harm.

facets.emotional: Constant guilt, shadow-suppression, sad religious life.
facets.cognitive: Mental civil war, fragmentation, depression from rejecting half of life.
facets.psychic: The soul splits the world rigidly into pure good and pure evil, unable to see the whole that holds both.

attributes.title: Nama (Name as First Reality)
attributes.aspect: ·   Chapter 7, Section 1 The nature of this aspect. Name (Nama) is the most basic reality we engage with—all knowledge is fundamentally name-knowledge. The Vedas, sciences, arts—all are systems of names. Recognizing this is the first teaching: meditate on Name to understand its scope, and discover that beyond it lies something greater.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework / principle — name as the first reality (nāma) · Valence: Neutral · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Cosmic (Ādhidaivika) · Causal status: Cause (name and form as the first differentiation) · Key relation: Nāma as the primal reality; kin to Vāc (Dim 27) and nāma-rūpa.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Framework / principle — name as the first reality (nāma)
coordinate.causal_status: Cause (name and form as the first differentiation)
coordinate.key_relation: Nāma as the primal reality; kin to Vāc (Dim 27) and nāma-rūpa

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Awareness of how thoroughly we live in named-reality. Recognition that names point to but don't capture reality.
facets.somatic: Body's experience recognized as filtered through naming.
facets.emotional: Emotional reactions seen as name-based.
facets.cognitive: Mind aware of its name-constructed world.
facets.psychic: The soul senses the creative power of name — how naming carves a world out of the undifferentiated — and uses words with care.

facets.emotional: Emotional life through pre-named filters.
facets.cognitive: Mind fixated on names as final reality.
facets.psychic: The soul mistakes the name for the named, lost in labels and definitions.

attributes.title: Allegorical Mahabharata (Inner Characters)
attributes.aspect: ·   Lahiri's character interpretations The nature of this aspect. Lahiri Mahasay's profound allegorical interpretation: every character of the Mahabharata represents an inner psychological force in the Kriyavan. Duryodhana represents the mind that refuses to do Kriya. Krishna represents Kutastha consciousness. The Pandavas are the pure tendencies; the Kauravas the impure. The battlefield Kurukshetra is the body. The Gita is Krishna (Kutastha) instructing Arjuna (soul) during the inner war.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework — the epic read as inner characters · Valence: Neutral (an interpretive key) · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Framework · Key relation: The Mahābhārata’s figures as the inner faculties and forces.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Framework — the epic read as inner characters
coordinate.key_relation: The Mahābhārata’s figures as the inner faculties and forces

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Recognition of all characters as inner psychological forces. Scripture read as instruction for inner work.
facets.somatic: Body recognized as Kurukshetra—the field of inner battle.
facets.emotional: Emotional patterns identified as Kaurava-tendencies to be transformed.
facets.cognitive: Mind sees scripture as map of inner psychology.
facets.psychic: The soul reads the great epic inwardly, recognizing its warring characters as forces within its own psyche.

balanced.text:

Psychic: The soul reads the great epic inwardly, recognizing its warring characters as forces within its own psyche.

facets.somatic: Body neglected in over-allegorization.
facets.emotional: Emotional richness of stories dismissed.
facets.cognitive: Mind locked in single interpretation.
facets.psychic: The soul over-allegorizes, reducing every story to a scheme and missing its living force.

attributes.title: Birth and Death as Correlatives (Sasyam Iva)
attributes.aspect: सस्यम् इव   ·   1.1.6 The nature of this aspect. Like corn growing and falling, growing again from fallen seed—human life follows the same pattern. Birth contains death; death contains rebirth. They are not opposites but two phases of one organic process. Understanding this dissolves the terror of death.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Physical · Ontic mode: Framework — a vision / pointer · Valence: Neutral — a pointer to be realized · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Individual, toward the Absolute · Causal status: Framework / realization · Key relation: birth and death as correlative like the grass (sasyam iva) — the cycle of coming and going.
coordinate.valence: Neutral — a pointer to be realized
coordinate.key_relation: birth and death as correlative like the grass (sasyam iva) — the cycle of coming and going

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Recognition of birth-death as one organic process. Neither attached to birth-celebration nor terrified by death-fear.
facets.somatic: Body lived as natural seed-cycle, no terror of decay.
facets.emotional: Emotional acceptance of both birth and death as natural correlatives.
facets.cognitive: Mind sees the organic unity of life and death.
facets.psychic: The soul accepts birth and death as a single correlated rhythm — like crops that ripen, fall, and rise again — and meets mortality without terror.

balanced.text:
Recognition of birth-death as one organic process. Neither attached to birth-celebration nor terrified by death-fear.

Cognitive: Mind sees the organic unity of life and death.

Psychic: The soul accepts birth and death as a single correlated rhythm — like crops that ripen, fall, and rise again — and meets mortality without terror.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Fatalistic acceptance—using the cycle as excuse for not living fully. 'Why bother, it all dies anyway.'
facets.somatic: Body neglected as 'just part of cycle.'
facets.emotional: Emotional disengagement justified by cycle-awareness.
facets.cognitive: Nihilistic interpretation of the cycle teaching.
facets.psychic: The soul becomes morbidly preoccupied with the cycle, losing the present to brooding on its turns.

attributes.title: Three Desires (Wealth, Worlds, Sons)
attributes.aspect: त्रि-एषणा   ·   3.5.1, 4.4.22 The nature of this aspect. Knowing the Self, sages give up the three desires: for offspring, for wealth, for worlds (heaven). These three eshanas (cravings) bind one to samsara. When transcended, one becomes a wandering monk.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Framework — the three desires (wealth, worlds, sons) · Valence: Affliction-set (to renounce) · Guṇa: Rajas · Scale: Individual / interpersonal · Causal status: Root cravings · Key relation: The three eṣaṇās to renounce for freedom; same as the Eśanas (Dim 12).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Framework — the three desires (wealth, worlds, sons)
coordinate.key_relation: The three eṣaṇās to renounce for freedom; same as the Eśanas (Dim 12)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Wise relationship with desires for family, prosperity, status—engaged but not bound. Eventually transcended.
facets.somatic: Body engaged in life without slavery to three desires.
facets.emotional: Emotional engagement with family/wealth/world without compulsive grasping.
facets.cognitive: Mind oriented beyond the three desires.
facets.psychic: The soul recognizes the three great desires that drive worldly life, and gradually sees through them toward the freedom beyond.

Cognitive: Mind oriented beyond the three desires.

Psychic: The soul recognizes the three great desires that drive worldly life, and gradually sees through them toward the freedom beyond.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Premature renunciation of three desires without genuine transcendence—forced asceticism.
facets.somatic: Forced rejection of legitimate engagements.
facets.emotional: Suppressed desires creating distortion.
facets.cognitive: Pseudo-renunciation without realization.
facets.psychic: The soul is wholly consumed by these desires, mistaking their satisfaction for the goal of life.

facets.overview: Total bondage to three desires—life entirely about offspring, wealth, status. Maximum samsara.
facets.somatic: Body driven by desire for progeny, wealth, status.
facets.emotional: Emotional life dominated by these three pursuits.
facets.cognitive: Mind entirely captured by three desires.
facets.psychic: The soul forcibly represses these natural drives without understanding, and the denied energy curdles into frustration.

attributes.title: Death and the Two Paths (Devayana and Pitryana)
attributes.aspect: देवयान-पितृयाण   ·   6.2.15-16 The nature of this aspect. Two paths after death: Devayana (path of the gods) for those who know Brahman—they pass through flame, day, bright fortnight, six months of sun's northern course, year, sun, moon, lightning—never to return to samsara. Pitryana (path of the fathers) for those of good works—they pass through smoke, night, dark fortnight, six months of sun's southern course—and return to be reborn.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework / doctrine — the two after-death paths · Valence: Neutral (a cosmological map) · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Cosmic (Ādhidaivika) · Causal status: Framework · Key relation: Devayāna (path of the gods, no return) vs Pitṛyāna (path of the fathers, return); kin to Course After Death (Dim 25).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Framework / doctrine — the two after-death paths
coordinate.key_relation: Devayāna (path of the gods, no return) vs Pitṛyāna (path of the fathers, return); kin to Course After Death (Dim 25)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Understanding of the two paths motivates choice—pursuing knowledge for liberation (Devayana) rather than just good karma (Pitryana).
facets.somatic: Body engaged in spiritual practice for Devayana.
facets.emotional: Emotional commitment to liberation, not just heavens.
facets.cognitive: Mind oriented toward knowledge for ultimate freedom.
facets.psychic: The soul orients its living toward the path of light, so its whole life becomes a preparation for the journey that does not return.

facets.cognitive: Mental obsession with post-mortem destiny.

facets.cognitive: Mind never considers post-mortem path.
facets.psychic: The soul gives no thought to its trajectory, living as if this span were the whole and final story.

attributes.title: Encompassing the Body (Reversing Identification)
attributes.aspect: शरीर-व्यापन   ·   Chapter 36 The nature of this aspect. Most identify the self as inside the body. The reversal—seeing body as inside the encompassing self—immediately transforms experience. The body becomes light because identification has dropped. Self expands beyond physical boundaries.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Psychic · Ontic mode: Practice / corrective — reversing the superimposition · Valence: Cultivated corrective · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Mechanism (undoes identification) · Key relation: the antidote to Identification with Body — reversing the body-superimposition.
coordinate.key_relation: the antidote to Identification with Body — reversing the body-superimposition

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When the encompassing experience is realized, the body floats in vast consciousness. Identification dissolves; the body becomes a small center in unlimited awareness.
facets.somatic: Body experiences lightness, no chronic heaviness, sense of expansiveness.
facets.emotional: Emotional freedom from body-bondage, vastness of feeling.
facets.cognitive: Mind no longer cramped in body-identity, sense of cosmic dimensions.
facets.psychic: The soul reverses the usual error, holding the body within awareness rather than awareness within the body.

balanced.text:

Emotional: Emotional freedom from body-bondage, vastness of feeling.

Cognitive: Mind no longer cramped in body-identity, sense of cosmic dimensions.

Psychic: The soul reverses the usual error, holding the body within awareness rather than awareness within the body.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Disembodied dissociation pretending to be encompassing—neglect of body's signals, ungrounded existence.
facets.somatic: Body neglected, signals ignored, accidents from inattention.
facets.emotional: Emotional disconnection from embodied life.
facets.cognitive: Floating in concept of expansion without genuine realization.
facets.psychic: The soul over-abstracts, treating the body as nothing and neglecting its real needs.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Total cramped identification within body—identifying with specific center (head or belly), feeling bounded by skin. Maximum sense of limitation.
facets.somatic: Heavy bodily presence, constant body-anxiety, every sensation magnified.
facets.emotional: Emotional life confined to body-experiences, narrow.
facets.cognitive: Mind cramped in body-identity, suffocating sense of limitation.
facets.psychic: The soul is wholly enclosed in the body, taking the flesh as the boundary of itself.

attributes.title: Personality as Mask (Persona)
attributes.aspect: व्यक्तित्व-मुखौटा   ·   Chapter 43 The nature of this aspect. Personality is not what we are but the various masks we wear. The original face—the actual nature—exists before personality, beneath it. We have not one personality but many, switching constantly. The original face is faceless.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Framework — a mechanism of bondage (superimposition) · Valence: Affliction-as-veil (to see through) · Guṇa: Tamas–Rajas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Mechanism of concealment · Key relation: the persona superimposed on the Self; kin to Asmitā (Dim 19).
coordinate.key_relation: the persona superimposed on the Self; kin to Asmitā (Dim 19)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Awareness of masks while wearing them—not getting trapped in personality. Functions socially with appropriate faces while knowing the faceless nature.
facets.somatic: Body wears expressions appropriately without identification with them.
facets.emotional: Emotional masks worn consciously, freedom from being any one mask.
facets.cognitive: Recognition of the original face beneath all personalities.
facets.psychic: The soul wears its personality as a mask it can set down, knowing the face beneath is not the mask.

balanced.text:

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Total identification with one mask as 'real me'—rigid persona that resists all other expression. Or pretending to have no masks while still using them.
facets.somatic: Face frozen in one expression, body in single persona.
facets.emotional: One emotional mask becomes prison.
facets.cognitive: Identity completely tied to particular persona.
facets.psychic: The soul polishes and defends the mask obsessively, the persona hardening into a prison.

facets.somatic: Body's expressions changing without awareness.
facets.emotional: Emotional identity shifting without recognition.
facets.cognitive: Mind completely identified with whatever mask is current.
facets.psychic: The soul is fused to its mask without remainder, unable to imagine a self beneath the role.

attributes.title: World as Dream (Reality of Projection)
attributes.aspect: स्वप्न-जगत्   ·   Chapter 49 The nature of this aspect. The world appears real only because we are not present to ourselves. In dream, we are absent; therefore dream becomes real. In life, similarly absent, the world becomes solid. Self-remembering—the practice of 'I am'—dissolves both world-and-dream illusion.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework — a mechanism of bondage (superimposition) · Valence: Affliction-as-veil (to see through) · Guṇa: Tamas–Rajas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Mechanism of concealment · Key relation: the projected world taken as independently real; kin to Māyā (Dim 28) and mithyā (Dim 27).
coordinate.key_relation: the projected world taken as independently real; kin to Māyā (Dim 28) and mithyā (Dim 27)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Self-remembering active—'I am' present in all activities. The world is recognized as dream-like, not real-versus-illusion but witnessed transparency.
facets.somatic: Body present in self-remembering, vibrant aliveness.
facets.emotional: Emotional life seen as dream-movements while self remembers.
facets.cognitive: Mind always rooted in 'I am' while perceiving the world-show.
facets.psychic: The soul recognizes the experienced world as a kind of waking dream projected by mind, and so holds it more lightly.

balanced.text:

facets.psychic: The soul uses 'it's all a dream' to derealize life and dodge its claims.

attributes.title: How Senses Fool Us (Indriya-Bhrama)
attributes.aspect: ·   Chapter 25 The nature of this aspect. The senses systematically deceive—presenting partial appearances as complete realities, ephemeral attractions as enduring goods, separate entities where there is connection. Understanding this deception is the prerequisite for liberation from sense-bondage.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Mental · Ontic mode: Framework — a mechanism of bondage (superimposition) · Valence: Affliction-as-veil (to see through) · Guṇa: Tamas–Rajas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Mechanism of concealment · Key relation: the senses’ misreporting (indriya-bhrama); kin to Viparyaya (Dim 20).
coordinate.key_relation: the senses’ misreporting (indriya-bhrama); kin to Viparyaya (Dim 20)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Aware participation with senses—using them while recognizing their inherent limitations and deceptions.
facets.somatic: Body's senses used with awareness of their partial nature.
facets.emotional: Emotional life informed by sense-data but not enslaved to it.
facets.cognitive: Mind recognizes sense-deception, uses senses without complete trust.
facets.psychic: The soul knows the senses report a partial, distorted world, and trusts them without being ruled by them.

excess.label: In excess

facets.somatic: Body's senses atrophied through forced denial.
facets.emotional: Emotional life cut off from sensory richness.
facets.cognitive: Mental rejection of all sensory information.
facets.psychic: The soul grows paranoid about all perception, paralyzed by distrust of every appearance.
