attributes.title: Disentanglement of Personality (Vyutthana)
attributes.aspect: ·   Chapter 29 The nature of this aspect. What we call 'personality' is a complex tangle of identifications, roles, conditioned patterns accumulated over a lifetime (and previous lives). Liberation is the patient disentanglement of these threads—not destruction but unweaving. Each thread released frees the underlying Self.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Practice / corrective — reversing the superimposition · Valence: Cultivated corrective · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Mechanism (undoes identification) · Key relation: the un-knotting of identification (vyutthāna) — a liberating move.
coordinate.key_relation: the un-knotting of identification (vyutthāna) — a liberating move

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Patient ongoing disentanglement of false identifications. Each strand released brings more spaciousness.
facets.somatic: Body progressively disentangled from identity-postures.
facets.emotional: Emotional patterns recognized and untangled one by one.
facets.cognitive: Mental identifications systematically loosened.
facets.psychic: The soul steps back from its conditioned personality, disentangling awareness from the character it had taken itself to be.

balanced.text:

facets.emotional: Emotional breakdown from forced disentanglement.
facets.cognitive: Mental dissolution without integration.
facets.psychic: The soul forces the disentanglement coldly, dissociating from its humanity rather than freeing itself within it.

facets.psychic: The soul stays wholly tangled in its personality, every conditioned trait felt as bedrock identity.

attributes.title: Identification with Body
attributes.aspect: देहाध्यास   ·   Chapter 22, 27 The nature of this aspect. Identification with the body is the fundamental confusion. We feel embodied; really, the body is contained in consciousness. This reversal of perspective destroys the bondage of body-identification.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Vital · Ontic mode: Framework — a mechanism of bondage (superimposition) · Valence: Affliction-as-veil (to see through) · Guṇa: Tamas–Rajas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Mechanism of concealment · Key relation: the primal misidentification (dehātma-buddhi); the root superimposition; kin to Deha-vāsanā (Dim 12).
coordinate.key_relation: the primal misidentification (dehātma-buddhi); the root superimposition; kin to Deha-vāsanā (Dim 12)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When body is known as instrument within consciousness (not consciousness within body), it becomes light. One is the encompassing space; body is just a minor center.
facets.somatic: Body felt as light, weightless, instrument-like, healthy and free.
facets.emotional: Emotional freedom from body-anxieties, no constant body-concern, peace.
facets.cognitive: Mind no longer identified with body changes, witness of body, freedom.
facets.psychic: The soul holds the body as instrument and home without mistaking it for the self that uses it.

balanced.text:
When body is known as instrument within consciousness (not consciousness within body), it becomes light. One is the encompassing space; body is just a minor center.

Cognitive: Mind no longer identified with body changes, witness of body, freedom.

Psychic: The soul holds the body as instrument and home without mistaking it for the self that uses it.

facets.cognitive: Mental life dominated by body-concerns, identity tied to physical appearance and condition.
facets.psychic: The soul over-attends to the body, its whole identity invested in form, appearance, and sensation.

attributes.title: Hypnotic Projection (Maya as Auto-Hypnosis)
attributes.aspect: माया   ·   Chapter 27 The nature of this aspect. Maya is not external illusion but our own hypnotic projection. We project beauty, ugliness, value, meaning—then take our projections as reality. The world we perceive is largely our own creation, and we suffer or enjoy according to our projections.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework — a mechanism of bondage (superimposition) · Valence: Affliction-as-veil (to see through) · Guṇa: Tamas–Rajas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Mechanism of concealment · Key relation: māyā as auto-hypnosis (bahir-mukha projection); kin to Māyā (Dim 28).
coordinate.key_relation: māyā as auto-hypnosis (bahir-mukha projection); kin to Māyā (Dim 28)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When projection is recognized, reality is seen as it is, free of overlay. The world is not denied but seen without the auto-hypnotic distortion.
facets.somatic: Body responds to actual reality, not projections.
facets.emotional: Emotional life based on what is, not on hypnotic overlays.
facets.cognitive: Mind clear of projection, seeing things in their nature without imposed meaning.
facets.psychic: The soul recognizes that it has, in effect, hypnotized itself into a limited identity, and begins the counter-suggestion of truth.

balanced.text:

facets.psychic: The soul fixates on 'it's all hypnosis,' using the idea to evade rather than to wake.

attributes.title: Externalization of Consciousness (Bahir-Mukha)
attributes.aspect: ·   Chapter 1, Fourth Brahmana The nature of this aspect. Consciousness has a tendency to externalise—to gaze outward, to become object. As this externalisation deepens, consciousness becomes more dense, more 'material.' What we call matter is just consciousness extremely externalised. Liberation is internalisation—the reverse current.

attributes.title: Adhyāsa — Superimposition, the Root of Bondage
attributes.aspect: ·   Throughout commentary The nature of this aspect. Adhyāsa is the superimposition of the not-self upon the Self — the foundational confusion by which the limitless witnessing awareness comes to experience itself as a limited body-mind. Śaṅkara opens his great commentary with it, because it is the single error from which all bondage flows: like mistaking a rope for a snake in dim light, the mind lays the attributes of the perishable onto the imperishable and then lives in the fear and craving appropriate to a perishable thing. Liberation is, precisely, the un-doing of this superimposition.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Mental · Ontic mode: Framework — the root mechanism (adhyāsa) · Valence: Affliction-as-veil (the root to see through) · Guṇa: Tamas-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Root mechanism of concealment · Key relation: Whole / root — superimposition itself, ground of all the bondage mechanisms; kin to Avidyā (Dim 19).
coordinate.key_relation: Whole / root — superimposition itself, ground of all the bondage mechanisms; kin to Avidyā (Dim 19)

balanced.label: Balanced — the superimposition recognized and seen through

facets.somatic: Body recognized as not-Self though still functioning.
facets.emotional: Emotions recognized as not-Self though still felt.
facets.cognitive: Mind's contents recognized as not-Self though still operating.
facets.psychic: The soul begins to catch the basic error in the act — the superimposing of self onto not-self — and each catching loosens the knot at the root.

additional_info.heading: 4. The Structures of Bondage
additional_info.text: On the foundation of superimposition, bondage builds recognizable structures. The knot of the heart (hṛdaya granthi) is the tight identification-complex binding the Self to the body-mind, which the Upaniṣads say is ‘cut’ at the moment of realization. The tree of saṁsāra is the whole branching organism of conditioned existence, rooted above in the unmanifest and spreading below into the world of action and reaction. And the vāsanās are the latent tendencies — the grooves worn by past action that incline the mind to repeat itself, the very stuff of which the binding habits are made.

attributes.title: Vasanas as Dream-Stuff (No Need to Remove)
attributes.aspect: ·   XVIII.6-7 The nature of this aspect. Ashtavakra's radical teaching on vasanas: the entire process of removing vasanas (latent tendencies) is unnecessary. Why? Because the vasanas, like the body-mind, are themselves part of the dream. No vasanas ever existed in Reality—why beat a snake that was always just a rope? Most traditions emphasize vasana-kshaya; Ashtavakra says: skip it through direct knowledge.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Mental · Ontic mode: Framework — a structure of bondage · Valence: Affliction (the binding structure) · Guṇa: Tamas–Rajas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Structure of bondage · Key relation: the vāsanās (Dim 12) seen as insubstantial dream-stuff — to see through, not fight.

facets.psychic: The soul sees its tendencies as insubstantial dream-stuff that loses its grip the moment it is seen as such.

attributes.aspect: वासना   ·   Chapter 15 The nature of this aspect. Vasana refers to deep mental tendencies, conditioned patterns, and karmic impressions in the unconscious mind. They are the subtle drives that motivate behavior even when not consciously recognized.

attributes.title: Graha-Atigraha (Grasper and Grasped Bondage)
attributes.aspect: ·   Chapter 3, Second Brahmana The nature of this aspect. Bondage is the mutual capture between senses (grahas) and objects (atigrahas). The eye grasps form; form also grasps the eye. Mind grasps thought; thought also grasps the mind. Eight pairs of grasper-grasped: prana-apana (smell), speech-name, eye-form, ear-sound, tongue-taste, mind-desire, hand-action, skin-touch. Liberation comes from breaking this mutual capture.

attributes.title: Tree of Samsara (Anatomy of Bondage)
attributes.aspect: ·   Through Sankara's metaphors The nature of this aspect. The complete anatomy of samsara as a living tree: rooted in tamas (unconscious ignorance), sprouting into body-identification, leafing into desires, watered by action-loops, bearing the fruit of sorrow. Each stage feeds the next. Liberation requires uprooting at the seed (ignorance) level.

facets.psychic: The soul perceives the whole tree of worldly existence and reaches to cut it at the root.

attributes.title: Vasana (Latent Tendency)
attributes.aspect: ·   Multiple verses The nature of this aspect. Vasana is the latent karmic seed—unconscious tendency formed by past actions stored in chitta. Modern psychology calls it conditioning. Vasanas drive action even against clear knowing. The cognition-affect split: buddhi knows it's harmful, manas desires it anyway, vasana provides momentum. Liberation requires vasana-kshaya (withering of tendencies).

attributes.title: Hṛdaya Granthi — The Knot of the Heart
attributes.aspect: हृदय ग्रन्थि   ·   4.4.7 The nature of this aspect. The knot of the heart is the tight identification by which the Self is bound to the body-mind — the felt cinch of ‘I am this person, this body, this story.’ The Upaniṣads describe its undoing in dramatic terms: when the Self is seen, the knot of the heart is cut, all doubts are dissolved, and the bondage of action falls away. As lived experience, the knot is the constant, low-grade contraction of self-protection — the bracing of a self that feels its existence to be at stake — and its loosening is felt as a profound and growing ease, as though a fist held tight for a lifetime were finally beginning to open.

additional_info.heading: 5. The Felt Experience of Bondage
additional_info.text: Bondage is not an abstraction; it has a definite texture in the body, the emotions, and the mind. The texts name its felt forms: vikṣepa, the scattering and distraction of a mind that cannot rest; the restlessness of ‘becoming’ rather than ‘being,’ the sense of always needing to get somewhere; the equation of the intensity of desire with the intensity of felt separation; tāpatraya, the three kinds of suffering (from oneself, from others, from the cosmos); and, beneath them all, mahad-bhayam, the great fear — the existential dread that underlies every smaller anxiety. There is even, the texts note, a final, subtle suffering: the fear of liberation itself, the ego’s terror of its own dissolution.

attributes.title: Last Barrier: Fear of Liberation Itself (Mokshat Vibhishika)
attributes.aspect: ·   III.8 - 'ascharyam mokshakamasya mokshad eva vibhishika' The nature of this aspect. Ashtavakra identifies the most subtle obstacle: fear of liberation itself. At the very threshold, the seeker realizes that moksha means dissolution of ego-individuality. Fear arises: 'But what about me? I'll cease!' This terror is the last barrier. Gaudapada's Karika confirms: 'The yogis fear from that (Brahman) which is fearless, perceiving fear in fearlessness itself.'

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Vital · Ontic mode: State — the fear of liberation itself · Valence: Affliction (the final resistance) · Guṇa: Tamas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Final barrier · Key relation: The ego’s fear of its own dissolution; kin to Abhiniveśa (Dim 19).
coordinate.ontic_mode: State — the fear of liberation itself
coordinate.key_relation: The ego’s fear of its own dissolution; kin to Abhiniveśa (Dim 19)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Recognition of the fear when it arises at the threshold; willingness to dissolve despite fear.
facets.somatic: Body willing to be 'lost' in the merger.
facets.emotional: Emotional courage at the threshold.
facets.cognitive: Mind willing to dissolve its own identity.
facets.psychic: The soul recognizes its own subtle fear of the freedom it seeks, and gently moves through the final resistance.

facets.emotional: Emotional spectacle of ego-death.
facets.cognitive: Mind performing surrender rather than surrendering.
facets.psychic: The soul magnifies the fear, clinging to bondage as safety at the very threshold of release.

facets.somatic: Body retreating to familiar identifications.
facets.emotional: Emotional terror of merger overwhelming.
facets.cognitive: Mind refusing to dissolve, clinging to 'me.'
facets.psychic: The soul does not even sense this last barrier, mistaking its avoidance of freedom for lack of interest.

attributes.title: Gajendra Moksha (Call in Crisis)
attributes.aspect: ·   Canto 8, Gajendra's story The nature of this aspect. Gajendra Moksha is one of the most beloved Bhagavatam stories. The elephant-king fought a crocodile for 1000 years using his own strength. Only when he surrendered completely and cried out to the Lord did Vishnu appear and save him. The lesson: prolonged self-effort eventually exhausts; sincere surrender brings immediate response. In daily crises: don't struggle alone forever. Call.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Psychic · Ontic mode: State / turning — the surrendered cry in extremity · Valence: The redemptive turn · Guṇa: Sattva emerging from Tamas · Scale: Individual, toward the Divine · Causal status: Turning point (the cry that draws grace) · Key relation: The crisis-call that liberates; kin to Prasāda / grace.
coordinate.ontic_mode: State / turning — the surrendered cry in extremity
coordinate.causal_status: Turning point (the cry that draws grace)
coordinate.key_relation: The crisis-call that liberates; kin to Prasāda / grace

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Daily struggles met with appropriate self-effort PLUS sincere prayer when needed. Not waiting until exhaustion to call.
facets.somatic: Body engaged in effort while heart also calls.
facets.emotional: Emotional balance between effort and surrender.
facets.cognitive: Mind both works and prays.
facets.psychic: The soul, like the elephant-king seized by the crocodile, cries out for help when its own strength fails, and grace answers.

facets.somatic: Body inactive expecting miracles.
facets.emotional: Emotional dependency.
facets.cognitive: Mental abdication of effort.
facets.psychic: The soul cries out endlessly without ever exhausting its self-reliance, performing helplessness.

facets.emotional: Emotional isolation in difficulty.

attributes.title: Seeing Through Delusion of Desire
attributes.aspect: ·   Chapter 26 The nature of this aspect. Desire is delusive—it promises an end (fulfillment) while actually being a self-perpetuating mechanism. The fulfillment of desire produces more desire, not contentment. Seeing through this delusion (not suppressing desire) liberates one from its operation.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Practice / method — a means to freedom · Valence: Cultivated method · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Means · Key relation: the seeing that disillusions desire and loosens Kāma (Dim 24) and Rāga (Dim 19).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Practice / method — a means to freedom
coordinate.key_relation: the seeing that disillusions desire and loosens Kāma (Dim 24) and Rāga (Dim 19)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Awareness of desire's nature without suppression. Each desire seen for what it is—a promise that won't be kept. This very seeing transforms one's relationship with desires.
facets.somatic: Body free from compulsive desire-driven behavior.
facets.emotional: Emotional freedom—desires arise but lose their commanding force.
facets.cognitive: Mind sees desire's mechanism clearly, no longer enthralled.
facets.psychic: The soul sees through the promise each desire makes, recognizing the fullness it seeks cannot be found in the object.

facets.emotional: Suppressed desires creating distortion.
facets.cognitive: Mind obsessed with what it is denying.
facets.psychic: The soul turns disillusionment into bitterness, cynically devaluing all desire and joy.

attributes.title: Removal of Sorrow (Duhkha-Nivritti)
attributes.aspect: ·   Chapter 27 The nature of this aspect. Sorrow has its root cause in avidya (ignorance) about one's true nature. No external acquisition can remove it because the cause is internal. Wisdom—seeing through ignorance—is the only genuine removal. Therapy addresses symptoms; jnana removes the root.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Psychic · Ontic mode: State — a mark of freedom · Valence: The fruit · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual, transcendent · Causal status: Fruit · Key relation: duḥkha-nivṛtti — the ceasing of sorrow; the negative face of liberation.
coordinate.key_relation: duḥkha-nivṛtti — the ceasing of sorrow; the negative face of liberation

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Wisdom-based freedom from sorrow—not happy-pretense but genuine release through realizing the deeper truth.
facets.somatic: Body relieved of sorrow's somatic burdens.
facets.emotional: Emotional freedom from grief-roots through wisdom.
facets.cognitive: Mind seeing through ignorance, sorrow at its root dissolved.
facets.psychic: The soul orients toward the cessation of suffering at its root, addressing the ignorance rather than merely managing the pain.

attributes.attribute: Intensity of Desire = Intensity of Separation
attributes.title: Intensity of Desire = Intensity of Separation
attributes.aspect: ·   Chapter 1, Fourth Brahmana The nature of this aspect. Desire's intensity reveals the felt separation. Strong desire = strong felt-separation. The mechanism: as consciousness materialises and objects become more solid, the separation between subject and object becomes more intense, generating proportionally intense desire. Reducing felt-separation reduces desire naturally.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Framework — desire as the measure of separation · Valence: Affliction-diagnostic · Guṇa: Rajas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Mechanism (craving marks the distance from wholeness) · Key relation: Desire as the felt index of separation; kin to Autsukya (Dim 9).

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.somatic: Body's intense desires recognized as symptoms of felt-separation.
facets.emotional: Emotional cravings understood as separation-pain.
facets.cognitive: Mind addresses root (separation) rather than symptom (desire).
facets.psychic: The soul reads the heat of its craving as the exact measure of its felt separation, and lets longing point it home.

attributes.title: Narada's Crisis (Ego Despite Vast Learning)
attributes.aspect: ·   Chapter 7, Section 1 The nature of this aspect. Narada represents the modern intellectual—mastering vast knowledge yet remaining in inner suffering. He had mastered the four Vedas, history, mathematics, astronomy, all sciences—still suffered. His crisis: 'Knowledge has not freed me; only the Self can.' The starting point of the great Bhuyo-Bhuya ascending teaching.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: State — learned despair (knowledge without peace) · Valence: Affliction (the crisis that opens inquiry) · Guṇa: Rajas–Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Turning point · Key relation: The crisis of the erudite; kin to Nirveda (Dim 9).

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.somatic: Body acknowledges that no amount of skill brings inner peace.
facets.emotional: Emotional courage to admit despite achievements, suffering remains.
facets.cognitive: Mind acknowledges that knowledge of objects cannot solve the self-problem.
facets.psychic: The soul, like Nārada, recognizes that all its learning has not touched the sorrowing ego, and turns toward the knowledge that liberates.

attributes.title: Becoming vs Being (Restlessness as Symptom)
attributes.aspect: अस्ति-भवन   ·   1.3.11 commentary The nature of this aspect. All restlessness, all activity, all becoming—reveals that we have not yet achieved our end. The world is in motion; things pass rather than exist. This perpetual becoming indicates an urge of being toward something. The end is Being, where becoming ceases—like the river reaching the ocean.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Framework — becoming vs being · Valence: Affliction-diagnostic · Guṇa: Rajas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Symptom (restlessness as the mark of bondage) · Key relation: The restlessness of becoming vs the rest of being.

facets.somatic: Body's activity directed toward Being, not just more becoming.
facets.emotional: Emotional engagement with becoming while aiming at Being.
facets.cognitive: Mind understands the urge of becoming as pointing to Being.
facets.psychic: The soul reads its chronic restlessness as the symptom of becoming, and turns toward the being it always already is.

attributes.title: The Great Fear (Mahad-Bhayam)
attributes.aspect: महद् भयम्   ·   2.3.2-3 The nature of this aspect. All cosmic forces operate under the Great Fear of the Supreme Power. Fire burns, sun shines, wind blows, Indra acts, Death runs—all from fear of the Lord. Only by knowing the Self does one transcend this Great Fear. Until then, all of life is shot through with subtle terror.

coordinate.key_relation: The great fear born of duality (Bṛhadāraṇyaka); kin to Bhaya (Dim 8)

facets.emotional: Emotional reverence rather than just fear—awe of the Great.
facets.cognitive: Mind understands the awesome power behind cosmic functioning.
facets.psychic: The soul meets the great background fear of existence consciously, tracing it to the root-ignorance from which it springs.

attributes.title: Vikshepa (Distractions of Mind)
attributes.aspect: विक्षेप   ·   Sutra 1.30, 1.31 The nature of this aspect. Vikshepa is the scattering/distraction of the mind, accompanied by five symptoms: duhkha (suffering/pain), daurmanasya (depression/depression of mind), anga-mejayatva (trembling of limbs), and shvasa-prashvasa (irregular inhalation and exhalation). These are the diagnostic markers of an obstructed mind.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental · Ontic mode: Mechanism — the mind’s scattering (vikṣepa) · Valence: Affliction (to still) · Guṇa: Rajas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Mechanism (the projecting / scattering power) · Key relation: The distracting power (with āvaraṇa); kin to the vṛttis (Dim 20).
coordinate.key_relation: The distracting power (with āvaraṇa); kin to the vṛttis (Dim 20)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When vikshepa is overcome, the mind becomes one-pointed (ekagra) and ready for yoga. Body, breath, and mind are coherent.
facets.somatic: Steady body without tremors, regular smooth breath, healthy nervous system.
facets.emotional: Emotional stability, no depression, peace.
facets.cognitive: Focused mind, no scattering, ready for concentration.
facets.psychic: The soul recognizes the scattering power of mind, and gathers its attention back again and again.

facets.psychic: The soul fights the distractions with a tension that scatters it further.

attributes.title: Viṣāda — Dejection as the Doorway
attributes.aspect: विषाद   ·   Chapter 1 (Arjuna Vishada Yoga) The nature of this aspect. Viṣāda is dejection, the existential crisis — the collapse of meaning, the sense that one’s whole way of living has reached a wall. It is the condition in which the Bhagavad Gītā opens: Arjuna, capable and accomplished, sits down in despair on the battlefield, unable to go on. The tradition’s profound insight is that viṣāda, though it feels like an ending, is often the doorway — the necessary crisis in which the old, bondage-built self exhausts itself and a genuine question can finally be asked. It is suffering that has become honest enough to turn a person around.

facets.overview: When Vishada is properly understood and transcended, a person uses moments of crisis as gateways to deeper wisdom. The crisis becomes catalyst for transformation rather than collapse.

facets.emotional: Emotional depth and wisdom from having faced crisis, healthy capacity to face difficulty, emotional resilience and transformation, deep peace.
facets.cognitive: Mental clarity emerging from crisis, wisdom gained through difficulty, ability to see beyond immediate appearances, transformed perspective.
facets.psychic: Dejection is met as a doorway; like Arjuna’s collapse, the soul’s crisis becomes the very opening through which a higher teaching can enter.

facets.overview: Inability to feel any dejection means inability to recognize when life's structures are inadequate. The person never has the necessary crisis that prompts deeper inquiry.
facets.somatic: Forced physical positivity, ignoring body's signals of distress, manic activity to avoid feeling.
facets.emotional: Emotional bypassing of necessary difficulty, forced positivity, inability to feel appropriate concern, emotional shallowness.
facets.cognitive: Mental denial of life's serious questions, refusal to face hard truths, superficial thinking, inability to engage with depth.
facets.psychic: The soul numbs or flees the crisis; unable to feel the productive dejection, it forecloses the breakdown that might have become a breakthrough.

additional_info.heading: 6. The Stages of Bondage — and the Vices that Build Them
additional_info.text: Bondage deepens by recognizable stages. In the first, there is only a subtle disharmony — a faint, deniable sense that something is off. In the second, active inner conflict — the open war of competing desires and aversions, the divided self. In the third, late bondage, a tamasic heaviness sets in, where even the conflict subsides into dullness, denial, and inertia. Driving these stages are the classic binding emotions — the triad of kāma, krodha, and lobha (lust, anger, greed) the Gītā calls the threefold gate of ruin, and their relatives: moha (delusion), mada (arrogant intoxication of success), mātsarya and asūyā (jealousy and chronic fault-finding), and karpaṇya-doṣa (the flaw of self-pity).

attributes.title: Stage 1: Subtle Disharmony (Early Bondage)
attributes.aspect: ·   Stage 1 in GPT diagnostic The nature of this aspect. Stage 1 of degradation: subtle disharmony. Buddhi still functions but is not fully commanding. Manas grows reactive. Ego identification becomes intermittent. The seeker notices something is wrong but cannot yet name it. Modern markers: anxiety without clear cause, over-identification with success/failure, periodic existential discomfort.

additional_info.heading: The binding emotions (a reference)
additional_info.text:
Binding state

Its character

Kāma–Krodha–Lobha

Lust, anger, greed — the threefold gate of ruin

Moha

Delusion, the obfuscation of clear seeing

Mada

Arrogance, the intoxication of success

Mātsarya / Asūyā

Jealousy and chronic, envious fault-finding

Karpaṇya-doṣa

The flaw of self-pity and weakness of resolve

Dvandva

Being thrown by the pairs of opposites

additional_info.heading: 7. The Turning Point: Crisis, Death, and Honest Inquiry
additional_info.text: The road does not turn by itself. The turning point comes when suffering — dejection, crisis, or the confrontation with death — meets an honest willingness to inquire rather than merely to escape. The Upaniṣads use death as the great teacher: the contemplation that ‘this body ends in ashes’ (bhasmāntam idaṁ śarīram), the questioning of what happens at the end (anta-praśna), the recognition that one’s very identification with the body is what makes death terrible. The Bhāgavatam dramatizes the turn through crisis: Gajendra the elephant, seized by the crocodile and exhausted of his own strength, cries out — and is answered. The turn is the moment self-effort exhausts itself and a deeper opening occurs.

attributes.title: Ajamila's Redemption (Even at Death, Calling Saves)
attributes.aspect: ·   Canto 6, Ajamila's story The nature of this aspect. Ajamila lived a deeply sinful life but happened to name his youngest son Narayana. When death approached and Yama's messengers came, Ajamila cried for his son 'Narayana!' — unconsciously calling the Divine. Vishnu's messengers came and rescued him. The lesson: even unintentional connection to the Divine has saving power; consciously cultivated devotion has incalculable power. Don't postpone practice.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Psychic · Ontic mode: State / turning — redemption even at death · Valence: The redemptive turn · Guṇa: Sattva emerging · Scale: Individual, toward the Divine · Causal status: Turning point (grace at the last moment) · Key relation: The saving power of the Name; kin to Gajendra Moksha.

facets.emotional: Emotional connection consciously built.
facets.cognitive: Mind weaving divine name into daily life.
facets.psychic: The soul learns that even a single sincere turning toward the Divine, even at the last hour, can redeem a wasted life.

attributes.title: Encountering Death (Death as Social Phenomenon)
attributes.aspect: मृत्यु   ·   Chapter 20 The nature of this aspect. Death is the unresolved problem at the root of all human anxiety. Yet death is not what we think—it is just the absence of life from this body, like darkness is absence of light. Don't fight death; search for life. Find the deathless flame within.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Psychic · Ontic mode: State / turning — the encounter with death · Valence: The awakening shock · Guṇa: Tamas opening to Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Turning point (death as teacher) · Key relation: Death as the great teacher; kin to Maraṇa (Dim 9) and Śani (Dim 18).

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.emotional: Peace with mortality, no terror of death, emotional freedom to live fully.
facets.cognitive: Clear understanding that what is essential is deathless, freedom from fear of ending.
facets.psychic: The soul lets the deaths it witnesses awaken it, using mortality as the great teacher it is.

attributes.title: Bondage at Death (Anta-Prashna)
attributes.aspect: ·   Chapter 3, Second Brahmana The nature of this aspect. Death is the moment when all patterns established in life come to fruition. The senses that grasped objects in life continue grasping at death. Liberation requires breaking these patterns BEFORE death. The whole spiritual life is preparation for the final moment when consciousness must navigate its accumulated patterns.

additional_info.heading: 8. The Phases of Liberation
additional_info.text: Once a person has turned, the unbinding itself proceeds in phases — a developmental sequence the tradition maps with care. It is worth stressing that these are gradual; even the teachings of ‘instant’ liberation assume a ground prepared by exactly this kind of ripening.

attributes.title: Phase 1: Karma-Yoga Purification (Ethical Stabilization)
attributes.aspect: ·   Sankara's first phase The nature of this aspect. First phase of transformation: karma-yoga (selfless action) and yama-niyama (ethical living) reduce the influence of rajas (restlessness) and tamas (inertia). This creates psychological stabilization—a precondition for deeper work. Without this foundation, deeper inquiry produces only conceptual results.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Stage — a phase of liberation · Valence: Cultivated stage · Guṇa: Rajas refined to Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Stage in the liberation sequence · Key relation: First phase — karma-yoga purification (ethical stabilization); kin to Karma Yoga (Dim 10).
coordinate.key_relation: First phase — karma-yoga purification (ethical stabilization); kin to Karma Yoga (Dim 10)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Disciplined ethical living, selfless action, gradual reduction of rajas-tamas. Stable psychological foundation forming.
facets.somatic: Body engaged in dharmic action without compulsion.
facets.emotional: Emotional stability from ethical alignment.
facets.cognitive: Mind progressively clearer through right action.
facets.psychic: The soul stabilizes its ethical ground through selfless action, the first phase that makes all later freedom possible.

balanced.text:

Psychic: The soul stabilizes its ethical ground through selfless action, the first phase that makes all later freedom possible.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Believing karma-yoga alone produces liberation. Stuck in this phase indefinitely.
facets.somatic: Body endlessly busy in selfless action without progressing.
facets.emotional: Emotional pride in service.
facets.cognitive: Mental confusion that action = liberation.
facets.psychic: The soul over-invests in good works as identity, the doer-ego inflating through service.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Skipping ethical foundation, jumping to inquiry without base. Pseudo-spirituality.
facets.somatic: Body undisciplined.
facets.emotional: Emotional reactivity continuing despite spiritual claims.
facets.cognitive: Mind discussing Brahman without foundation.
facets.psychic: The soul skips the ethical foundation, and its higher aspirations have no stable ground to stand on.

attributes.title: Phase 2: Buddhi Restoration (Cognitive Re-education)
attributes.aspect: ·   Sankara's second phase The nature of this aspect. Second phase: cognitive re-education through receptive hearing and sustained contemplation. The buddhi (executive cognition) is progressively restored to its rightful role of commanding manas (emotional appraisal). Without this, the strongest emotions and impulses continue to override clear thinking.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental · Ontic mode: Stage — a phase of liberation · Valence: Cultivated stage · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Stage in the liberation sequence · Key relation: Second phase — buddhi restoration (cognitive re-education); restores Buddhi (Dim 3 / 28).
coordinate.key_relation: Second phase — buddhi restoration (cognitive re-education); restores Buddhi (Dim 3 / 28)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Buddhi progressively reclaiming authority. Clear thinking commands emotional appraisal. Wise responses replace blind reactions.
facets.somatic: Body's responses guided by buddhi.
facets.emotional: Emotions still felt but no longer overriding wisdom.
facets.cognitive: Mind discriminating, restored to executive function.
facets.psychic: The soul re-educates its discernment, the intellect restored to its rightful place as charioteer.

Psychic: The soul re-educates its discernment, the intellect restored to its rightful place as charioteer.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Over-intellectualization. Buddhi dominates and dismisses manas entirely. Cold rationality.
facets.somatic: Body disconnected from feeling.
facets.emotional: Emotional life suppressed by buddhi.
facets.cognitive: Mental dominance creating affective deadness.
facets.psychic: The soul over-relies on cognitive re-education, mistaking clarified concepts for transformation.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Manas continues to override buddhi. Emotions and impulses drive behavior; intellect rationalizes after the fact.
facets.somatic: Body driven by emotional impulse.
facets.emotional: Emotional reactivity continues dominantly.
facets.cognitive: Mind serves emotions rather than commanding them.
facets.psychic: The soul never repairs its discernment, and the runaway senses keep the chariot off the road.

attributes.title: Phase 4: Ego Release (Identity Deconstruction)
attributes.aspect: ·   Sankara's fourth phase The nature of this aspect. Fourth phase: progressive dis-identification through neti-neti (not this, not this). The ego (ahamkara) is recognized as superimposition, not essence. Roles can be performed without identity-fusion. This is existential liberation—the false self dissolves while functional self continues.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Mental · Ontic mode: Stage — a phase of liberation · Valence: Cultivated stage · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Stage in the liberation sequence · Key relation: Fourth phase — ego release (identity deconstruction); dissolves Asmitā (Dim 19).
coordinate.key_relation: Fourth phase — ego release (identity deconstruction); dissolves Asmitā (Dim 19)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Ego seen as function not essence. Roles played without fusion. Existential liberation while continuing to function.
facets.somatic: Body functions without identification.
facets.emotional: Emotional life without ego-defense.
facets.cognitive: Mind sees ego as appearance, not reality.
facets.psychic: The soul releases its grip on a fixed identity, the constructed self gently deconstructed.

facets.emotional: Emotional dissolution rather than liberation.
facets.cognitive: Mental fragmentation called transcendence.
facets.psychic: The soul forces ego-dissolution prematurely, dismantling a self it still needs to function.

facets.overview: Ego strongly entrenched. Total identification with role and personality.
facets.somatic: Body completely fused with role identity.
facets.emotional: Emotional life serving ego-defense.
facets.cognitive: Mind entirely organized around 'I am the doer.'
facets.psychic: The soul clings to its ego intact, and the final freedom stays barred by the one who would be free.

attributes.title: Phase 5: Abidance (Stable Harmony)
attributes.aspect: ·   Sankara's final state The nature of this aspect. Final phase: stable abidance in realization. All four functions of antahkarana harmoniously integrated. Cognition is clear; affect is peaceful; will is non-compulsive; identity is witness-awareness. The vasanas (latent tendencies) progressively wither. This is jivanmukti—liberation while living.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: Stage — a phase of liberation · Valence: Cultivated stage · Guṇa: Beyond the guṇas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Stage in the liberation sequence · Key relation: Fifth phase — abidance (stable harmony); kin to Sahaja Avastha (Dim 26).
coordinate.key_relation: Fifth phase — abidance (stable harmony); kin to Sahaja Avastha (Dim 26)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: All faculties harmonized in the witnessing awareness. Vasanas progressively withering. Effortless abidance.
facets.somatic: Body functions in spontaneous flow.
facets.emotional: Emotional life as movement in awareness, not bondage.
facets.cognitive: Mind crystal clear, witnessing presence stable.
facets.psychic: The soul rests in stable abidance, the realized freedom no longer a peak experience but the ordinary ground.

balanced.text:
All faculties harmonized in the witnessing awareness. Vasanas progressively withering. Effortless abidance.

Somatic: Body functions in spontaneous flow.

Emotional: Emotional life as movement in awareness, not bondage.

Cognitive: Mind crystal clear, witnessing presence stable.

Psychic: The soul rests in stable abidance, the realized freedom no longer a peak experience but the ordinary ground.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Claiming abidance while still showing all the markers of earlier stages. Spiritual delusion.
facets.somatic: Body shows signs of compulsion despite claims.
facets.emotional: Emotional reactivity continues underneath claim.
facets.cognitive: Mental claim without genuine stable witnessing.
facets.psychic: The soul clings to its abidance as a special attainment, the very gripping disturbing the rest.

excess.text:

Emotional: Emotional reactivity continues underneath claim.

Cognitive: Mental claim without genuine stable witnessing.

Psychic: The soul clings to its abidance as a special attainment, the very gripping disturbing the rest.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Never reaching abidance. Spiritual life remains as effort, never effortless. Phase 3: Manas Re-orientation. The affective integration in which the feeling-mind is gradually re-oriented from its old cravings and aversions toward the new center — the heart re-educated to find its rest in being rather than in becoming. The arc is coherent: first the conduct is stabilized (karma-yoga), then the intellect is re-educated to see correctly (buddhi), then the emotions are integrated around the new seeing (manas), then the ego-identity itself is released, and finally there is stable abidance in the natural state — the freedom that no longer needs to be maintained by effort.
facets.somatic: Body in continued strain of practice.
facets.emotional: Emotional life still battleground.
facets.cognitive: Mind never knows effortless witnessing.
facets.psychic: The soul cannot stabilize, its glimpses of freedom flickering and lost for want of abidance.

additional_info.heading: 9. The Marks of Freedom: Liberation in Life
additional_info.text: How does one recognize a person in whom the knot has loosened? The texts are unanimous and concrete. There is sthitaprajña, stable wisdom — the equanimity that the Gītā describes as undisturbed in pleasure and pain alike. There is egolessness, the dropping of the constant self-reference. There is a spontaneity in which right action flows from one’s nature without the labored deliberation of the bound self — even an ‘effortless inaction,’ a doing that does not feel like doing. There is an abiding inner happiness (sukha) independent of conditions, a freedom beyond likes and dislikes (svarājya, self-rule), and — importantly — the recognition that this freedom can be lived fully in the midst of ordinary life, as a householder, not only in withdrawal.

attributes.title: Ending of Desire as Freedom (Jivan Mukti)
attributes.sanskrit: जीवन्मुक्ति   ·   Chapter 39 - 'The ending of desire is freedom'
attributes.aspect: जीवन्मुक्ति   ·   Chapter 39 - 'The ending of desire is freedom' The nature of this aspect. Jivan Mukti is freedom in life itself—not after death. It is the ending of desire through understanding, not suppression. Understood desire releases its energy for spiritual upward movement. This is the difference between repression and transcendence.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: State — a mark of freedom · Valence: The fruit · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Individual, transcendent · Causal status: Fruit · Key relation: desirelessness as freedom — jīvanmukti; the extinguishing of craving.
coordinate.key_relation: desirelessness as freedom — jīvanmukti; the extinguishing of craving

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Desires understood and naturally dropped. Energy conserved, available for higher movement. Freedom in living—Jivan Mukti.
facets.somatic: Body free from compulsive pursuit, energy retained.
facets.emotional: Emotional freedom from desire-suffering, contentment.
facets.cognitive: Mind free from future-projection, present-centered, available for higher dimensions.
facets.psychic: The soul finds freedom not in getting what it wants but in the ending of the wanting itself, full where it once was hungry.

balanced.text:

Emotional: Emotional freedom from desire-suffering, contentment.

Cognitive: Mind free from future-projection, present-centered, available for higher dimensions.

Psychic: The soul finds freedom not in getting what it wants but in the ending of the wanting itself, full where it once was hungry.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Forced desire-suppression—body and mind in war with natural urges. Energy goes underground, distorts.
facets.somatic: Body in conflict with suppressed desires, somatic symptoms.
facets.emotional: Suppressed desires creating anxiety, eventual eruption.
facets.cognitive: Mind obsessed with what it suppresses, false ascetic identity.
facets.psychic: The soul forces desirelessness, suppressing natural wants and calling the deadness freedom.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Total slavery to endless desire—every fulfilled desire creates new desires. Constant becoming, never being. Energy completely dispersed.
facets.somatic: Body exhausted by endless pursuit, depleted.
facets.emotional: Chronic dissatisfaction, suffering from wanting.
facets.cognitive: Mind perpetually planning, no present-being possible.
facets.psychic: The soul remains ruled by desire, mistaking each new craving for life itself.

attributes.title: Spontaneity (Dharma as Natural Being)
attributes.aspect: धर्म-स्वाभाविकता   ·   Chapter 45 The nature of this aspect. True spontaneity is impossible while mind interferes—mind always brings the past. Spontaneous action happens only in the present, fresh, never repeating. Through samadhi, one comes back to dharma—the natural, spontaneous nature without distortion by mind.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Psychic · Ontic mode: State — a mark of freedom · Valence: The fruit · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Individual, transcendent · Causal status: Fruit · Key relation: spontaneous right action — dharma as natural being; kin to Sahaj Karma (Dim 16).
coordinate.key_relation: spontaneous right action — dharma as natural being; kin to Sahaj Karma (Dim 16)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Natural responses arising freshly in each moment—not reactions from past memory but live engagement with present. Dharma flowing.
facets.somatic: Body acts freshly, not from habit—each moment new.
facets.emotional: Emotional responses fresh, not preprogrammed reactions.
facets.cognitive: Mind serves rather than dominates—responses flow from present awareness, not past patterns.
facets.psychic: The soul acts spontaneously from its true nature, right action flowing without calculation.

facets.emotional: Reactive emotions claimed as authentic responses.
facets.cognitive: Confusion between reactive and responsive.
facets.psychic: The soul mistakes mere impulsiveness for spontaneity, license dressed as freedom.

facets.somatic: Body locked in habitual patterns.
facets.emotional: Emotional life entirely reactive from past.
facets.cognitive: Mind functioning as repetitive program, no fresh response.
facets.psychic: The soul acts only from rule and fear, never trusting the natural rightness of its own depths.

attributes.title: Egolessness as Jivanmukti
attributes.aspect: अहं-शून्यता   ·   Chapter 46 The nature of this aspect. Jivanmukti is not adding something but the dropping of ego. Ego can take any form—even spiritual ego is dangerous. Watching for ego's arising is the key practice. When 'I' doesn't arise, only 'am-ness' remains—vast, infinite Brahman.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: State — a mark of freedom · Valence: The fruit · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Individual, transcendent · Causal status: Fruit · Key relation: the egoless state; kin to Naiṣkarmya (Dim 16).
coordinate.key_relation: the egoless state; kin to Naiṣkarmya (Dim 16)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Continuous observation prevents ego-formation. Even spiritual achievements don't create ego. 'I am' becomes simply 'amness'—Jivanmukta.
facets.somatic: Body functions without ego-pride, healthy non-identification.
facets.emotional: Emotional freedom from ego-driven feelings, peace.
facets.cognitive: Mind without 'I' as center, vast amness instead.
facets.psychic: The soul lives free of the ego-knot, present and effective without a separate self at the center.

facets.cognitive: Mind subtly building ego on supposed achievements.
facets.psychic: The soul performs egolessness, a subtle spiritual ego congratulating itself on having none.

facets.cognitive: Mind entirely organized around 'I'-construction.

attributes.title: Disciple's Transformation (Total Receptivity)
attributes.aspect: शिष्य-परिवर्तन   ·   Chapter 51 - Disciple's exclamation The nature of this aspect. The whole Adhyatma Upanishad has only one disciple-speaking moment—at the end, after total transformation. Throughout, complete silent receptivity. The transformation isn't through arguing or asking but through total receptive listening to the master's communion.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Psychic · Ontic mode: State — a mark of freedom · Valence: The fruit · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Individual, transcendent · Causal status: Fruit · Key relation: the ripened, total receptivity of the disciple; kin to Śraddhā (Dim 23).
coordinate.key_relation: the ripened, total receptivity of the disciple; kin to Śraddhā (Dim 23)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Total receptivity to teaching, ready to take the jump into unknown. Transformation through openness rather than analysis.
facets.somatic: Body open and receptive, ready for transformation.
facets.emotional: Emotional openness to whatever the teaching brings.
facets.cognitive: Mind open, not defending current view, ready to be changed.
facets.psychic: The soul becomes wholly receptive, an empty vessel into which the teaching can finally pour.

facets.emotional: False emotional surrender hiding resistance.
facets.cognitive: Pseudo-receptivity, mental performance of openness.
facets.psychic: The soul's receptivity slides into dependence, surrendering its discernment along with its resistance.

facets.somatic: Body tensed against teaching.
facets.emotional: Heart closed, no emotional opening.
facets.cognitive: Mind constantly arguing, never receiving, blocked transformation.
facets.psychic: The soul stays defended and full of itself, with no room for the teaching to enter.

attributes.title: Effortless Inaction (Master Idler)
attributes.aspect: ·   XVI.4 The nature of this aspect. Ashtavakra's radical depiction of the liberated being: the alasya-dhurinasya (chief among the lazy). The Jivanmukta is the master idler—even blinking is too much exertion. Yet this is not pathological laziness; it is the cessation of compulsive doership. Actions happen as destiny brings them; the person no longer 'does' anything as separate doer.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Psychic · Ontic mode: State — a mark of freedom · Valence: The fruit · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Individual, transcendent · Causal status: Fruit · Key relation: action-in-inaction — the ‘master idler’; kin to Naiṣkarmya (Dim 16).

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Effortless action arising from destiny without ego-doership. Like a child acting from spontaneity, not strategy.
facets.somatic: Body's actions arise without compulsive force.
facets.emotional: Emotional life flowing without ego-strain.
facets.cognitive: Mind not generating endless plans and projects.
facets.psychic: The soul acts from stillness, accomplishing without strain, doing-by-non-doing in the manner of the sage.

facets.psychic: The soul mistakes idleness for the effortless action of the free, using 'non-doing' to excuse sloth.

attributes.title: Householder Jivanmukta (Liberation in Family Life)
attributes.aspect: ·   XVIII.84 The nature of this aspect. Ashtavakra explicitly affirms: Jivanmukti is possible in family life. The householder Jivanmukta lives without attachment to wife and children, unperturbed by family criticism. King Janaka himself is the model—ruling a kingdom, with wife and family, yet fully liberated. This contradicts the renunciate-only model. Sannyas is not required for liberation.

facets.emotional: Emotional connection with family without attachment.
facets.cognitive: Mind free while body operates in worldly context.
facets.psychic: The soul lives its freedom in the midst of family and work, needing no cave or robe to be free.

attributes.title: Disciple's Joy at Self-Realization
attributes.aspect: ·   Chapter 2 The nature of this aspect. Ashtavakra Gita Chapter 2 records Janaka's spontaneous joy after instruction. He bursts forth with verses like 'Oh wonderful! I am the pure consciousness, beyond all this. How long was I deluded by mistaking myself for the body!' This natural overflowing recognition is the marker of genuine realization—not learned reverence but spontaneous wonder.

facets.cognitive: Mind exclaims at what was always so.
facets.psychic: The soul knows the incomparable joy of recognizing its own true nature, a gladness springing from being rather than circumstance.

attributes.title: Test of the Disciple's Realization
attributes.aspect: ·   Chapter 3 The nature of this aspect. Ashtavakra's chapter 3 is the master's test of the disciple. Markers of genuine realization: no need for samadhi-states (already realized); no liberation-seeking (already free); no entanglement in reasoning (transcends categories); spontaneous response. The unrealized still asks; the realized rests.

facets.cognitive: Mind no longer seeks; rests.
facets.psychic: The soul welcomes the testing of its realization in real life, knowing that only what holds under pressure is real.

attributes.title: Sukha (Happiness as Inner State)
attributes.aspect: ·   Chapter 7, Section 22 The nature of this aspect. Sukha is the inner experience of happiness/joy—the actual felt-state for which all spiritual practice exists. But ordinary sukha from objects is fleeting; true sukha comes from Bhuma alone. The recognition that happiness can be cultivated as inner state independent of circumstances is liberating.

attributes.title: Four Selves Realization (Indra-Prajapati)
attributes.aspect: ·   Chapter 8, Sections 7-12 The nature of this aspect. The four-stage realization of Self through Prajapati-Indra dialogue: (1) Body as Self (most stop here), (2) Dream-self (subtle body), (3) Deep-sleep self (causal), (4) True Self as Spirit (Turiya). Indra's four-fold returning reveals the importance of not settling at partial realizations. The whole journey takes 101 years.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework — the fourfold progressive realization of the Self · Valence: The graded fruit · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Sequence · Key relation: The Indra-Prajāpati dialogue (Chāndogya) — the four selves.

attributes.title: Sthitaprajña — The One of Stable Wisdom
attributes.aspect: स्थितप्रज्ञ   ·   Chapter 2 The nature of this aspect. The sthitaprajña, described in the second chapter of the Gītā, is the person of settled wisdom — undisturbed in sorrow and unelated in pleasure, free from craving, fear, and anger, drawing the senses in like a tortoise drawing in its limbs. This is not the coldness of suppression but the stability of one who has found a ground that the pairs of opposites no longer move. As a mark of freedom, it is the most testable: not special experiences or knowledge, but the simple, observable steadiness of a person no longer at the mercy of their own reactions.

additional_info.heading: 10. Grace and the Cry for Help
additional_info.text: Running alongside the anatomy of self-effort is a quieter thread the tradition never quite lets go of: grace. There are moments, the texts insist, when self-effort exhausts itself and what completes the turning is not one’s own strength but a kind of given-ness — prasāda, the tranquility that descends rather than is achieved. Gajendra is saved not by his struggle but by his surrender; Ajāmila, a fallen man, is redeemed at the very end by helplessly calling the divine name; Kuntī, remarkably, prays for adversity, because it was in calamity that grace came near. The thread does not contradict the anatomy of effort; it completes it. The work is one’s own to do — and there is a point past which the doing gives way to a receiving.

attributes.title: Prasada (Tranquility/Grace)
attributes.aspect: प्रसाद   ·   Chapter 2, Chapter 10 The nature of this aspect. Prasada is a state of profound mental peace, clarity, and grace achieved when the senses are fully controlled and freed from likes and dislikes. It is a state of divine blessing and inner luminosity.

facets.cognitive: Mental clarity and luminosity, freedom from mental confusion, deep peace and stillness, spiritual insight and wisdom, mind functioning at its highest clarity.
facets.psychic: The soul receives the tranquil clarity of grace, a settling that comes as gift rather than achievement.

attributes.title: Kunti's Prayer (Welcome Adversity)
attributes.aspect: ·   Canto 1, Kunti's prayers (1.8.25) The nature of this aspect. Kunti's prayers contain a radical teaching. She prays that calamities continue to come to her so she keeps seeing Krishna. The insight: prosperity tends to lead to forgetting; difficulty brings remembrance. The most spiritually fortunate are not always the materially fortunate. In daily life: reframe difficulties as opportunities for deepening connection rather than just suffering to escape.

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Difficulties reframed as spiritual opportunities. Each crisis deepens connection rather than destroying.
facets.somatic: Body endures with grace, learning through difficulty.
facets.emotional: Emotional transformation through trials.
facets.cognitive: Mind sees difficulties as gifts of spiritual deepening.
facets.psychic: The soul, like Kuntī, welcomes adversity as the very thing that keeps it turned toward the Divine.

facets.emotional: Emotional addiction to crisis-mode.
facets.cognitive: Mental glorification of suffering.
facets.psychic: The soul courts suffering deliberately, mistaking self-imposed hardship for devotion.

facets.somatic: Body fights every difficulty.
facets.emotional: Emotional complaint about all hardships.
facets.cognitive: Mind sees no purpose in difficulty.
facets.psychic: The soul flees all adversity, and in comfort forgets the Divine it sought only in distress.

additional_info.heading: 10b. Further States of Bondage and Freedom
additional_info.text: Beyond the principal stations of the road, the corpus names a wealth of further states — binding emotions and their opposites, modes of self-offering, definitions of liberation, and the subtle counterfeits of each. Every one is examined below in its balanced, excessive, and deficient expression.

attributes.title: Four Steps: Vairagya, Gyan, Uparati, Shanti
attributes.aspect: वैराग्य-ज्ञान-उपरति-शान्ति   ·   Chapter 42 The nature of this aspect. Four sequential and self-verifying steps. Each step's authenticity is proven only by the next arising. False vairagya leaves no real gyan; false gyan leaves no real uparati; false uparati leaves no real shanti. Forge ahead only if previous step is verified.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework — a fourfold ladder to freedom · Valence: Cultivated sequence · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Sequence · Key relation: Dispassion → knowledge → cessation → peace; kin to Sādhana Chatuṣṭaya (Dim 23).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Framework — a fourfold ladder to freedom
coordinate.key_relation: Dispassion → knowledge → cessation → peace; kin to Sādhana Chatuṣṭaya (Dim 23)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: All four progressively unfolding—non-attachment naturally leading to inner knowing, naturally to total relaxation, naturally to absolute silence.
facets.somatic: Body progressively settling—from sense-dispersal to inner flame to deep rest to silence.
facets.emotional: Emotional unfolding—detachment, illumination, relaxation, peace.
facets.cognitive: Mental progression to total cessation, each stage verified by the next.
facets.psychic: The soul moves through dispassion, knowledge, withdrawal, and peace in their natural order, each preparing the next.

balanced.text:

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Skipping steps—claiming silence without true relaxation, claiming knowing without real non-attachment. Pseudo progression.
facets.somatic: Body forced into appearing meditative without genuine readiness.
facets.emotional: Forced peace without authentic relaxation.
facets.cognitive: Claiming higher states without foundation.
facets.psychic: The soul rushes or forces the steps, grasping at peace before dispassion has ripened.

facets.overview: Stuck at first step or before it—no movement toward vairagya, complete outward dispersion.
facets.somatic: Body completely outward-dispersed.
facets.emotional: Emotional life entirely externally directed.
facets.cognitive: Mind perpetually scattered, no movement toward inner.
facets.psychic: The soul attempts none of the steps, and peace stays a word with no path beneath it.

attributes.title: Three Paths: Bliss, Consciousness, Existence (Sat-Chid-Ananda)
attributes.aspect: सच्चिदानन्द त्रिमार्ग   ·   Chapter 42 - Three paths The nature of this aspect. The divine has three attributes—Existence (Sat), Consciousness (Chid), Bliss (Ananda). Three paths corresponding to these attributes lead to the same realization. Bliss path is visible (devotees), consciousness path is visible (mindful sages), existence path is invisible (hidden masters).

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework — a vision / distinction · Valence: Neutral — a pointer to be realized · Guṇa: Beyond the guṇas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Framework · Key relation: three doors to the one Reality via Being, Consciousness, and Bliss; kin to Satchidananda (Dim 28).
coordinate.valence: Neutral — a pointer to be realized
coordinate.key_relation: three doors to the one Reality via Being, Consciousness, and Bliss; kin to Satchidananda (Dim 28)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: One's natural path chosen wisely. Whichever attribute one cultivates, the other two follow automatically when realization occurs.
facets.somatic: Body shaped by the chosen path—blissful, conscious, or existential.
facets.emotional: Emotional life flowing through the chosen quality.
facets.cognitive: Mind aligned with chosen attribute, opening to the trinity.
facets.psychic: The soul approaches the Absolute through being, consciousness, or bliss, taking the door that fits it.

facets.somatic: Body in wrong-path strain.
facets.emotional: Emotional confusion from mixed paths.
facets.cognitive: Mental confusion about which attribute is foundation.
facets.psychic: The soul absolutizes one door, insisting its way is the only way.

facets.somatic: Body without spiritual practice direction.
facets.emotional: Emotional life without spiritual deepening.
facets.cognitive: Mind without spiritual purpose.
facets.psychic: The soul finds no door at all, the Absolute remaining an abstraction it never approaches.

attributes.title: Instant Recipe for Mukti (20-Verse Liberation)
attributes.aspect: ·   I.1-20 The nature of this aspect. Ashtavakra's compressed teaching: One could get instant liberation if only one were to separate oneself from the body and remain effortlessly resting in pure Consciousness. The 'recipe': separate yourself from body-mind; rest in pure 'am-ness'; simply BE without thinking 'I am'. Nisarga Datta Maharaj reportedly attained enlightenment in less than three years through this sole sadhana.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Practice / method — a means to freedom · Valence: Cultivated method · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Means · Key relation: a condensed, compact teaching for liberation.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Practice / method — a means to freedom
coordinate.key_relation: a condensed, compact teaching for liberation

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Direct application: separate, rest, simply BE. Practiced daily for hours, leads to recognition.
facets.somatic: Body relaxed in being-recognition.
facets.emotional: Emotional peace from resting in pure existence.
facets.cognitive: Mind in pure 'am-ness' without conceptualizing it.
facets.psychic: The soul takes to heart that liberation can be pointed out in a few verses to a ripe seeker, and lets the simple truth land.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Repeating 'I AM' as mantra rather than simply remaining. Treating the recipe as technique.
facets.somatic: Body strained from repetition.
facets.emotional: Emotional anxiety about doing it right.
facets.cognitive: Mind making 'I AM' into another concept.
facets.psychic: The soul collects 'instant' teachings as a shortcut, skipping the ripening that makes them work.

facets.cognitive: Mind takes 'I am woman, I am 70, I am engineer' as final.
facets.psychic: The soul dismisses the possibility of swift recognition, certain freedom must be endlessly far.

attributes.title: The Pathless Path (No Steps Required)
attributes.aspect: ·   XV.15 The nature of this aspect. Ashtavakra's radical teaching: there are no steps in the path. Most spiritual traditions prescribe gradations. Ashtavakra denies them. Even the Mahavakya 'I am Brahman' is inferior—because it presupposes 'I' looking at 'Brahman.' Beyond this, simply BE as 'Sarvam khalvidam Brahma' (all this is Brahman)—merge without observer-observed structure.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: State / goal — liberation realized · Valence: The goal · Guṇa: Beyond the guṇas · Scale: Transcendent · Causal status: Fruit · Key relation: the immediate, pathless recognition — no steps required; kin to Quantum Flight (Dim 27).
coordinate.key_relation: the immediate, pathless recognition — no steps required; kin to Quantum Flight (Dim 27)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Recognition of pathlessness. No goal to reach because the destination is what one IS.
facets.somatic: Body engaged in life without spiritual-seeking.
facets.emotional: Emotional peace from not-seeking.
facets.cognitive: Mind beyond observer-observed structure.
facets.psychic: The soul senses that, since it is already what it seeks, the deepest truth is pathless — a recognition, not a journey.

facets.emotional: Emotional avoidance disguised as pathlessness.
facets.cognitive: Mental complacency rationalized.
facets.psychic: The soul uses 'there is no path' to abandon all practice prematurely, a bypass for the unready.

facets.cognitive: Mind perpetually on path, never resting.

attributes.title: Bhogi-Yogi Equivalence (Both On Same Plane)
attributes.aspect: ·   XVI.2 The nature of this aspect. Ashtavakra's profound psychological insight: the worldly enjoyer (bhogi) and the renouncing yogi are mentally identical—both fixated on enjoyment, one grasping, one rejecting. Both bind. Liberation requires going beyond both: the witness who neither grasps nor rejects. Renunciation is not a higher state than enjoyment—they are mirror images.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework — a vision / distinction · Valence: Neutral insight · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Framework · Key relation: the non-difference of enjoyer and yogi in the realized — both on one plane.
coordinate.key_relation: the non-difference of enjoyer and yogi in the realized — both on one plane

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Witness attitude beyond both pursuit and rejection of enjoyment. Choiceless perception.
facets.somatic: Body engages with what arises, doesn't pursue or reject.
facets.emotional: Emotional freedom from both grasping and renouncing.
facets.cognitive: Mind beyond bhogi-yogi duality.
facets.psychic: The soul sees that enjoyer and renouncer can stand on the same ground when both act without attachment.

facets.somatic: Body in indulgence claiming witness-state.
facets.emotional: Emotional license disguised as transcendence.
facets.cognitive: Mental rationalization of grasping.
facets.psychic: The soul uses the equivalence to justify indulgence, calling its bhoga a yoga.

facets.emotional: Emotional life caught in approach-avoidance.
facets.cognitive: Mind locked in dualistic approach to objects.
facets.psychic: The soul splits the world into worldly and spiritual, missing the freedom available within ordinary enjoyment.

attributes.title: Direct Perception Method (No Sadhana Needed)
attributes.aspect: ·   Atmananda's Introduction The nature of this aspect. Sri Atmananda's distinctive teaching from Ashtavakra Gita: the Direct Perception Method requires no sadhana (preparatory practice). You are made to PERCEIVE Truth positively. Since your own Being is what you most directly experience, references to it must be reasonable. This is contrary to gradual-path teachings that require years of preparation.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: Practice / method — a means to freedom · Valence: Cultivated method · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Means · Key relation: direct perception of the Self (aparokṣānubhūti); kin to the Direct Method (Dim 27).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Practice / method — a means to freedom
coordinate.key_relation: direct perception of the Self (aparokṣānubhūti); kin to the Direct Method (Dim 27)

facets.somatic: Body recognized as Self's instrument, not as obstruction.
facets.emotional: Emotional life recognized as Self's expression, not as enemy.
facets.cognitive: Mind directly perceives its own Source without effort.
facets.psychic: The soul recognizes that awareness can know itself directly, without intermediary practice, here and now.

facets.emotional: Emotional life in continual preparation mode.
facets.cognitive: Mind believes recognition is always future.
facets.psychic: The soul believes only long practice can ever yield truth, and never tries the direct look.

attributes.title: Easy Way (No Effort Required)
attributes.aspect: ·   Throughout The nature of this aspect. Ashtavakra's distinctive 'easy way': liberation comes not through effort but through recognition. Effort presupposes someone (the doer) who is striving—but the doer is precisely the false identity that needs to be seen through. Therefore, paradoxically, the path is easy: simply recognize what is already true. Yet it is the subtlest because effort itself becomes obstacle.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: State / goal — liberation realized · Valence: The goal · Guṇa: Beyond the guṇas · Scale: Transcendent · Causal status: Fruit · Key relation: liberation as effortless recognition — no effort required.

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Recognition that liberation is easy because it is already accomplished. Effort transformed into receptive recognition.
facets.somatic: Body relaxed, not striving.
facets.emotional: Emotional peace from non-effort.
facets.cognitive: Mind receptive to what is already true.
facets.psychic: The soul discovers the effortless recognition beneath all its striving, the ease that was always available.

attributes.title: Moksha (Liberation/Freedom)
attributes.aspect: मोक्ष   ·   Chapter 2, Chapter 15 The nature of this aspect. Moksha is liberation from the cycle of birth and death, from suffering, and from illusion. It represents the ultimate goal of spiritual practice—freedom from bondage to the material world and ego.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic · Ontic mode: State / goal — liberation (mokṣa) · Valence: The goal · Guṇa: Beyond the guṇas · Scale: Transcendent · Causal status: Fruit · Key relation: The fourth puruṣārtha; the aim of the whole framework; kin to Niḥśreyasa (Dim 28).
coordinate.key_relation: The fourth puruṣārtha; the aim of the whole framework; kin to Niḥśreyasa (Dim 28)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: The pursuit of Moksha with proper understanding leads to decreasing attachment, increasing wisdom, freedom from fear and suffering, and a life lived with inner freedom.
facets.somatic: Physical health from decreasing stress and reactivity, body becomes a vehicle for spiritual life, freedom from disease caused by ignorance, light and ethereal quality, natural disengagement from harmful habits.
facets.emotional: Freedom from fear of death and suffering, decreasing emotional reactivity and attachment, emotional peace and bliss, freedom from guilt and shame, emotional detachment without coldness, increasing love and compassion.
facets.cognitive: Freedom from false beliefs and illusions, clarity about true nature of reality, transcendence of ego and self-identification, mental peace and stillness, wisdom about nature of existence.
facets.psychic: The soul orients its whole life toward liberation, the freedom that is its birthright and final good.

balanced.text:

Cognitive: Freedom from false beliefs and illusions, clarity about true nature of reality, transcendence of ego and self-identification, mental peace and stillness, wisdom about nature of existence.

Psychic: The soul orients its whole life toward liberation, the freedom that is its birthright and final good.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Excessive focus on Moksha can lead to escapism, neglect of responsibilities, rejection of natural life, difficulty maintaining relationships, or spiritual bypassing.
facets.somatic: Neglect of physical health and body care, withdrawal from physical world and activity, weakness from lack of engagement, physical detachment and numbness, inability to function in material reality.
facets.emotional: Emotional withdrawal and coldness, loss of normal human relationships, emotional bypassing of psychological work, detachment that looks like enlightenment but is avoidance, lack of compassion for those still engaged.
facets.cognitive: Escapism from life responsibilities, dissociation from present experience, mental disconnection and spaciness, lack of interest in helping others, inability to bring wisdom into practical life.
facets.psychic: The soul turns mokṣa into one more object of craving, the grasping for freedom itself a bondage.
