attributes.title: Bhakti (Devotion vs Love)
attributes.sanskrit: भक्ति   ·   Second of the four paths
attributes.aspect: भक्ति   ·   Second of the four paths The nature of this aspect. Bhakti is unconditional reverence for life itself, undirected love that flows toward all existence. Unlike romantic love (which divides into beloved/unloved), devotion has no counterpart. It transforms the entire existence into the beloved.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Psychic · Ontic mode: Faculty / distinction — devotion distinguished from mere love · Valence: Virtue to cultivate · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual, toward the Divine · Causal status: Mechanism · Key relation: The capacity underlying Bhakti Yoga (Dim 10), distinguished from ordinary love.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Faculty / distinction — devotion distinguished from mere love
coordinate.key_relation: The capacity underlying Bhakti Yoga (Dim 10), distinguished from ordinary love

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When Bhakti is mature, devotion flows toward all existence without exclusion. A stone, tree, river—anything becomes object of devotion. No jealousy or possessiveness arises because the whole is loved.
facets.somatic: Open heart, warmth flowing toward all beings, healthy body radiating universal care.
facets.emotional: Emotional unconditional love, freedom from possessive attachment, deep reverence for all life.
facets.cognitive: Mind united with the whole, no division between loved/unloved, devotional clarity.
facets.psychic: The soul's devotion ripens from possessive love into selfless adoration that seeks the Beloved's sake, not its own.

balanced.text:

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: When devotion is mistaken for love (as in 'devotion to Rama against Krishna'), it becomes sectarian. Religious conflicts arise from love-phenomenon misunderstood as devotion.
facets.somatic: Body tense in defense of chosen deity, exclusive ritualism damaging health.
facets.emotional: Emotional jealousy disguised as devotion, hatred toward those of other paths, sectarian passions.
facets.cognitive: Mental exclusivism, fanaticism, debating superiority of one's god, missing the universal.
facets.psychic: The soul's devotion stays possessive, loving the Divine for what it gives.

facets.somatic: Closed heart center, restricted breath, lack of emotional vitality.
facets.emotional: Emotional coldness, inability to surrender, mistrust of all that requires devotion.
facets.cognitive: Mind without heart-warmth, harsh intellectualism, no devotional unfolding.
facets.psychic: The soul has no devotion at all, facing existence without a thread of the heart's longing.

attributes.title: Dhyana (Meditation as No-Music)
attributes.sanskrit: ध्यान   ·   Third of the four paths
attributes.aspect: ध्यान   ·   Third of the four paths The nature of this aspect. Dhyana is not an effort but the natural shadow of faith and devotion. It is the cessation of mental chatter, the inner silence in which presence with all existence becomes possible. It cannot be forced—it flowers when the inner ground is prepared.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Practice — meditation as inner silence (‘no-music’) · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Means · Key relation: Same as Dhyāna the limb (Dim 11); meditation as the ceasing of inner noise.
coordinate.key_relation: Same as Dhyāna the limb (Dim 11); meditation as the ceasing of inner noise

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: When meditation arises spontaneously from devotion, it is effortless—the mind naturally becomes silent in the presence of the beloved (which is now all of existence).
facets.somatic: Body settled in natural stillness, nervous system at rest, breath slow and refined.
facets.emotional: Emotional silence beyond turbulence, peace as natural state, depth of feeling without agitation.
facets.cognitive: Mind naturally silent, no inner talk, awareness without object, presence to existence.
facets.psychic: The soul rests in meditation as a silence beyond all forms, the no-music in which the meditator dissolves.

balanced.text:
When meditation arises spontaneously from devotion, it is effortless—the mind naturally becomes silent in the presence of the beloved (which is now all of existence).

Somatic: Body settled in natural stillness, nervous system at rest, breath slow and refined.

Emotional: Emotional silence beyond turbulence, peace as natural state, depth of feeling without agitation.

Cognitive: Mind naturally silent, no inner talk, awareness without object, presence to existence.

Psychic: The soul rests in meditation as a silence beyond all forms, the no-music in which the meditator dissolves.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Forced meditation without devotional foundation becomes an arduous mental discipline that often fails or creates new tensions. The mind cannot be forced into silence.
facets.somatic: Tension from forced posture, headaches from straining, body rebelling against forced stillness.
facets.emotional: Frustration with practice, emotional suppression mistaken for peace, masochistic spirituality.
facets.cognitive: Mind fighting itself to become silent—creating more noise, forced concentration that creates strain.
facets.psychic: The soul grasps at meditative states, straining for silence and so disturbing it.

facets.somatic: Restless body, chronic agitation, nervous system never finds rest.
facets.emotional: Constant emotional turbulence, no inner peace, reactive to every event.
facets.cognitive: Endless inner talk, scattered mind, no foundation for higher states.
facets.psychic: The soul never meditates, its surface never still enough to glimpse the depths.

attributes.title: Two Paths: Awareness and Methods (Samkhya and Yoga)
attributes.aspect: साङ्ख्य-योग   ·   Chapter 34 The nature of this aspect. The two great paths to the divine: direct awareness (samkhya) where one simply witnesses all activities; or methods (yoga) where techniques create the conditions for awareness to arise. Most modern minds need methods first; awareness then flowers.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Framework — a vision / distinction · Valence: Neutral (two valid roads) · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Framework · Key relation: the two roads — Sāṁkhya (knowledge) and Yoga (practice), one goal (Gītā 5).
coordinate.key_relation: the two roads — Sāṁkhya (knowledge) and Yoga (practice), one goal (Gītā 5)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Both paths used wisely according to one's nature—methods to create conditions, awareness as the essence within methods. Neither rigid following of method nor abandoning of technique.
facets.somatic: Body engaged in methods that bring awareness, healthy practice.
facets.emotional: Emotional engagement with practice while awareness deepens.
facets.cognitive: Mind awake through methods, methods serving awareness.
facets.psychic: The soul honors both the path of pure discernment and the path of method, choosing as its temperament requires.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Mechanical obsession with methods without awareness—doing the technique without being present. Or grasping pure awareness without preparing the vessel.
facets.somatic: Body going through motions without the inner awareness.
facets.emotional: Emotional going-through-motions of practice without transformation.
facets.cognitive: Mental rituals without depth, or mental claims of awareness without ground.
facets.psychic: The soul absolutizes one path, dismissing the other as inferior.

facets.emotional: Emotional reactivity without method or awareness.
facets.cognitive: Mind in complete unconsciousness, no movement toward liberation.
facets.psychic: The soul follows neither, drifting without either the knowledge or the technique that frees.

additional_info.heading: Scenario A — The Binding Path
additional_info.text:
Internal landscape.

The dejection is unbearable, and she does everything to escape it. She numbs it with distraction and consumption, or she dramatizes it into a story of victimhood, or she flees into frantic ‘becoming’ — a new project, a new identity to outrun the emptiness. The crisis, which was an open door, is firmly shut. The superimposition is never questioned; the knot, if anything, pulls tighter.

Conduct (the new action).

She rebuilds the same kind of life on the same foundation, reaching again for the desires and aversions that bound her, learning nothing the crisis had to teach. The vāsanās are re-grooved; the cycle resumes.

Outcome (the deepening groove).

Bondage deepens by a stage. The early disharmony that the crisis exposed is papered over rather than resolved, and the next crisis — which will come — starts from a more bound place.
additional_info.source_section: 11. The Two Paths: One Crisis, Two Outcomes

additional_info.heading: Scenario B — The Liberating Path
additional_info.text:
Internal landscape.

She lets the dejection be honest. Instead of fleeing it, she sits in it long enough to let it ask its real question: ‘Who is it that suffers? What did I take myself to be, that its loss could end me?’ The crisis exhausts the old self enough that, for the first time, the superimposition becomes visible — she glimpses that she had laid her whole identity onto things that were never her. The viṣāda has become the doorway.

Conduct (the pivot).

She does not rebuild blindly; she inquires. She turns toward the practices of unbinding — stabilizing her conduct, re-educating her seeing, integrating the grief — and, where her own strength runs out, she allows a surrender, a receiving, rather than another frantic effort. The crisis is metabolized rather than fled.

Outcome (the groove dissolving).

Because the suffering was met rather than escaped, it does its work: a vāsanā is exhausted rather than re-grooved, the knot loosens by a degree, and a little more of the freedom that was always there shows through. This is the whole anatomy in miniature — not the avoidance of suffering, but its transformation into the very doorway of liberation.
additional_info.source_section: 11. The Two Paths: One Crisis, Two Outcomes

additional_info.heading: 11. The Two Paths: One Crisis, Two Outcomes
additional_info.text: The whole anatomy turns on a single hinge: what a person does with suffering. Here is one crisis shown along the two paths — the Binding Path, in which the suffering is fled or numbed and bondage resumes, and the Liberating Path, in which the same suffering becomes the doorway. The scenario: a serious setback — a career collapse, a loss — brings Maya to a genuine viṣāda, a crisis in which her whole way of living feels exhausted and meaningless.
additional_info.source_section: 11. The Two Paths: One Crisis, Two Outcomes

attributes.title: Bhuyo-Bhuya: Pramaa and Pramada (Right Knowing and Heedlessness)
attributes.aspect: ·   Chapter 7, Section 22-23 The nature of this aspect. Pramada is the spiritual disease of carelessness/heedlessness—forgetting one's true nature, losing remembrance, slipping into automatic patterns. Even highly developed seekers fall through pramada. The antidote is apramada (heedfulness)—continuous remembrance and care.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental · Ontic mode: Framework — a vision / distinction · Valence: Neutral diagnostic · Guṇa: Sattva vs Tamas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Framework · Key relation: the oscillation of pramā (right knowing) and pramāda (heedlessness); kin to Pramāda (Dim 21).
coordinate.key_relation: the oscillation of pramā (right knowing) and pramāda (heedlessness); kin to Pramāda (Dim 21)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Continuous apramada—heedful attention throughout practice and life. Steady remembrance prevents loss of ground.
facets.somatic: Body in continuous mindful action.
facets.emotional: Emotional alertness without anxiety.
facets.cognitive: Mind in continuous heedful attention.
facets.psychic: The soul cultivates right knowing again and again, vigilant against the heedlessness that lets the truth slip.

attributes.title: Bhrigu's Tapas (Progressive Truth-Discovery)
attributes.aspect: ·   Bhrigu Valli The nature of this aspect. Bhrigu's progressive inquiry through tapas: Varuna gives clue ('that from which beings are born...'). Bhrigu doesn't just remember—he goes and PRACTICES tapas to know. Each level reached through tapas: food, then prana, then mind, then intellect, then ananda (bliss). The model of authentic discovery vs. intellectual acceptance.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Practice / method — a means to freedom · Valence: Cultivated method · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Means · Key relation: Bhṛgu’s graded inquiry to Ānanda (Taittirīya); kin to Bhrigu-Varuni Vidya (Dim 25).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Practice / method — a means to freedom

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Authentic tapas-based discovery at each level. Not accepting teaching intellectually but realizing through practice.
facets.somatic: Body engaged in genuine tapas, discovering each level.
facets.emotional: Emotional commitment to discovery over hearsay.
facets.cognitive: Mind discovers through practice what cannot be merely learned.
facets.psychic: The soul, like Bhṛgu, discovers truth in stages through sustained inquiry, each level surrendered for the deeper.

attributes.title: Karma-Jnana Samuchchaya (Combined Action-Knowledge)
attributes.aspect: ·   Isa verse 11 The nature of this aspect. The crucial teaching: neither pure karma nor pure jnana suffices alone. Karma alone (action without wisdom) keeps in samsara; jnana alone (knowledge without grounding action) is dangerous incomplete realization. Both together—karma as the body, jnana as the soul—is the complete path.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Vital · Ontic mode: Framework — a vision / distinction · Valence: Neutral (a debated synthesis) · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Framework · Key relation: the combined path of works and knowledge; kin to Vidya-and-Avidya (Dim 28).
coordinate.key_relation: the combined path of works and knowledge; kin to Vidya-and-Avidya (Dim 28)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Integrated path: action grounded in wisdom; wisdom expressed through action. Neither escape into knowledge nor lost in activity.
facets.somatic: Body engaged in karma supported by jnana.
facets.emotional: Emotional balance between action and contemplation.
facets.cognitive: Mind unifies action with knowledge.
facets.psychic: The soul weaves action and knowledge together, neither renouncing the world nor bound by it.

balanced.text:

Psychic: The soul weaves action and knowledge together, neither renouncing the world nor bound by it.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Either over-emphasis on action alone or over-emphasis on knowledge alone. Imbalance toward one extreme.
facets.somatic: Body either compulsively active or completely withdrawn.
facets.emotional: Emotional life unbalanced toward one pole.
facets.cognitive: Mind either escapist-contemplative or compulsive-active.
facets.psychic: The soul over-balances toward one — action without wisdom, or wisdom without engagement.

facets.emotional: Emotional drift without engagement.
facets.cognitive: Mind without action or knowledge orientation.
facets.psychic: The soul has neither living knowledge nor purified action, ungrounded in both.

attributes.title: Breath Stillness as Liberation (Niranjana State)
attributes.aspect: ·   Essence of the entire Gita The nature of this aspect. Lahiri Mahasay's distinctive teaching: liberation comes through actual breath stillness, not philosophical insight alone. Through Kriya practice, breath naturally becomes slower, subtler, and eventually still. In this stillness, mind also stops; in mental stillness, the Self is recognized. Breath, mind, and consciousness are linked: still the breath and mind stops; stop the mind and Self is realized.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Psychic · Ontic mode: State / practice — the stainless stillness of stilled breath (niranjana) · Valence: The fruit · Guṇa: Sattva, beyond · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Fruit · Key relation: Kin to Prāṇāyāma (Dim 11) and Nāda (Dim 26).
coordinate.ontic_mode: State / practice — the stainless stillness of stilled breath (niranjana)
coordinate.key_relation: Kin to Prāṇāyāma (Dim 11) and Nāda (Dim 26)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Breath naturally slowing through Kriya. Stillness arising spontaneously, not forced. Liberation glimpsed in breath-pauses.
facets.somatic: Body needs less breath as it stills; subtle pranic balance.
facets.emotional: Emotional life still as breath stills.
facets.cognitive: Mind stops as breath stops; Self glimpsed.
facets.psychic: The soul finds that as the breath stills, the mind stills, and a stainless, liberated clarity opens.

facets.cognitive: Mental disturbance from improper practice.
facets.psychic: The soul forces the breath toward stillness, the straining defeating the calm it seeks.

attributes.title: Samkhya Yoga as Stillness (Lahiri's Definition)
attributes.aspect: ·   Essence of the entire Gita The nature of this aspect. Lahiri redefines Samkhya Yoga of the Gita not as philosophical Sankhya but as the practical state achieved through pranayama: peacefulness, steadiness, and contemplation of Formless Immovable Stillness. This is not theoretical sankhya but Kriya's experiential samkhya. The expansion of Kriya naturally produces this state.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Psychic · Ontic mode: Practice / method — a means to freedom · Valence: Cultivated method · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Means · Key relation: Lahiri’s definition of yoga as inner stillness; kin to Citta-Vṛtti-Nirodha (Dim 11).
coordinate.ontic_mode: Practice / method — a means to freedom

facets.somatic: Body steady from pranayama.
facets.emotional: Emotional peace from pranic balance.
facets.cognitive: Mind contemplating Formless Immovable Stillness.
facets.psychic: The soul understands yoga as the stilling of movement, resting in the motionless awareness beneath all activity.

attributes.title: Karma without Bondage (Tena Tyaktena Bhunjithah)
attributes.aspect: तेन त्यक्तेन भुञ्जीथाः   ·   Verse 1-2 The nature of this aspect. By renouncing through action, enjoy. Perform karma in this world (as enjoined) for a hundred years—thus action does not bind you. Renunciation is not non-action but action without attachment to fruits.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital–Mental · Ontic mode: Practice / method — a means to freedom · Valence: Cultivated method · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Means · Key relation: ‘enjoy through renunciation’ (Īśāvāsya 1); kin to Karma Yoga (Dim 10) and Naiṣkarmya (Dim 16).

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.somatic: Body fully engaged in dharmic action, healthy through purposeful activity.
facets.emotional: Emotional freedom from result-anxiety, joy in doing without attachment.
facets.cognitive: Mind acts wisely, doesn't grasp outcomes, freedom in middle of activity.
facets.psychic: The soul enjoys the world by renouncing ownership of it, acting fully while claiming no fruit, and so stays free.

attributes.title: Atmahatya (Self-Slayers/Killers of the Self)
attributes.aspect: आत्महनः   ·   Verse 3 The nature of this aspect. Demoniac worlds covered with blinding darkness are inhabited by 'slayers of the Self.' These are not violent killers but those who deny their true Self through identification with body, ignorance, and worldly pursuit.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Mental · Ontic mode: Affliction — the ‘slayers of the Self’ · Valence: Affliction (the deepest error) · Guṇa: Tamas · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Distortion · Key relation: Those who slay the Self through ignorance (Īśāvāsya 3) — self-neglect as the true self-harm.

facets.emotional: Emotional connection to the deeper Self, not identified only with surface personality.
facets.cognitive: Mind oriented toward Self-realization, not just worldly knowledge.
facets.psychic: The soul heeds the Upaniṣad's warning against slaying the Self through ignorance, and turns toward self-knowledge.

attributes.title: Atma Sakshatkar (Direct Self-Knowledge)
attributes.aspect: आत्म साक्षात्कार   ·   1.2.23 The nature of this aspect. This Self cannot be attained by study of scriptures, by intellect, or by much hearing. He whom the Self chooses obtains him. To such, the Self reveals its true nature.

attributes.title: Sarvadakshina (Total Sacrifice Including Self)
attributes.aspect: सर्वदक्षिणा   ·   1.1.1-4 The nature of this aspect. Sarvadakshina is the sacrifice of everything—not just possessions but oneself. The complete giving is the necessary preparation for the final stage of life. Gautama performed it in form (giving things) but not spirit (keeping the self). Nachiketa understood the true requirement: the self too must be offered.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Vital · Ontic mode: Practice — the sacrifice in which the self is offered · Valence: Virtue / the total offering · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual, toward the Divine · Causal status: Means · Key relation: The all-giving sacrifice; kin to Yajña (Dim 28) and Atma-Nivedanam.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Practice — the sacrifice in which the self is offered
coordinate.key_relation: The all-giving sacrifice; kin to Yajña (Dim 28) and Atma-Nivedanam

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.somatic: Body engaged in genuine sacrifice without retention.
facets.emotional: Emotional readiness to surrender even attachment to self.
facets.cognitive: Mind committed to complete giving, not partial.
facets.psychic: The soul offers everything, including itself, as the final sacrificial gift, holding nothing back.

attributes.title: Threefold Self-Offering (Gross-Subtle-Causal)
attributes.aspect: त्रिविध आत्म-समर्पण   ·   1.1.4 (implicit in Nachiketa's thrice-asking) The nature of this aspect. The complete self-offering happens in three stages corresponding to the three bodies/three worlds. Physical body, subtle body (mental/astral), and causal body (root tendencies) must each be surrendered. This is what sannyasa-vow includes—renouncing pleasures of all three worlds.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Vital–Psychic · Ontic mode: Practice — offering the three bodies · Valence: Virtue / graded surrender · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual, toward the Divine · Causal status: Means · Key relation: Offering the gross, subtle, and causal selves; kin to the three bodies (Dim 4).

facets.somatic: Physical body surrendered—not destroyed but consecrated.
facets.emotional: Emotional/mental body surrendered—patterns released.
facets.cognitive: Causal/unconscious patterns surrendered through deep practice.
facets.psychic: The soul offers itself at every level — body, mind, and the root of identity — so that nothing of the separate self is withheld.

attributes.title: Bhrigu's Inquiry (Tapas as Brahman)
attributes.aspect: भृगु जिज्ञासा   ·   Bhrigu Valli 3.1-6 The nature of this aspect. Bhrigu asks his father Varuna about Brahman. Varuna says: 'That from which beings are born, by which they live, into which they return—desire to know That. That is Brahman.' Bhrigu reaches Brahman through tapas, identifying Brahman successively with food, prana, mind, intellect, bliss.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Practice / method — a means to freedom · Valence: Cultivated method · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Means · Key relation: ‘know tapas as Brahman’ (Taittirīya); kin to Bhrigu’s Tapas.

facets.cognitive: Mind progressively penetrating to subtler levels.

attributes.title: Ego-Identity Test (Witnessing Capacity)
attributes.aspect: ·   GPT diagnostic questions The nature of this aspect. Five questions reveal one's stage of spiritual development. They test the witnessing capacity—the actual evidence of progress. Without witnessing: thoughts are obeyed; desires only intensify; suffering is just endured; silence feels empty; peace depends on conditions. With witnessing: opposite of each.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: Practice / test — testing identity by the capacity to witness · Valence: Cultivated method · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Mechanism · Key relation: Whatever can be witnessed is not the Self — the witness-test of identity.
coordinate.ontic_mode: Practice / test — testing identity by the capacity to witness
coordinate.key_relation: Whatever can be witnessed is not the Self — the witness-test of identity

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Increasing capacity to witness thoughts, see through desires, question suffering, feel silence as full, find peace independent of conditions.
facets.somatic: Body actions no longer driven by every impulse.
facets.emotional: Emotional patterns seen through rather than obeyed.
facets.cognitive: Mind has witnessing space between perception and reaction.
facets.psychic: The soul tests its freedom by its capacity to witness — to watch even its strongest reactions without being swept away.

Cognitive: Mind has witnessing space between perception and reaction.

Psychic: The soul tests its freedom by its capacity to witness — to watch even its strongest reactions without being swept away.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Claiming high witnessing while showing reactive markers. Self-deception.
facets.somatic: Body still in reactive patterns despite claims.
facets.emotional: Emotional reactivity covered by spiritual rhetoric.
facets.cognitive: Mental claim of witnessing without actual capacity.
facets.psychic: The soul turns witnessing into cold detachment, watching from a defended distance.

facets.overview: No witnessing capacity. Every thought obeyed, every desire pursued, suffering only endured, silence feels empty, peace conditional.

facets.emotional: Emotional reactions are total identity.
facets.cognitive: Mind has no witness-space.
facets.psychic: The soul cannot witness at all, wholly merged with each reaction as it arises.

attributes.attribute: The Lived Experience of Practice Itself
attributes.title: The Lived Experience of Practice Itself

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Psychic · Ontic mode: State / practice — practice (sādhana) as lived · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Ground / bridge · Key relation: Genus — the lived act of practice of which all the disciplines below are forms.
coordinate.key_relation: Genus — the lived act of practice of which all the disciplines below are forms

balanced.label: Balanced — living practice that transforms

facets.overview: Held in balance, sādhana is a living thing — done regularly but not rigidly, with sincerity rather than strain, and always pointed beyond its own technique toward the realization it serves. The practice gradually saturates ordinary life: the inquiry continues in the supermarket, the remembrance hums beneath the workday, the equanimity carries into conflict. The fruit is not a special meditative state walled off from living but a steady, deepening transformation of how one inhabits every hour — the body more at ease, the emotions more workable, the mind more often resting in its ground.

balanced.text: Held in balance, sādhana is a living thing — done regularly but not rigidly, with sincerity rather than strain, and always pointed beyond its own technique toward the realization it serves. The practice gradually saturates ordinary life: the inquiry continues in the supermarket, the remembrance hums beneath the workday, the equanimity carries into conflict. The fruit is not a special meditative state walled off from living but a steady, deepening transformation of how one inhabits every hour — the body more at ease, the emotions more workable, the mind more often resting in its ground.

excess.label: In excess — the practice as performance, ambition, or rigidity

facets.overview: Practice in excess turns the means into an idol. It can become rigid and mechanical — the technique performed perfectly and emptily, the form mistaken for the substance. It can become spiritual ambition — the striving for states, powers, and milestones, the subtle competitiveness, the practice that feeds the very ego it was meant to dissolve. And it can become bypass — a retreat into practice as avoidance of the relationships and responsibilities of ordinary life. The texts repeatedly warn that practice grasped by the ego becomes one more form of bondage, however holy its appearance.

excess.text: Practice in excess turns the means into an idol. It can become rigid and mechanical — the technique performed perfectly and emptily, the form mistaken for the substance. It can become spiritual ambition — the striving for states, powers, and milestones, the subtle competitiveness, the practice that feeds the very ego it was meant to dissolve. And it can become bypass — a retreat into practice as avoidance of the relationships and responsibilities of ordinary life. The texts repeatedly warn that practice grasped by the ego becomes one more form of bondage, however holy its appearance.

deficient.label: Deficient — no practice; the truth left as mere words

facets.overview: Where practice is deficient, the vision is collected as information and never embodied. The person reads, attends, discusses — and remains unchanged, because the bridge from understanding to realization was never walked. This is the most common condition: a head full of teachings and a life run by the same old grooves, the truth admired from a distance but never made one’s own. The tradition’s pragmatists — Lahiri among them — insist bluntly on this point: practice over words; it is the doing, not the discoursing, that transforms.

additional_info.heading: 4. The Path of Inquiry (Jñāna / Ātma-Vichāra)
additional_info.text: The path of knowledge proceeds by investigation rather than belief. Its method is the three-fold śravaṇa–manana–nididhyāsana — hearing the truth from a competent source, reflecting on it until doubts are resolved, and meditating on it until it is realized. Its sharpest tool is ātma-vichāra, direct self-inquiry — the persistent turning of attention back upon its own source, asking ‘who am I?’ or ‘to whom does this thought arise?’ Its characteristic stance is sākṣi-bhāva, the witness-attitude — resting as the detached awareness in which all experience appears — and its great negative instrument is neti-neti, the discarding of all that one is not.

attributes.attribute: Witnessing as Path Out of Body
attributes.title: Witnessing as Path Out of Body
attributes.aspect: साक्षित्व   ·   Chapter 37 The nature of this aspect. Witnessing as systematic practice in everyday activities—eating, walking, working—gradually breaks body-identification. The body acts; consciousness witnesses. This dual-awareness eventually leads to out-of-body experience and freedom.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Psychic–Mental · Ontic mode: Practice / method — self-inquiry (the path of Jñāna) · Valence: Cultivated method · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Means · Key relation: witnessing as the path out of body-identification; kin to the witness (Dim 27).

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Witnessing established in every activity—body acts, consciousness watches. Life becomes a drama in which one plays roles consciously while knowing oneself as the actor, not the role.
facets.somatic: Body performs its functions with awareness; healthy engagement without identification.
facets.emotional: Emotions arise as movements of body-mind; witness remains free.
facets.cognitive: Continuous witness-awareness, recognition of activities as not-Self.
facets.psychic: The soul witnesses the body from awareness, and the simple act of watching begins to loosen the identification with flesh.

facets.cognitive: Forced witness-claim creates split between observer and observed.
facets.psychic: The soul turns witnessing into cold dissociation, splitting from the body rather than freeing itself within it.

facets.emotional: Drowning in every emotion as 'me,' no freedom.
facets.cognitive: Mind fully identified with its contents, slavery to role-identification.
facets.psychic: The soul never steps back to watch, fused to the body and its every sensation.

attributes.title: Beyond Neti-Neti (Even Negation Superfluous)
attributes.aspect: ·   XVIII.16 The nature of this aspect. Ashtavakra transcends even the famous Neti-Neti method. For one established in his teaching, the concept of the world itself transforms—seeing the world doesn't impact him. Therefore the need to negate ('not this, not this') becomes superfluous. The world is seen but no longer mistaken; the negation has already happened through recognition.

facets.cognitive: Mind beyond positive affirmation and negative denial—simply recognizing.
facets.psychic: The soul rests where even the negating 'not this' falls away, abiding as what cannot be denied.

attributes.title: Witness Consciousness (Sakshi-Drishti as Radical)
attributes.aspect: ·   Multiple verses The nature of this aspect. Ashtavakra's distinctive witness teaching: you are not in the world; the world is in you. You witness the body, senses, mind, all experience. As witness, you are unaffected by what is witnessed. This is not effortful technique but direct recognition. The witness has never moved, never changed, never been touched.

attributes.title: Kapila Lesson: Sankhya for Daily Discrimination
attributes.aspect: ·   Canto 3 - Kapila's teaching to Devahuti The nature of this aspect. Kapila Muni teaches his mother Devahuti the Sankhya philosophy — the discrimination between prakriti (matter, including body and mind) and purusha (consciousness, the witness). In daily life, this means developing the practical capacity to step back: 'What is happening is in prakriti; I am the witness.' Single most transformative cognitive shift available. Kapila taught his mother Devahuti the secret of Sankhya: prakriti and purusha. In daily life: develop the capacity to discriminate between consciousness (you) and material processes (events, body, mind). This single distinction transforms daily experience.

facets.psychic: The soul uses Sāṅkhya's discrimination daily, separating the watching Purusha from the moving play of nature.

attributes.title: Detached Perception (Sakshi-Drishti)
attributes.aspect: ·   Chapter 30 The nature of this aspect. Detached perception is a transformed mode of seeing—not the absence of seeing but seeing without entanglement. One perceives reality, including emotional reality, without immediate reactive engagement. This creates space for wise response rather than blind reaction.

facets.cognitive: Mental clarity from non-reactive perception.
facets.psychic: The soul perceives with a quiet detachment, present to experience without being captured by it.

attributes.title: Witnessing (Sakshi-Bhava)
attributes.aspect: साक्षी भाव   ·   Chapter 28, 30 The nature of this aspect. Sakshi-bhava is dual-attention—aware of object and aware of being aware. Most experience is one-pointed (lost in object); witnessing maintains both perspectives, transcending both eventually.

facets.cognitive: Thoughts move, witness sees—mind becomes transparent to awareness.
facets.psychic: The soul cultivates the witness-attitude, holding thoughts and feelings as passing weather it observes.

attributes.title: Mirror Consciousness (Pure Witness)
attributes.aspect: दर्पण चेतना   ·   Chapter 30 The nature of this aspect. Consciousness is like a mirror—it reflects all experiences but is never touched by them. The fiction is that what is reflected becomes part of us. Knowing oneself as the mirror, not the reflections, is liberation.

facets.cognitive: Mind's contents reflected, never absorbed. Always available as pure space.
facets.psychic: The soul rests as a mirror, reflecting all that passes without being stained or moved by any of it.

attributes.title: Bhuyo-Bhuya Progressive Inquiry (Ladder)
attributes.aspect: ·   Chapter 7 (26 sections) The nature of this aspect. The whole 7th chapter of Chhandogya is one continuous progressive teaching with 26 sequential meditations. Each is provisionally identified with Brahman, then transcended for a higher recognition. This forms a systematic ladder of inquiry that practitioners can climb step by step, ensuring no level is missed.

attributes.title: Bhuyo-Bhuya (More and More - Progressive Inquiry)
attributes.aspect: भूयो भूय   ·   7.1-26 (Narada-Sanatkumara dialogue) The nature of this aspect. Narada asks Sanatkumara to teach him beyond his vast learning. Sanatkumara teaches progressively: 'meditate on name' (vac), then 'higher than name is speech,' then 'higher than speech is mind,' then samkalpa, chitta, dhyana, vijnana, bala, anna, apa, tejas, akasha, smrti, asha, prana. Each step is 'bhuyo-bhuya'—more and more inquiry. Finally arrives at Bhuma (Infinite).

attributes.title: Neti-Neti (Not This, Not This)
attributes.aspect: नेति नेति   ·   2.3.6, 3.9.26, 4.2.4, 4.4.22, 4.5.15 The nature of this aspect. Of the Self one can only say 'not this, not this.' For there is nothing higher than this. Negation as the method—any specific characterization is denied because Self exceeds all conceptual definition.

attributes.title: Ātma-Vichāra — Direct Self-Inquiry
attributes.aspect: ·   Throughout text The nature of this aspect. Ātma-vichāra is the direct investigation into the nature of the self — the persistent turning of attention away from its objects and back toward the one who is aware, asking ‘who am I?’ or tracing every experience back to the ‘I’ to which it appears. Unlike practices that add something (a mantra, an image, a state), inquiry subtracts: it dissolves the false identifications until what remains is the simple, self-evident awareness that was looking all along. It is the most direct of practices and, for that reason, the one most easily turned into mere mental activity rather than living investigation.

additional_info.heading: 5. Sound and Name (Japa, Mantra, Kīrtana)
additional_info.text: Among the most universal of practices is the use of sacred sound — the repetition of a mantra (japa), the singing of the divine name (kīrtana), the meditation on OM. The tradition holds that sound is not merely symbolic but transformative: OM, the praṇava, is called the ‘sound-hammer’ that breaks the knots of the mind, and its four mātrās (A–U–M and the silence that follows) map the whole of consciousness from waking to the transcendent. The repeated name gives the restless mind a single, sanctifying object to rest on, gradually replacing the churn of ordinary thought with a current of remembrance.

attributes.title: Kirtanam (Chanting/Singing the Divine Name)
attributes.aspect: ·   7.5.23 - Second of nine bhakti processes The nature of this aspect. Kirtanam is using the voice for chanting divine names, singing devotional songs, or speaking sacred truths. In daily life, this means transforming the use of speech itself — singing during chores, chanting while walking, using verbal energy for elevation rather than dispersal. Even soft chanting (japa) under the breath counts. The vibration physically affects the body. Kirtanam means singing/chanting — using one's voice for divine names rather than gossip or complaint. In daily life: singing while cooking, walking, doing chores transforms the mood of these activities. Voice becomes instrument of devotion.

attributes.title: Saintly Company (Satsang as Daily Need)
attributes.aspect: ·   'Those who attained, how do they talk, recognize them' The nature of this aspect. The Bhagavata emphasizes the transformative power of saintly company (satsang). Signs of a saint: their conversation gravitates toward the Divine; they have compassion for all; they are one-pointed yet inclusive. In daily life this means actively seeking elevating company (in-person or through books/recordings) and minimizing degrading company. Like associates like; you become what you keep company with. Bhagavata emphasizes: keep company of saints. Recognize them by how they talk — always speaking of the Divine, compassion for all. In daily life: consciously seek satsang (saintly company); minimize toxic company. The company you keep determines what you become.

attributes.title: Dissociating Objects From Their Connections
attributes.aspect: ·   Chapter 31 The nature of this aspect. Every object comes to us pre-coded with connections—possession, function, judgment, history. Dissociating objects from these accreted connections is a profound practice. The cup becomes simply a cup, not 'my cup,' 'my mother's cup,' 'the cup I bought.' Pure perception emerges.

attributes.title: Pranava Sound (Internal Om)
attributes.aspect: ·   Anantavijaya conch interpretation The nature of this aspect. Pranava (Om) is heard internally as actual sound as Kriya progresses. Different stages produce different inner sounds: bees-buzzing, flute, lute, bell, conch, thunder, lion's roar. These are not metaphors but actual auditory experiences. The conches of the Mahabharata are Lahiri's allegory for these inner sounds.

attributes.title: Stages of Inner Sound (Nada Yoga in Kriya)
attributes.aspect: ·   Conch interpretations The nature of this aspect. As Kriya advances, the practitioner hears progressively subtler inner sounds. Lahiri identifies the Mahabharata conches as these sounds: Panchajanya=bees/flute/lute/bell/thunder; Devadatta=bell sustain; Poundra=lion roar; Anantavijaya=Pranava unbroken; Sughosha=beautiful sound; Manipushpaka=pure sound. Each is a milestone of Kriya progress.

attributes.title: OM as Akshara (Imperishable Syllable)
attributes.aspect: ओम् अक्षरम्   ·   1.2.15-17 The nature of this aspect. The goal that all Vedas declare, that all austerities aim at, that for which they live in brahmacharya—I tell briefly: it is OM. This syllable is Brahman; this syllable is supreme. Whoever knows it gets what he desires.

attributes.title: Om Meditation (Three Matras)

attributes.title: Udgitha (Meditation on Om as the Sacred Singing)
attributes.aspect: उद्गीथ   ·   1.1.1-10, 1.2-13 The nature of this aspect. Udgitha is the singing of Om—the sacred syllable as the essence of speech (Rik), prana (Saman), and life itself. 'Ut'=above, 'gi'=lifted, 'tha'=established. The whole Chapter 1 develops profound meditations on Udgitha as transformation practice. The gods conquered the demons through prana-as-Udgitha alone, the only one demons couldn't pierce with evil.

attributes.title: OM / Praṇava — the Sacred Sound
attributes.aspect: ॐ / प्रणव   ·   Chapter 26 The nature of this aspect. OM, the praṇava, is held by the tradition as the sound-form of the Absolute itself — the ‘imperishable syllable’ whose four parts (A, U, M, and the silence beyond them) correspond to the waking, dream, deep-sleep, and transcendent states. As practice, the sounding or inner repetition of OM gathers the scattered mind, harmonizes the breath, and opens, through its concluding silence, toward the wordless awareness it represents. It is at once the simplest of practices — a single syllable — and a complete map of consciousness held in sound.

additional_info.heading: 6. Meditation and Absorption (Dhyāna)
additional_info.text: Dhyāna — sustained meditation — is the practice of holding the mind one-pointedly on a chosen object (or in objectless awareness) until it absorbs. It is the culmination of the inquiry-path’s nididhyāsana and a path in its own right. The tradition offers many supports: the heart-space (as in Śāṇḍilya and Dahara Vidyā), the inner light, the space (ākāśa), the point-and-star (bindu), the inner sound. The object matters less than the steadiness; what transforms is the repeated gathering of the scattered mind into one place until the dividing line between meditator and meditated-upon begins to dissolve.

attributes.title: One-Pointedness (Daily Focus)
attributes.aspect: ·   'be one pointed, one minded' The nature of this aspect. The Bhagavata's strong teaching: one-pointedness. In modern distraction-saturated life, this is revolutionary. Choose one main practice, one main teacher's lineage, one main scripture for sustained study. Don't surface-scan across dozens. Depth produces results; surface-breadth produces only confusion. Daily life requires this single-pointed commitment in some area to bear fruit anywhere. Bhagavata insists: be one-minded. In daily life — modern distraction-addicted life — this is revolutionary. Choose one practice, one teacher, one path, and sustain. Surface-scanning across many produces nothing. Depth in one transforms.

attributes.title: Atma-Nyaya (Spike-and-Bow Meditation)
attributes.aspect: ·   Mundaka 2.2.3-4 The nature of this aspect. The famous archery meditation: Take OM as the bow, the Atman (Self) as the arrow, Brahman as the target. With one-pointed concentration, shoot the arrow. Like the arrow becoming one with the target, become one with Brahman. This is one of the most direct meditation prescriptions in Upanishads.

attributes.title: Bindu and Star Meditation
attributes.aspect: ·   Five-step essence The nature of this aspect. As Kriya advances, specific inner phenomena are perceived: Bindu (a point of inner light) and Star (a radiant star-like inner vision from throat to forehead). These are not visualizations but actual perceptions of subtle reality. Lahiri lists them as integral to the five-step essence of the entire Gita. They mark stages of progress and serve as anchors for further deepening.

facets.psychic: The soul concentrates on the inner point of light, the bindu, gathering all awareness into a single radiant center.

attributes.title: Sandilya Vidya (Heart Meditation)
attributes.aspect: शाण्डिल्य विद्या   ·   3.14.1-4 The nature of this aspect. All this is Brahman. From it all comes, into it all returns. Tranquil, one should meditate on it as Self in the heart-cave—smaller than a grain of rice, vaster than the universe. Containing all karmas, all desires, all smells, all tastes.

attributes.title: Nididhyasana (Deep Sustained Meditation)
attributes.aspect: ·   Three-stage learning The nature of this aspect. Nididhyasana is sustained deep meditation on the truth understood through shravana and manana. Beyond intellectual conviction, it is the abiding contemplation that transforms understanding into realization. This is where actual liberation happens; the previous two stages merely prepare.

attributes.title: Dhyāna — Sustained Meditation
attributes.aspect: ध्यान   ·   Chapter 6, Chapter 12 The nature of this aspect. Dhyāna is the unbroken flow of attention toward a single object or ground — the deepening of momentary concentration into sustained absorption. In the path of knowledge it appears as nididhyāsana, the prolonged dwelling upon the realized truth until it saturates one’s being. Its mark is one-pointedness: the gathering of the ordinarily scattered mind into a single, steady current, held long enough that the agitations subside and the underlying stillness shows through.

additional_info.heading: 7. The Path of Devotion (Bhakti)
additional_info.text: Bhakti — the path of love and self-offering — is for many the most accessible and the most complete of practices, engaging the heart directly. The Bhāgavatam codifies it as navavidhā bhakti, the nine-fold devotion — nine distinct ways of turning love toward the divine, any one of which, practiced wholeheartedly, suffices. Beyond technique, its essence is ātma-nivedana, the total self-offering in which the devotee surrenders not only acts and possessions but the very sense of a separate self — the bhakti path’s equivalent of the jñānī’s dissolution of the ego.

additional_info.heading: The nine limbs of devotion (Navavidhā Bhakti)
additional_info.text:
Limb

The practice

Śravaṇam

Hearing the stories and glories of the divine

Kīrtanam

Singing and chanting the divine name

Smaraṇam

Continual remembrance of the divine

Pāda-sevanam

Service at the feet — humble, loving service

Arcanam

Ritual worship and offering

Vandanam

Reverential prayer and prostration

Dāsyam

The attitude of the devoted servant

Sakhyam

Friendship and intimate companionship with the divine

Ātma-nivedanam

Total self-surrender — the offering of the whole self

The nine are not a ladder to be climbed in order but a spectrum of temperaments: some hearts open through hearing, others through service, others through the intimacy of friendship or the completeness of surrender. The tradition’s generosity is that each is a full path — and that the simplest, sincerely practiced, carries as far as the most elaborate.

attributes.title: Arcanam (Daily Worship/Ritual)
attributes.aspect: ·   7.5.23 - Fifth of nine bhakti processes The nature of this aspect. Arcanam is formal worship — daily lighting of lamps, offering of flowers, food, prayer at a household altar or temple. In modern life this can be as simple as five minutes daily before a small altar with photo/image of one's chosen deity. The discipline of daily worship creates a sacred zone and anchor in daily life that strengthens all other practices. Arcanam is daily worship — lighting a lamp, offering flowers, ringing a bell, offering food. Even five minutes daily transforms a home. Creates a 'sacred zone' anchor in daily life. Outer worship strengthens inner orientation.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Physical–Vital · Ontic mode: Practice — a contemplative / devotional discipline · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual, toward the Divine · Causal status: Means · Key relation: daily worship and ritual (arcanam); a limb of Bhakti.

attributes.title: Narada Lesson: Daily Devotional Travel (Bhakti Movement)
attributes.aspect: ·   Canto 1 - Narada's life-story The nature of this aspect. Narada Muni, the divine sage, travels through all worlds carrying his veena and singing the divine name. He is the model of devotional movement — bhakti is not confined to temple but moves with one. In daily life, this means taking devotion with you everywhere: at work, in errands, in meetings. The work, market, meetings become sites of devotion through inner orientation. Narada travels the worlds singing 'Narayana, Narayana,' bringing devotion wherever he goes. In daily life: take devotion with you wherever you go. Be a 'walking satsang.' Devotion is portable; it goes with you to work, market, errands.

attributes.title: Vandanam — Prayer as the Heart’s Offering
attributes.aspect: ·   7.5.23 - Sixth of nine bhakti processes The nature of this aspect. Vandanam is the offering of prayer — both the formal recitation of sacred verses and the informal, intimate conversation of the heart with the divine. As one of the nine limbs of devotion, it is the daily unburdening in which the devotee lays their concerns, their gratitude, and ultimately their very self before something larger than themselves; in its deepest reach it shades into ātma-nivedana, the total self-offering in which not only one’s words but one’s whole selfhood is surrendered. Sincerely practiced, prayer is the simplest and most available doorway of bhakti.

Cognitive: Mind learns to bring concerns to the Divine.

Psychic: The soul unburdens itself in prayer, laying its concerns and its very self before something larger; in its depth, prayer shades into total self-offering.

facets.cognitive: Mind treating prayer transactionally.
facets.psychic: Prayer shrinks to petition; the soul reduces the sacred conversation to anxious asking and bargaining, the relationship lost in the request.

facets.cognitive: Mind has no Divine listener.
facets.psychic: The soul prays not at all; carrying every burden alone, it stays closed to the relief and the larger relationship prayer would open.

additional_info.heading: 8. The Path of Sacred Action (Karma-Yoga / Seva)
additional_info.text: For the active temperament, the path is not withdrawal but transformed engagement — karma-yoga, action offered without attachment to its fruits, so that the very work of life becomes a practice. Daily life is reframed as yajña, a continual sacred offering; service (seva, pāda-sevanam) becomes worship in motion; and the whole economy of one’s living is held in trusteeship — using what one needs and holding the rest in trust — with aparigraha, non-possessiveness, as its liberating principle. The Gītā’s great teaching anchors it: act, but renounce attachment to the outcome (tena tyaktena bhuñjīthāḥ).

attributes.title: Pada-Sevanam (Service to the Divine)
attributes.aspect: ·   7.5.23 - Fourth of nine bhakti processes The nature of this aspect. Pada-sevanam means serving the feet of the Divine — symbolically serving the Lord, practically serving all beings as forms of the Divine. In daily life this means transforming ordinary acts of service (cooking for family, helping at work, caring for ill, even paying bills) into sacred offerings. The transformation is in the inner orientation, not the outer act. Pada-sevanam is service — serving the feet of the Lord, but also serving all beings as forms of the Divine. In daily life: every act of service becomes worship. Caring for family, helping colleagues, kindness to strangers — all become spiritual practice when offered to the Divine.

attributes.title: Yagya Lesson: Daily Life as Sacred Offering
attributes.aspect: ·   Canto 4 - Yagya avatar The nature of this aspect. The Yagya avatar embodies sacred offering. In daily life, the principle: every act can be transformed into yagya by inner orientation. Eating becomes yagya when food is mentally offered first. Work becomes yagya when results are released. Speech becomes yagya when words are offered as worship. The outer act is unchanged; the inner transformation is total. Yagya avatar personifies the spirit of sacrifice. In daily life: every act becomes yagya when offered as sacred. Eating becomes yagya (food offered first); work becomes yagya (offering of effort); even sleep becomes yagya (offering of body to rest).

attributes.title: Kriya Yoga (The Action Technique)
attributes.aspect: ·   Throughout Lahiri's Gita commentaries The nature of this aspect. Kriya Yoga is the direct, practical technique of self-realization revealed by Mahavatar Babaji to Lahiri Mahasay. It is not philosophical inquiry but a concrete pranayama-meditation method that operates directly on the spine, breath, and attention. The technique works without intellectual mediation; results are felt, seen, and known directly in the body-mind.

attributes.title: Karma-Yoga — Daily Life as Sacred Offering
attributes.aspect: ·   Canto 10 - Krishna The nature of this aspect. Karma-yoga transforms ordinary action into practice by changing not what one does but the spirit in which it is done: acting wholeheartedly while offering the fruits, so that work becomes a continual sacrifice (yajña) rather than a means of acquisition. Its genius is that it requires no withdrawal from life — the office, the kitchen, the duties of family all become the field of practice. Its discipline is the steady relinquishing of attachment to outcomes, which frees action from anxiety and turns the whole of daily life into a sacred offering.

additional_info.heading: 9. The Foundational Supports
additional_info.text: Beneath and around the four paths run a set of supporting disciplines that the tradition treats as nearly indispensable. Mauna, inner and outer silence, quiets the noise in which clarity cannot arise. Tapas, austerity, builds the inner heat and concentrated will that practice requires. Brahmacharya, the wise stewardship of vital energy, conserves the force that practice channels. Satsaṅga, the company of the wise and sincere, is named as a daily need — the single most reliable support, since we become like those we keep company with. And abhyāsa, sustained practice over time, is the quiet condition of all the rest: not intensity but consistency, the patient return to the practice day after day, is what finally wears the new groove.

attributes.title: Right Channelisation of Energy
attributes.aspect: ·   Chapter 20 The nature of this aspect. Energy is a finite resource. Where we direct it determines what we become. Downward channelisation (sense-objects, gossip, anger, worry) dissipates and degrades. Upward channelisation (contemplation, service, devotion) accumulates and elevates. Skilled energy-channeling is essential to lived spirituality.

attributes.title: Harmonisation of Mind and Breath
attributes.aspect: ·   Chapter 24 The nature of this aspect. Mind and breath are two faces of the same vital force. Their movements mirror each other—anxious thoughts produce shallow breath; deep breath calms the mind. Conscious harmonisation creates a powerful spiritual lever. Through breath, the mind is reached; through the mind, breath is regulated.

attributes.title: Sanak Kumara Lesson: Brahmacharya for Daily Conscience
attributes.aspect: ·   Canto 3 - Four Kumaras The nature of this aspect. The First Avatar (Four Kumaras) teaches brahmacharya as the foundational quality. In daily life, this is not just sexual restraint but the broader principle of energy-conservation — not dissipating life-energy in trivial sense-pursuits, gossip, mental wandering. Energy conserved is energy available for the highest purpose. Purifies all four inner instruments. The Four Kumaras (Sanak, Sanandan, Sanatan, Sanatkumar) embody brahmacharya — not just celibacy but conserving energy for the highest purpose. In daily life: don't dissipate energy carelessly; conserve for the most important. Purifies manas, buddhi, chit, ahankar.

attributes.title: Varaha Lesson: Santosha (Contentment in Daily Life)
attributes.aspect: ·   Canto 3 - Varaha The nature of this aspect. Varaha's avatar teaches santosha (contentment in any situation). The boar-form Vishnu lifts the earth from the cosmic waters — symbolically, lifting consciousness above immediate difficulties. In daily life, santosha is not passivity but the capacity to remain inwardly content regardless of external circumstances. This is the foundation upon which all spiritual progress is built. Varaha lifts the earth from cosmic waters — symbol of santosha (contentment) in all situations. The lesson for daily life: like Varaha lifting earth, lift one's perspective above difficulties. Remain contented whatever situation arises.

attributes.title: Abhyasa (Sustained Practice)
attributes.aspect: अभ्यास   ·   Sutra 1.12, 1.13, 1.14 The nature of this aspect. Abhyasa is sustained practice—the persistent effort to remain in the yogic state. Patanjali says it must be done for a long time, without interruption, with reverence to become firmly grounded (Sutra 1.14).

facets.psychic: The soul gives itself to steady, long-continued practice, trusting that repetition alone bears fruit.

additional_info.heading: 10. Avatar-Modeled Lessons for Daily Life
additional_info.text:
The Bhāgavatam offers a distinctive form of practical teaching: each divine descent (avatāra) models a specific quality to be practiced in ordinary life. These translate lofty principles into memorable, daily reminders.

Avatar lesson

The daily practice it models

Rāma — Maryādā

Living within sacred limits; integrity and right boundaries

Kṛṣṇa — Karma-Yoga

Skillful, detached action in the midst of the world

Narasiṁha — Protection

Trust in divine protection in moments of crisis

Vāmana — Humility

Humility before the divine; the smallness that contains all

Paraśurāma — Righteous force

Distinguishing righteous firmness from personal rage

Ṛṣabha — Conduct

Personal conduct (ācaraṇa) as the ground of practice

Varāha — Contentment

Santoṣa — contentment in daily life

attributes.title: Rishabha Lesson: Personal Conduct (Acharana)
attributes.aspect: ·   Canto 5 - Rishabhadeva The nature of this aspect. Rishabhadeva's avatar teaches that spirituality manifests in everyday conduct (acharana). It is not theoretical knowledge but how you behave — how you speak to your servant, how you eat, how you keep your room, how you respond to inconvenience. Rishabha lived radical conduct that exemplified his teaching. In daily life: examine your micro-behaviors; that's where your real spirituality shows. Rishabhadeva embodies the spirit of personal conduct (acharana). In daily life: how you conduct yourself moment-to-moment is your real spirituality. Theory means nothing without exemplary conduct in small daily things.

attributes.title: Narasimha Lesson: Divine Protection in Crisis
attributes.aspect: ·   Canto 7 - Narasimha and Prahlada The nature of this aspect. The Narasimha story teaches: when ordinary protection fails, sincere devotion calls forth extraordinary divine response. Prahlada's father tried every form of harm; nothing succeeded because Prahlada had pure faith. When the crisis reached its peak, Narasimha emerged from a pillar — utterly unexpected. In daily life: in deepest crisis, sincere prayer is never abandoned. Help comes, often in unexpected forms. Narasimha appears from a pillar when no one else can save Prahlada. The lesson: divine protection comes in seemingly impossible circumstances. When ordinary help fails, sincere call to the Divine is answered in unexpected forms.

facets.cognitive: Mind anchored in trust even when help isn't visible.
facets.psychic: The soul trusts that the Divine meets it in its deepest crisis, as Narasiṁha met Prahlāda, and so faces danger unafraid.

attributes.title: Vamana Lesson: Humility Before the Divine
attributes.aspect: ·   Canto 8 - Vamana and Bali The nature of this aspect. Vamana's story teaches humility at the height of prosperity. King Bali had conquered heaven itself, yet when Vamana (a small brahmin boy) asked for three steps of land, Bali agreed. When the Divine grew immense and covered all three worlds with three steps, Bali offered his head for the third step. The lesson: prosperity does not require pride; surrender what you possess when called. King Bali, even when prosperous, accepted Vamana's three steps and offered his head when no other ground remained. The lesson: surrender what you possess when the Divine asks; humility at the height of success is the highest virtue.

facets.cognitive: Mind sees possessions as Divine's loan.
facets.psychic: The soul keeps a true humility before the Divine, like Vāmana's small form that yet spanned the worlds.

attributes.title: Parashurama Lesson: Righteous Anger (Vs. Personal Rage)
attributes.aspect: ·   Canto 9 - Parashurama The nature of this aspect. Parashurama, the warrior-brahmin, used force against corrupt rulers but his anger was always dharmic — for justice, not personal grievance. The lesson for daily life: not all anger is wrong. Anger about injustice, abuse, dharma-violation has its place. But ego-anger (someone offended me, I didn't get my way) is destructive. Discriminate between these two qualitatively different forms. Parashurama destroyed corrupt Kshatriyas but his anger was dharmic, not personal. The lesson: anger has a place when it's about injustice and dharma, not personal grievance. Discriminate between righteous indignation and ego-anger.

facets.cognitive: Mind discriminates between dharmic and personal anger.
facets.psychic: The soul learns to wield clean, righteous anger in service of justice, distinct from the heat of personal rage.

attributes.title: Rama Lesson: Maryada (Living Within Sacred Limits)
attributes.aspect: ·   Canto 9 - Rama The nature of this aspect. Rama is Maryada Purushottama — the supreme example of living within sacred limits (maryada). His exile to forest at father's word, his treatment of wife, his rule as king — all exemplify dharmic limits voluntarily honored. In daily life: keep your word, honor commitments, follow dharmic protocols even when no one is watching. Sacred limits create dignity. Sri Rama is Maryada Purushottama — the perfect man living within sacred limits. The lesson: follow dharma even when difficult; honor one's word; live with sacred limits even when free to transgress. Daily integrity matters.

Cognitive: Mind constantly distracted, no continuity of presence.

Psychic: The soul drifts in negligence, the truth slipping away again and again for want of attention.

additional_info.heading: 10b. Further Practices
additional_info.text: Beyond the principal families, the corpus is rich with further practices — modes of listening and remembrance, energetic and kriyā techniques, attitudes of service and friendship, disciplines of silence and dispassion, and the supports of the teacher and the sacred text. Each is examined below in its balanced, excessive, and deficient expression.

attributes.title: Choiceless Awareness (Mind as Choice-Maker)
attributes.sanskrit: अविकल्प चेतना   ·   Chapter 40 - 'Be free of choices'
attributes.aspect: अविकल्प चेतना   ·   Chapter 40 - 'Be free of choices' The nature of this aspect. Mind functions through choice—dividing reality into pairs and choosing one. But the pairs are one continuum. To choose love is to choose hate's eventual arrival. Either embrace both, or remain choiceless. Both dissolve the dividing mind.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental–Psychic · Ontic mode: State — an attained condition · Valence: The fruit · Guṇa: Sattva, tending beyond · Scale: Individual, transcendent · Causal status: Fruit · Key relation: choiceless awareness — prior to the choosing mind; kin to Choicelessness (Dim 28).
coordinate.key_relation: choiceless awareness — prior to the choosing mind; kin to Choicelessness (Dim 28)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Choiceless awareness—seeing all opposites as one continuum. Mind dissolves, existence appears as one. Life and death, love and hate accepted as one process.
facets.somatic: Body relaxed without internal conflict between preferences.
facets.emotional: Emotional acceptance of all polarities, peace beyond preference.
facets.cognitive: Mind beyond duality, choiceless witness, freedom from internal civil war.
facets.psychic: The soul rests in choiceless awareness, watching preferences arise and pass without being driven by them.

balanced.text:
Choiceless awareness—seeing all opposites as one continuum. Mind dissolves, existence appears as one. Life and death, love and hate accepted as one process.

Somatic: Body relaxed without internal conflict between preferences.

Emotional: Emotional acceptance of all polarities, peace beyond preference.

Cognitive: Mind beyond duality, choiceless witness, freedom from internal civil war.

Psychic: The soul rests in choiceless awareness, watching preferences arise and pass without being driven by them.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Forced 'choicelessness' pretending no preferences while internally still divided. False detachment.
facets.somatic: Body's natural preferences suppressed.
facets.emotional: Pretended neutrality covering real choosing.
facets.cognitive: Intellectual choicelessness without genuine realization.
facets.psychic: The soul makes 'choicelessness' an excuse for passivity, refusing necessary decisions.

excess.text:
Forced 'choicelessness' pretending no preferences while internally still divided. False detachment.

Somatic: Body's natural preferences suppressed.

Emotional: Pretended neutrality covering real choosing.

Cognitive: Intellectual choicelessness without genuine realization.

Psychic: The soul makes 'choicelessness' an excuse for passivity, refusing necessary decisions.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Constant compulsive choosing—every event triggers choice between preferences. Maximum mental noise and suffering.
facets.somatic: Body in tension between preferring/rejecting.
facets.emotional: Emotional turmoil from constant choice-making.
facets.cognitive: Mind in perpetual civil war between opposites.
facets.psychic: The soul is wholly identified with the choosing mind, swept by every like and dislike.

deficient.text:
Constant compulsive choosing—every event triggers choice between preferences. Maximum mental noise and suffering.

Somatic: Body in tension between preferring/rejecting.

Emotional: Emotional turmoil from constant choice-making.

Cognitive: Mind in perpetual civil war between opposites.

Psychic: The soul is wholly identified with the choosing mind, swept by every like and dislike.

attributes.title: Right Listening (Shravana)
attributes.sanskrit: श्रवण   ·   Chapter 44 - First of four steps
attributes.aspect: श्रवण   ·   Chapter 44 - First of four steps The nature of this aspect. Shravana is not casual hearing but total listening with one's whole being. The mind goes silent; only receptivity remains. This kind of listening allows the secret teachings (TAT TWAMASI) to penetrate the disciple's being like seeds entering a prepared womb.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental · Ontic mode: Practice — a contemplative / devotional discipline · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Means · Key relation: right listening to the teaching (śravaṇa) — the first move of Jñāna.
coordinate.key_relation: right listening to the teaching (śravaṇa) — the first move of Jñāna

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Becoming pure receptivity—the whole being listening, no thinking interfering. Teachings enter as seeds into ready ground.
facets.somatic: Body completely still in listening posture, every cell receptive.
facets.emotional: Emotional openness to teaching, surrender of resistance.
facets.cognitive: Mind completely silent during listening, total presence.
facets.psychic: The soul listens rightly to the truth, receptive and attentive, letting the teaching actually land.

balanced.text:
Becoming pure receptivity—the whole being listening, no thinking interfering. Teachings enter as seeds into ready ground.

Somatic: Body completely still in listening posture, every cell receptive.

Emotional: Emotional openness to teaching, surrender of resistance.

Cognitive: Mind completely silent during listening, total presence.

Psychic: The soul listens rightly to the truth, receptive and attentive, letting the teaching actually land.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Mechanical listening pretending to be total—physically present but mentally constructing arguments or comparing with own knowledge.
facets.somatic: Body present but tense, false receptivity.
facets.emotional: Hidden resistance behind apparent openness.
facets.cognitive: Mind silently arguing while pretending to listen.
facets.psychic: The soul listens to accumulate or to debate, the hearing feeding the intellect rather than the heart.

excess.text:
Mechanical listening pretending to be total—physically present but mentally constructing arguments or comparing with own knowledge.

Somatic: Body present but tense, false receptivity.

Emotional: Hidden resistance behind apparent openness.

Cognitive: Mind silently arguing while pretending to listen.

Psychic: The soul listens to accumulate or to debate, the hearing feeding the intellect rather than the heart.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Fragmentary listening—physical ears hearing while mind elsewhere. No transformation possible.
facets.somatic: Body present but attention scattered.
facets.emotional: No emotional opening to teaching.
facets.cognitive: Mind running parallel commentary, not receiving.
facets.psychic: The soul never truly listens, the truth bouncing off a closed or distracted mind.

deficient.text:
Fragmentary listening—physical ears hearing while mind elsewhere. No transformation possible.

Somatic: Body present but attention scattered.

Emotional: No emotional opening to teaching.

Cognitive: Mind running parallel commentary, not receiving.

Psychic: The soul never truly listens, the truth bouncing off a closed or distracted mind.

attributes.title: Right Thinking (Yes-Saying)
attributes.aspect: मनन   ·   Chapter 44 - Second step The nature of this aspect. Right thinking is not no-saying but yes-saying. Most thinking starts with 'no'—because no strengthens ego. Right thinking reverses this: yes is the starting point; reason is used to support yes; no comes only when truly impossible to say yes.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Mental · Ontic mode: Practice — a contemplative / devotional discipline · Valence: Cultivated practice · Guṇa: Sattva-dominant · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Means · Key relation: affirmative right thinking — the ‘yes-saying’ mind.
coordinate.key_relation: affirmative right thinking — the ‘yes-saying’ mind

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Yes-orientation as foundation. Reason is used to find how to say yes. Receptivity replaces resistance. Profound relaxation in being.
facets.somatic: Body relaxed in yes-orientation, no chronic resistance-tension.
facets.emotional: Emotional openness, warmth, embracing.
facets.cognitive: Mind oriented toward acceptance, using reason to support yes-saying.
facets.psychic: The soul meets life with a fundamental yes, an affirmation that opens rather than resists.

balanced.text:
Yes-orientation as foundation. Reason is used to find how to say yes. Receptivity replaces resistance. Profound relaxation in being.

Somatic: Body relaxed in yes-orientation, no chronic resistance-tension.

Emotional: Emotional openness, warmth, embracing.

Cognitive: Mind oriented toward acceptance, using reason to support yes-saying.

Psychic: The soul meets life with a fundamental yes, an affirmation that opens rather than resists.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Indiscriminate yes-saying—agreeing to everything, losing discrimination. Or saying yes while inwardly resisting.
facets.somatic: Body forced into accepting what damages it.
facets.emotional: Emotional doormat, accepting abuse.
facets.cognitive: Loss of healthy critical faculty, blanket affirmation.
facets.psychic: The soul's positivity turns to forced denial of real difficulty, a yes that papers over the no.

excess.text:
Indiscriminate yes-saying—agreeing to everything, losing discrimination. Or saying yes while inwardly resisting.

Somatic: Body forced into accepting what damages it.

Emotional: Emotional doormat, accepting abuse.

Cognitive: Loss of healthy critical faculty, blanket affirmation.

Psychic: The soul's positivity turns to forced denial of real difficulty, a yes that papers over the no.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Compulsive no-saying as identity—every question first met with denial. Ego built on rejection.
facets.somatic: Body in chronic resistance, tense, defensive.
facets.emotional: Emotional walls, rejection as identity.
facets.cognitive: Mind oriented toward finding what to reject, contraction.
facets.psychic: The soul meets life with chronic negation, a reflexive no that closes every door.

deficient.text:
Compulsive no-saying as identity—every question first met with denial. Ego built on rejection.

Somatic: Body in chronic resistance, tense, defensive.

Emotional: Emotional walls, rejection as identity.

Cognitive: Mind oriented toward finding what to reject, contraction.

attributes.title: Tantric Approach to Desire (Awareness Not Suppression)
attributes.aspect: तन्त्र-वैराग्य   ·   Chapter 46 The nature of this aspect. Authentic non-attachment is not suppression or escape but the dissolution of attraction through complete awareness. Tantra moves into desire-objects with awareness—not indulgent but observant. The full conscious experience reveals the truth that breaks the spell.

coordinate.raw: Seat: Vital · Ontic mode: Framework — a vision / distinction · Valence: Neutral — a pointer to be realized · Guṇa: Sattva · Scale: Individual · Causal status: Framework · Key relation: meeting desire with awareness, not suppression (the tantric approach).
coordinate.valence: Neutral — a pointer to be realized
coordinate.key_relation: meeting desire with awareness, not suppression (the tantric approach)

balanced.label: Balanced / refined

facets.overview: Aware engagement with desire-objects until they reveal their true nature. Attraction dissolves through complete seeing, not suppression. Genuine vairagya.
facets.somatic: Body engages without bondage, knowing the nature of objects.
facets.emotional: Emotional freedom from compulsive attraction, neither suppression nor indulgence.
facets.cognitive: Mind clear about nature of objects through awareness, naturally non-grasping.
facets.psychic: The soul meets desire with awareness rather than suppression, letting the energy be seen and so transformed.

balanced.text:
Aware engagement with desire-objects until they reveal their true nature. Attraction dissolves through complete seeing, not suppression. Genuine vairagya.

Somatic: Body engages without bondage, knowing the nature of objects.

Emotional: Emotional freedom from compulsive attraction, neither suppression nor indulgence.

Cognitive: Mind clear about nature of objects through awareness, naturally non-grasping.

Psychic: The soul meets desire with awareness rather than suppression, letting the energy be seen and so transformed.

excess.label: In excess

facets.overview: Indulgence claiming to be tantric awareness—using the doctrine to justify endless engagement without real awareness.
facets.somatic: Body in indulgence claiming awareness.
facets.emotional: Emotional indulgence without genuine seeing.
facets.cognitive: Self-deception about doing 'tantric practice' that is just indulgence.
facets.psychic: The soul uses 'awareness not suppression' to license indulgence, the watching merely nominal.

excess.text:
Indulgence claiming to be tantric awareness—using the doctrine to justify endless engagement without real awareness.

deficient.label: Deficient

facets.overview: Closed-eye renunciation that strengthens desire—escaping to monastery while mind obsesses with desires. False vairagya.
facets.somatic: Body suppressed while mind obsesses.
facets.emotional: Suppressed emotions seeking outlet.
facets.cognitive: Mind more obsessed with what is denied than what is enjoyed.
facets.psychic: The soul only suppresses its desires, the forced denial breeding the frustration it meant to escape.

Emotional: Suppressed emotions seeking outlet.
